topics.title: Fundamentals of Visual Testing, Tools, Applications, and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Visual Testing, Tools, Applications, and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Visual Testing (VT)**, also termed **Visual Inspection (VI)** or **Visual Examination (VE)**, is defined by **ASTM E1316** and **ASME Section V, Article 9** as the qualitative observation and quantitative measurement of the surface condition of a component, material, or assembly using the unaided human eye or optical aids, performed to evaluate the presence, character, and severity of surface-breaking discontinuities, geometric deviations, and service-induced damage **without altering the part's fitness for service**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMET724)** context, VT is positioned as the **primary, first-line NDT technique** that must be executed **before, during, and after** the application of any other NDT method (MT, PT, UT, RT) — establishing the baseline of any integrity assessment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Doctor's Eye"

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before ordering an MRI, CT scan, or blood test, the doctor **first looks** at the patient — observes skin color, swelling, posture, gait, and external symptoms. This **first-pass observation** is fast, cheap, and rules out obvious problems. VT plays the identical role in engineering: the inspector acts as a "doctor for machines," using sight as the primary diagnostic tool before recommending costlier tests like radiography or ultrasonics.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** VT accounts for nearly **80–90% of all NDT inspections** performed in industry by volume, simply because almost every other NDT method *requires* a prior visual examination of the surface.

## 1.3 Classification of Visual Testing (Hierarchical View)

```
Visual Testing (VT)
├── 1. Direct Visual Testing (DVT)  → Eye / optical aid within 600 mm of surface
├── 2. Remote Visual Testing (RVT)  → Aid required (borescope, fiberscope, videoscope)
└── 3. Enhanced / Computer-Assisted VT → Digital image capture, AI defect recognition
```

- **DVT (Direct Visual Testing):** Inspector's eye is within **600 mm** of the test surface, with viewing angle ≥ **30°** to the surface. Governed by **ASME Section V, T-641**.
- **RVT (Remote Visual Testing):** Used when the area is inaccessible — uses **borescopes, fiberscopes, videoscopes, or pan-and-tilt cameras**. Governed by **ASME Section V, T-642**.

## 1.4 Standards Governing Visual Testing

| Standard | Scope / Application |
|---|---|
| **ASTM E1316** | Standard Terminology for NDT |
| **ASTM E337** | Standard Practice for Visual Examination of Welds |
| **ASME Section V, Article 9** | Visual Examination (Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code) |
| **ASME B&PV Sec. VIII, UW-35** | VT acceptance for welded pressure vessels |
| **AWS D1.1 / D1.5** | Visual inspection criteria for welds (steel & bridges) |
| **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** | Personnel qualification and certification |
| **ISO 9712** | Qualification of NDT personnel |
| **API 1104** | Pipeline welding inspection |

---

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes understanding the **physics of light, human visual acuity, tooling, and the limitations** that determine *when* VT is sufficient and *when* it must be supplemented with other NDT methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Visual Perception — Why VT Works

The human eye functions as a complex optical system. Understanding the physics behind visual acuity is essential for KTU examination questions on "limitations of VT."

### 2.1.1 The Human Eye as an Optical Instrument

The eye's near-point distance (the closest a healthy eye can focus) is standardized as **D = 250 mm (25 cm)**. Visual acuity is measured using the **Snellen fraction** — the standard reference being **20/20 vision** in the Imperial system (equivalent to **6/6** in the metric system).

### 2.1.2 Snellen Fraction Definition

$$\text{Visual Acuity} = \frac{\text{Test Distance (ft or m)}}{\text{Distance at which a normal eye reads the same line (ft or m)}}$$

- A person with **20/20 vision** can read at **20 feet** what a normal eye reads at **20 feet**.
- A person with **20/40 vision** must view from **20 feet** what a normal eye can read from **40 feet** — i.e., **half the visual acuity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Snellen Acuity 20/20 ≈ Angular Resolution of 1 arc-minute (1/60°)** — this is the **diffraction-limited resolution** of the human eye for high-contrast targets.

## 2.2 Light, Illumination & Wavelength Considerations

For VT, illumination level is **not optional — it is regulated**. ASME and AWS both specify minimum lighting levels in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**.

- **Minimum recommended:** **500 lux (≈ 50 foot-candles)** for general VT.
- **High-contrast precision inspection:** **1000–1500 lux**.
- **Direct sunlight:** ≈ **50,000 lux** (overkill but useful for outdoor work).

> 1 **foot-candle (fc) = 10.76 lux**

### 2.2.1 Wavelength Dependency on Surface Defect Detection

Defect visibility improves with **shorter wavelengths** (blue/UV) because of superior **diffraction-limited resolution**. The **Rayleigh criterion** governs the smallest resolvable detail:

$$\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = **angular resolution** (radians) — smallest angle the eye can resolve
- $\lambda$ = **wavelength of light** (m)
- $D$ = **diameter of the eye's pupil** (m) — typically **2–4 mm** in bright light, up to **7 mm** in darkness

For a normal eye under bright daylight (D = 2 mm, $\lambda$ = 550 nm green-yellow peak):

$$\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 1.15 \text{ arc-minute}$$

This **≈ 1 arc-minute** is the physical basis of the **20/20 Snellen standard**.

## 2.3 Magnification Theory of VT Optical Aids

### 2.3.1 Magnifying Glass (Simple Magnifier)

The angular magnification M of a simple magnifier, when the image forms at the **near point (D = 250 mm)**, is given by:

$$M = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

When the image is relaxed (formed at infinity, less eye strain):

$$M = \frac{D}{f}$$

Where:
- $D$ = **near-point distance** of the eye = **250 mm**
- $f$ = **focal length** of the magnifier (mm)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Worked Micro-Example:** A 50 mm focal length magnifier gives $M = 1 + 250/50 = \mathbf{6\times}$ — meaning a 0.5 mm crack appears as a 3 mm detail to the inspector.

### 2.3.2 Compound Microscope (Used in Metallurgical VT)

For microscopes used in **fractographic / metallographic visual examination**:

$$M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{objective}} \times M_{\text{eyepiece}} = -\frac{L}{f_{\text{obj}}} \times \frac{D}{f_{\text{eye}}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = **optical tube length** (standard **160 mm** or **∞-corrected**)
- $f_{\text{obj}}$ = **focal length of objective**
- $f_{\text{eye}}$ = **focal length of eyepiece**

### 2.3.3 Borescope / Fiberscope — Field of View & Resolution

The **Field of View (FOV)** of a borescope tip is a cone defined by the **angle of view** $\alpha$ (typically **30° to 120°**):

$$\text{FOV diameter at distance } L = 2 \cdot L \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

The **Depth of Field (DOF)** — the range over which the image appears acceptably sharp — depends on aperture $A$, object distance $L$, and the acceptable **circle of confusion $c$**:

$$\text{DOF} \approx 2 \cdot N \cdot c \cdot \left(1 + \frac{L}{f}\right)$$

Where $N$ = f-number, $c$ = permissible circle of confusion (typically **0.025 mm** for CCD sensors).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$ | Rayleigh criterion | Min. resolvable defect width |
| 2 | $M = 1 + D/f$ | Simple magnifier | Power of a hand lens |
| 3 | $M = D/f$ | Magnifier at infinity | Comfortable magnification |
| 4 | $M_{\text{total}} = M_o \times M_e$ | Compound optics | Microscope / borescope |
| 5 | $\text{FOV} = 2 L \tan(\alpha/2)$ | Borescope cone | Viewing area at distance L |
| 6 | $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$ | Photometric | Light conversion |
| 7 | 20/20 = 1 arc-minute | Snellen standard | Eye acuity benchmark |
| 8 | $\text{Inspection time} = A / (v \cdot w)$ | Coverage | Time = Area / (speed × width) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** If asked to compute a **magnification**, always specify whether the image is at the **near point (D = 250 mm)** or at **infinity (relaxed eye)**. The choice changes the formula by the additive **+1** term.

## 2.5 Operational Procedure (KTU Board Exam Frequently Asked)

The standard sequence of any VT, per **ASME Sec. V, T-641**:

1. **Review documentation** → Drawings, specs, codes, prior reports.
2. **Prepare the surface** → Clean, remove oil, scale, paint if necessary.
3. **Ensure adequate lighting** → Minimum **500 lux**, no glare.
4. **Establish viewing geometry** → Eye within **600 mm**, angle ≥ **30°**.
5. **Perform systematic scan** → Pattern (e.g., parallel, cross-hatch, circumferential).
6. **Identify & characterize defects** → Type, size, location.
7. **Compare against acceptance criteria** → Per code (AWS, ASME, etc.).
8. **Document & report** → Photograph, sketch, location map.
9. **Mark defects if required** → Dye, chalk, or low-stress punch.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** VT is the **only** NDT method legally mandatory *before* all other NDT in many codes (ASME, AWS). Failure to perform VT first is one of the most common **Audit Non-Conformities** in ISO 9001 / 3834 certified fabrication shops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Detectable Crack Width from Human Visual Acuity

We will derive the **smallest crack width visible to the naked eye** under standard lighting, which is a classic KTU numerical problem.

### Given Data
- Eye-to-object distance: $L_e = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ (near point)
- Standard Snellen acuity: 20/20, i.e., $\theta_{\min} = 1 \text{ arc-minute}$
- Conversion: $1 \text{ arc-minute} = \dfrac{\pi}{180 \times 60} \text{ rad} = 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$

### Step 1 — Convert Acuity to Radians

$$\theta_{\min} = 1 \text{ arc-minute} = \frac{\pi}{10800} \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

*Conversion logic: 1 full revolution = $2\pi$ rad = 360° = 21,600 arc-minutes. So 1 arc-minute = $2\pi / 21600$ rad.*

### Step 2 — Apply the Small-Angle Approximation

For small angles, the **arc length $s$** on a circle of radius $r$ is:

$$s = r \cdot \theta$$

When the eye views an object at distance $L_e$, the smallest resolvable feature length $w_{\min}$ is:

$$w_{\min} = L_e \cdot \theta_{\min}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$w_{\min} = 0.25 \text{ m} \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

$$w_{\min} = 7.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0727 \text{ mm} \approx 73 \text{ μm}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** The human eye, at 20/20 acuity, can theoretically resolve a **feature ≈ 73 μm wide** when held at the near point. In practice (with glare, fatigue, low contrast, dirty surfaces), the working limit is **0.1–0.2 mm**. **This is the physical reason VT cannot reliably detect sub-100 μm cracks.**

### Step 5 — Effect of a Magnifier

If the inspector uses a 10× magnifier at the near point:

$$M = 1 + \frac{250}{f} \Rightarrow f = \frac{250}{M - 1} = \frac{250}{9} \approx 27.8 \text{ mm}$$

Effective minimum crack width detectable:

$$w_{\min}^{\text{with mag}} = \frac{0.0727 \text{ mm}}{10} \approx 7.27 \text{ μm}$$

In practice, optical aberrations and lighting cap this at **~10–20 μm** for a 10× lens.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Field of View Calculation for a Borescope Tip

### Given Data
- Borescope tip angle of view: $\alpha = 60°$
- Tip-to-defect distance: $L = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Question: What is the diameter of the inspected circle at the defect plane?

### Step 1 — Apply the FOV Equation

$$\text{FOV} = 2 L \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\text{FOV} = 2 \times 50 \times \tan\left(\frac{60°}{2}\right)$$

$$\text{FOV} = 100 \times \tan(30°)$$

$$\text{FOV} = 100 \times 0.5774 = 57.74 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Final Answer with Units

$$\boxed{\text{FOV Diameter} \approx 57.74 \text{ mm at } L = 50 \text{ mm}}$$

*Conversion logic: $\tan(30°)$ arises from the half-angle geometry of a circular viewing cone.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inspection Time Estimation for a Welded Plate

A frequently asked KTU problem: "Estimate the time required to visually inspect a welded plate."

### Given Data
- Plate dimensions: $2 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ weld bead
- Inspector walking speed: $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
- Width of effective field of view (with torch-light inspection): $w = 0.15 \text{ m}$ (= 150 mm)
- Inspection is done in **parallel passes** along the long axis.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Passes Required

$$N_{\text{passes}} = \frac{\text{Width}}{w} = \frac{1 \text{ m}}{0.15 \text{ m}} = 6.67 \approx 7 \text{ passes}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Length of Each Pass

Each pass runs along the **2 m length** of the plate. So total walking distance:

$$d_{\text{total}} = 7 \times 2 = 14 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Time

$$t = \frac{d_{\text{total}}}{v} = \frac{14}{0.5} = 28 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 4 — Add Real-World Overhead

VT inspections in industry use a **productivity factor of 0.6–0.7** (accounting for stopping, marking, repositioning, defect documentation):

$$t_{\text{actual}} = \frac{28}{0.7} = 40 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Takeaway:** At ~40 seconds for a 2 m² plate, an inspector can complete ≈ **180 m² of weld VT per hour** — making VT the **most cost-effective** NDT method by a factor of **5×–10×** over radiography or ultrasonics.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: DVT vs RVT

| Parameter | Direct Visual Testing (DVT) | Remote Visual Testing (RVT) |
|---|---|---|
| Eye-to-surface distance | ≤ **600 mm** | > 600 mm (via borescope/camera) |
| Required viewing angle | ≥ **30°** | Flexible — any practical angle |
| Tools | Naked eye, magnifier, flashlight, mirror | Borescope, fiberscope, videoscope |
| Accessible areas | Open, exposed surfaces | Internal cavities, hidden zones |
| Resolution | **0.1–0.2 mm** (naked) to **0.01 mm** (microscope) | **0.05–0.2 mm** (limited by optics) |
| Documentation | Manual notes / photographs | Digital video, still capture, measurement |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate to high (instrument cost) |
| Training | ASNT Level I / II | ASNT Level II (specialized) |
| Typical use | Welds, castings, ship hulls | Gas turbines, engines, pipes, tanks |
| Limitations | Surface only, line of sight | Limited FOV, lower resolution, fragile |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many students write *"VT can detect internal defects."* This is **incorrect**. VT detects **only surface-breaking or surface-adjacent** defects. The phrase *"internal"* always belongs to **RT, UT, or CT** — not VT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — The Visual Testing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Job Specification and Code Review] --> B[Surface Preparation]
    B --> C[Lighting Verification minimum 500 lux]
    C --> D[Tool Selection]
    D --> D1[Naked Eye]
    D --> D2[Magnifier 2x to 20x]
    D --> D3[Mirror or Periscope]
    D --> D4[Borescope Rigid]
    D --> D5[Fiberscope Flexible]
    D --> D6[Videoscope Digital]
    D --> D7[Microscope]
    D1 --> E[Systematic Visual Scan]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    E --> F[Defect Identification]
    F --> F1[Type: Crack, Porosity, Undercut, Corrosion]
    F --> F2[Size: Length, Width, Depth estimate]
    F --> F3[Location: GPS, Grid, Sketch]
    F1 --> G{Acceptance Criteria Check}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G -->|Pass| H[Document and Mark OK]
    G -->|Fail| I[Reject and Recommend Repair]
    H --> J[Final Report with Photographs]
    I --> J
    J --> K[ASNT Certified Inspector Signature]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Architecture Logic:** The flow above is the **canonical VT workflow** accepted across **ASME, AWS, and API codes**. Any deviation (e.g., skipping surface preparation) is a **documentation defect** during audits.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — VT Tool Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VT[Visual Testing Tools] --> UN[Unaided Inspection]
    VT --> OP[Optical Aids]
    VT --> RM[Remote Viewing]
    VT --> DG[Digital Documentation]
    UN --> UN1[Naked Eye]
    UN --> UN2[Eyeglasses for Inspector]
    OP --> OP1[Magnifying Glass 2x to 20x]
    OP --> OP2[Stereo Microscope]
    OP --> OP3[Profile Projector]
    OP --> OP4[Comparison Gauges]
    RM --> RM1[Mirror and Periscope]
    RM --> RM2[Rigid Borescope]
    RM --> RM3[Fiberscope]
    RM --> RM4[Videoscope with CCD]
    RM --> RM5[Pan Tilt Zoom Camera]
    DG --> DG1[Digital Camera DSLR]
    DG --> DG2[High Speed Video]
    DG --> DG3[Thermal Imager Indirect VT for Hot Parts]
    DG --> DG4[3D Laser Scanner]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation — Human Eye Optical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ[Object Light Source] --> COR[Cornea Refraction]
    COR --> PUP[Pupil Aperture 2 to 7 mm]
    PUP --> LEN[Lens Variable Focus Accommodation]
    LEN --> VIT[Vitreous Humor]
    VIT --> RET[Retina Photoreceptors]
    RET --> ROD[Rods Low Light Scotopic]
    RET --> CON[Cones High Acuity Photopic]
    CON --> FOV[Sharp Focus at Fovea Centralis]
    ROD --> PER[Peripheral Vision]
    RET --> OPT[Optic Nerve]
    OPT --> VIS[Visual Cortex Brain]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The **fovea centralis** is responsible for the **20/20 acuity** used in VT. The inspector must move the eye (or the workpiece) so that the defect image falls on the fovea — not in the peripheral field, which has **≈ 5× to 10× lower resolution**.

## 4.4 Borescope Tip Schematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LS[Light Source Cold Halogen or LED] --> FOB1[Fiber Optic Light Guide]
    FOB1 --> TIP[Borescope Tip]
    TIP --> OBJ[Objective Lens 30 to 120 deg FOV]
    OBJ --> REL[Relay Lens System or Coherent Fiber Bundle]
    REL --> EYE[Eyepiece or CCD Sensor]
    LS2[External Light] --> SCN[Inspected Surface]
    SCN --> REF[Reflected Light]
    REF --> OBJ
    EYE --> MON[Monitor or Inspector Eye]
    MON --> CAP[Digital Image Capture]
    CAP --> SW[Measurement Software]
    SW --> RPT[Inspection Report]
```

## 4.5 Acceptance / Rejection Decision Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Defect Detected] --> Q1{Is the defect surface breaking?}
    Q1 -->|No| RT[Use RT, UT, or MT instead]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does it exceed code limit?}
    Q2 -->|No| AC[Accept, Document as Permissible]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Type of defect?}
    Q3 --> Q3A[Crack]
    Q3 --> Q3B[Porosity]
    Q3 --> Q3C[Undercut]
    Q3 --> Q3D[Overlap]
    Q3 --> Q3E[Corrosion]
    Q3A --> RP[Reject Mandatory Repair Grind and Re weld]
    Q3B --> EV[Evaluate per code Clause]
    Q3C --> EV
    Q3D --> RP
    Q3E --> EV
    EV --> DEC{Within tolerance?}
    DEC -->|Yes| AC
    DEC -->|No| RP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Visual Testing (VT). List any four tools used in VT and state one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (1 mark):** Visual Testing is a method of NDT in which the surface condition of a component is examined qualitatively and quantitatively using the unaided human eye or optical aids, without affecting its serviceability, to detect surface-breaking discontinuities.

**Tools (1½ marks — any four):**  
1. **Hand-held magnifier** (2×–10×)  
2. **Borescope** (rigid) for internal surfaces  
3. **Fiberscope** (flexible) for curved cavities  
4. **Videoscope** with CCD camera for recording  
5. **Mirror / periscope** for indirect line of sight  
6. **High-intensity flashlight** (≥ 500 lux)  
7. **Digital camera / DSLR** for documentation  

**Limitation (½ mark):**  
VT can detect **only surface or near-surface defects** and is **ineffective on subsurface, internal, or coated defects** without prior surface preparation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare Direct Visual Testing (DVT) and Remote Visual Testing (RVT). Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DVT | RVT |
|---|---|---|
| Distance | Eye within **600 mm** | > 600 mm via borescope |
| Application | Open surfaces | Internal cavities, hidden zones |

**DVT advantages (1 mark):**  
- High resolution (resolves up to **0.05 mm** with magnifier)  
- No instrument cost; portable, fast

**RVT advantages (1 mark):**  
- Accesses **inaccessible** areas (turbine blades, engine cylinders)  
- Allows **permanent digital recording** and measurement

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The student is expected to attempt **one full question** (either Q-A or Q-B). Each sub-part should take ≈ **15–20 minutes**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the various tools and equipment used in visual testing with their working principles.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### 1. Unaided Visual Inspection (Naked Eye)
- The eye, with **20/20 acuity (1 arc-minute resolution)**, is the original instrument.
- Useful for general surface surveys, large weld beads, big castings, hulls.
- Limited by **D = 250 mm** near point and **ambient lighting** ≥ 500 lux.

#### 2. Magnifying Aids
- **Hand magnifier** ($M = 1 + D/f$): $f = 25$ mm → $M = 11\times$.
- **Stereo microscope**: Two optical paths → 3D depth perception → used for **fractography** and **corrosion pit profiling**.
- **Profile projector / optical comparator**: Enlarged silhouette (10×–100×) projected on screen → for **thread profile and gear-tooth inspection**.

#### 3. Mirrors and Periscopes
- Used where line-of-sight is blocked.
- Periscopes (rigid) and adjustable **inspection mirrors** extend viewing into T-joints, behind brackets, etc.

#### 4. Borescope (Rigid)
- A rigid tube with **objective lens + relay lens + eyepiece**.
- Lengths: **50 mm to 1500 mm**; diameters: **1.7 mm to 25 mm**.
- Angled tip versions: **0°, 30°, 70°, 90°** to inspect sideways.
- Tip FOV: typically **30° to 120°**.

#### 5. Fiberscope (Flexible)
- Image transmitted via **coherent fiber-optic bundle** (~10,000–30,000 fibers).
- Articulating tip deflects **±180° in two planes** (4-way articulation).
- Lengths: **1 m to 6 m**; insertion diameter: **4 mm to 12 mm**.

#### 6. Videoscope (Digital)
- Replaces eyepiece with a **CCD/CMOS sensor** at the tip.
- **All-digital signal** → no fiber bundle degradation.
- Built-in **measurement software** (stereo-measurement, comparison-to-CAD, crack-length measurement).

#### 7. Light Sources
- **Cold halogen lamps** (avoid overheating).
- **LED light guides** — long life, low heat.
- **Fiber-optic light transmission** to remote tip.

#### 8. Documentation Tools
- **Digital cameras** (DSLR with macro lens) → 24 MP resolution.
- **High-speed video** for crack propagation.
- **3D laser scanners** → point-cloud defect mapping.

**Working Principle Summary Table (write in exam):**

| Tool | Working Principle |
|---|---|
| Borescope | Objective → Relay lens → Eyepiece |
| Fiberscope | Coherent fiber bundle image relay |
| Videoscope | Objective → CCD/CMOS → Digital display |
| Microscope | Objective + Eyepiece compound optics |
| Profile projector | Silhouette on screen, magnified |

---

**(b) Discuss the applications and limitations of visual testing in various industries with examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### Applications of VT

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Welding (Fabrication)** | Inspection of weld surface — cracks, undercut, porosity, spatter, reinforcement height, leg length. |
| **Castings & Forgings** | Surface cracks, laps, folds, bursts, hot tears, shift in forgings. |
| **Aerospace** | Pre-flight checks of fuselage, engine borescope inspection of turbine blades, combustion chambers. |
| **Oil & Gas (Pipelines)** | Right-of-way visual surveys, coating inspection, corrosion mapping, valve and flange checks. |
| **Power Plants** | Annual boiler inspection, turbine blade RVT, condenser tube borescopy. |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull plating, weld seams, propeller blades, rudder, sea-cock inspection. |
| **Civil / Infrastructure** | Bridge cables, bearings, expansion joints, RCC rebar exposure. |
| **Automotive** | Engine block, cylinder head, casting porosity, paint finish, assembly line fit-checks. |
| **Railways** | Wheel tread, axle journals, brake disc wear, coach body corrosion. |
| **Pressure Vessels** | ASME-mandated VT before and after hydrostatic test. |

#### Limitations of VT

1. **Surface only** — cannot detect subsurface or embedded defects.
2. **Operator dependent** — quality depends on inspector's **acuity, training, fatigue, motivation**.
3. **Line-of-sight required** — internal areas unreachable without disassembly or RVT tools.
4. **Coatings hide defects** — paint, oil, scale, and rust must be removed first (additional cost).
5. **Subjective measurement** — no precise "go/no-go" without measurement gauges.
6. **Environmental constraints** — poor lighting, fumes, confined spaces, high temperature restrict work.
7. **Small / tight cracks (< 0.1 mm)** are **invisible** to the naked eye and even hard to detect with magnifiers under field conditions.
8. **No depth information** — VT cannot measure the **depth** of a crack; it only sees the **surface opening**.
9. **Not suitable for high-temperature components** (e.g., furnace tubes) without specialized RVT cooling adapters.
10. **Time-consuming for large areas** — must scan systematically; line-by-line inspection of large structures (e.g., 100 m tank shell) is laborious.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> When the question asks *"Discuss the **limitations** of VT"*, students often list only 2–3 points and lose up to **3 marks**. The model answer above shows **10 distinct limitations** — students are advised to write at least **5–6** with **industry-specific examples** to score full marks.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Describe the procedure for visual inspection of welds. List the various surface defects detected by VT in welds.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### Step-by-Step Procedure (per AWS D1.1 / ASME Sec. V)

1. **Pre-inspection preparation**
   - Review **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and **PQR**.
   - Verify welder **qualification certificates**.
   - Confirm **drawing, code, and acceptance criteria**.

2. **Before welding (Pre-weld / Fit-up VT)**
   - Check **edge preparation** (angle, root gap, root face).
   - Inspect **cleanliness** — no rust, oil, paint, moisture in groove.
   - Verify **alignment** of plates, tacks, and backing (if any).
   - Confirm **joint geometry** per drawing.

3. **During welding (In-process VT)**
   - Monitor **interpass cleaning** — slag removal, wire brushing.
   - Check **bead shape, weaving pattern, sequence**.
   - Verify **welding parameters** (current, voltage) by observation of arc and bead.

4. **After welding (Post-weld VT)**
   - Allow weld to cool to **ambient** (no VT on hot weld).
   - Remove **spatter, slag, flux residue** by wire brushing / grinding.
   - Ensure **clean, dry, well-lit** surface (≥ **500 lux**).
   - **Systematic scan** — parallel passes from one end to the other.
   - **100% visual coverage** of the full weld length.
   - **Measure** — crown height, leg length, weld width, undercut depth using **weld gauge (Hi-Lo gauge, Fillet weld gauge)**.
   - **Photograph** each defect with ruler / scale for record.

5. **Reporting**
   - Issue **Visual Inspection Report** with: inspector name, certification, code used, defects found, photographs, sketches, acceptance status, signature.

#### Surface Defects Detected by VT in Welds

| # | Defect | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crack** | Linear discontinuity — most serious; immediate reject |
| 2 | **Undercut** | Groove at weld toe due to excess current |
| 3 | **Porosity** | Cavities from trapped gas |
| 4 | **Slag inclusion** | Non-metallic solid entrapped in weld |
| 5 | **Spatter** | Molten metal droplets on base metal |
| 6 | **Overlap** | Weld metal flows over base metal without fusion |
| 7 | **Incomplete fusion** | Lack of fusion between weld and base metal |
| 8 | **Insufficient throat** | Fillet weld leg too short |
| 9 | **Excess reinforcement** | Weld crown too high |
| 10 | **Concave / convex profile** | Improper bead shape |
| 11 | **Arc strikes** | Stray arc damage on base metal |
| 12 | **Crater pipe / crater crack** | Shrinkage cavity at weld termination |
| 13 | **Surface laminations** | In base metal revealed by welding |
| 14 | **Distortion** | Warping due to uneven heat input |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **"incomplete fusion"** (a *planar* defect between weld and base metal) with **"lack of penetration"** (a *planar* defect at the root of a butt joint). Both are **internal / sub-surface** — VT can only detect them when they **break the surface** (e.g., root bead incomplete penetration in a single-sided weld).

---

**(b) Explain the human visual system, visual acuity (Snellen fraction), and the factors affecting the efficiency of visual inspection.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### 1. The Human Eye as an Optical System

The eye is analogous to a camera:

| Eye Part | Camera Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Cornea + Lens | Objective lens | Refract light, focus on retina |
| Pupil | Aperture diaphragm | Controls light admitted (2–7 mm) |
| Iris | Aperture ring | Adjusts pupil diameter |
| Retina | Film / sensor | Receives image (rods + cones) |
| Fovea centralis | Center of frame | **Highest acuity** (20/20 region) |
| Optic nerve | Data cable | Transmits signal to brain |
| Vitreous humor | Optical medium | Transparent filler |

#### 2. Visual Acuity — Snellen Fraction

$$\text{Visual Acuity} = \frac{\text{Test Distance}}{\text{Distance Normal Eye Reads Same Line}}$$

- **20/20 = 6/6** = normal vision, 1 arc-minute resolution.
- **20/40 = 6/12** = half-normal; inspector needs **2× larger** features to see.
- **20/200 = 6/60** = legally blind (in most jurisdictions).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mandatory for KTU:** Always state that the **near point is 250 mm** when discussing visual acuity. This value appears in magnification formula derivations.

#### 3. Factors Affecting VT Efficiency

1. **Inspector-related**
   - Visual acuity (Snellen fraction)
   - **Color vision** (defects in red/blue/green shades distinguishable?)
   - **Experience and training** (ASNT Level II/III)
   - **Fatigue, motivation, alertness**
   - **Age** (presbyopia reduces accommodation)

2. **Lighting**
   - **Intensity** (≥ 500 lux recommended)
   - **Direction** (oblique light reveals surface texture)
   - **Color temperature** (cool white ≈ 5000 K best for metallic surfaces)
   - **Glare** must be avoided

3. **Surface condition**
   - **Cleanliness** (no oil, paint, scale)
   - **Roughness** (rough surfaces mask fine cracks)
   - **Reflectivity** (shiny surfaces may need polarized light)

4. **Component-related**
   - **Geometry / accessibility**
   - **Size** of the feature to be detected
   - **Temperature** (hot parts require cool mirrors / IR VT)
   - **Vibration / motion** during inspection

5. **Environmental**
   - **Confined space** entry requirements
   - **Atmospheric** conditions (humidity, dust)
   - **Ambient light interference** (outdoor vs indoor)

6. **Equipment-related**
   - **Magnification** of aids used
   - **Resolution & FOV** of borescope/videoscope
   - **Calibration** of measurement scales
   - **Image capture quality**

#### 4. Angular Resolution and Diffraction Limit

For an eye with pupil diameter $D = 2 \text{ mm}$ in bright light, peak wavelength $\lambda = 550 \text{ nm}$ (yellow-green, the eye's peak sensitivity):

$$\theta = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 1.22 \times \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\theta = 3.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 1.15 \text{ arc-minute} \approx \mathbf{20/20 \text{ vision}}$$

This is the **physical proof** of the Snellen standard and forms the foundation of all VT resolution discussions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1**

- ☐ **VT definition:** ASTM E1316 / ASME Sec. V Article 9; qualitative + quantitative; surface only; non-contact.
- ☐ **VT is the FIRST NDT method** — mandatory before MT, PT, RT, UT in most codes.
- ☐ **DVT** = eye within **600 mm**, angle ≥ 30°; **RVT** = via borescope/fiberscope/videoscope.
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** ASTM E1316, ASTM E337, ASME V Art. 9, AWS D1.1, ISO 9712, ASNT SNT-TC-1A.
- ☐ **Snellen 20/20 = 1 arc-minute** angular resolution; near point **D = 250 mm**.
- ☐ **Rayleigh criterion:** $\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$ — fundamental diffraction limit.
- ☐ **Simple magnifier formula:** $M = 1 + D/f$ (at near point); $M = D/f$ (at infinity).
- ☐ **Borescope FOV:** $\text{FOV} = 2L \tan(\alpha / 2)$.
- ☐ **Lighting requirement:** Minimum **500 lux**; high-precision **≥ 1000 lux**.
- ☐ **Tools list:** Naked eye, mirror, magnifier, borescope, fiberscope, videoscope, microscope, profile projector, digital camera, 3D laser scanner.
- ☐ **Weld defects detected:** Crack, undercut, porosity, slag, spatter, overlap, crater crack, arc strike, distortion.
- ☐ **10+ limitations** of VT — must be able to list at least 5–6 with engineering examples.
- ☐ **VT cannot measure crack depth** — only surface opening width and length.
- ☐ **Cannot detect subsurface defects** — needs RT, UT, or CT.
- ☐ **Operator dependent** — ASNT certification is mandatory.
- ☐ **Inspection coverage rate:** ≈ **150–200 m²/hour** for typical weld plate VT.
- ☐ **RVT preferred for:** Turbines, engines, heat exchangers, condensers, tanks, boilers, pipelines.
- ☐ **Inspection procedure sequence:** Documentation review → Surface prep → Lighting → Tool selection → Systematic scan → Identification → Acceptance check → Reporting.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B question, always include a **labeled diagram, a comparison table, and a procedural step-list** — KTU examiners award **separate marks for "neatness, diagrams, and presentation"** (typically 1–2 marks). Pure textual answers, even if technically correct, lose these presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision, Lighting, Material Attributes, and Environmental Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vision, Lighting, Material Attributes, and Environmental Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Visual Testing (VT) — The Foundation of NDT

**Visual Testing (VT)** is the oldest, most widely used, and arguably the most fundamental Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method. As per **ASME Section V, Article 9** and **ASTM E2702**, VT is the process of evaluating a component's surface and structural integrity through direct human observation — either with the naked eye (**direct VT**) or with optical aids such as borescopes, mirrors, magnifiers, and cameras (**remote VT**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Approximately **80% of all NDT inspections begin with visual testing**. It is often the first screening step before deploying more sophisticated methods like ultrasonic, radiographic, or magnetic particle testing.

### 1.2 Why This Module Matters in NDT

Before any advanced NDT technique can be applied, the inspector must clearly *see* the surface. The accuracy of visual inspection depends on **four interconnected pillars**:

1. **Vision** – The capability of the human inspector's eye and brain.
2. **Lighting** – The illumination system used to reveal features.
3. **Material Attributes** – The physical and optical properties of the part being inspected.
4. **Environmental Factors** – The ambient surroundings (dust, glare, temperature, humidity).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor's Stethoscope Checkup:**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before ordering an MRI (an advanced tool), the doctor first *looks* at the patient's skin, *lights up* the throat with a torch, *observes* the texture, color, and swelling, and works in a *clean, well-lit room*. Similarly, in NDT, the inspector must first examine the component with proper vision, lighting, awareness of material properties, and a controlled environment. Miss any one pillar, and even a serious defect can go unnoticed — like a doctor missing a rash in a dimly lit room.

### 1.4 The Human Eye as a Sensor — Quick Definition

The **human eye** is a biological optical instrument with a variable-aperture lens (iris), a light-sensitive retina (rods and cones), and neural processing (visual cortex). For NDT purposes, the eye is treated as an **optical sensor** with a measurable **visual acuity** (sharpness) and **contrast sensitivity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terms to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Visual Acuity:** The eye's ability to distinguish fine detail (typically measured as a Snellen fraction, e.g., **20/20** vision).
> - **Photopic Vision:** Daylight vision mediated by **cone cells** (high acuity, color perception).
> - **Scotopic Vision:** Low-light vision mediated by **rod cells** (high sensitivity, no color).
> - **Mesopic Vision:** Transitional range between photopic and scotopic (twilight).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Human Eye for NDT Inspection

The eye is composed of the following optical components that directly affect visual inspection quality:

| Component | Function | NDT Relevance |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Cornea** | Transparent front layer; provides ~⅔ of eye's refractive power | First refracting surface for incoming light |
| **Iris** | Adjustable aperture controlling light entry | Controls pupil diameter (2–8 mm) |
| **Lens** | Variable-focus element (accommodation) | Focuses on near (~25 cm) to far objects |
| **Retina** | Light-sensitive layer (rods + cones) | Image formation plane |
| **Fovea** | Region of highest cone density | Critical for detecting small defects |
| **Optic Nerve** | Transmits signals to the brain | Processing latency affects inspection speed |

### 2.2 Photopic vs. Scotopic Vision — The Heart of Inspection Lighting

- **Photopic vision (cone-mediated):** Operates at luminance levels above approximately **3 cd/m²**. Provides **high spatial acuity** and **color discrimination**. **Required for detecting color-coded defects** (e.g., dye penetrant indications).
- **Scotopic vision (rod-mediated):** Operates below **0.001 cd/m²**. Provides only **achromatic (grayscale)** perception. **Used in low-light conditions** like fluorescent penetrant or magnetic particle inspection under UV.
- **Mesopic vision:** Between 0.001 and 3 cd/m² — both rods and cones active.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The transition between photopic and scotopic vision is governed by the **Purkinje effect**, where the eye's peak sensitivity shifts from **555 nm (green-yellow, photopic)** to **507 nm (blue-green, scotopic)** as light levels drop. This is why red safelights are used in darkrooms — red light (~700 nm) is poorly detected by rods, preserving dark adaptation.

### 2.3 Lighting Fundamentals for NDT

Lighting in VT falls into three broad categories:

#### 2.3.1 Types of Lighting

1. **Directional Lighting** — A focused beam at an angle (often low angle, 5°–30°) to highlight surface discontinuities via shadow formation. Best for detecting **cracks, laps, and surface pits**.
2. **Diffuse Lighting** — Soft, scattered light minimizing shadows. Used for **color comparison** (e.g., dye penetrant evaluation).
3. **Transmitted/Backlighting** — Light passes through the part. Used for **transparent/translucent materials** (glass, thin plastics) to detect internal voids.
4. **Specialty Lighting** — UV-A (365 nm) for fluorescent PT/MT, polarized light for stress analysis.

#### 2.3.2 Illuminance, Luminance & Contrast

- **Illuminance (E):** Luminous flux per unit area, measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**.
- **Luminance (L):** Light emitted/reflected per unit area, measured in **candela per square meter (cd/m²)** — what the eye actually perceives.
- **Contrast (C):** The difference in luminance between a feature and its background.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown syntax.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| Illuminance (Inverse Square Law) | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | $E$ in lux, $I$ in candela, $d$ in meters |
| Cosine Law of Illumination | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | $\theta$ = angle of incidence to normal |
| Luminance (Lambertsian surface) | $L = \dfrac{\rho E}{\pi}$ | $\rho$ = reflectance, $\pi$ for diffuse reflector |
| Michelson Contrast | $C = \dfrac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$ | $0 \le C \le 1$ (or expressed as %) |
| Weber Contrast | $C_{W} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{b}}$ | $L_{b}$ = background luminance |
| Snell's Law (Refraction) | $n_{1} \sin\theta_{1} = n_{2} \sin\theta_{2}$ | $n$ = refractive index |
| Visual Acuity (Snellen) | $VA = \dfrac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{normal}}}$ | 20/20 = 1.0 (normal), 20/40 = 0.5 |
| Threshold Contrast Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{C_{\text{threshold}}}$ | Higher S = better defect detection |
| Solid Angle of Light Source | $\Omega = \dfrac{A}{r^{2}}$ | Steradians (sr) |
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi_{v} = K_{m} \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda}\, d\lambda$ | $K_{m} = 683$ lm/W (photopic max) |
| Dark Adaptation Time | $t \approx 30 \text{ min}$ for full rod adaptation | Critical for fluorescent NDT |

### 2.5 Material Attributes Affecting Visual Inspection

The **surface and optical characteristics** of the workpiece directly influence defect detectability:

| Attribute | Effect on Inspection | Mitigation |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|
| **Surface Roughness ($R_a$)** | High roughness scatters light, hiding small defects | Polish / etch before inspection |
| **Reflectivity ($\rho$)** | Highly reflective surfaces create glare | Use polarizing filters; diffuse light |
| **Color** | Affects contrast against penetrant/dye | Standardize lighting (D65, 5400 K) |
| **Geometry / Curvature** | Complex shapes cast shadows and limit access | Use borescopes, mirrors, robotic VT |
| **Surface Contamination** | Oil, paint, scale obscure defects | Pre-cleaning (solvent, brushing) |
| **Material Type** | Ferromagnetic vs. non-magnetic dictates applicable NDT methods | Choose correct inspection method |

### 2.6 Environmental Factors

| Factor | Effect on VT | Standard/Threshold |
|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Ambient Light** | Reduces contrast of fluorescent indications | Inspect in darkened booth (< 20 lux for MT) |
| **Dust / Particulates** | False indications in PT | ISO 14644-1 cleanroom standards |
| **Temperature** | Affects operator comfort and PT/MT chemistry | Typically 15–30°C for NDT |
| **Humidity** | Can cause corrosion masking; affects PT drying | 30–70% RH typical |
| **Vibration** | Causes blur in remote/robotic VT | Isolation mounts required |
| **Operator Fatigue** | Reduces detection accuracy after 30 min | ASNT ILI-P recommends 20-min breaks |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These parameters directly govern **inspection reliability**. In aerospace (e.g., Boeing/Airbus), **visual inspection standards** dictate minimum illuminance of **1000 lux** on the surface, **contrast ratio ≥ 0.4**, and **inspector vision corrected to 20/25 or better** (per **ASTM E2862** and **NAS 410**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law of Illumination

The **Inverse Square Law** is foundational in NDT lighting design. It is derived as follows:

**Starting Premise:** A point light source emits luminous intensity $I$ (in candela, cd) uniformly in all directions. The luminous flux $\Phi_v$ passing through a spherical surface of radius $d$ is constant (conservation of energy in photometric terms).

**Step 1 — Solid Angle Definition:**
A sphere of radius $d$ has surface area $A = 4\pi d^{2}$.
The total solid angle subtended by a closed sphere is $\Omega = 4\pi$ steradians.

**Step 2 — Flux Per Unit Solid Angle:**
By definition of luminous intensity:

$$
I = \frac{\Phi_{v}}{\Omega}
$$

Rearranging for total flux:

$$
\Phi_{v} = I \cdot \Omega = 4\pi I
$$

**Step 3 — Illuminance Definition:**
Illuminance $E$ is flux per unit area:

$$
E = \frac{\Phi_{v}}{A} = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^{2}} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}}
$$

**Result:** Doubling the distance from the light source reduces illuminance by a factor of **four** (not two). This is a critical design consideration when positioning NDT inspection lamps.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine Law of Illumination

When light strikes a surface at an angle $\theta$ from the **normal** (perpendicular), the projected area is reduced by $\cos\theta$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Area Reduction:**
The effective illuminated area is $A_{\text{eff}} = A \cos\theta$.

**Step 2 — Apply Illuminance Definition:**

$$
E = \frac{\Phi_{v}}{A \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Phi_{v} = I \cdot \Omega$** and integrate over the solid angle subtended. For a point source, $\Omega = A/d^{2}$:

$$
E = \frac{I \cdot (A/d^{2})}{A \cos\theta} = \frac{I}{d^{2} \cos\theta}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}}
$$

**Practical Implication for NDT:** Inspecting a curved pipe from an angle ($\theta = 60°$) reduces effective illuminance by 50% compared to normal incidence. Inspectors must either increase lamp intensity or reduce distance.

### 3.3 Derivation of Michelson Contrast

**Step 1 — Define Luminance Values:**
Let $L_b$ = background luminance, $L_f$ = feature luminance.

**Step 2 — Define Maximum and Minimum:**

$$
L_{\max} = \max(L_{b}, L_{f}), \quad L_{\min} = \min(L_{b}, L_{f})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Definition:**

$$
C = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**
For a fluorescent penetrant indication on a polished metal surface:
- $L_f = 80$ cd/m² (bright indication)
- $L_b = 20$ cd/m² (dark background)

$$
C = \frac{80 - 20}{80 + 20} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU board favorite):** The minimum acceptable contrast for reliable human visual detection is typically **C ≥ 0.30 to 0.40**. Below this, defects may be missed.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Snellen Visual Acuity Formula

**Step 1 — Standardization:**
A "normal" eye can resolve an angular detail of **1 arcminute** $= \dfrac{1}{60}$ degree $= \dfrac{\pi}{10800}$ rad.

**Step 2 — Test Setup:**
A Snellen chart has lines where each letter subtends 5 arcminutes total height, with critical detail of 1 arcminute at a standard test distance $d_{\text{normal}} = 20$ feet (6 m).

**Step 3 — Read Subject's Acuity:**
A subject reading the same line at distance $d_{\text{test}}$ has acuity:

$$
\boxed{VA = \frac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{normal}}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Example:**
A person reading at 20 ft what a normal eye reads at 40 ft has $VA = 20/40 = 0.5$. NDT inspectors typically require **$VA \ge 0.5$ (20/40)** at minimum, and **$VA \ge 0.8$ (20/25)** for critical aerospace inspections.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Lighting Selection by Defect Type

| Defect Type | Recommended Lighting | Angle of Incidence | Key Parameter |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Surface cracks (tight)** | Low-angle directional | 5°–15° | Shadow contrast |
| **Porosity (open)** | Diffuse or coaxial | 0°–45° | Luminance contrast |
| **Weld undercuts** | Low-angle + diffuse mix | 10°–30° | Shadow length |
| **Corrosion pitting** | High-angle diffuse | 45°–75° | Color/shadow |
| **Leaks in tanks** | Transmitted (back) light | N/A | Transmission loss |
| **Fluorescent indications** | UV-A (365 nm) in dark booth | Direct, shielded | Irradiance ≥ 1000 µW/cm² |
| **Color contrast (PT)** | White light, D65 | Diffuse | Color temperature 6500 K |

### 3.6 Luminance Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A surface has reflectance $\rho = 0.6$ and is illuminated with $E = 1000$ lx. Find the luminance assuming a Lambertian (diffuse) reflector.

**Step 1 — Apply Lambertian formula:**

$$
L = \frac{\rho E}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
L = \frac{0.6 \times 1000}{3.14159} = \frac{600}{3.14159}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute result:**

$$
\boxed{L \approx 190.99 \text{ cd/m}^{2} \approx 191 \text{ cd/m}^{2}}
$$

This corresponds to bright office-lighting conditions, well within the photopic vision range — suitable for detailed VT.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Human Eye Cross-Section — Block Diagram (Adapted Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INCIDENT LIGHT RAYS"] --> nodeB["CORNEA\nPrimary Refraction"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ANTERIOR CHAMBER\nAqueous Humor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["IRIS\nVariable Aperture 2-8 mm"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["LENS\nAccommodation 25cm to Infinity"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["VITREOUS HUMOR\nTransparent Gel"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RETINA\nRods and Cones"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["FOVEA\nHighest Acuity Region"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["OPTIC NERVE\nSignal Transmission"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["VISUAL CORTEX\nImage Processing"]
    nodeH -.-> nodeI

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeJ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 The Four Pillars of Effective Visual Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["PILLAR 1 VISION"]
        nodeA1["Human Eye Anatomy"]
        nodeA2["Visual Acuity 20/20"]
        nodeA3["Photopic and Scotopic"]
        nodeA4["Color Perception"]
        nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    end

    subgraph P2["PILLAR 2 LIGHTING"]
        nodeB1["Directional Light"]
        nodeB2["Diffuse Light"]
        nodeB3["UV A 365 nm"]
        nodeB4["Back Lighting"]
        nodeB1 --> nodeB2
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    end

    subgraph P3["PILLAR 3 MATERIAL ATTRIBUTES"]
        nodeC1["Surface Roughness Ra"]
        nodeC2["Reflectivity rho"]
        nodeC3["Color and Texture"]
        nodeC4["Geometry and Access"]
        nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3 --> nodeC4
    end

    subgraph P4["PILLAR 4 ENVIRONMENT"]
        nodeD1["Ambient Light Control"]
        nodeD2["Cleanliness and Dust"]
        nodeD3["Temperature 15 to 30 C"]
        nodeD4["Operator Fatigue"]
        nodeD1 --> nodeD2 --> nodeD3 --> nodeD4
    end

    nodeA4 --> nodeOUT["RELIABLE VT INSPECTION"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeOUT
    nodeC4 --> nodeOUT
    nodeD4 --> nodeOUT

    style nodeOUT fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style P1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 NDT Visual Inspection Workflow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START Component Received for NDT"] --> nodePlan["Plan Inspection\nDefine Surface and Geometry"]
    nodePlan --> nodeClean["Pre Cleaning\nRemove Oil Scale Paint"]
    nodeClean --> nodeEnv["Environment Setup\nLighting Booth and Ventilation"]
    nodeEnv --> nodeCheck{"Visual Acuity Test\nGreater than 20/40"}

    nodeCheck -- "Fail" --> nodeReject["Reject Inspector\nSend for Eye Checkup"]
    nodeCheck -- "Pass" --> nodeDarkAdapt["Dark Adaptation 20 min\nIf UV Method Used"]

    nodeDarkAdapt --> nodeIllum["Set Illuminance\nE Greater than 1000 lux"]
    nodeIllum --> nodeInspect["Perform Visual Scan\nDirect or Remote VT"]
    nodeInspect --> nodeRecord["Record Indications\nPhoto and Dimensional Data"]

    nodeRecord --> nodeEval{"Defect Found?"}
    nodeEval -- "No" --> nodePass["PASS Component Cleared"]
    nodeEval -- "Yes" --> nodeClassify["Classify Defect\nType Size Location"]
    nodeClassify --> nodeDecision{"Accept or Reject?"}
    nodeDecision -- "Accept" --> nodePass
    nodeDecision -- "Reject" --> nodeRepair["Send for Repair or Scrap"]

    style nodeStart fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePass fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeReject fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeRepair fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeInspect fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Lighting-Detectability Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Material Type and\nSurface Condition"] --> nodeB{"Crack or\nDiscontinuity?"}
    nodeB -- "Yes, Tight Crack" --> nodeC1["LOW ANGLE LIGHT 5 to 15 deg\nHigh Contrast Shadows"]
    nodeB -- "Yes, Open Defect" --> nodeC2["DIFFUSE LIGHT 30 to 60 deg\nColor and Luminance Contrast"]
    nodeB -- "Yes, Subsurface" --> nodeC3["UV A 365 nm\nFluorescent PT or MT"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeD["Verify Detection\nwith ASME V Article 9"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD

    nodeD --> nodeE["Document with\nASTM E2862 or E2702"]

    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
