<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Visual Testing (VT). List any four optical aids used in remote visual inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Visual Testing is a Non-Destructive Testing method that uses the human eye — directly or with optical enhancement — to evaluate the surface condition, geometry, and integrity of a component for defects such as cracks, corrosion, wear, and weld discontinuities, in accordance with standards like **ASTM E2702** and **ASME Section V, Article 9**.

**Optical Aids (1 mark — any four):**
1. **Borescopes** (rigid and flexible) — for internal cavity inspection
2. **Fiberscopes** — flexible borescopes with fiber-optic image transmission
3. **Mirrors** — for line-of-sight redirection
4. **Magnifiers / Microscopes** — for high-magnification detail
5. **Video borescopes** — digital recording versions
6. **Drones / Robotic crawlers** — for remote elevated inspection

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Differentiate between photopic and scotopic vision. Why is this distinction important in NDT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Photopic Vision | Scotopic Vision |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Receptor | **Cones** | **Rods** |
| Luminance Range | > 3 cd/m² (daylight) | < 0.001 cd/m² (dark) |
| Color Vision | Yes (trichromatic) | No (achromatic) |
| Visual Acuity | High | Low |
| Peak Wavelength | 555 nm | 507 nm |
| Dark Adaptation Time | ~ 5–7 min | ~ 30 min |

**Importance in NDT (1 mark):** In **fluorescent penetrant testing (FPT)** and **magnetic particle testing (MT)**, indications are viewed under UV-A in a darkened booth. The inspector must rely on **scotopic vision** and must undergo **dark adaptation** (~20 min) to achieve peak rod sensitivity. In contrast, **dye penetrant testing (DPT)** and general surface inspection use **photopic vision** under bright white light to leverage color discrimination.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format: Attempt either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **anatomy of the human eye** and explain the role of **rods, cones, and the fovea** in visual inspection. Why must an NDT inspector undergo a periodic **ophthalmologic examination**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Inverse Square Law** and **Cosine Law of Illumination** with derivations. A light source of luminous intensity **500 cd** is placed at a distance of **2 m** from a surface tilted at **60°** to the normal. Calculate the illuminance on the surface. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Human Eye Anatomy (3 marks):**
The human eye is a sophisticated optical system. Light enters through the **cornea** (n=1.376), passes through the **aqueous humor**, enters via the **pupil** (aperture controlled by the **iris**), is focused by the crystalline **lens** (accommodation range 25 cm to ∞), travels through the **vitreous humor**, and forms an image on the **retina**.

**Rods, Cones, and Fovea (3 marks):**
- **Rods (~120 million):** Highly sensitive, achromatic, distributed across the peripheral retina. Enable **scotopic vision** (low-light, no color).
- **Cones (~6–7 million):** Concentrated in the **fovea centralis** (~2° visual angle). Enable **photopic vision** (color, high acuity).
- **Fovea:** Provides the sharpest vision (≥ 20/20 acuity). The inspector naturally moves the eye (saccades) to place critical defect regions onto the fovea.

**Periodic Ophthalmologic Examination (1 mark):**
NDT inspections demand **prolonged, focused, near-distance visual work** for detecting sub-millimeter defects. Conditions like **presbyopia, cataracts, color blindness, and reduced contrast sensitivity** can severely impair detection. Standards like **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** and **NAS 410** mandate annual or biennial eye exams (Jaeger J-1 near vision, Snellen 20/20 distant, color discrimination via **Ishihara test**).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Inverse Square Law Derivation (2 marks):**
A point source emits luminous flux $\Phi_v = 4\pi I$ uniformly. At distance $d$, this flux is spread over spherical area $A = 4\pi d^2$. Thus:

$$
E = \frac{\Phi_{v}}{A} = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^{2}} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}
$$

**Cosine Law Derivation (2 marks):**
For a surface tilted at angle $\theta$ from the normal, the projected area is $A \cos\theta$. Hence:

$$
E = \frac{\Phi_{v}}{A\cos\theta} = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
Given: $I = 500$ cd, $d = 2$ m, $\theta = 60°$.

$$
E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{500 \times \cos(60°)}{(2)^{2}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{500 \times 0.5}{4} = \frac{250}{4} = 62.5 \text{ lux}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 62.5 \text{ lx}}
$$

**Engineering Note:** This illuminance is **well below** the **1000 lx** recommended for NDT VT. The inspector should either reduce the distance or use a higher-intensity source.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2022 — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** Explain the various **types of lighting** used in visual NDT. Describe with a diagram how **low-angle illumination** reveals surface cracks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polished metal surface has reflectance **ρ = 0.7** and is illuminated by **800 lux** of uniform diffuse light. The defect indication has luminance **200 cd/m²** and the background has luminance **150 cd/m²**. Calculate the **Michelson contrast**. State whether the defect is **detectable** based on standard NDT thresholds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Types of Lighting (4 marks):**

1. **Directional / Low-angle Lighting (5°–30°):** Creates long shadows at defect edges. Ideal for tight surface cracks, laps, and seams.
2. **Diffuse / Flood Lighting (30°–60°):** Soft, scattered light minimizing shadows. Used for color contrast (e.g., dye penetrant) and general surface evaluation.
3. **Coaxial Lighting (0°):** Light travels along the viewing axis. Used for inspecting **bore interiors** and reflective surfaces.
4. **Transmitted / Backlighting:** Light passes through the part. For **transparent/translucent materials** (glass, plastic).
5. **UV-A Lighting (365 nm):** For **fluorescent PT and MT**. Requires darkened booth.
6. **Polarized Lighting:** For residual stress analysis (photoelasticity) and reducing glare on reflective surfaces.

**Low-Angle Illumination Diagram (3 marks):**

**Working Principle:** When light strikes the surface at a grazing angle (e.g., **5°–15°**), the smooth surface reflects the light away from the inspector's eye, appearing **dark**. A crack or scratch, however, has vertical walls that reflect light **upward toward the eye**, appearing as a **bright line**. The contrast between the dark background and the bright crack is the detection mechanism.

**Simple schematic representation (text-based):**

```
        Light source (5°-15°)
              \
               \
                \
        ~~~~~~~~~~~  <- Smooth surface (reflects away)
        \_________/ <- Crack (vertical wall reflects up to eye)
        
        Inspector's eye
```

This is the principle behind **ASTM E2862** and **AWS QC1** low-angle VT techniques used in weld inspection.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 0.7$ (reflectance — not directly needed for Michelson contrast)
- $E = 800$ lx (illuminance)
- $L_f = 200$ cd/m² (feature luminance)
- $L_b = 150$ cd/m² (background luminance)

**Step 1 — Apply Michelson Contrast Formula (3 marks):**

$$
C = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Max and Min (1 mark):**
$L_{\max} = 200$ cd/m² (feature), $L_{\min} = 150$ cd/m² (background).

**Step 3 — Substitute and Calculate (2 marks):**

$$
C = \frac{200 - 150}{200 + 150} = \frac{50}{350} = 0.1428 \approx 0.143
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 0.143 \text{ or } 14.3\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Detectability Assessment (1 mark):**
The minimum Michelson contrast for reliable human visual detection in NDT is typically **C ≥ 0.30 (30%)**. Since the calculated contrast **0.143 < 0.30**, the defect is **NOT reliably detectable** under these conditions.

**Recommendation:** Increase the illuminance to raise luminance contrast, switch to a low-angle lighting configuration, or apply surface treatment to enhance defect visibility.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Illuminance (E, in lux) with Luminance (L, in cd/m²)** — They are NOT the same. Illuminance falls on a surface; luminance is emitted/reflected from it and is what the eye perceives.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine term** in the cosine law — many students write $E = I/d^2$ even for tilted surfaces. The angle $\theta$ is measured from the **normal**, not the surface.
> 3. **In Michelson contrast**, students often use the wrong denominator (using only $L_{\max}$ or $L_{\min}$). The correct denominator is $L_{\max} + L_{\min}$.
> 4. **Not specifying photopic vs. scotopic** in vision-based answers — KTU examiners award marks for correctly identifying the receptor type and luminance range.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — Always state lux, cd/m², sr, etc., explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- ✅ **Visual Testing (VT)** is the most fundamental NDT method, governed by **ASTM E2702** and **ASME V, Article 9**.
- ✅ The **human eye** consists of cornea → iris → lens → retina (rods + cones) → optic nerve → visual cortex.
- ✅ **Photopic vision** = cones, daylight, color, high acuity. **Scotopic vision** = rods, dark, no color, high sensitivity. **Purkinje shift** = 555 nm → 507 nm.
- ✅ **Snellen acuity** 20/20 is normal. NDT typically requires **20/40 or better** (20/25 for aerospace).
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I/d^{2}$ — doubling distance quarters illuminance.
- ✅ **Cosine Law:** $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^{2}$ — angle measured from normal.
- ✅ **Michelson Contrast:** $C = (L_{\max} - L_{\min}) / (L_{\max} + L_{\min})$; minimum threshold ≈ **0.30**.
- ✅ **Luminance of Lambertian surface:** $L = \rho E / \pi$.
- ✅ **Low-angle lighting (5°–15°)** is used to detect tight surface cracks via shadow contrast.
- ✅ **UV-A at 365 nm** is used for fluorescent PT/MT in a darkened booth (ambient light < 20 lux).
- ✅ **Material attributes** affecting VT: surface roughness ($R_a$), reflectance ($\rho$), color, geometry, contamination.
- ✅ **Environmental factors:** ambient light, dust, temperature (15–30°C), humidity (30–70% RH), operator fatigue.
- ✅ **Dark adaptation** time ≈ **20–30 minutes** for full rod sensitivity (critical for fluorescent NDT).
- ✅ Inspector certification standards: **ASNT SNT-TC-1A, NAS 410, ISO 9712** — mandate ophthalmologic exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Perception and Direct and Indirect Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Perception and Direct and Indirect Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visual Testing (VT) — The Oldest NDT Method

**Visual Testing (VT)**, as defined by **ASME Section V, Article 9** and **ASTM E1316**, is the process of evaluating a material, component, or assembly for surface discontinuities, geometric deviations, and serviceability through direct or indirect human observation, often supplemented with optical aids. It is the **primary, lowest-cost, and most widely employed NDT technique** (accounting for over **60%–80%** of all in-service inspections worldwide).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Visual Testing is positioned at the top of the NDT hierarchy because almost every other NDT method ultimately relies on a *visual confirmation* of the indicated flaw.

## 1.2 What is "Visual Perception"?

**Visual perception** is the brain's ability to interpret the surrounding environment by processing information that is contained in visible light. In NDT, it is the chain of *physiological + cognitive* events by which an inspector converts photons reflected from a test surface into a defect decision.

The complete chain is:

$$\text{Source} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Object} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Eye} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Optic Nerve} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Brain} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Decision}$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Doctor's First Check-up"**
> Imagine visiting a doctor for a stomach ache. Before ordering an X-ray, MRI, or blood test, the doctor first **looks** at you, **asks** questions, and **observes** your skin colour, posture, and eyes. This first non-invasive visual check filters out 70–80% of routine issues.
> In the same way, a quality engineer **first inspects a weld or a casting with the naked eye (or with simple optical aids) before deploying expensive ultrasonics or radiography.** Visual Testing is the "first check-up" of any NDT workflow.

## 1.4 Direct vs. Indirect Visual Methods

| Term | Working Distance | Aids Used | Inspection Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Visual Testing (DVT)** | ≤ **600 mm** from the eye to the surface | Naked eye, hand magnifier, torch, gauge | Surface must be **directly accessible** |
| **Indirect Visual Testing (IVT)** | Eye placed **> 600 mm** from the surface, or surface inaccessible | Mirrors, borescopes, fiberscopes, videoscopes, CCTV, high-speed cameras, microscopes | Used for **hidden, internal, hazardous, or remote** areas |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** "DVT = I can *touch* the surface; IVT = I *cannot* touch the surface."

## 1.5 Key Terminology the Examiner Will Look For

- **Visual Acuity (V):** Sharpness of vision, measured as the reciprocal of the angular size (in arc minutes) of the smallest object that can be resolved. Normal human acuity ≈ **1.0 (corresponding to 1 arc-minute)**.
- **Contrast (C):** Difference in luminance between a defect and its background.
- **Field of View (FOV):** The angular extent observable by the eye (typically **200° horizontally**, **130° vertically**).
- **Resolving Power:** Minimum distance between two points the eye can distinguish as separate.
- **Photopic vs. Scotopic Vision:** Daylight (cone) vs. dim-light (rod) vision. NDT inspections use **photopic** conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Eye — Optical Block Diagram

The eye behaves like a high-performance **35 mm camera** with a variable-aperture lens (the iris), a focusing element (the lens), and a light-sensitive emulsion (the retina). For NDT purposes, only three parameters are critical:

1. **Aperture (Pupil Diameter, d):** Varies from **2 mm** in bright light to **7 mm** in darkness.
2. **Focal Length (f):** ≈ **17 mm**.
3. **Spectral Sensitivity Peak:** **λ ≈ 555 nm** (green-yellow, photopic vision).

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Visual Perception in NDT

A discontinuity can be *physically present* but *perceptually missed*. The following six factors determine detectability:

### (i) Illumination Level (E)
- Measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**.
- Conversion: $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$.
- **ASME V / AWS D1.1 minimum:** **1080 lx (100 fc)** for general surfaces; **5380 lx (500 fc)** for fine-detail work like weld toes.
- **Rule of thumb:** If you cannot comfortably read a 12-point newspaper at the inspection distance, lighting is inadequate.

### (ii) Direction of Light
- Grazing light (low-angle illumination, **< 15°** to the surface) emphasizes **cracks, laps, and undercuts** because they cast long shadows.
- Diffuse light reveals **colour or tonal contrast** (corrosion, scale).
- Direct/axial light reveals **specular highlights** (polished surfaces, pitting).

### (iii) Contrast
Weber's Law defines the **just-noticeable difference (JND)**:

$$C = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{b}} \quad \text{(Weber's Fraction)}$$

where $\Delta L$ is luminance difference between defect and background, $L_{b}$ is background luminance.
- The human eye needs **ΔL/L ≥ 0.02 (2%)** to detect a tonal difference.

### (iv) Angular Size (Resolving Power)
The **Rayleigh criterion** for a circular aperture (the pupil) gives the smallest resolvable angular separation:

$$\theta_{min} \;=\; 1.22 \,\frac{\lambda}{d} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

For $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm}$ and $d = 2 \text{ mm}$ (bright photopic):

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \times \frac{555 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 1.17 \text{ arc-min}$$

> This matches the experimental value of **1 arc-minute** used as the standard for "normal" visual acuity.

The **minimum defect size (m)** visible at viewing distance $D$:

$$m \;=\; \theta_{min} \times D \;\approx\; 0.3 \text{ mm at } D = 250 \text{ mm}$$

### (v) Inspection Time & Fatigue
- Tired inspectors lose up to **30% detection efficiency** after 30 minutes of continuous search.
- Best practice: 20–30 min inspection blocks followed by rest.

### (vi) Inspector Qualification
- **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**, **ISO 9712**, and **AWS QC1** certify VT inspectors in three levels.

## 2.3 Direct Visual Methods (DVT) — Equipment & Procedure

| Equipment | Use | Magnification / Spec |
|---|---|---|
| Naked eye | General sweep, large defects | 1×, viewing distance 250–600 mm |
| Handheld magnifier | Fine cracks, weld undercuts | 2× – 10× |
| Illuminated magnifier | Low-contrast defects (e.g., cracks in aluminium) | 3× – 5×, built-in LED |
| Dumpy / Engineer’s level | Tank shell roundness, column verticality | Optical collimation |
| Measuring microscope | Crack-length measurement on coupons | 10× – 50× with reticle |
| Surface gauge / straight edge | Waviness, flatness | Feeler gauges to **0.05 mm** |
| Penetrant kit (pre-cleaner + visible dye) | Used as an *aid* to DVT for tight cracks | ASTM E165 / ASME V Art. 6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** Examiners love asking *"List 4 direct visual inspection aids."* Memorise at least 6 from the table above.

## 2.4 Indirect Visual Methods (IVT) — Equipment Catalogue

### (a) Mirrors
- Simple dental/dentist-type mirrors for **inside corners, behind flanges**.
- Use **first-surface mirrors** (no ghost image) in critical work.
- Tip-area mirrors can extend reach to **> 1.5 m**.

### (b) Borescopes (Rigid)
- A rigid tube containing a **relay-lens (Hopkins) system** or a fibre-optic image bundle.
- Typical lengths: **100 mm – 1500 mm**; diameters: **2 mm – 25 mm**.
- Viewing directions: **0°, 30°, 70°, 90°, 110°**.
- Light is fed through fibre-optic light guides from an external illuminator.

### (c) Fiberscopes (Flexible)
- Coherent fibre-optic image bundle (≈ **10 000 fibres**) with distal objective.
- Articulation: **2-way or 4-way**, **±180°** bend, **> 1 m** length.
- Disadvantage: image is **pixellated** (each fibre = 1 pixel).

### (d) Videoscopes (Modern Digital)
- **CCD/CMOS sensor** at the tip, image displayed on LCD.
- Image is **non-pixellated**, can be recorded, annotated, transmitted.
- Diameters: **3.9 mm – 8 mm**; lengths: **2 m – 30 m**.
- Industry standard: **Olympus IPLEX**, **GE Mentor Visual iQ**.

### (e) CCTV / Remote-Viewing Cameras
- Used for **underwater hulls, reactor vessels, storage tanks**.
- Often paired with **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles)**.
- Pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ) capability; operation distances up to **several km** via fibre.

### (f) High-Speed Cameras & Stroboscopes
- Inspect **rotating machinery** (turbine blades, printing rollers).
- Frame rates **> 10 000 fps** freeze fast motion.
- Stroboscopes: **20 Hz – 20 kHz** flash rates.

### (g) Optical Comparators / Profile Projectors
- Magnification **10× – 500×**; projected silhouette is overlaid on a **D-size drawing template**.
- Used in **dimensional QC** of machined parts, threads, stampings.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\theta_{min}$ | Eye resolving power | $\theta_{min} = 1.22\,\lambda / d$ | rad |
| $m$ | Min. visible defect | $m = \theta_{min} \times D$ | mm |
| $C$ | Weber contrast | $C = \Delta L / L_{b}$ | dimensionless |
| $V$ | Visual acuity (Snellen) | $V = d_{test} / d_{normal}$ | — |
| $E$ | Illuminance conversion | $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$ | lx, fc |
| $E_{min}$ | Min. inspection light | **1080 lx (100 fc)** general; **5380 lx (500 fc)** detail | lx |
| $NA$ | Borescope numerical aperture | $NA = n \sin\alpha$ | — |
| $M$ | Magnification | $M = 25.4 / f_{eyepiece} \times D_{obj}/D_{tube}$ | — |
| $f$ | Eye focal length | **17 mm** | mm |
| $d$ | Pupil diameter (photopic) | **2 mm** | mm |
| $\lambda_{peak}$ | Peak eye sensitivity | **555 nm** | nm |
| $M_{acuity}$ | Standard Snellen line | **6/6 (20/20)** $\equiv$ **1 arc-min** | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Numbers to Memorise for KTU Viva:**
> 1 arc-minute resolving power • 17 mm focal length • 555 nm peak sensitivity • 1080 lx minimum light • 0.3 mm minimum defect at 250 mm viewing distance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolving Power of the Human Eye

**Given:**
- Wavelength of light used: $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm} = 555 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$
- Photopic pupil diameter: $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

**Find:** The minimum angular separation $\theta_{min}$ that the eye can resolve.

**Step 1 — Start with the Rayleigh Criterion for a circular aperture.**

The Rayleigh criterion defines the smallest angle at which two point sources produce distinguishable diffraction patterns (one Airy disc's centre coincides with the first minimum of the other).

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \;\frac{\lambda}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values (SI units).**

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \times \frac{555 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$1.22 \times 555 \times 10^{-9} = 677.1 \times 10^{-9} = 6.771 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{6.771 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.3855 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 5 — Convert radians to arc-minutes (1 rad = 3438 arc-min).**

$$\theta_{min} = 3.3855 \times 10^{-4} \times 3438 \approx 1.164 \text{ arc-min}$$

**Result (rounded for KTU):**

$$\boxed{\;\theta_{min} \;\approx\; 1 \text{ arc-minute} \;\approx\; 2.91 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}\;}$$

This confirms the standard 6/6 (20/20) Snellen acuity used clinically and in NDT inspector qualification.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Detectable Defect Size on a Surface

**Given:** Inspection at near-distance $D = 250 \text{ mm}$ (the standard "near-point" of vision). Eye resolving power $\theta_{min} = 0.3 \text{ mrad} = 1 \text{ arc-min}$.

**Find:** The minimum defect width $m$ that an inspector can just detect.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the viewing triangle.**

For small angles, the arc length on the retina approximates the chord:

$$m \;\approx\; \theta_{min} \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (consistent units: mm).**

$$\theta_{min} = 0.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

$$m = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 250 = 7.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor used by ASME/AWS.**

Inspection codes use $m \approx 0.3 \text{ mm}$ at $D = 250 \text{ mm}$ (a 4× safety margin to account for contrast, fatigue, surface roughness).

**Final Working Equation (use this in KTU problems):**

$$\boxed{\;m_{min} \;\approx\; 0.3 \text{ mm at } D = 250 \text{ mm} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; 0.1 \times D\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weber's Contrast for Defect Detectability

**Given:** A dark crack on a bright steel surface. Background luminance $L_{b} = 200 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$, crack luminance $L_{c} = 180 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Write Weber's fraction.**

$$C = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{b}} = \frac{\vert L_{c} - L_{b} \vert}{L_{b}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$C = \frac{\vert 180 - 200 \vert}{200} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Compare with JND threshold.**

The eye's just-noticeable difference is $C_{JND} = 0.02$.

Since $C = 0.10 > 0.02$, the crack is **easily detectable**.

**Step 4 — Counter-example:** If $L_{c} = 198 \text{ cd/m}^{2}$:

$$C = \frac{2}{200} = 0.01 < 0.02 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{invisible without aid}$$

This proves why **penetrant testing** is used for tight, low-contrast cracks.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Borescope Image Size

**Question:** A rigid borescope of length $L = 500 \text{ mm}$, objective focal length $f_{obj} = 4 \text{ mm}$, eyepiece focal length $f_{eye} = 25 \text{ mm}$ is used to view a defect 200 mm beyond the objective. Calculate the magnification.

**Step 1 — Image distance from objective (lens formula).**

$$\frac{1}{v_{obj}} - \frac{1}{u_{obj}} = \frac{1}{f_{obj}}$$

With $u_{obj} = -200 \text{ mm}$ (object on left):

$$\frac{1}{v_{obj}} = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{200} = \frac{50 - 1}{200} = \frac{49}{200}$$

$$v_{obj} = \frac{200}{49} = 4.08 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral magnification of objective.**

$$M_{obj} = -\frac{v_{obj}}{u_{obj}} = -\frac{4.08}{-200} = 0.0204$$

**Step 3 — Magnification of eyepiece (simple magnifier, $D = 250 \text{ mm}$).**

$$M_{eye} = \frac{D}{f_{eye}} = \frac{250}{25} = 10$$

**Step 4 — Total magnification.**

$$M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{eye} = 0.0204 \times 10 = 0.204$$

**Interpretation:** A 5 mm defect appears as **0.204 × 5 ≈ 1.02 mm** on the retina-equivalent — **NOT magnified overall** (it appears at *near-eye size*). This is why borescopes also include a **zoom module** to obtain useful magnification.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use DVT vs IVT

| Inspection Condition | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Weld cap on a pressure vessel (accessible) | **DVT** with 10× magnifier | Direct line of sight; ASME V Art. 9 |
| Inside a 50 mm bore of a turbine blade | **IVT (Borescope)** | Physical access impossible |
| Reactor vessel during service (high radiation) | **IVT (CCTV / Periscope)** | Personnel safety |
| Submerged ship hull plate | **IVT (ROV camera)** | Diver-free, safer |
| Cracks in aluminium under white scale | **DVT + Dye Penetrant** | Contrast enhancement |
| Micro-cracks on a turbine disc | **IVT (Microscope / Video)** | Sub-mm resolution |
| Moving conveyor belt welds | **IVT (High-speed / line-scan camera)** | Real-time automation |
| Welds in a thick-walled pipe from inside | **IVT (Fiberscope)** | Articulation around bends |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block Diagram of the Human Visual Perception Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source Sun or Lamp] --> B[Test Object Surface]
    B --> C[Reflected Light Photons]
    C --> D[Cornea Refraction]
    D --> E[Pupil Aperture 2 to 7 mm]
    E --> F[Lens Variable Focus]
    F --> G[Retina Rods and Cones]
    G --> H[Optic Nerve Signal]
    H --> I[Visual Cortex Brain]
    I --> J[Defect Decision]
    J --> K[Inspector Records Finding]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a *transducer* — a small loss of fidelity at any stage reduces final defect detectability. The weakest link is usually the **brain's interpretation** (Pattern Recognition), which is why training and qualification matter.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Classification of Visual Testing Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VT[Visual Testing VT] --> DVT[Direct Visual Testing DVT]
    VT --> IVT[Indirect Visual Testing IVT]

    DVT --> D1[Naked Eye]
    DVT --> D2[Handheld Magnifier]
    DVT --> D3[Engineer Level or Surface Gauge]
    DVT --> D4[Measuring Microscope]

    IVT --> I1[Optical Aids]
    IVT --> I2[Remote Imaging]
    IVT --> I3[Motion Analysis]

    I1 --> I1a[Mirrors]
    I1 --> I1b[Rigid Borescope]
    I1 --> I1c[Fiberscope]
    I1 --> I1d[Videoscope]
    I1 --> I1e[Profile Projector]

    I2 --> I2a[CCTV Camera]
    I2 --> I2b[ROV with Camera]
    I2 --> I2c[Periscope]

    I3 --> I3a[High Speed Camera]
    I3 --> I3b[Stroboscope]
    I3 --> I3c[Thermal Imaging Camera]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Schematic of a Modern Videoscope (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Light Source 150W Metal Halide] --> B[Fibre Optic Light Guide]
    B --> C[Distal Tip 6mm]
    C --> D[Illumination of Internal Surface]
    D --> E[Reflected Image Captured by CMOS Sensor]
    E --> F[Image Processor DSP in Handle]
    F --> G[5 inch LCD Monitor]
    F --> H[USB or SD Card Storage]
    F --> I[Articulation Cables 4 way]
    C --> I
```

> **Function:** Light is *delivered* through the fibre bundle to illuminate the inaccessible area; the CMOS sensor *captures* the reflected image; the DSP *enhances and digitises* it for live display and archival storage. This is the engineering embodiment of an **indirect** visual method.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Lighting Geometry for Crack Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Light Source] -->|Angle alpha less than 15 degrees| B[Test Surface]
    B --> C[Defect Crack or Lap]
    C -->|Casts Long Shadow| D[Inspector Eye]
    A -->|Direct Illumination| D
    D --> E[Perceived High Contrast Crack]
```

> **Engineering insight:** At $\alpha < 15°$ (grazing angle), even sub-millimetre cracks cast shadows tens of millimetres long, increasing their **apparent angular size** by 5–10× and pushing them above the eye's 1 arc-minute threshold.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Visual Testing (VT). List any four direct visual testing aids used in NDT.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

Visual Testing is the process of evaluating a component's surface condition, dimensions, and structural integrity through direct or indirect human observation, often supplemented by optical instruments, **without impairing the usefulness of the part** (ASME V, Article 9). It is the oldest, simplest, and most cost-effective NDT method.

**Four direct visual testing aids:**
1. **Handheld magnifying glass (2×–10×)** – for surface crack sizing.
2. **Illuminated magnifier with built-in LED** – for low-contrast defects.
3. **Engineer's level / dumpy level** – for measuring verticality and roundness of tanks.
4. **Measuring microscope with reticle** – for accurate crack length measurement on cross-sections.
5. *(Optional: surface gauge with feeler stock; steel rule; vernier calliper).*

VT is governed by codes such as **ASME V Art. 9**, **AWS D1.1**, and **ASTM E270**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain the term "visual acuity" and state the standard value for a normal human eye.**

**Model Answer:**

Visual acuity (V) is a quantitative measure of the eye's ability to distinguish fine detail. It is defined as the **reciprocal of the angular size (in arc-minutes) of the smallest object that can be resolved at a standard viewing distance of 250 mm (or 20 ft, for the Snellen chart).**

$$V = \frac{d_{test}}{d_{normal}}$$

For a normal, healthy adult human eye, the standard value of visual acuity is **V = 1.0**, which corresponds to a **resolving power of 1 arc-minute (≈ 0.3 mrad or 0.00029 rad)**. This means two points separated by an angle of 1 arc-minute appear as distinct entities.

In NDT inspector qualification (per **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** and **ISO 9712**), a near-vision acuity of **Jaeger 1 (J1) or Times New Roman N4.5** at **300 mm** is mandatory, with optional colour-vision testing (Ishihara plates) for penetrant and radiographic inspectors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Module Internal Choice Option 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **anatomy of the human eye** as it relates to NDT visual perception. Discuss the role of rods, cones, and the visual cortex in defect detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, classify the **direct and indirect methods of visual testing** and explain any **three indirect methods** in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Anatomy of the Human Eye (7 marks)**

| Structure | Optical Function | NDT Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Cornea** | Primary refraction (≈ 43 D) | Protective window |
| **Iris** | Variable aperture (pupil 2–7 mm) | Camera diaphragm |
| **Lens** | Variable focal length (f ≈ 17 mm) | Autofocus element |
| **Retina** | Photographic emulsion | CCD/CMOS sensor |
| **Rods** | Scotopic (low-light) vision, ~120 million, periphery | "Search" detectors |
| **Cones** | Photopic (bright-light) vision, ~6 million, fovea | "Resolution" detectors |
| **Optic Nerve** | Signal conduit | Data cable |
| **Visual Cortex** | Pattern recognition, decision | Image-processing CPU |

**Key points for full marks:**
1. Cones operate in bright light (≥ 3 cd/m²) and provide colour vision with peak sensitivity at **λ = 555 nm (green-yellow)** — NDT inspections must therefore be performed under **photopic conditions**.
2. Rods operate in dim light but produce **achromatic** images — unsuitable for colour-based defect classification.
3. The **fovea centralis** has the highest density of cones and provides 1 arc-minute acuity. It must be aimed directly at the suspect defect.
4. The **visual cortex** applies Gestalt principles (closure, continuity, similarity) — the inspector's training and experience determine whether a *real* flaw is distinguished from a *fabricated* one. This is why **false calls** are common in untrained inspection.
5. Eye fatigue reduces detection by 30% in 30 min — hence mandatory rest cycles.

**(b) Direct vs Indirect Methods (7 marks)**

*(Refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4 for the block diagram.)*

**Direct methods (DVT):** The eye is within 600 mm of the surface. Examples: naked eye, magnifier, surface gauge, measuring microscope.

**Indirect methods (IVT):** The eye is beyond 600 mm, or the surface is inaccessible.

**Three indirect methods in detail:**

**1. Rigid Borescope (3 marks)**
- Construction: stainless-steel tube, **Hopkins rod-lens relay system** (alternating glass rods of differing refractive index), eyepiece.
- Lengths: 100–1500 mm; diameters: 2–25 mm; viewing directions: 0°, 30°, 70°, 90°, 110°.
- Light is delivered via a **fibre-optic light guide** from an external illuminator (typically 150 W halogen or LED).
- Used in: aircraft engine bores, gun barrels, turbine casings.
- Advantages: high optical resolution, rugged, no electronics at tip.
- Limitations: rigid — cannot navigate bends.

**2. Flexible Fiberscope (2 marks)**
- Construction: coherent bundle of ~10 000 glass fibres (each 10 µm), distal objective, eyepiece, articulation cables.
- Tip articulation: 2-way or 4-way, ±180°.
- Limitation: image is **pixellated** (one fibre = one pixel) — resolution limited by fibre diameter.

**3. Videoscope (2 marks)**
- Modern digital device: **CCD/CMOS sensor** at the tip, image displayed on a 5–8 inch LCD.
- Advantages: non-pixellated image, video recording, measurement overlays, image enhancement (brightness, contrast, negative).
- Diameters 3.9–8 mm, lengths 2–30 m, **IPLEX / Mentor Visual iQ** are industry standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in VT Answers:**
> 1. Writing *"DVT = naked eye only"* — **wrong.** DVT includes hand magnifiers, levels, and measuring microscopes. The discriminator is *physical accessibility*, not the absence of aids.
> 2. Confusing **borescope vs. fiberscope** — examiners WILL deduct 1–2 marks if you interchange them. Borescope = rigid Hopkins optics; Fiberscope = flexible fibre bundle.
> 3. Stating *"visual acuity is 6/6"* without explaining that **6/6 corresponds to 1 arc-minute resolving power** — partial credit only.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **illumination levels (1080 lx general, 5380 lx detail)** — a guaranteed 1-mark loss in questions on lighting.

---

### Question B (Module Internal Choice Option 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2022]**

**(a)** Define **resolving power of the eye**. Derive the minimum defect size that can be detected at a viewing distance of 250 mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of lighting in visual inspection** with reference to type, intensity, and direction of light. What are the minimum illumination levels prescribed by ASME V and AWS D1.1? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Resolving Power & Minimum Defect Size (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Resolving power is the minimum angular separation between two points at which they appear as distinct entities. The standard value for a normal human eye is **1 arc-minute (≈ 0.3 mrad)**.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the Rayleigh criterion for a circular aperture (pupil):

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \;\frac{\lambda}{d}$$

For photopic vision: $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm}$, $d = 2 \text{ mm}$.

$$\theta_{min} = 1.22 \times \frac{555 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.385 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 1 \text{ arc-min}$$

For small angles, the minimum defect size on the test surface is:

$$m_{min} = \theta_{min} \times D$$

At the standard near-point viewing distance $D = 250 \text{ mm}$:

$$m_{min} = 3.385 \times 10^{-4} \times 250 = 0.0846 \text{ mm} \approx 0.08 \text{ mm}$$

However, in practice (per ASME/AWS codes), a **4× safety margin** is applied for contrast, surface finish, and inspector fatigue:

$$\boxed{\;m_{min,\,practical} \approx 0.3 \text{ mm at } D = 250 \text{ mm}\;}$$

Equivalently: $m_{min} \approx 0.1 \times D$ for a normal-sighted inspector.

**(b) Role of Lighting in Visual Inspection (7 marks)**

Lighting is the single most important *environmental* factor in VT. Inadequate lighting accounts for ~70% of missed defects in field inspections.

**Type of Light (2 marks):**
- **Natural daylight** (5500 K) — best colour rendering but uncontrollable.
- **Incandescent (2800 K)** — warm, good for crack detection on grey surfaces.
- **Fluorescent (4000 K)** — diffuse, good for general inspection.
- **LED (5000–6500 K)** — preferred today; shadow-free, long life, dimmable.
- **Mercury-vapour / sodium-vapour** — poor colour rendering, not recommended.

**Intensity (3 marks):**
Measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**. $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$.

| Code | Minimum Illumination |
|---|---|
| **ASME V, Article 9** | **1080 lx (100 fc)** general; **5380 lx (500 fc)** for cracks/undercuts |
| **AWS D1.1** | **538 lx (50 fc)** minimum; **1076 lx (100 fc)** for welds |
| **ASTM E270** | Matches ASME V |

**Rule of thumb:** If the inspector cannot comfortably read a 12-point newspaper at the inspection distance, the lighting is insufficient.

**Direction of Light (2 marks):**
- **Direct (axial) light** — reveals surface texture, specular highlights.
- **Diffuse light** — reduces shadows; good for colour/contrast.
- **Grazing light (< 15° to surface)** — emphasizes cracks, laps, undercuts via long shadows. **This is the most powerful technique for crack detection.**
- **Back-lighting** — used for translucent materials or to silhouette a part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Rapid-Fire Points for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Visual Testing:**

- [ ] **VT = Visual Testing** = oldest, cheapest, most widely used NDT method.
- [ ] **ASME V, Article 9** is the primary code for VT.
- [ ] **DVT** = eye within **600 mm**; **IVT** = eye beyond **600 mm** OR inaccessible.
- [ ] **Visual acuity (normal) = 1 arc-minute ≈ 0.3 mrad ≈ 6/6 (Snellen).**
- [ ] **Resolving power formula:** $\theta_{min} = 1.22\,\lambda/d$.
- [ ] **Eye peak sensitivity:** $\lambda = 555 \text{ nm}$ (green-yellow, photopic).
- [ ] **Eye focal length:** $f \approx 17 \text{ mm}$; **pupil:** 2–7 mm.
- [ ] **Minimum defect size:** $m_{min} \approx 0.3 \text{ mm at } D = 250 \text{ mm}$.
- [ ] **Weber contrast threshold:** $C_{JND} = 0.02$ (2%).
- [ ] **Minimum illumination (ASME V):** **1080 lx (100 fc)** general; **5380 lx (500 fc)** detail.
- [ ] **Conversion:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$.
- [ ] **Grazing light (< 15°)** is the best technique for crack detection.
- [ ] **DVT aids:** naked eye, magnifier (2–10×), engineer's level, surface gauge, measuring microscope.
- [ ] **IVT aids:** mirrors, borescope (rigid), fiberscope (flexible), videoscope, CCTV, ROV, high-speed camera, stroboscope, profile projector.
- [ ] **Borescope** = rigid Hopkins rod-lens; **Fiberscope** = flexible coherent fibre bundle; **Videoscope** = digital CCD/CMOS at tip.
- [ ] **Rods** = scotopic, achromatic, ~120 M; **Cones** = photopic, chromatic, ~6 M.
- [ ] **Fovea centralis** = highest cone density → 1 arc-min acuity.
- [ ] **Eye fatigue** reduces detection by 30% in 30 min — rest cycles mandatory.
- [ ] **Inspector qualifications:** ASNT SNT-TC-1A, ISO 9712, AWS QC1.
- [ ] **Mandatory vision test:** Jaeger 1 (J1) or N4.5 at 300 mm; colour test (Ishihara) for PT/RT inspectors.

> **Closing Note for KTU Aspirants:** *Visual Testing is the simplest NDT method on paper, but the examiner rewards the depth of physiological and physical insight you bring. Always link your answer back to the **eye → brain → decision** chain, cite the **1080 lx / 5380 lx** numbers, and never confuse the three scopes (borescope, fiberscope, videoscope).* Master these, and full marks are yours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mirrors, Magnifiers, Boroscopes, Fibroscopes, and Closed Circuit Television
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mirrors, Magnifiers, Boroscopes, Fibroscopes, and Closed Circuit Television

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Visual Inspection Aids in NDT

Visual examination is the **oldest and most fundamental** method of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT). It relies on the human eye, either directly or with the aid of optical instruments, to detect surface discontinuities such as **cracks, corrosion, wear, foreign object debris (FOD), and loose parts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Visual testing (VT) is recognized as the primary NDT method because every other NDT technique must ultimately conclude with a visual confirmation of the defect indication.

When the inspection area is **not directly accessible** to the naked eye (e.g., inside a turbine blade, behind a wall, in a nuclear reactor vessel), engineers use **optical accessories** that transmit or redirect light from the defect location back to the observer.

### 1.2 The Five Primary Optical Aids (Module 1 Scope)

| S.No | Optical Aid | Primary Function in NDT |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Mirrors** | Redirect line-of-sight at an angle |
| 2 | **Magnifiers** | Enlarge surface features for visibility |
| 3 | **Boroscopes** | Inspect straight, internal cavities |
| 4 | **Fibroscopes** | Inspect curved, internal cavities |
| 5 | **CCTV** | Remote viewing in hazardous zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All five aids fall under **Visual Testing (VT)** — codified by **ASNT Code No. CP-189** and **ASTM E1316** as a legitimate NDT discipline carrying **SNT-TC-1A** certification.

---

## 1.3 Mirrors — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
**Mirrors** in NDT are precisely polished reflective surfaces (usually first-surface aluminized glass) used to **redirect the line-of-sight** of an inspector to areas that cannot be viewed directly. They obey the **Law of Reflection**: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

### Conceptual Analogy
> Think of a **periscope in a submarine**. A sailor standing below sea level uses two parallel mirrors angled at **45°** to "see" what's happening above the water. In NDT, the same principle lets an inspector look *inside* a pressure vessel without cutting it open.

### Standard Types Used in NDT
1. **Flat Mirrors** — Produce a virtual, same-size image. Used in simple periscopes and hand-held inspection mirrors.
2. **Concave Mirrors** — Produce magnified virtual images when the object is within the focal length. Useful for illumination gathering.
3. **Convex Mirrors** — Produce diminished, upright virtual images with a **wide field of view**. Used as inspection mirrors in tight corners (e.g., aircraft wing tanks).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, remember: **Convex = Wide field (security mirror analogy)** and **Concave = Focused beam (torch/headlight analogy)**.

---

## 1.4 Magnifiers — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
A **magnifier** is a single positive (converging) lens — or a system of lenses — that produces a **virtual, erect, and enlarged image** of an object placed within its focal length, thereby enabling the human eye to resolve finer surface details than it could unaided.

### Conceptual Analogy
> A watchmaker places a small biconvex lens close to a tiny gear. The gear's teeth, which looked like a blur to the naked eye, suddenly become large and clear. **That single lens is a magnifier.**

### Key Optical Parameter
The **Magnifying Power (MP)** or **Angular Magnification (M)** is given as:

$$MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

where **D = 25 cm** (the *Least Distance of Distinct Vision* or *Near Point*) and **f** is the focal length of the lens in cm.

> [!NOTE]
> The **near point of the human eye = 25 cm** is a *physiological standard*, not a variable. This is frequently tested in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 1.5 Boroscopes — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
A **Boroscope** (from Greek *bora* = "to bore" + *scope* = "to look") is a **rigid optical instrument** consisting of a tube containing a series of precisely aligned lenses and relay lenses (or gradient-index rods), used for **direct visual inspection of the interior of cavities, bores, and hollow components**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine looking down a long, hollow drinking straw. Without help, you see only darkness. If you thread a rigid tube of lenses down the straw and shine a small light at the far end, you can now see the bottom clearly. **That rigid tube of lenses is a boroscope.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Boroscopes are **rigid** — they cannot bend. They are ideal for straight bores such as gun barrels, engine cylinders, and heat-exchanger tubes of limited length.

---

## 1.6 Fibroscopes — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
A **Fibroscope** (also called a *flexible fiberscope* or *fiber-optic borescope*) replaces the rigid relay-lens train of a boroscope with a **coherent bundle of optical fibers**, allowing the distal tip to be **articulated** through bends to reach curved or tortuous internal geometries.

### Conceptual Analogy
> Take a bundle of thousands of thin, transparent "noodles" (fibers), each thinner than a human hair. Light entering one end of each noodle emerges from the other end — even if the noodle is bent into a U-shape. **A fibroscope is essentially a flexible bundle of these light-carrying noodles.**

### Two Fiber Types Used
- **Illumination Fibers** — Non-coherent bundle carrying *cold light* from an external source to the tip.
- **Image Fibers** — *Coherent bundle* (each fiber has a fixed spatial relationship at both ends) carrying the *image* back to the eyepiece.

---

## 1.7 Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
**Closed Circuit Television (CCTV)** in NDT is a remote visual inspection system that uses a **miniature camera** (often charge-coupled device, CCD, or CMOS) mounted at the inspection head, connected via **electrical cables** (the "closed circuit") to a remote **monitor, recorder, and image processor**, allowing inspection of areas that are **hazardous, radioactive, very hot, or otherwise inaccessible to a human observer**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> A security guard in a control room watches live video from cameras placed in a bank vault. He never enters the vault himself, but he sees everything inside. **CCTV in NDT works identically — the "vault" is a reactor core or a jet engine, and the "control room" is the inspection station.**

> [!TIP]
> The term *Closed Circuit* distinguishes it from broadcast TV: **the signal travels along a private, dedicated cable**, not through the public airwaves.

> [!WARNING]
> CCTV systems do **NOT** use fiber optics for image transmission (that would be a fibroscope). They use **electrical video signals**, typically analog (composite, Y/C) or digital (HD-SDI, GigE Vision, USB3 Vision).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mirrors — Image Formation Theory

### 2.1.1 Law of Reflection
For any mirror used in NDT, the **angle of incidence** $\theta_i$ measured from the normal equals the **angle of reflection** $\theta_r$:

$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$

The **incident ray, reflected ray, and normal** all lie in the same plane.

### 2.1.2 Mirror Equation
For curved mirrors, the relationship between object distance ($u$), image distance ($v$), and focal length ($f$) is:

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

The **magnification** produced by the mirror is:

$$m = -\frac{v}{u} = \frac{h_i}{h_o}$$

### 2.1.3 Sign Convention (Cartesian — KTU Standard)
- Distances measured **from the pole** of the mirror.
- Distances measured in the **direction of incident light** = **positive**.
- Distances measured **against incident light** = **negative**.
- Heights **above principal axis** = **positive**; **below** = **negative**.

### 2.1.4 Image Characteristics Table

| Mirror Type | Object Position | Image Position | Image Nature | Use in NDT |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|--------------|------------|
| Concave | At infinity | At focus F | Real, inverted, point-sized | Telescope objective, focusing parallel light |
| Concave | Beyond C | Between F and C | Real, inverted, diminished | Inspection lamp reflector |
| Concave | At C | At C | Real, inverted, same size | Image inverter |
| Concave | Between F and C | Beyond C | Real, inverted, magnified | Projector / specimen magnifier |
| Concave | At F | At infinity | Real, inverted, highly magnified | Collimator |
| Concave | Between F and Pole | Behind mirror | Virtual, erect, magnified | **Shaving / make-up mirror** |
| Convex | Anywhere | Between P and F (behind) | Virtual, erect, diminished | **Rear-view mirror, NDT inspection corner mirror** |
| Plane | Anywhere | Same distance behind | Virtual, erect, same size | Periscope |

> [!NOTE]
> Convex mirrors in NDT inspection: because the image is always **virtual, erect, and diminished**, the inspector sees a *wider area* than a flat mirror of the same size would show. This is critical for inspecting the **interior corners of aircraft wings and tank compartments**.

---

## 2.2 Magnifiers — Optical Theory

### 2.2.1 Simple Magnifier Construction
A simple magnifier is a **single converging (biconvex or plano-convex) lens** of short focal length (typically **2.5 cm to 10 cm**).

### 2.2.2 Working Principle
The lens is held such that the **object lies within the focal length** ($u < f$). The eye sees a **virtual, erect, magnified image** that appears at the **least distance of distinct vision (D = 25 cm)** for comfortable viewing.

### 2.2.3 Magnifying Power Formula Derivation

**Case 1 — Image formed at Near Point (D = 25 cm):**

$$MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

**Case 2 — Image formed at infinity (relaxed eye, more comfortable):**

$$MP = \frac{D}{f}$$

### 2.2.4 Compound Microscope (For Comparison)
Used in NDT for examining small cracks (e.g., in turbine blades at high magnification). The magnifying power is:

$$MP_{compound} = -\frac{L}{f_o} \times \left(1 + \frac{D}{f_e}\right)$$

where $L$ is the tube length, $f_o$ is the focal length of the objective, and $f_e$ is the focal length of the eyepiece.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, the **simple magnifier formula** is far more frequently tested than the compound microscope formula.

---

## 2.3 Boroscopes — Optical Train Theory

### 2.3.1 Components of a Boroscope
A boroscope consists of:
1. **Eyepiece** — Magnifies the image formed by the relay system.
2. **Relay Lens System** — A series of precisely spaced converging lenses that *transfer* the image down the rigid tube. Each lens pair forms a real, inverted intermediate image.
3. **Objective Lens** — At the distal (viewing) end; forms the first real image of the object.
4. **Illumination Channel** — Either a coaxial fiber-optic light guide or an integral incandescent lamp; provides light at the inspection tip.
5. **Mirror / Prism at Tip** — Allows **side-viewing (90°)** or **forward-viewing (0°)**.

### 2.3.2 Relay Lens Imaging
In a typical relay system, if the object is placed at distance $u_1$ from the first lens of focal length $f_1$:

$$\frac{1}{v_1} = \frac{1}{f_1} - \frac{1}{u_1}$$

This image becomes the object for the second relay lens, and the process continues down the tube.

### 2.3.3 Limitations of Boroscopes
- **Rigid** — cannot follow curves.
- **Image quality degrades** with length due to cumulative lens aberrations.
- **Typical practical length ≤ 1.5 m** without severe image degradation.
- **Diameter limited** by the need to fit relay lenses inside the tube.

---

## 2.4 Fibroscopes — Fiber Optic Theory

### 2.4.1 Principle of Total Internal Reflection
A fiber optic strand consists of a **core** (high refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by **cladding** (lower refractive index $n_2$). Light entering the core at an angle less than the **acceptance angle** $\theta_a$ undergoes **total internal reflection** at the core-cladding interface and travels the entire length of the fiber.

### 2.4.2 Numerical Aperture (NA)
The light-gathering ability of a fiber is defined by the **Numerical Aperture**:

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sin\theta_a$$

### 2.4.3 Coherent vs. Incoherent Bundles
| Property | Coherent Bundle | Incoherent Bundle |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Fiber arrangement | Fixed at both ends | Random |
| Function | Image transmission | Light transmission |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Use in fibroscope | Image fibers | Illumination fibers |

### 2.4.4 Image Resolution
The **resolution** of a fibroscope is limited by the **center-to-center spacing of the individual fibers** in the coherent bundle. A bundle with **10 µm fiber diameter** can resolve features down to about **50 µm** (limited by the Nyquist sampling theorem for fiber arrays).

### 2.4.5 Articulation Mechanism
Modern industrial fibroscopes have a **four-way articulating tip** controlled by wires (Bowden cables). Typical articulation range is **±180°** in two perpendicular planes, giving **360°** coverage.

---

## 2.5 CCTV — Remote Visual Inspection Theory

### 2.5.1 System Architecture
A CCTV-based Remote Visual Inspection (RVI) system consists of:
1. **Camera Head** — Miniature CCD/CMOS camera (diameter 6–60 mm) with integral LED illumination.
2. **Cable** — Coaxial or fiber-optic hybrid cable carrying power, video, and control signals.
3. **Control Unit / Light Source** — Provides power, regulates illumination, and sometimes controls motorized focus / pan / zoom.
4. **Display & Recorder** — LCD monitor and digital video recorder for documentation.
5. **Image Processing Software** — For measurement (e.g., crack length quantification using stereo photogrammetry).

### 2.5.2 Advantages Over Fiber Optics
- **Higher resolution** (HD/4K possible).
- **No fiber mosaic artifacts** in the image.
- **Stereo measurement** possible with dual-camera heads.
- **Recording is straightforward** (digital video).

### 2.5.3 Industrial Applications
- Inspection of **nuclear reactor pressure vessels** (highly radioactive — human access forbidden).
- Inspection of **boiler tubes** in operating power stations.
- Inspection of **aircraft engine turbine blades** during overhaul.
- **Underwater pipeline** inspection.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize every row.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Law of Reflection | $\theta_i = \theta_r$ | Angles from normal | Degrees or radians |
| 2 | Mirror Equation | $\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$ | u,v,f = distances from pole | cm or m |
| 3 | Mirror Magnification | $m = -\frac{v}{u} = \frac{h_i}{h_o}$ | Ratio of image to object height | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Simple Magnifier MP (image at D) | $MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$ | D = near point distance | **D = 25 cm** |
| 5 | Simple Magnifier MP (image at $\infty$) | $MP = \frac{D}{f}$ | Relaxed eye case | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Compound Microscope MP | $MP = -\frac{L}{f_o}\left(1 + \frac{D}{f_e}\right)$ | L = tube length | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Numerical Aperture of Fiber | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | n₁=core, n₂=cladding index | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | Half-angle of acceptance cone | Degrees |
| 9 | Critical Angle (TIR condition) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Minimum angle for TIR | Degrees |
| 10 | Linear Magnification (lens) | $m = \frac{v}{u}$ | For thin lens | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Lens Equation (Thin Lens) | $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Sign convention per Cartesian | cm |
| 12 | Power of a Lens | $P = \frac{1}{f_{(m)}}$ | f in metres | Dioptres (D) |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical difference to remember:** Mirror equation uses **+ u** (Cartesian convention from pole to object is negative, but in NDT textbooks the simplified form is given with both signs as written above). For KTU board exams, **always state the sign convention** you are using before solving.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Tool | Real Industrial Application |
|------|----------------------------|
| Convex Mirror | Inspecting the rear face of a pipe without entering it; checking blind spots in aircraft wings |
| Magnifier (10×) | Surface crack verification on welded joints, aircraft skin |
| Boroscope | Engine cylinder bore inspection in automotive workshops (cylinder walls, piston crowns) |
| Fibroscope | Gas turbine blade inspection through fuel nozzle ports; airframe inspection behind rivets |
| CCTV | Nuclear reactor vessel inspection during outages; underwater hull inspection of ships |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnifying Power of a Simple Magnifier (Image at Near Point D)

### Given
- A converging lens of focal length $f$.
- An object of height $h_o$ placed at distance $u$ from the lens, **within the focal length** ($u < f$).
- The eye views the virtual image at the **least distance of distinct vision, $D = 25\text{ cm}$**.

### To Prove
$$MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of Magnifying Power.**
Magnifying power is defined as the ratio of the **angle subtended at the eye by the image** (when seen through the magnifier) to the **angle subtended by the object** when placed at the near point without the magnifier:

$$MP = \frac{\theta_{image}}{\theta_{object,at\,D}}$$

**Step 2 — Angle subtended by the object at near point D.**
For a small object of height $h_o$ placed at the near point, the angle is:

$$\theta_{object} \approx \tan\theta_{object} = \frac{h_o}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the thin-lens equation.**
For the virtual image formed at distance $v = -D$ (negative because it is on the same side as the object in the sign convention), and the object at distance $u$:

$$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Substituting $v = -D$:

$$\frac{1}{-D} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Rearranging for $\frac{1}{u}$:

$$\frac{1}{u} = -\frac{1}{D} - \frac{1}{f} = -\left(\frac{1}{D} + \frac{1}{f}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$u = -\frac{Df}{D+f}$$

**Step 4 — Height of the virtual image.**
From the magnification formula $m = \frac{v}{u}$:

$$h_i = m \cdot h_o = \frac{v}{u} \cdot h_o$$

Substituting $v = -D$ and $u = -\frac{Df}{D+f}$:

$$h_i = \frac{-D}{-\frac{Df}{D+f}} \cdot h_o = \frac{-D(D+f)}{-Df} \cdot h_o = \frac{D+f}{f} \cdot h_o$$

**Step 5 — Angle subtended by the image at the eye.**
Since the virtual image is at distance $D$ from the eye:

$$\theta_{image} \approx \tan\theta_{image} = \frac{h_i}{D} = \frac{(D+f) \cdot h_o}{f \cdot D}$$

**Step 6 — Form the ratio.**

$$MP = \frac{\theta_{image}}{\theta_{object}} = \frac{\dfrac{(D+f)\,h_o}{f\,D}}{\dfrac{h_o}{D}}$$

$$MP = \frac{(D+f)\,h_o}{f\,D} \times \frac{D}{h_o}$$

$$MP = \frac{D+f}{f} = \frac{D}{f} + 1$$

$$\boxed{MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}}$$

**Hence proved.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Aperture of an Optical Fiber

### Given
- Core refractive index $= n_1$
- Cladding refractive index $= n_2$, where $n_1 > n_2$
- External medium (air) refractive index $n_0 = 1$

### To Prove
$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the air–core interface.**
Let $\theta_a$ be the half-angle of the cone of light accepted by the fiber (acceptance angle), and $\theta_1$ be the refracted angle inside the core:

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

Since $n_0 = 1$:

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Express $\theta_1$ in terms of the angle at the core-cladding boundary.**
The ray inside the core meets the core-cladding interface. Let $\phi$ be the angle of incidence at this boundary. By geometry (the ray is refracted from air along the axis, then travels along the fiber):

$$\theta_1 + \phi = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 90° - \theta_1$$

$$\sin\phi = \sin(90° - \theta_1) = \cos\theta_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Total Internal Reflection condition.**
For TIR, $\phi \geq \theta_c$, where the critical angle satisfies:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

For the *maximum* acceptance angle (just at the verge of TIR), $\phi = \theta_c$:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sin\theta_1$ using the Pythagorean identity.**
Since $\sin^2\theta_1 + \cos^2\theta_1 = 1$:

$$\sin^2\theta_1 = 1 - \cos^2\theta_1 = 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

$$\sin\theta_1 = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into Step 1.**

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

$$\boxed{\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = NA}$$

**Hence proved.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Position and Nature of Image by a Concave Mirror (Object at C)

### Given
- A concave mirror of focal length $f$.
- Object placed at the **Centre of Curvature (C)**.

### To Prove
The image is **real, inverted, same size, and located at C**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the relation between R and f.**
For a spherical mirror, the radius of curvature $R$ is related to the focal length by:

$$R = 2f$$

So the position of C is at $u = -R = -2f$ (Cartesian sign convention — object on the left, so negative).

**Step 2 — Apply the mirror equation.**

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Substitute $u = -2f$:

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{-2f} = \frac{1}{f}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{2 + 1}{2f} = \frac{3}{2f}$$

$$v = \frac{2f}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Wait — correction.**
Re-examine the sign convention. With object placed *on the principal axis in front of the mirror*, the object distance is conventionally written as $u = -2f$ in the *New Cartesian* convention, **but** in the older convention used in many NDT textbooks:

$$u = +2f \text{ (positive)}, \quad v = +2f \text{ (positive)}, \quad f = +f \text{ (positive for concave)}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{2 - 1}{2f} = \frac{1}{2f}$$

$$\boxed{v = 2f}$$

**Step 4 — Determine magnification.**

$$m = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{2f}{2f} = -1$$

The **negative sign** indicates the image is **inverted**, and **|m| = 1** indicates the image is **the same size** as the object.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
When an object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror, the image is:
- Located **at the centre of curvature** (on the same side as the object — *real*).
- **Inverted**.
- **Same size** as the object.

> [!TIP]
> This property is exploited in NDT to project a magnified, real image onto a screen (e.g., optical comparator for crack measurement).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of All Five Aids

| Parameter | Mirror | Magnifier | Boroscope | Fibroscope | CCTV |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------|------------|------|
| **Primary physical principle** | Reflection | Refraction | Refraction (relay lenses) | Total Internal Reflection | Charge-coupled device |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid (but rotatable) | Rigid, hand-held | Rigid tube | Articulated tip (flexible) | Fully flexible cable |
| **Typical reach** | < 1 m | < 0.3 m | 0.1 – 1.5 m | 1 – 10 m | 5 – 100 m |
| **Typical diameter** | 5 – 150 mm | 25 – 75 mm | 1.6 – 25 mm | 4 – 16 mm | 6 – 60 mm |
| **Magnification** | 1× (virtual) to 5× | 2× – 20× | 4× – 50× | 4× – 50× | Up to 200× (digital zoom) |
| **Image recording** | Direct observation | Direct observation | Possible with adapter | Possible with adapter | **Native digital recording** |
| **Stereo measurement** | No | No | Limited | Limited | Yes (with stereo head) |
| **Best for** | Surface visual | Surface cracks | Straight bores | Curved paths | Hazardous zones |
| **Limitation** | Line of sight blocked | Limited depth of field | Cannot bend | Fiber mosaic artifacts | Cable length loss |
| **Cost (typical)** | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very high |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem
A simple magnifier has a focal length of **5 cm**.
**(a)** Find its magnifying power when the image is formed at the near point (D = 25 cm).
**(b)** Find the magnifying power when the image is formed at infinity.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Use the formula for image formed at the near point:

$$MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

Substitute $D = 25$ cm and $f = 5$ cm:

$$MP = 1 + \frac{25}{5} = 1 + 5$$

$$\boxed{MP = 6\times}$$

**Part (b):** Use the formula for image formed at infinity (relaxed eye):

$$MP = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{25}{5}$$

$$\boxed{MP = 5\times}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** With a 5 cm focal length magnifier, an inspector can see a **6× larger view** at the comfortable near point, or a **5× larger view** with a relaxed eye. Choosing the relaxed-eye mode is preferred for prolonged inspection tasks to reduce eye fatigue.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Discussion — Why CCTV Cannot Replace a Fibroscope Entirely

CCTV and fibroscopes both enable **remote visual inspection**, but they are **complementary, not substitutable**:

1. **CCTV cameras cannot operate inside very narrow bores** (< 6 mm diameter) because the camera chip itself is physically too large. A 1 mm coherent fiber bundle can fit where a camera cannot.
2. **Fibroscopes do not require electrical power** at the tip — only light. This is critical in **explosive atmospheres** (e.g., petrochemical plant inspection) where any spark from a CCTV camera head could trigger an explosion.
3. **CCTV provides higher resolution** and supports digital zoom, image processing, and stereo photogrammetry for crack-depth measurement.
> [!NOTE]
> **KTU takeaway:** Use **fibroscopes in narrow, curved, or explosive atmospheres**; use **CCTV in wide, straight, hazardous (radiological/thermal) environments**.

---
