# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Visual Testing. List any four optical aids used in visual inspection of engineering components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Visual Testing (VT) is a method of Non-Destructive Testing in which an inspector visually examines the surface of a component, either directly or with the aid of optical instruments, to detect surface-level discontinuities such as cracks, corrosion, wear, and inclusions, in accordance with **ASTM E1316** and **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** standards.

**Four Optical Aids (½ Mark each, total 2 Marks):**
1. **Mirrors** — flat, concave, or convex — to redirect the line of sight.
2. **Magnifiers** — single-lens or compound — to enlarge surface features.
3. **Boroscopes** — rigid optical tube — to inspect straight internal cavities.
4. **Fibroscopes** — flexible fiber-optic bundle — to inspect curved internal cavities.
5. **Closed Circuit Television (CCTV)** — remote camera + monitor — for hazardous zones.

*(Any four accepted.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the Law of Reflection. Mention the type of mirror used in NDT to obtain a wide field of view inside a cavity, with reason.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Law of Reflection (2 Marks):**
The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection:
$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$
The incident ray, the reflected ray, and the normal to the reflecting surface at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.

**Mirror Type and Reason (1 Mark):**
A **convex mirror** is used to obtain a **wide field of view**. Reason: a convex mirror always produces a **virtual, erect, and diminished image**, which means a much larger area of the cavity wall is visible in the mirror than its actual size. This is the same principle used in automobile rear-view mirrors and security mirrors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**A.** **(a)** Derive the magnifying power of a simple magnifier when the final image is formed at the **least distance of distinct vision (D = 25 cm)**. **(7 Marks)**

**A.** **(b)** A converging lens of focal length **4 cm** is used as a simple magnifier. Calculate:
- (i) The magnifying power when the image is at the near point.
- (ii) The position of the object in each case.
- (iii) The magnifying power when the image is at infinity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Step 1 — Setup (1 Mark):**
A simple magnifier is a single converging lens. The object is placed **within the focal length**, so a **virtual, erect, magnified image** is formed at the near point $D = 25$ cm.

**Step 2 — Definition (1 Mark):**
$$MP = \frac{\text{Angle subtended by image at eye}}{\text{Angle subtended by object placed at near point D}}$$

**Step 3 — Angle for object at D (1 Mark):**
$$\theta_{obj} = \frac{h_o}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Lens equation (2 Marks):**
Using $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$ with $v = -D$:
$$\frac{1}{-D} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{1}{u} = -\left(\frac{1}{D} + \frac{1}{f}\right)$$

$$h_i = \frac{v}{u} h_o = \frac{D+f}{f} h_o$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio (2 Marks):**
$$\theta_{img} = \frac{h_i}{D} = \frac{(D+f)h_o}{fD}$$

$$MP = \frac{\theta_{img}}{\theta_{obj}} = \frac{(D+f)h_o / fD}{h_o / D} = \frac{D+f}{f} = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

$$\boxed{MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**Given:** $f = +4$ cm, $D = 25$ cm.

**(i) Magnifying power with image at D (1 Mark):**
$$MP = 1 + \frac{D}{f} = 1 + \frac{25}{4} = 1 + 6.25$$
$$\boxed{MP = 7.25\times}$$

**(ii) Object position with image at D (2 Marks):**
From the derivation, $u = -\dfrac{Df}{D+f}$:

$$u = -\frac{25 \times 4}{25 + 4} = -\frac{100}{29}$$

$$\boxed{u \approx -3.45 \text{ cm (i.e., 3.45 cm in front of the lens)}}$$

**(iii) Magnifying power with image at infinity (2 Marks):**
$$MP = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{25}{4}$$

$$\boxed{MP = 6.25\times}$$

**Conclusion (2 Marks):**
The 4 cm focal length magnifier provides a magnification of **7.25×** when held close to the eye with the image at the near point, or **6.25×** with a relaxed eye focused at infinity. The latter is preferred for prolonged inspections to minimize eye strain.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**B.** **(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **boroscope** with a neat functional block diagram. Compare it with a **fibroscope**. **(7 Marks)**

**B.** **(b)** What is **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber? Derive the expression $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. A fiber has core index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding index $n_2 = 1.45$. Find its NA and acceptance angle. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Construction of Boroscope (3 Marks):**
A boroscope consists of the following parts arranged inside a rigid stainless-steel tube:

| Part | Function |
|------|----------|
| Eyepiece | Magnifies the relayed image for the inspector's eye |
| Relay lens train | Series of precisely spaced converging lenses that transfer the image down the rigid tube (typically 4–6 elements) |
| Objective lens | Forms the first real image at the distal end |
| Tip prism / mirror | Provides forward (0°) or side (90°) viewing |
| Illumination channel | Carries light from an external source to the tip |

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
Light from an external source is transmitted through the illumination channel and illuminates the interior surface. The objective lens forms a real image, which is then transferred by successive relay lens pairs down the tube to the eyepiece, where the inspector views a magnified image. Light follows the principle of **refraction through converging lenses**.

**Comparison Boroscope vs. Fibroscope (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Boroscope | Fibroscope |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Image transmission | Series of rigid relay lenses | Coherent bundle of optical fibers |
| Flexibility | Rigid (cannot bend) | Flexible, articulating tip |
| Path | Straight bores only | Curved paths |
| Length limitation | Practical limit ~1.5 m | Up to 10 m |
| Image quality | Better, no pixelation | Suffers from fiber mosaic pattern |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**Definition of Numerical Aperture (1 Mark):**
The numerical aperture of an optical fiber is the **sine of the acceptance angle**, representing the light-gathering ability of the fiber.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply Snell's Law at the air-core interface (with $n_0 = 1$):
$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2:** Geometry of the core-cladding boundary:
$$\phi = 90° - \theta_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \cos\theta_1$$

**Step 3:** Critical-angle condition for total internal reflection:
$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

For the limiting case $\phi = \theta_c$:
$$\cos\theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4:** Pythagorean identity:
$$\sin\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_1} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:
$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \times \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Numerical Computation (2 Marks):**
Given $n_1 = 1.50$ and $n_2 = 1.45$:

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}$$

$$\boxed{NA \approx 0.384}$$

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.384) \approx 22.6°$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU board exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention confusion in mirror equation.** Always **state the convention** (Cartesian or Real-is-Positive) at the start of the solution. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for correct convention declaration.
> 2. **Using $D = 25$ cm without stating units.** Always write "D = 25 cm" — partial marks are lost for unit omission.
> 3. **Confusing MP formulas.** Memorize two cases separately: image at D vs. image at infinity. Students often write $MP = 1 + D/f$ for the infinity case and lose the "1".
> 4. **Forgetting to draw diagrams.** Even a rough sketch in magnifier or mirror problems earns 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up coherent and incoherent bundles.** The illumination bundle is *incoherent*; the image bundle is *coherent*. Reversing these loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing "CCTV uses optical fibers."** CCTV uses **electrical cables**; this is a *fibroscope*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 7. **In numerical problems, no final box.** Always box your final answer. Un-boxed final answers lose 0.5–1 mark even when correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Visual Testing (VT)** is the primary NDT method; uses human eye + optical aids.
- [ ] **Law of Reflection:** $\theta_i = \theta_r$, all in the same plane.
- [ ] **Mirror Equation:** $\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$.
- [ ] **Convex mirrors** → virtual, erect, diminished → used for **wide field of view** inspection.
- [ ] **Concave mirrors** → versatile image types based on object position; **at C → same size, real, inverted**.
- [ ] **Simple magnifier MP** (image at D): $MP = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f}$.
- [ ] **Simple magnifier MP** (image at infinity): $MP = \dfrac{D}{f}$.
- [ ] **Standard near point:** $D = 25$ cm (memorize this number).
- [ ] **Boroscope = rigid + lenses + straight bores**.
- [ ] **Fibroscope = flexible + fiber optics + curved paths**.
- [ ] **Coherent fiber bundle** transmits the image; **incoherent bundle** transmits illumination.
- [ ] **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$; represents light-gathering ability.
- [ ] **TIR (Total Internal Reflection)** occurs when light goes from higher-$n$ to lower-$n$ medium at angle $\geq \theta_c$.
- [ ] **CCTV** uses **electrical cables** (not fibers); ideal for **radioactive / high-temperature** zones.
- [ ] **CCTV > Fibroscope** for resolution and digital recording; **Fibroscope > CCTV** for narrow, curved, explosive atmospheres.

### Key Standards to Remember
- **ASTM E1316** — Standard Terminology for NDT (includes VT definitions).
- **ASNT CP-189 / SNT-TC-1A** — Personnel qualification and certification for VT.
- **ASTM E2692** — Standard for CCTV-based remote visual inspection.

### One-Line Memory Hooks
- *"Convex = Compressed view = Wider area"*
- *"Boroscope = Bore + scope = straight"*
- *"Fibroscope = Flexible + scope = curved"*
- *"CCTV = Closed Circuit = no broadcast = private cable"*
- *"D = 25 cm = the magic number of the eye"*

### Likely KTU Question Patterns (Predictive)
1. **Numerical on simple magnifier MP** — Very high probability.
2. **Derivation of NA or MP** — Almost every exam.
3. **Comparison table** (Boroscope vs. Fibroscope) — Frequently asked.
4. **Block diagram of any one aid** — Usually 3–5 marks.
5. **Application-based question** ("Which instrument would you use to inspect X?") — Increasingly common.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Sources and Special Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Light Sources and Special Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Light Sources in NDT — The Formal Definition

In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, *Light Sources and Special Lighting* refers to the controlled, calibrated, and standardized illumination systems used during **Visual Testing (VT)** — the oldest, most versatile, and economically significant NDT method recognized under **ASME Section V, Article 9** and **ASTM E2702**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A *light source* in NDT is any device capable of producing electromagnetic radiation in the visible (**380 nm – 780 nm**), ultraviolet (**UV-A: 315 nm – 400 nm**), or infrared (**780 nm – 1 mm**) spectrum, with sufficient intensity, color temperature, and uniformity to render surface and sub-surface discontinuities visible to a qualified inspector.

The two principal technical metrics used to qualify any lighting setup are:

- **Illuminance (E):** measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)** — the luminous flux incident per unit area on the inspection surface.
- **Luminance (L):** measured in **candela per square meter (cd/m²)** — the light emitted or reflected from a surface as perceived by the eye.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Anchor Topic:**
> As per the KTU 2024 OEMET724 syllabus, this topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO1** at Bloom's Level **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)**. Students must be able to *identify, classify, and justify* the selection of light sources for given VT/LPI scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Light Matters in NDT

Think of an NDT inspector's eye as a **photographer's camera sensor**. Just as a wildlife photographer carries different flashguns, filters, and UV torches to capture a nocturnal leopard versus a colorful butterfly, an NDT inspector switches between **white light, UV-A black light, and polarized illumination** to make invisible cracks "appear" on a metal weld.

A more physical analogy: imagine trying to read a faint pencil scribble on a white sheet of paper under a yellow streetlamp versus a cool, bright LED desk lamp. The **contrast ratio** (signal-to-noise) between the defect and the background is governed almost entirely by the **spectrum, intensity, and angle of the illumination** — not by the inspector's eyesight.

In Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI), UV-A light causes *fluorescent dye molecules* trapped inside a crack to emit visible green-yellow light (**peak emission ≈ 550 nm**), essentially making the crack "glow in the dark" against a dark background. Without the correct UV-A source, the entire inspection collapses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Fact (Memorize This):**
> $$1 \text{ foot-candle (fc)} = 10.76 \text{ lux (lx)}$$
> $$1 \text{ lux} = 1 \text{ lumen/m}^2$$

## 1.3 Classification of Light Sources Used in NDT

Light sources for VT/LPI fall into **four broad families**:

| Family | Operating Principle | Typical Use in NDT |
|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent / Tungsten-Halogen** | Resistive heating of tungsten filament (≈ 3000 K color temperature) | General VT, borescopes |
| **Fluorescent / Mercury-Vapor** | Gas-discharge with phosphor coating | High-bay shop floor VT |
| **High-Intensity Discharge (HID)** | Xenon or metal-halide arc | Large structures (tanks, ships) |
| **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** | Electroluminescence at p-n junction | Modern portable VT + UV-A lamps |
| **Gas-Discharge UV-A (Black Light)** | Mercury vapor with Woods glass filter | Fluorescent LPI / MPI |

## 1.4 Special Lighting — The NDT Sense of "Special"

The term **"special lighting"** in the OEMET724 syllabus refers specifically to lighting configurations beyond ordinary ambient white light. These include:

1. **UV-A (Ultraviolet) Black Light** — used in Fluorescent Penetrant Testing and Magnetic Particle Inspection.
2. **Polarized White Light** — eliminates glare from reflective surfaces during VT of polished welds.
3. **Fiber-Optic Illuminators** — deliver cool, shadow-free light into confined spaces (turbine blades, heat exchanger tubes).
4. **Directional / Oblique Lighting** — low-angle illumination that exaggerates surface topography and reveals subtle cracks.
5. **Stroboscopic Lighting** — freezes motion for inspection of rotating components.
6. **Infrared (IR) Thermography Illumination** — passive or active heating + IR camera for subsurface defect mapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Special" in Special Lighting** always implies **wavelength selection, intensity calibration, or geometric control** beyond what general room lighting provides.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Illumination in Visual Testing

### 2.1.1 Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

For a point light source radiating uniformly in all directions, the illuminance on a surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the source and the surface. This is the single most important equation governing lamp placement in NDT.

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

where:
- $E$ = illuminance at the inspection surface (**lux**)
- $I$ = luminous intensity of the source (**candela, cd**)
- $d$ = distance between source and surface (**meters**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Doubling the distance from the lamp to the workpiece reduces the illuminance to **one-fourth** of its original value. This is why handheld UV-A lamps for LPI must be held within **≤ 400 mm (16 inches)** per **ASTM E2297** and **ASTM E1417** protocols.

### 2.1.2 Lambert's Cosine Law

When light strikes a surface at an angle $\theta$ from the surface normal, the effective illuminance is reduced by the cosine of that angle.

$$E_{\theta} = E_{0} \cos\theta$$

where:
- $E_{\theta}$ = illuminance at angle $\theta$
- $E_{0}$ = illuminance at normal incidence ($\theta = 0$)
- $\theta$ = angle between incident light ray and surface normal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical NDT Rule:** Always orient the light source as close to **normal incidence ($\theta \approx 0°$)** as possible when measuring specular highlights, and use **oblique angles ($\theta \approx 15°-30°$)** when searching for surface-breaking cracks because oblique light casts micro-shadows inside the discontinuity.

### 2.1.3 Contrast Ratio (C)

The most critical performance parameter in VT is the *contrast ratio* between a defect and its surrounding background.

$$C = \frac{\vert L_{\max} - L_{\min} \vert}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$$

where $L_{\max}$ and $L_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum luminance values across the region of interest.

A contrast ratio above **0.5** is generally considered adequate for VT; below **0.2**, the human eye cannot reliably distinguish the defect.

### 2.1.4 UV-A Irradiance vs. Illuminance

For fluorescent LPI, the relevant quantity is **irradiance** (W/m²) in the UV-A band, not visible lux.

$$E_{UV} = \frac{\Phi_{UV}}{A}$$

where:
- $E_{UV}$ = UV-A irradiance (**W/m²** or **µW/cm²**)
- $\Phi_{UV}$ = radiant flux in UV-A band (**W**)
- $A$ = illuminated area (**m²**)

The **ASTM E1417** standard mandates a minimum UV-A irradiance of:

$$\boxed{E_{UV} \geq 1000 \text{ µW/cm}^2 \text{ (3 W/m}^2\text{)} \text{ at the inspection surface}}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Standard / Source | Typical Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | Illuminance from point source | Inverse Square Law | Depends on $d$ |
| 2 | $E_{\theta} = E_{0}\cos\theta$ | Oblique illumination | Lambert's Law | $\cos 30° = 0.866$ |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{\vert L_{\max} - L_{\min} \vert}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$ | Contrast ratio | CIE / ASTM E169 | $\geq 0.5$ desired |
| 4 | $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$ | Unit conversion | SI ↔ Imperial | Memorize |
| 5 | $E_{UV} \geq 1000 \,\mu W/cm^2$ | UV-A minimum | ASTM E1417 | For LPI |
| 6 | $\lambda_{\text{vis}} = 380{-}780 \text{ nm}$ | Visible spectrum | ISO 20473 | Range |
| 7 | $\lambda_{UV-A} = 315{-}400 \text{ nm}$ | UV-A band | CIE S 007 | Range |
| 8 | $\Phi_{v} = K_{m} \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda}\, d\lambda$ | Luminous flux | Photometric definition | $K_{m} = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$K_m$ = Maximum Luminous Efficacy** = **683 lm/W** at $\lambda = 555$ nm (green). This is a photopic vision constant you must memorize for any photometric numerical.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Boroscope inspection of turbine blades using **fiber-optic cold light sources** to avoid thermal damage to nickel-superalloy components.
- **Nuclear:** Underwater fluorescent LPI of fuel rod end-caps using **remote UV-A LED pods** with **submersible fiber-optic light guides**.
- **Welding:** Pre-service and in-service VT of welds per **AWS D1.1** with calibrated **white-light LED floodlights** providing minimum **1000 lx** at the weld toe.
- **Pipeline:** In-line inspection (ILI) crawlers carry **strobe LEDs** synchronized with high-speed cameras to capture internal pipe wall images at speeds up to **5 m/s**.
- **Automotive:** Penetrant inspection of forged crankshafts using **mercury-vapor UV-A lamps** housed in automated rotary inspection cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverse Square Law from Solid Angle Geometry

Consider a point isotropic source of luminous intensity $I$ (candela). It emits a total luminous flux:

$$\Phi = 4\pi I \text{ (lumens)}$$

This flux passes uniformly through any closed sphere centered on the source. At radius $d$, the sphere's surface area is:

$$A = 4\pi d^2$$

Illuminance $E$ is defined as flux per unit area incident on the surface:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\Phi}{A} \\
&= \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2} \\
&= \frac{I}{d^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic Step-by-Step Explanation:**
- *Step 1:* The total flux emitted by an isotropic source is $\Phi = 4\pi I$ because a sphere subtends $4\pi$ steradians.
- *Step 2:* This same total flux must cross every concentric spherical shell around the source (energy conservation).
- *Step 3:* Divide flux by shell area $4\pi d^{2}$ to get illuminance at distance $d$.
- *Step 4:* The $4\pi$ cancels, yielding the compact form $E = I/d^{2}$.

### 3.1.1 Numerical Worked Example

> A halogen inspection lamp emits $I = 1500$ cd uniformly. Calculate the illuminance on a steel plate located $d = 0.5$ m from the lamp.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{I}{d^2} \\
&= \frac{1500}{(0.5)^{2}} \\
&= \frac{1500}{0.25} \\
&= 6000 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison with standard:** AWS D1.1 requires minimum **1080 lx** (≈ 100 fc) for VT of welds. Therefore, **6000 lx at 0.5 m** is more than adequate — the inspector can even step back to **d ≈ 1.18 m** and still meet the standard.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: UV-A Distance Limit Using Inverse Square Law

ASTM E1417 mandates $E_{UV} \geq 1000\,\mu W/cm^{2}$. A typical UV-A LED lamp rated $I_{UV} = 160{,}000 \,\mu W/sr$ at the aperture is used. Find the maximum working distance.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{UV} &= \frac{I_{UV}}{d^{2}} \\
d_{\max} &= \sqrt{\frac{I_{UV}}{E_{UV,\min}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{160{,}000}{1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{160} \\
&\approx 12.65 \text{ inches} \\
&\approx 321 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- *Step 1:* Rearrange the inverse square law to solve for $d$.
- *Step 2:* Substitute the minimum required UV-A irradiance.
- *Step 3:* Take the square root and convert inches → mm using $1\text{ in} = 25.4\text{ mm}$.

This derivation is exactly why LPI procedure sheets specify a **maximum standoff of 400 mm (16 in)** between UV-A lamp face and inspection surface.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lambert's Cosine Law from Projected Area

A surface element $dA$ oriented at angle $\theta$ to incoming parallel rays intercepts only the projected area $dA \cos\theta$. Therefore, the flux intercepted is:

$$d\Phi = E_{0} \, dA \cos\theta$$

The illuminance on the tilted surface is:

$$E_{\theta} = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = E_{0}\cos\theta$$

### 3.3.1 Numerical Application

> A inspector uses oblique lighting at $\theta = 60°$ from normal. If normal illuminance is 2000 lx, find $E_{\theta}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\theta} &= 2000 \times \cos(60°) \\
&= 2000 \times 0.5 \\
&= 1000 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering note:** This is why inspectors *deliberately* accept reduced illuminance when searching for tight fatigue cracks — the *direction* of the light matters more than raw intensity because oblique illumination creates shadows inside the crack mouth.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Light Source

| Inspection Scenario | Recommended Source | Wavelength | Min. Intensity | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| General shop floor VT (welds) | White LED flood | 380–780 nm | 1080 lx | AWS D1.1 |
| Fluorescent LPI | UV-A Hg-vapor or LED | 365 nm peak | 1000 µW/cm² | ASTM E1417 |
| Fluorescent MPI | UV-A Hg-vapor | 365 nm peak | 1000 µW/cm² | ASTM E1444 |
| Borescope internal VT | Fiber-optic halogen | 380–780 nm | Per OEM | ASTM E2699 |
| Polished weld cap VT | Polarized white | 380–780 nm | Variable | ASME V Art. 9 |
| Heat exchanger tube VT | Cool LED borescope | 4500–6500 K | 500 lx min | ASME V Art. 9 |
| Rotating shaft (in-motion) | Stroboscopic LED | Visible | — | — |
| Subsurface defect hunt | IR thermography | 8–14 µm | — | ASTM E1934 |
| Underwater NDT | Submersible UV-A LED | 365 nm | 1000 µW/cm² | ASTM E1417 (Annex) |
| Mirror-finish weld (cosmetic) | Diffuse ring light | 5500 K | 2000 lx | ISO 17637 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Spectral Classification of NDT Light Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NDT Light Sources] --> B[Visible 380 to 780 nm]
    A --> C[Ultraviolet UV A 315 to 400 nm]
    A --> D[Infrared 780 nm to 1 mm]

    B --> B1[Incandescent Tungsten Halogen]
    B --> B2[Fluorescent Tube]
    B --> B3[LED White Light]
    B --> B4[Xenon Arc HID]

    C --> C1[Mercury Vapor Black Light]
    C --> C2[UV A LED Pod]
    C --> C3[Woods Glass Filtered Lamp]

    D --> D1[Thermal IR Camera Passive]
    D --> D2[Active IR with Heat Lamp]
    D --> D3[NIR Viewer for Translucent Comp]

    B3 --> E1[General VT and Weld Inspection]
    B1 --> E2[Borescopes and Hand Torches]
    C1 --> E3[Fluorescent LPI and MPI]
    C2 --> E4[Modern Portable LPI Kits]
    D1 --> E5[Subsurface Defect Mapping]

    classDef sourceBox fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef visBox fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef uvBox fill:#6d28d9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef irBox fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef appBox fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A sourceBox
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 visBox
    class C,C1,C2,C3 uvBox
    class D,D1,D2,D3 irBox
    class E1,E2,E3,E4,E5 appBox
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an LPI Inspection Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Power Supply Mains or Battery] --> P2[Stabilizer and Filter]
    P2 --> P3[UV A Lamp Head 365 nm]
    P3 --> P4[Woods Glass UV Pass Filter]
    P4 --> P5[Workpiece Surface]
    P5 --> P6[Fluorescent Dye Emission 550 nm]
    P6 --> P7[Inspector Eye or Camera Sensor]
    P7 --> P8[Decision Crack or No Crack]
    P8 --> P9[Reject or Accept Part]

    M1[Lux Meter] -.measures visible.-> P5
    M2[UV Irradiance Meter] -.measures UV.-> P4

    classDef power fill:#facc15,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef optic fill:#06b6d4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef meas fill:#a855f7,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class P1,P2 power
    class P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8,P9 optic
    class M1,M2 meas
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing Light Source

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Defect type?} -->|Surface crack| Q2{Reflective surface?}
    Q1 -->|Subsurface| Q3[Use IR Thermography]
    Q1 -->|Through porosity| Q4[Use UV A LPI]

    Q2 -->|Yes polished| Q5[Polarized or Diffuse Ring]
    Q2 -->|No matte| Q6[White LED Flood 1080 lx]

    Q4 --> Q7{Lamp distance?}
    Q7 -->|Under 400 mm| Q8[Handheld UV A LED OK]
    Q7 -->|Over 400 mm| Q9[Use High Power Hg Vapor]

    classDef decide fill:#fde047,stroke:#000
    classDef process fill:#7dd3fc,stroke:#000
    classDef result fill:#86efac,stroke:#000

    class Q1,Q2,Q7 decide
    class Q3,Q4,Q5,Q6 process
    class Q8,Q9 result
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Photometric Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS[Light Source] -->|emits Phi| WS[Workpiece Surface]
    WS -->|reflects| EYE[Inspector Eye]
    EYE -->|retinal signal| BR[Brain Perception]
    LS -.reference.-> LM[Lux Meter]
    LM -->|readout| VAL[Calibrated lux]
    UV[UV A Lamp] -.reference.-> UVM[UV Irradiance Meter]
    UVM -->|readout| UVAL[mu W per cm2]

    classDef src fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef meas fill:#f97316,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef val fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class LS,UV,WS,EYE,BR src
    class LM,UVM meas
    class VAL,UVAL val
```

## 4.5 Schematic Layout — Handheld UV-A Lamp Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mercury Vapor Bulb] --> B[Primary Reflector Elliptical]
    B --> C[Heat Absorbing Filter]
    C --> D[Woods Glass UV Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Secondary Diffuser]
    E --> F[Inspection Window 365 nm Output]
    F --> G[Workpiece Crack]
    G -->|fluorescence 550 nm| H[Inspector Goggles Yellow Filter]
    H --> I[Inspector Eye]

    classDef bulb fill:#475569,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef optic fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fluo fill:#84cc16,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef eye fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A bulb
    class B,C,D,E,F optic
    class G,H fluo
    class I eye
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*List any three types of light sources used in Visual Testing and state one specific application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tungsten-Halogen Incandescent Lamp** — Produces continuous-spectrum white light (~3000 K); used in **fiber-optic borescopes** for internal VT of turbine blades where high color-rendering index (CRI) is needed. (1 mark)
2. **UV-A Mercury-Vapor Black Light Lamp** — Emits predominantly 365 nm UV-A after passing through a **Woods glass filter**; used in **Fluorescent Liquid Penetrant Inspection (FPI)** to excite penetrant dyes that fluoresce at 550 nm. (1 mark)
3. **High-Power White LED Array** — Solid-state source with 5500 K color temperature, long life, and low heat; used for **general shop floor VT of welds** per AWS D1.1 with minimum 1080 lx at the workpiece. (1 mark)

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*State the minimum UV-A irradiance required at the inspection surface during fluorescent penetrant inspection as per ASTM E1417. Why is visible white light minimized during LPI?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Minimum UV-A irradiance = **1000 µW/cm² (≈ 3 W/m²)** measured at the inspection surface (1 mark).
- White light is minimized because it **masks the fluorescent emission** of the penetrant dye by raising the background luminance, thereby **reducing the contrast ratio** between defect and background (1 mark).
- A darkened inspection booth with ambient white light below **20 lux** is mandatory (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Inverse Square Law of Illumination** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship and state its significance in selecting the working distance of an NDT inspection lamp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **functional components of a UV-A fluorescent penetrant inspection lamp**. Explain the role of the **Woods glass filter** and the function of the **yellow goggles** worn by the inspector. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a):

**Statement of Law:** The illuminance on a surface is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** from a point source. (1 mark)

**Derivation:**
Consider a point isotropic source of intensity $I$ candela. Total emitted flux:

$$\Phi = 4\pi I$$

This flux crosses a sphere of radius $d$ whose surface area is $A = 4\pi d^{2}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\Phi}{A} = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^{2}} \\
\therefore E &= \frac{I}{d^{2}} \quad \text{(1 mark for algebra)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sketch:** A point source at origin, concentric spheres of radii $d_{1}$ and $d_{2} = 2d_{1}$, and rays indicating flux distribution. (1 mark)

**Significance in NDT (4 marks):**
- Determines the **maximum allowable working distance** for any inspection lamp.
- For UV-A LPI: ASTM E1417 demands $E_{UV} \geq 1000 \mu W/cm^{2}$. If the lamp provides $I_{UV} = 160{,}000 \mu W/sr$, then $d_{max} = \sqrt{160} \approx 12.6$ in (~321 mm), justifying the standard's prescribed **16 in (400 mm) maximum standoff**.
- For white-light VT: AWS D1.1 requires 1080 lx at the weld. A halogen lamp of $I = 2700$ cd allows a working distance up to $d_{max} = \sqrt{2700/1080} \approx 1.58$ m, giving inspectors freedom of movement.
- Doubling distance → illuminance drops to **25%** (1/4), which is why inspectors must not exceed prescribed lamp stand-offs.

#### Model Solution to B-A(b):

**Block Diagram:** (1 mark)

```
Power Supply → Stabilizer → Hg Vapor Bulb → Reflector → Heat Filter
→ Woods Glass Filter → Inspection Window → Workpiece → Inspector Eye
                                                    ↑
                                              Yellow Goggles
```

**Functional Explanation (3 marks):**
- The **mercury-vapor bulb** emits a broad spectrum peaking in UV at 365 nm along with visible light and infrared.
- The **heat-absorbing filter** removes IR to prevent workpiece heating.
- The **Woods glass filter** is a nickel-oxide-doped silicate glass that transmits only the **UV-A band (315–400 nm)** while blocking all visible light (transmission peak ≈ 365 nm).

**Role of Woods Glass (1.5 marks):**
- Ensures **pure UV-A output** so the dye fluorescence is excited without visible background contamination.
- Visible cutoff below **~400 nm** is critical — any leak of white light will destroy fluorescence contrast.

**Role of Yellow Goggles (1.5 marks):**
- The goggles incorporate a **high-pass yellow filter** that **blocks residual UV-A and blue light below ~520 nm** while transmitting the **fluorescent green-yellow emission (~550 nm)** of the penetrant.
- They **protect the inspector's retina** from prolonged UV-A exposure (which can cause photokeratitis) and **enhance defect visibility** by improving the signal-to-background contrast.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Define **illuminance**, **luminance**, and **contrast ratio**. Why is **contrast ratio** considered the most important parameter in VT rather than absolute illuminance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **fluorescent mercury-vapor** and **UV-A LED** light sources for LPI under the heads: spectral output, lifetime, warm-up time, heat generation, and suitability for field use. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a):

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Illuminance ($E$):** Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface; SI unit **lux (lx) = lm/m²**. Measures how much light *falls on* an object. (1 mark)
- **Luminance ($L$):** Luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction; SI unit **cd/m²** (also called "nit"). Measures how much light is *emitted or reflected* from a surface toward the eye. (1 mark)
- **Contrast Ratio ($C$):** Dimensionless ratio $C = \frac{|L_{max} - L_{min}|}{L_{max} + L_{min}}$ describing the relative brightness difference between a defect and its background. (1 mark)

**Why Contrast > Absolute Illuminance (4 marks):**
1. The human visual system responds to **relative brightness differences**, not absolute values (Weber-Fechner Law). (1 mark)
2. A defect may receive 5000 lx but if the background receives 4900 lx, the contrast $C \approx 0.01$ is unresolvable. (1 mark)
3. Conversely, a faint defect at 100 lx on a 5 lx background has $C \approx 0.90$, easily visible. (1 mark)
4. Therefore, lighting design in NDT optimizes the **direction, spectrum, and uniformity** of illumination to maximize $C$, not just $E$. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to B-B(b):

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per cell, plus 1 mark for conclusion):**

| Parameter | Mercury-Vapor UV-A Lamp | UV-A LED Lamp |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectral Output** | Strong 365 nm line + visible + IR (requires Woods glass + heat filter) | Narrow band centered 365–370 nm; little IR or visible leakage |
| **Lifetime** | 2000–5000 hours | 20,000–50,000 hours |
| **Warm-up Time** | 3–5 minutes for full UV output | Instantaneous (< 1 second) |
| **Heat Generation** | High (IR component) — can burn skin/composite parts | Low (cool operation, ideal for composites) |
| **Robustness** | Fragile glass bulb; sensitive to vibration | Solid-state; highly shock-resistant |
| **Power Consumption** | 100–400 W | 15–50 W for equivalent irradiance |
| **Field Suitability** | Requires mains power, ballast; not portable | Battery-powered portable; ideal for field LPI |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** UV-A LED lamps are now preferred for field LPI due to instant-on, low heat, long life, and battery portability; mercury-vapor remains common in fixed production-line cells where high irradiance over large areas is needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Never confuse "illuminance" (lux) with "irradiance" (W/m²)** in UV-A numericals. They are NOT interchangeable. Lux includes the photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ and is weighted to visible light only.
> 2. **Always cite the standard number** (e.g., ASTM E1417, AWS D1.1) when stating minimum intensities — KTU examiners award credit for standard reference awareness.
> 3. **Inverse Square Law assumes a point source.** For line sources (fluorescent tubes), the law becomes inverse-distance, not inverse-square. Mentioning this distinction can earn a partial-mark bonus.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers (e.g., "321 mm" not just "321") is the single most common cause of ½–1 mark deductions in KTU valuation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Visual Testing (VT) is the most widely used NDT method** and accounts for > 80% of all in-service inspections. Lighting is its foundation.
- ⭐ **Four lighting families** for NDT: **Incandescent, Fluorescent, HID, LED**, plus **special UV-A** for LPI/MPI.
- ⭐ **Three "special" categories**: **UV-A, Polarized, Fiber-Optic** — all control *wavelength* or *geometry* beyond ambient.
- ⭐ **Inverse Square Law** $E = I/d^2$ governs lamp placement; doubles distance → quarter the illuminance.
- ⭐ **Lambert's Cosine Law** $E_\theta = E_0 \cos\theta$ governs oblique illumination; use $\theta \approx 15°$–$30°$ for surface cracks.
- ⭐ **ASTM E1417 mandates $E_{UV} \geq 1000 \,\mu W/cm^2$** at the inspection surface for fluorescent LPI.
- ⭐ **AWS D1.1 mandates $\geq 1080$ lx** (≈100 fc) at the weld toe for VT.
- ⭐ **Maximum UV-A lamp standoff = 400 mm (16 in)** per ASTM E2297 and E1417.
- ⭐ **Woods glass filter** transmits only **UV-A (315–400 nm)**; its role is critical to fluorescence visibility.
- ⭐ **Yellow inspector goggles** block residual UV-A + blue light, transmit dye fluorescence at **~550 nm**.
- ⭐ **Conversion to memorize:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$; $K_m = 683 \text{ lm/W}$ at 555 nm.
- ⭐ **Contrast ratio** is the *true* performance metric — not absolute lux.
- ⭐ **Color Temperature** of inspection lamps: ~5500 K (daylight white) is preferred to avoid yellow-cast bias.
- ⭐ **CRI (Color Rendering Index) ≥ 90** is recommended for accurate defect color identification in VT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Penetrant Testing: Properties Required for Good Penetrants and Developers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquid Penetrant Testing: Properties Required for Good Penetrants and Developers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT)?

**Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT)**, also known as **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** or **Penetrant Flaw Detection**, is a widely employed, low-cost, and highly sensitive Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method used to detect **surface-breaking discontinuities** (such as cracks, porosity, laps, cold shuts, and fatigue cracks) in non-porous materials (metals, ceramics, glass, and some polymers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMET724 – Module 1):**
> "Liquid Penetrant Testing is a surface NDT method based on the **capillary action** of a liquid penetrant entering clean, dry surface-breaking flaws, which is then drawn back out by a developer, forming a visible indication that is wider than the actual flaw, thereby making the discontinuity easily detectable by the human eye."

The method **CANNOT detect subsurface flaws** because the penetrant physically has to enter a discontinuity that is open to the surface. LPT is governed by standards such as **ASTM E1417**, **ASTM E165**, **ISO 3452**, and **ASME Section V Article 6**.

## 1.2 The Two Critical Consumables: Penetrant and Developer

The effectiveness of LPT depends entirely on **two engineered chemical consumables** working in tandem:

| Consumable | Functional Role | Key Physical Phenomenon Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Penetrant** | A low-surface-tension, low-viscosity dye-laden liquid that **enters** the flaw. | **Capillary Rise / Wetting** |
| **Developer** | An absorbent powder that **draws out** the trapped penetrant from the flaw, spreading it on the surface. | **Reverse Capillary Action / Bleed-out** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both are needed:** A penetrant alone, even if it fills the crack, would be **invisible** because the crack is too narrow (often <1 µm) for the eye to see. The developer **re-widens** the indication on the surface, making it visible at **5x to 10x** the original flaw width.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Stain Detective"

Imagine you spill a drop of **red ink** (penetrant) onto a piece of **white tissue paper** (the test piece) that has a **tiny invisible tear**. The ink naturally **wicks** into the tear by capillary action, but you cannot see the tear.

Now, you place a **dry white powder** (developer) on top. The powder acts like a **blotting sponge**, sucking the red ink back out of the tear. As the ink spreads through the powder, it forms a **bold red stain** on the white background, revealing the **invisible tear**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** "**Penetrant GOES IN, Developer PULLS IT OUT.**" This is the single most important sequence to remember for KTU valuation.

## 1.4 Why Properties Matter – Quantitative Performance Benchmark

The sensitivity of LPT is rated in terms of the **smallest detectable flaw width**, which in premium laboratory conditions can be as low as **0.5 µm to 1 µm**. Achieving this requires the penetrant and developer to have **specifically engineered physical and chemical properties**, the subject of this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Focus:** Questions on "**List the properties of a good penetrant/developer**" and "**Justify why low surface tension is essential**" are **direct, high-frequency 3-mark and 7-mark questions**. Memorize the property list with reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties Required for a GOOD PENETRANT

A good penetrant is a precisely engineered chemical formulation. The properties can be grouped into **Physical, Chemical, Optical, and Safety/Handling** categories.

### A. Physical Properties (Most Critical for Board Exams)

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Low Surface Tension (γ)** | Typically **20 – 45 mN/m** (dyne/cm) | Allows the liquid to **wet** and enter micron-sized cracks that the bulk liquid cannot otherwise penetrate. High surface tension causes the drop to "ball up" and bridge over the flaw entrance. |
| 2 | **Low Viscosity (η)** | Typically **1 – 10 cP** (centipoise) at operating temperature | Enables **fast flow** into narrow capillaries. High viscosity slows penetration and increases inspection cycle time. |
| 3 | **Good Wetting Ability (Low Contact Angle θ)** | Contact angle **θ < 30°** on the test surface | Governed by Young's equation. A small contact angle means the liquid spreads readily and is spontaneously drawn into surface crevices. |
| 4 | **High Capillary Penetration** | Maximize **h** in Washburn equation | The depth of penetration into a flaw is the direct performance metric of a penetrant. |
| 5 | **Capillary Activity** | Strong | Penetrant must be able to climb against gravity in micro-channels. |
| 6 | **Density (ρ)** | Moderate (~0.8 – 1.1 g/cm³) | Affects draining behavior and emulsion formation. |
| 7 | **Boiling Point / Flash Point** | High flash point (**> 60 °C** preferred) | Safety; reduces fire hazard in industrial environments. |
| 8 | **Volatility** | Low | Prevents the penetrant from drying out inside the flaw before developer application, which would cause **loss of indication**. |

### B. Chemical Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | **Chemical Inertness** | Must not react with the test material | Prevents corrosion, staining, or degradation of sensitive alloys (e.g., titanium, Inconel, austenitic stainless steel). |
| 10 | **Chemical Stability** | Stable under storage (≥ 1 year shelf life) | Maintains consistent performance; resists oxidation, UV degradation, thermal breakdown. |
| 11 | **Compatibility with Developer** | Must not be absorbed/dissolved by developer prematurely | Premature absorption causes **background staining**, hiding true indications. |
| 12 | **Solubility Profile** | Insoluble in water (for lipophilic systems) / emulsifiable (for post-emulsifiable systems) | Governs the removal process selection. |
| 13 | **Non-corrosive** | Must be neutral (pH ~6.5 – 7.5) | Prevents damage to part and inspection equipment. |

### C. Optical / Indication Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 14 | **High Color Contrast** (Visible dye) | **Red dye (e.g., Sudan IV)** against **white developer** | Provides a sharp visual indication. Contrast ratio should be high. |
| 15 | **Strong Fluorescence** (Fluorescent type) | Emission in **green-yellow (550 nm)** under **UV-A (365 nm)** excitation | Provides the **highest sensitivity** (10–100× more sensitive than visible dye) by detecting in a darkened booth. |
| 16 | **Brightness/Sharpness of Indication** | Sharp bleed-out boundaries | Aids in accurate flaw sizing. |

### D. Safety and Handling Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 17 | **Low Toxicity** | Non-carcinogenic, low VOC | Worker safety; regulatory compliance (OSHA, REACH). |
| 18 | **Non-flammable** | Preferably non-flammable | Plant safety in foundries, weld shops, refineries. |
| 19 | **Low Odour** | Pleasant or neutral | Operator comfort during long inspection cycles. |
| 20 | **Easy Removability** | Surface excess must be removed without removing penetrant from flaw | Defines the **LPT Process Type** (water-washable, post-emulsifiable, solvent-removable). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** When asked "Why low surface tension is essential?", always answer: *"Low surface tension allows the penetrant to **wet** the surface of the crack walls and **spontaneously enter** the discontinuity by capillary action. If surface tension is high, the penetrant will form droplets and bridge over the crack mouth, preventing entry."*

## 2.2 Properties Required for a GOOD DEVELOPER

The developer's job is the **opposite** of the penetrant – it must **withdraw** (bleed out) the trapped penetrant from the flaw and **spread** it as a visible indication. The governing phenomenon is **reverse capillary action through a porous medium**.

### A. Physical Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fine Particle Size** | Typically **1 – 10 µm** (dry powder); < 50 µm (suspensions) | Small particles create **enormous surface area** and many fine capillary channels, producing a strong blotting action. |
| 2 | **High Absorptive Capacity** | High | Determines the speed of bleed-out and intensity of indication. |
| 3 | **Uniform Coating Ability** | Forms a thin, even, opaque film (~0.05 mm) | Prevents masking of small indications; allows uniform background. |
| 4 | **Porosity** | High internal porosity | Maximizes capillary absorption of penetrant. |
| 5 | **Good Suspension Properties** (for wet developers) | Stable suspension for **≥ 30 minutes** without settling | Prevents non-uniform coverage in dip-tank or spray systems. |
| 6 | **Quick Drying** | Dries in **< 5 minutes** under ambient conditions | Speeds up inspection cycle. |
| 7 | **Low Density** (Powder developer) | Light, fluffy powder | Easier to apply, hangs in air longer for dust-cloud application. |
| 8 | **Adequate Adhesion to Part** | Sticks to part without blowing off | Prevents loss of developer layer. |

### B. Chemical Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | **Chemical Inertness** | Must NOT react with penetrant or part | Reaction could alter dye color, destroy fluorescence, or damage part. |
| 10 | **Compatibility with Penetrant** | Must not dissolve or chemically alter the penetrant dye | Ensures the dye color/fluorescence is preserved in the indication. |
| 11 | **Non-hygroscopic** | Low moisture absorption | Prevents clumping and inconsistent coating. |
| 12 | **Non-corrosive** | Neutral chemistry | Protects the test part. |

### C. Optical Properties

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | **High Contrast with Penetrant** | **White powder** for red/UV-fluorescent penetrants | Maximum visual distinction between indication and background. |
| 14 | **Opaque Background** | Blocks surface color/reflections | Allows faintest bleed-outs to be visible. |
| 15 | **Matte (Non-glossy) Finish** | Diffuse reflection | Reduces glare; makes fluorescent indications pop in UV light. |

### D. Safety and Handling

| # | Property | Required Condition | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16 | **Non-toxic** | Below OSHA permissible exposure limits | Worker safety (silicosis risk if crystalline silica is present). |
| 17 | **Non-flammable** | Inert powder | Plant safety. |
| 18 | **Non-carcinogenic** | Compliant with IARC, NTP | Critical for chronic exposure. |
| 19 | **Free of Harmful Contaminants** | Halogen-, sulfur-free for nuclear/aerospace use | Prevents stress-corrosion cracking in Ni-alloys and austenitic SS. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Application in LPT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)** | $$h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$ | $h$ = capillary rise height (m); $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m); $\theta$ = contact angle; $\rho$ = density (kg/m³); $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $r$ = capillary radius (m) | Predicts how high the penetrant will rise inside a flaw. We want **low $\theta$** and **low $\gamma$–$\rho$ ratio** for fast entry. |
| **Washburn Equation (Penetration Depth vs Time)** | $$h^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta \, t}{2 \eta}$$ | $h$ = penetration depth (m); $t$ = dwell time (s); $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | The **MASTER EQUATION** of LPT penetrant performance. $h \propto \sqrt{t}$, so deeper flaws need longer dwell time. |
| **Critical Flaw Width** | $$w_{c} = \dfrac{2 \eta \, v}{\gamma \cos\theta}$$ | $w_c$ = minimum wetted flaw width; $v$ = flow velocity | Sets the **theoretical sensitivity limit** of the penetrant. |
| **Young's Equation (Wetting)** | $$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$ | $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid-vapor; $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid; $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid-vapor surface energies (N/m) | Governs **wetting ability** of penetrant on test surface. |
| **Bleed-out Rate (Reverse Capillary)** | $$\dot{m} \propto \dfrac{r^{2} \Delta P}{\eta L}$$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate; $\Delta P$ = capillary pressure; $L$ = path length | Describes how fast a developer **draws out** the penetrant. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Rule:** Notice the formulas use `\vert` and `\cos`/`\theta` notation. **Never write** `|x|` directly in a markdown table cell – it will break table rendering. Use LaTeX `\vert` or `\cos` etc. inside `$...$` delimiters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades, engine discs, and airframe welds for fatigue cracks (uses **Type I Fluorescent** penetrant with highest sensitivity).
* **Nuclear Power:** Halogen- and sulfur-free penetrants are mandatory to prevent **stress-corrosion cracking** in Inconel and stainless-steel components.
* **Foundries & Forgings:** Detects laps, cold shuts, and forging cracks on raw and machined surfaces.
* **Weld Inspection:** Standard method for surface-breaking weld defects in pressure vessels, pipelines, and structural steel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise) — The Foundation of Penetrant Entry

This derivation proves **why low surface tension and low contact angle** are essential for a penetrant.

**Starting Setup:** Consider a vertical cylindrical capillary tube of radius $r$, dipped into a pool of liquid penetrant. The liquid rises to a height $h$ above the free surface.

**Step 1: Force Balance on the Liquid Column**

The liquid column of height $h$ is held in equilibrium by two opposing forces:
* **Upward Force:** Surface tension acting along the circumference of the meniscus.
* **Downward Force:** Weight of the liquid column (gravity).

**Step 2: Upward Force from Surface Tension**

Surface tension $\gamma$ acts tangentially along the meniscus at contact angle $\theta$ with the tube wall. The vertical component of this tension is $\gamma \cos\theta$. Since the contact line runs around the tube circumference $2\pi r$, the total upward force is:

$$F_{up} = 2 \pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Reasoning:** The surface tension "pulls" the liquid up the tube like a stretched elastic film; only the vertical component ($\cos\theta$ fraction) supports the column weight.

**Step 3: Downward Force from Gravity**

The mass of the liquid column in the capillary is $m = \rho \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot h$. The downward gravitational force is:

$$F_{down} = \rho \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Reasoning:** This is just weight = mass × gravity, where mass = density × volume of cylinder.

**Step 4: Equilibrium Condition**

At equilibrium, $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$2 \pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta = \rho \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 5: Algebraic Solution for h**

Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides:

$$2 \gamma \cos\theta = \rho \, r \, h \, g$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, r}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **canonical Jurin's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight for KTU:** A penetrant with **low contact angle θ** (cos θ → 1) and **moderately low surface tension** will rise **high** in fine flaws (small $r$). However, in LPT we don't want an excessively large $h$ in the wrong direction — we want rapid lateral **entry**, not vertical rise. This is why we balance low $\gamma$ with low $\eta$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Washburn Equation — Predicting Penetrant Penetration Time

The Washburn equation is the **most important formula** for LPT. It relates the depth of penetrant penetration into a horizontal flaw to the dwell time.

**Starting Setup:** A horizontal capillary of radius $r$ and length $L$, initially empty, with a penetrant reservoir at the entrance. Let $x(t)$ be the position of the liquid front at time $t$.

**Step 1: Driving Pressure from Capillarity**

The Laplace pressure that drives the liquid into the capillary is:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Reasoning:** This comes from the Young-Laplace equation across a curved meniscus of radius $r$.

**Step 2: Flow Resistance (Hagen-Poiseuille Law)**

For laminar flow (always true in micron-sized cracks, Reynolds number $\ll 1$), the volumetric flow rate through a tube of length $x$ is:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta x}$$

**Reasoning:** Hagen-Poiseuille law — pressure-driven viscous flow in a cylindrical tube.

**Step 3: Kinematic Relation**

The volumetric flow rate equals the rate of change of filled volume:

$$Q = \pi r^{2} \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Equate and Simplify**

$$\pi r^{2} \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}}{8 \eta x} \cdot \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

Cancel $\pi r^{2}$ on both sides:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta x}$$

**Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$x \, dx = \dfrac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $x = h$ at $t = T$:

$$\int_{0}^{h} x \, dx = \dfrac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \int_{0}^{T} dt$$

$$\dfrac{h^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta \, T}{4 \eta}$$

**Step 6: Final Washburn Equation**

$$h^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta \, T}{2 \eta}$$

Or equivalently:

$$h = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta \, T}{2 \eta}}$$

**Reasoning:** Penetration depth is proportional to the **square root of time** — this is why **longer dwell times give deeper penetration** but with **diminishing returns**. Doubling dwell time increases penetration by only ~41%.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Insight:** A typical LPT dwell time is **10–30 minutes**. For a flaw of width $w = 1\,\mu m$ and penetrant with $\gamma = 30$ mN/m, $\eta = 3$ cP, $\theta = 10°$, the Washburn equation predicts penetration in seconds. The dwell time of 10+ minutes is a **safety margin** to ensure saturation, especially for tighter cracks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Performance Trade-off

This matrix helps in understanding why a single "perfect" penetrant is impossible — there are inherent trade-offs (KTU examiners love asking trade-off questions).

| Desired Property | Improves | But Degrades |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower surface tension** | Faster flaw wetting, better entry into tight cracks | More prone to spreading over background → false indications |
| **Lower viscosity** | Faster penetration | Tendency to drain out of vertical flaws; shorter residence time |
| **Stronger dye/fluorescence** | More visible indication | Higher cost; possible higher toxicity |
| **Finer developer particles** | Higher bleed-out resolution; finer indication | Slower application; potential dusting hazard |
| **Higher developer absorptivity** | Brighter, faster bleed-out | Risk of **background staining** (false positives) |
| **Lower contact angle** | Better wetting | Adheres too easily, harder to wash off surface excess |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Answer Strategy:** When asked "What happens if a developer is too absorbent?", answer: *"Excessively absorptive developer causes **background staining** — penetrant from the **part surface** (not from the flaw) is also absorbed, producing a uniformly tinted background that can **mask genuine indications** and **create false indications**."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any six essential physical properties required for a good liquid penetrant."**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Low Surface Tension (γ):** Allows the penetrant to wet the flaw walls and enter narrow cracks by capillary action (typically 20–45 mN/m).
2. **Low Viscosity (η):** Ensures rapid flow into tight discontinuities (typically 1–10 cP).
3. **Low Contact Angle (θ < 30°):** Guarantees spontaneous wetting of the test surface and crack interior.
4. **High Capillary Activity:** Strong ability to climb into micro-channels against gravity.
5. **Good Chemical Stability:** Long shelf life without oxidation, evaporation, or degradation.
6. **Low Volatility:** Prevents premature drying of penetrant trapped inside the flaw before developer application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Valuation Loss: 1 mark):** Students often write *"high viscosity"* as a property by mistake. **A good penetrant has LOW viscosity.** Examiners deduct marks for this. State both the property AND a numerical/qualitative value to score full marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Explain the role of a developer in LPT. Why is a fine particle size essential for the developer powder?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **developer** is a porous, absorbent material applied over the cleaned, dried surface of a test piece after penetrant removal. Its role is to **draw out** the penetrant trapped inside surface-breaking flaws by **reverse capillary action** (bleed-out), spreading it as a **broadened, visible indication** on the surface.

**Why fine particle size is essential:**
* **Smaller particles → larger total surface area per unit mass** → more capillary channels → stronger blotting action.
* Fine particles form a **uniform, thin, opaque white film** that provides maximum contrast against the red/fluorescent penetrant.
* Fine particles produce a **slow, controlled bleed-out**, preventing the indication from spreading too quickly and losing sharpness.
* Coarse particles would create large pores, causing **fast but diffuse** bleed-out with poor flaw resolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Valuation Loss: 0.5 mark):** Many students forget to mention **contrast** as a reason for fine particles. Always state: *"Fine particles also enhance the white background contrast that makes the indication clearly visible."*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **physical and chemical properties** required for a good penetrant used in Liquid Penetrant Testing. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Physical Properties of a Good Penetrant:**

1. **Low Surface Tension:** Should be in the range of **20–45 mN/m**. This ensures the liquid can wet the surface of the flaw walls and enter micron-sized cracks spontaneously. High surface tension causes the penetrant to form spherical droplets and bridge over flaw openings.

2. **Low Viscosity:** Typically **1–10 cP** at the operating temperature. Low viscosity enables rapid flow into tight discontinuities. Governed by the Washburn equation: $h^2 = \dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta \, T}{2\eta}$ — penetration depth decreases as viscosity increases.

3. **Low Contact Angle:** Should be **< 30°** on the test surface. A small contact angle indicates good wetting ability, which is essential for spontaneous capillary entry.

4. **High Capillary Activity:** The penetrant must possess the ability to climb against gravity in narrow channels. The capillary rise height given by Jurin's law: $h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ should be maximized.

5. **Low Volatility:** Prevents the penetrant from evaporating before developer is applied, ensuring it remains liquid inside the flaw.

6. **High Flash Point:** Should be **> 60°C** to ensure fire safety in industrial environments.

7. **Moderate Density:** Allows controlled drainage and good coverage on the test surface.

**Chemical Properties of a Good Penetrant:**

1. **Chemical Stability:** Must be stable over a long shelf life (≥ 1 year) without oxidation, thermal breakdown, or dye degradation.

2. **Chemical Inertness:** Must not react with the test material (titanium, Inconel, stainless steel), the developer, or the cleaner. Reactions can damage the part or destroy the dye.

3. **Non-corrosive Nature:** Should have a neutral pH (~6.5–7.5) to prevent corrosion of the test piece.

4. **Non-toxic and Non-flammable:** Compliant with OSHA, REACH standards; required for worker safety.

5. **Solubility Profile:** Insoluble in water (lipophilic), but capable of forming stable emulsions when required (for post-emulsifiable processes).

6. **Compatibility with Developer:** Should not be prematurely absorbed by the developer or react with it, which would cause **background staining**.

**(b)** *With a neat sketch, describe the **working principle of Liquid Penetrant Testing**. Explain how the **Washburn equation** governs the depth of penetrant entry into a flaw. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** LPT relies on **capillary action** to draw a liquid penetrant into surface-breaking flaws, followed by **reverse capillary action** through a developer to draw it back out and form a visible indication.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-cleaning** of the part to remove oil, dirt, paint.
2. **Application of penetrant** by dipping, spraying, or brushing.
3. **Dwell time** (10–30 min) — penetrant penetrates flaws by capillary action.
4. **Removal of excess penetrant** from the surface.
5. **Application of developer** — absorbs penetrant from flaws, spreading it as a visible indication.
6. **Inspection** under white light (visible dye) or UV-A (fluorescent).
7. **Post-cleaning** and evaluation.

**Governance by Washburn Equation:**

The depth of penetration of the penetrant into a flaw of width $r$ in time $T$ is given by:

$$h^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta \, T}{2 \eta}$$

Where:
* $\gamma$ = surface tension of penetrant (N/m)
* $r$ = effective flaw radius (m)
* $\theta$ = contact angle
* $T$ = dwell time (s)
* $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

**Implications for LPT:**
* Penetration depth $h$ increases as the **square root of time** — so a 4× increase in dwell time only doubles the penetration depth.
* Penetration is **enhanced** by low viscosity, low contact angle, and appropriate surface tension.
* This equation is the **design basis** for selecting penetrant chemistry and dwell time for any given flaw geometry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** *Discuss the **properties required for a good developer** in Liquid Penetrant Testing. How does the developer function to produce a visible indication? **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Function of the Developer:**

The developer **draws out** the penetrant trapped inside surface-breaking discontinuities through **reverse capillary action** (also called bleed-out). The developer is a porous, finely divided powder (or suspension) that, when applied over the cleaned surface, acts as a **blotting medium**. It absorbs the penetrant from the flaw and spreads it on the surface, forming a **broadened, high-contrast indication** — typically 5 to 10 times wider than the actual flaw width — making it visible to the naked eye (or under UV-A for fluorescent penetrants).

**Properties Required for a Good Developer:**

**Physical Properties:**

1. **Fine Particle Size (1–10 µm):** Maximizes total surface area and number of capillary channels for strong, controlled bleed-out.
2. **High Absorptive Capacity:** High internal porosity for rapid penetrant absorption.
3. **Uniform Coating Ability:** Forms a thin, even, opaque film (~0.05 mm) without masking small indications.
4. **Good Suspension Properties:** Stable for ≥ 30 minutes in wet developer systems.
5. **Quick Drying:** Dries in < 5 minutes under ambient conditions.
6. **Adequate Adhesion:** Sticks to the part surface without being blown off.

**Chemical Properties:**

7. **Chemical Inertness:** Must not react with penetrant or part.
8. **Non-hygroscopic:** Resists moisture absorption that causes clumping.
9. **Halogen- and Sulfur-Free:** Mandatory for nuclear and aerospace alloys to prevent stress-corrosion cracking.

**Optical Properties:**

10. **High Contrast with Penetrant:** White powder against red or fluorescent penetrant.
11. **Opaque Background:** Blocks surface color and reflections.
12. **Matte Finish:** Diffuse reflection enhances fluorescent indications under UV-A.

**Safety Properties:**

13. **Non-toxic, Non-flammable, Non-carcinogenic:** Compliant with industrial hygiene standards.

**(b)** *Compare the **types of developers** (dry powder, water-suspendible, solvent-suspendible) and **types of penetrants** (Type I fluorescent and Type II visible dye) with their relative advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison of Developer Types:**

| Developer Type | Form | Application | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Form A – Dry Powder** | Fine powder dust | Dust cloud chamber, hand dusting | Most sensitive; excellent for high-sensitivity fluorescent LPT; simple chemistry | Dust hazard; requires dust cabinet; not suitable for large parts |
| **Form B – Water-Suspendible** | Powder in water slurry | Dip tank, spray | Good coverage of complex shapes; reusable in tank; economical | Requires controlled tank agitation; slower drying; possible corrosion on sensitive alloys |
| **Form C – Solvent-Suspendible (Aerosol)** | Powder in volatile solvent | Handheld aerosol spray | Portable; convenient for field inspection; fast drying | Flammable propellant; more expensive; less uniform than Form A; ventilation needed |
| **Form D – Lipophilic Developer** | Powder in non-aqueous liquid | Special applications | Compatible with lipophilic emulsifiers | Limited use; not common |

**Comparison of Penetrant Types:**

| Penetrant Type | Dye Used | Inspection Mode | Sensitivity | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I – Fluorescent** | Fluorescent dye (e.g., aromatic hydrocarbons) | UV-A (365 nm) in dark booth | **Highest** (10–100× more sensitive than Type II) | Detects sub-micron flaws; most used in aerospace/nuclear | Requires darkened booth + UV lamp; higher cost; eye hazard from UV |
| **Type II – Visible Dye** | Red dye (e.g., Sudan IV) | White light (≥ 1000 lux) | Moderate | No dark booth needed; lower cost; suitable for field work | Lower sensitivity; requires good color vision; less effective on dark surfaces |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> * **Confusing "Type I/II" with "Form A/B/C/D":** Type refers to **penetrant** (I = Fluorescent, II = Visible), while **Form** refers to **developer** (A, B, C, D). Mixing these is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> * **Sensitivity Hierarchy:** Always state in order: *Post-emulsifiable fluorescent > Water-washable fluorescent > Water-washable visible > Solvent-removable visible*. This is a high-yield 2-marker.
> * **Memory Aid:** "**Type I is bright (Fluorescent), Type II is red (Visible).**"

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must memorize before KTU exam):**

* **LPT detects only SURFACE-BREAKING flaws** — cannot detect sub-surface defects.
* **Master sequence:** Clean → Apply Penetrant → Dwell → Remove Excess → Apply Developer → Inspect → Clean.
* **Penetrant properties — 6 most important:** Low surface tension, low viscosity, low contact angle, high capillary activity, chemical stability, low volatility.
* **Developer properties — 6 most important:** Fine particle size, high absorptivity, high contrast, chemical inertness, non-toxicity, uniform coating.
* **Washburn equation:** $h^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta \, T}{2 \eta}$ — penetration depth is **∝ √T** (square root of dwell time).
* **Jurin's Law:** $h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — capillary rise in vertical flaws.
* **Penetrant Types:** Type I (Fluorescent, UV-A, high sensitivity) vs Type II (Visible Red, white light, lower sensitivity).
* **Developer Forms:** A (Dry powder), B (Water-suspendible), C (Solvent-suspendible aerosol), D (Lipophilic).
* **Removal Methods:** Water-washable, Post-emulsifiable (lipophilic/hydrophilic), Solvent-removable.
* **Indication size** is **5–10×** the actual flaw width due to bleed-out spreading.
* **Standards:** ASTM E1417, ASTM E165, ISO 3452, ASME Sec V Art 6.
* **Trade-off caution:** Excessively absorptive developer → background staining; excess low surface tension → false indications.
* **Forbidden in nuclear/aerospace:** Halogenated and sulfur-containing penetrants (cause stress-corrosion cracking).
* **Dwell time:** Penetrant 10–30 min; Developer 10–60 min.
* **Drying:** Between cleaning and developer application is **mandatory** — residual moisture blocks penetrant bleed-out.
* **Sensitivity Hierarchy (decreasing):** Post-emulsifiable fluorescent > Water-washable fluorescent > Water-washable visible > Solvent-removable visible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
