### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a) Explain the different types of false (non-relevant) indications encountered in Liquid Penetrant Inspection. Discuss how each can be identified and prevented. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
False indications are marks produced by the LPI process that do not represent actual defects. They arise from process errors, material conditions, or part geometry.

**Classification with Identification and Prevention (6 marks):**

1. **Residual Penetrant Bleed-out**
   - *Cause:* Inadequate emulsification or rinsing.
   - *Identification:* Diffuse, smudged, disappears on re-cleaning.
   - *Prevention:* Standardize emulsifier dwell time (1–3 min); use water spray pressure 30–40 psi; verify cleanliness with UV lamp.

2. **Surface Contamination**
   - *Cause:* Oil, grease, paint, marker ink.
   - *Identification:* Random pattern, removable by solvent wipe.
   - *Prevention:* Pre-clean per ASTM E165; degrease using approved solvent or alkaline cleaner.

3. **Rough Surface / Machining Marks**
   - *Cause:* Surface roughness Ra > 3.2 μm traps penetrant.
   - *Identification:* Pattern follows machining direction; parallel grooves.
   - *Prevention:* Grind or polish; control machining parameters.

4. **Tool Marks and Scratches**
   - *Cause:* Mechanical scoring that resembles cracks.
   - *Identification:* Straight, uniform, located in low-stress regions; reproducible.
   - *Prevention:* Compare with calibrated test block; verify under 10× magnification.

5. **Over-applied Developer**
   - *Cause:* Thick, uneven developer coating.
   - *Identification:* Diffuse, powdery smudges.
   - *Prevention:* Use proper spray technique; hold can 200–300 mm away; apply thin, uniform coat.

6. **Weld Spatter and Cold Shut**
   - *Cause:* Surface irregularities trap penetrant.
   - *Identification:* Located on welds, geometric.
   - *Prevention:* Grind smooth; pre-clean.

**Conclusion (Optional 0 mark, but adds impression):**
A systematic re-cleaning and re-inspection cycle is the gold-standard method to confirm whether an indication is relevant or non-relevant.

---

**Q: (b) Discuss the safety precautions to be followed during Liquid Penetrant Inspection. Include chemical, UV, and fire hazards in your answer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Chemical Hazards (2 marks):**
- Penetrants, emulsifiers, and developers contain **petroleum distillates, surfactants, and aromatic hydrocarbons** that are skin/eye irritants.
- **Precautions:** Wear **nitrile gloves**, **safety goggles**, and **lab apron**; use chemicals only in well-ventilated areas; refer to **MSDS** for each product; wash hands after handling.

**2. UV Radiation Hazards (2 marks):**
- Fluorescent LPI uses **UV-A at 365 nm** which can cause *photokeratitis* and *cataracts* on prolonged exposure.
- **Precautions:** Wear **UV-blocking goggles (OD ≥ 5 at 365 nm)**; use **face shield**; never look directly into the lamp; install **lamp shields** and **automatic timers**; ensure irradiance ≤ 5000 μW/cm² at surface.

**3. Fire and Explosion Hazards (2 marks):**
- Many penetrants and cleaners are **flammable** (flash point as low as 38 °C for some).
- **Precautions:** Store in **flammable cabinets**; ground all containers; keep away from ignition sources; use **Class B fire extinguishers**; provide explosion-proof electrical fittings in spray booths.

**4. Ventilation and Waste Disposal (1 mark):**
- Use **local exhaust ventilation** (fume hood, downdraft table) to maintain vapor concentration below TLV.
- Dispose of used penetrant, developer, and rinsate as **hazardous chemical waste** per local regulation; do not pour down drain.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q: (a) With the help of a flowchart, explain the various stages of LPI where false indications can be introduced. Discuss at least five distinct causes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Flowchart (4 marks):**

```
[Pre-cleaning] → [Penetrant Application] → [Dwell Time]
        ↓                ↓                      ↓
   Oil residue      Over-spray          Insufficient dwell
        ↓                ↓                      ↓
[Emulsification] → [Rinsing] → [Drying] → [Developer] → [Inspection]
        ↓                ↓          ↓            ↓             ↓
   Over-emulsify  Residual     Over-temp   Excess        Misread
   Under-emulsify penetrant    (>70°C)     developer     specular
                                                            reflection
```

**Five Distinct Causes (3 marks):**

1. **Inadequate pre-cleaning** — oil/grease traps penetrant → smudges.
2. **Over-application of penetrant** — thick film; hard to remove → bleed-out in developer.
3. **Insufficient rinsing** — residual penetrant bleeds under developer → false linear.
4. **Excess developer** — smudges, hides real defects, creates false marks.
5. **High drying temperature (>70 °C)** — evaporates penetrant from real defects → false **negatives**.

---

**Q: (b) Prepare a detailed safety protocol checklist for an LPI operator working in an aerospace inspection shop. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Safety Item | Specification / Action |
|---|-------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **PPE - Hand Protection** | Nitrile gloves, change every shift; no latex (allergen). |
| 2 | **PPE - Eye Protection** | UV-blocking goggles (OD ≥ 5 at 365 nm) + side shields. |
| 3 | **PPE - Body Protection** | Lab coat / apron, closed-toe safety shoes. |
| 4 | **PPE - Respiratory** | Half-mask respirator with organic-vapor cartridge if TLV exceeded. |
| 5 | **Ventilation** | Downdraft table with face velocity ≥ 75 ft/min. |
| 6 | **MSDS Accessibility** | Hard copy at workstation; operator must sign acknowledgment. |
| 7 | **UV Lamp** | Verify irradiance ≥ 1000 μW/cm² weekly with calibrated radiometer. |
| 8 | **Chemical Storage** | Flammable cabinet; segregate oxidizers; ground all containers. |
| 9 | **Fire Safety** | Class B extinguisher within 3 m; no smoking sign. |
| 10 | **Waste Disposal** | Collect rinsate in labeled hazardous waste drum; never drain. |
| 11 | **Training** | Annual LPI Level I/II per NAS 410 / EN 4179. |
| 12 | **Emergency** | Eyewash station within 10 m; emergency shower; first-aid kit. |
| 13 | **Documentation** | Inspection log, lamp-hour log, chemical batch log. |

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):** PPE – 2 marks; Chemical/UV/Fire – 3 marks; Documentation/Training – 2 marks.

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.

- ✅ **False indication** = indication **not** caused by an actual defect.
- ✅ **Relevant indication** = indication **from** an actual defect; basis of acceptance.
- ✅ **Linear indication:** Length > **3 × Width** (ASTM E165) → rejectable.
- ✅ **Rounded indication:** Length ≤ 3 × Width → may be porosity; check limits.
- ✅ **Sensitivity levels (LPI):** L → M → H → UH (Low → Ultra-High).
- ✅ **UV-A wavelength:** **365 ± 5 nm**; minimum irradiance **1000 μW/cm²**.
- ✅ **OD of UV goggles:** **≥ 5** at 365 nm.
- ✅ **Drying temp:** **≤ 70 °C** to avoid false negatives.
- ✅ **Emulsifier dwell:** **1–3 minutes** (over-emulsifying = false negatives).
- ✅ **Developing time:** **10–60 minutes**; inspect before drying out.
- ✅ **Top 5 false indication sources:** Residual penetrant, contamination, over-developer, tool marks, weld spatter.
- ✅ **NIOSH Safety Hierarchy:** Elimination → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **Chemical hazards:** Skin/eye irritation, inhalation, flammability, aquatic toxicity.
- ✅ **UV hazard:** Photokeratitis, cataract; mitigated by OD 5 eyewear.
- ✅ **Standards to memorize:** ASTM E165, ASTM E1417, ISO 3452, IS 3658.
- ✅ **Master diagnostic rule:** *Re-clean → re-inspect. Persists = real. Vanishes = false.*
- ✅ **Inglis equation** for stress concentration: $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/b}$; linear gives $K_t \approx 4.46$.
- ✅ **UV-A TLV:** $1 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$ for ≤ 1000 s; dose limit $H \leq 1 \; \text{J/cm}^2$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Magnetization: Head Shot Technique, Cold Shot Technique, Central Conductor Testing, and Magnetization Using Yokes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Methods of Magnetization: Head Shot Technique, Cold Shot Technique, Central Conductor Testing, and Magnetization Using Yokes # ⚡ Methods of Magnetization in Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) In **Module 2** of *OEMET724 – Non-Destructive Testing*, we shift from theory to the **shop-floor practices** of Magnetic Particle Testing. To detect a surface or near-surface flaw, the test piece **must first be magnetized**. The four standard industrial methods of inducing this magnetic flux are the **Head Shot, Coil Shot, Central Conductor, and Yoke** techniques. --- ## 1. Why Magnetization Direction Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle (KTU 2024 - CO2):** > A defect is detectable **only when it lies roughly perpendicular (≈ 90°) to the direction of the magnetic flux lines**. If the flux runs parallel to the crack, the crack remains "invisible." Therefore, every magnetization method is essentially a way to *orient* the magnetic field across the suspected defect orientation. ### Conceptual Analogy 🪔 Imagine a flowing river. A **log lying across the river** (perpendicular to flow) creates a visible disturbance (turbulence). A **log lying along the river** (parallel to flow) does not. The crack acts as the log, and the magnetic flux lines act as the river. Hence, we must *aim* the river at the crack. --- ## 2. Overview of the Four Methods | Sl. No. | Method | Where Current Flows | Field Direction | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Head Shot** | Through the workpiece (between prods) | Circular, between contact points | Local surface inspection | | 2 | **Coil Shot (Cold Shot)** | Through an external coil only | Longitudinal, along coil axis | Long shafts, bars | | 3 | **Central Conductor** | Through a central bar/cable | Circumferential around the part | Hollow cylinders, tubes | | 4 | **Yoke (Permanent / Electromagnetic)** | Through yoke windings → air gap → part | Between the pole pieces | Portable field inspection | > [!NOTE] > **Clarification for KTU Students:** The term **"Cold Shot"** occasionally appearing in regional study material refers to the **Coil Shot Technique**, because *no current passes through the hot workpiece itself* — current flows only through the surrounding coil, keeping the part "cold." --- # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis of Each Method ## 2.1 The Head Shot Technique (Prod Magnetization) The **Head Shot** (also called *Prod Technique* or *Direct Contact Method*) uses two **prods** — pointed copper electrodes tipped with braided leads — pressed onto the surface of the test piece. Heavy current (typically **1000 A – 4000 A**) from a **magnetic particle power pack** is forced *through the metal* between the two contact points. ### Operating Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. The operator cleans the contact area and places the prods roughly **75 mm – 200 mm apart**. 2. Magnetizing current is switched on (the **"shot"**) — usually **AC, HWDC, or FWDC**. 3. Current travels **through the bulk of the workpiece** from prod A to prod B. 4. By the **Right-Hand Rule**, a **circular magnetic field** is induced in the volume between the prods. 5. The field is **strongest at the surface** directly between the two contacts and **weakest deep inside**. 6. Dry or wet magnetic particles are applied → they collect at flux leakage sites. ### Why "Head Shot"? In old foundries, the operator would *touch* (head) the part with the prods and *shoot* (pulse) the current — hence the name. ### Key Drawback > [!WARNING] > **Arc Burns & Local Heating:** Direct prod contact causes **arc strikes** that can damage the surface and produce *false indications*. Always use lead or copper braiding and ensure firm contact pressure. Spacing rule of thumb: **prod spacing = 4 × prod face diameter**. --- ## 2.2 The Coil Shot Technique (Cold Shot) Here, the **workpiece itself is placed inside (or alongside) a heavy copper coil**, and high current is pulsed through the coil — *not through the part*. This is why it is called a **"cold" shot**: the test piece remains electrically isolated and physically cooler. ### Operating Logic 1. The part is laid inside a multi-turn coil (typically **3–5 turns** for small parts, **1 turn** for long shafts). 2. A high current pulse magnetizes the coil → the coil acts like an **electromagnet**. 3. Magnetic flux lines run **longitudinally along the axis of the part**. 4. Defects running **transverse to the axis** (e.g., circumferential cracks) are detected. ### The "L/D Ratio Rule" — Critical for KTU Exams! The strength of magnetization depends on the ratio of part length $L$ to part diameter $D$. $$ k = \frac{L}{D} $$ | $L/D$ Ratio | Action Required | |---|---| | $k \geq 10$ | No special attention — magnetization is sufficient | | $3 \leq k < 10$ | Place pole pieces / end caps to channel flux | | $k < 3$ | Use **Cross magnetization** or auxiliary yokes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula:** > For a short part (low $L/D$), the required ampere-turns to achieve saturation is given empirically as: > > $$NI = \frac{K}{\left(\frac{L}{D} + 2\right)}$$ > > where $K$ is a constant depending on material permeability (≈ 45,000 for mild steel). ### Real-World Utility Used in **foundry inspection** of crankshafts, axles, and pinions where transverse cracks must be detected. --- ## 2.3 Central Conductor Testing Designed specifically for **hollow cylindrical components** — pipes, tubes, pressure vessels, gun barrels, and boiler tubes. A **current-carrying conductor** (a heavy copper cable or solid bar) is passed through the bore of the part. ### Operating Logic 1. A flexible cable or rigid bar is threaded through the hollow part. 2. Magnetizing current is passed through this **central conductor**. 3. By the Right-Hand Rule, a **circumferential magnetic field** is induced *inside the wall* of the hollow cylinder. 4. The flux lines wrap around the bore → **longitudinal cracks** (parallel to the cylinder axis) are detected. ### Advantages Over Head Shot - **No prod marks** on the component. - Can magnetize the **entire length simultaneously** by threading the conductor once. - Uniform field, ideal for production-line inspection. ### Modification: Threading Through Multiple Turns For greater magnetization, the conductor can be looped back through the bore $N$ times. The effective magnetizing field scales with $N$: $$ H_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot \frac{I}{2\pi r} $$ where $r$ is the mean radius of the cylinder wall. --- ## 2.4 Magnetization Using Yokes A **yoke** is a portable magnetic device that creates flux *between two pole pieces* placed on either side of the test area. Two variants exist: | Yoke Type | Flux Source | Best For | |---|---|---| | **Permanent Magnet Yoke** (Al-Ni-Co or Nd-Fe-B) | Always ON | Field inspections, no power needed | | **Electromagnetic (AC) Yoke** | 230 V mains | Workshop — surface defects | | **Electromagnetic (DC) Yoke** | Rectified supply | Sub-surface defects | ### Operating Logic 1. Pole pieces are placed on the cleaned surface with a small gap (≈ 10–50 mm). 2. Flux enters one pole, travels **through the part**, and exits the other pole. 3. The flux path is **perpendicular to the line joining the poles**. 4. Defects lying **perpendicular to this line** produce leakage fields. ### Critical Performance Check — The Lift Test > [!NOTE] > **ASTM E709 Standard:** A yoke must be capable of lifting a **4.5 kg (10 lb) steel block** when its poles straddle a **25 mm air gap**. This confirms adequate field strength before inspection. ### Advantages - **Truly portable** — used on-site (bridges, pipelines, welded structures). - **No electrical contact** required with the part. - Safe for inspecting assembled machinery without disassembly. ### Limitation Field strength **falls off rapidly** with distance from the pole faces — limited to local inspection. --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $H = \dfrac{NI}{2\pi r}$ | Magnetic field at radius $r$ from a straight conductor | A/m | | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Flux density inside material | Tesla (T) | | $k = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Coil-shot $L/D$ ratio for longitudinal magnetization | dimensionless | | $NI = \dfrac{K}{L/D + 2}$ | Ampere-turns required for saturation | ampere-turns (AT) | | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | Permeability of free space — **fundamental constant** | H/m | | Pole spacing ≈ $4 \times$ prod face diameter | Head-shot spacing rule | mm | | Yoke lift ≥ **4.5 kg** over **25 mm** gap | ASTM E709 acceptance criterion | kg / mm | --- # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor (Basis for Head Shot & Central Conductor) **Starting Point — Ampère's Circuital Law:** $$ \oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enclosed}} $$ **Step 1:** For a long straight conductor carrying current $I$, by symmetry the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is constant in magnitude on any circular path of radius $r$ concentric with the wire, and tangent to that path. **Step 2:** Substitute $dl = r \, d\theta$ and $H \cdot dl$ integrated over the full circle: $$ \oint H \, dl = H \cdot (2\pi r) = I_{\text{enclosed}} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $H$: $$ H = \frac{I}{2\pi r} $$ **Step 4:** The flux density in air (μr = 1): $$ B = \mu_0 H = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $$ **Step 5:** For the **central conductor test**, if the conductor is looped $N$ times through the part: $$ H_{\text{inside wall}} = \frac{NI}{2\pi r_{\text{mean}}} $$ > This is the master equation that explains **why** the central conductor creates a circumferential field inside the tube wall — the Right-Hand Rule and Ampère's Law are two faces of the same physical coin. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | Defect Orientation | Part Geometry | Recommended Method | |---|---|---| | Transverse cracks on solid shaft | Solid round bar | **Coil Shot** | | Longitudinal cracks on hollow tube | Pipe / tube | **Central Conductor** | | Local surface crack near weld | Any ferrous part | **Head Shot (Prods)** | | Field inspection, no power | Welded structure | **Permanent Yoke** | | Sub-surface defects | Cast steel housing | **DC Electromagnetic Yoke** | | Long axial shaft, small $L/D$ | Stubby gear blank | **Coil + Pole pieces** | --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) **Problem:** A steel tube of **mean radius 50 mm** is inspected using a **central conductor**. The magnetizing current is **1500 A**, looped **3 times** through the bore. Calculate the magnetizing field strength $H$ inside the wall. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} H &= \frac{NI}{2\pi r_{\text{mean}}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{3 \times 1500}{2 \pi \times 0.050} \\[6pt] &= \frac{4500}{0.3142} \\[6pt] &= 14{,}324 \ \text{A/m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} B &= \mu_0 \mu_r H \\ &= (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(200)(14{,}324) \quad (\mu_r \approx 200 \text{ for mild steel}) \\[6pt] &= 3.60 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{T} \\ &= 3.60 \ \text{mT} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Typical saturation flux density for mild steel is **≈ 1.6 T**. A field of 3.6 mT is too weak — the operator would need to increase $I$ or $N$. This kind of calculation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. --- ## 3.4 Current Type Selection Matrix | Current Type | Penetration Depth | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **AC (50 Hz)** | Surface only (~1–2 mm) | Surface-breaking cracks | | **HWDC** (Half-Wave DC) | Moderate (~3–6 mm) | Sub-surface defects | | **FWDC** (Full-Wave DC) | Deep (entire cross-section) | Deep sub-surface, low-alloy steels | | **AC + DC (Surge)** | Variable | Residual magnetization | > [!IMPORTANT] > In **Head Shot**, AC is preferred for surface defects because it gives strong particle mobility due to the rapidly reversing field — particles literally **vibrate** into the leakage field. --- # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Magnetization Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Inspect a Ferromagnetic Part"] --> B{"What is the
Defect Orientation?"} B -- "Transverse to axis" --> C{"Solid or Hollow?"} B -- "Longitudinal / axial" --> D["Use HEAD SHOT
(Prods on surface)"] B -- "Unknown / mixed" --> E["Use YOKE
(Portable)"] C -- "Solid shaft / bar" --> F["Use COIL SHOT
(L/D ratio matters)"] C -- "Hollow tube / pipe" --> G["Use CENTRAL CONDUCTOR
(Cable through bore)"] F --> H{"Is L/D >= 10?"} H -- "Yes" --> I["Magnetize directly
(sufficient flux)"] H -- "No" --> J["Add pole pieces
or use cross magnetization"] G --> K{"Loop cable
N times?"} K -- "Yes" --> L["H = NI / 2*pi*r increases N-fold"] K -- "No" --> M["Single pass
H = I / 2*pi*r"] D --> N{"Current type?"} N -- "AC" --> O["Surface cracks"] N -- "DC" --> P["Sub-surface defects"] ``` ## 4.2 Setup Schematics — All Four Methods Compared ```mermaid graph LR subgraph HS["HEAD SHOT SETUP"] H1["Prod A
(Contact Point)"] -- "Current through
workpiece" --> H2["Prod B
(Contact Point)"] H2 --> H3["Circular Magnetic
Field between prods"] end subgraph CS["COIL SHOT SETUP"] C1["Magnetizing
Coil (N turns)"] -- "Flux through
workpiece" --> C2["Longitudinal
Field along axis"] C2 --> C3["Detects
Transverse Cracks"] end subgraph CC["CENTRAL CONDUCTOR SETUP"] CC1["Current-carrying
cable in bore"] -- "Field wraps
around tube" --> CC2["Circumferential
Field in wall"] CC2 --> CC3["Detects
Longitudinal Cracks"] end subgraph YK["YOKE SETUP"] Y1["Pole Piece 1"] -- "Flux through
air gap & part" --> Y2["Pole Piece 2"] Y2 --> Y3["Field between
poles"] end ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Head Shot Inspection ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Op as Operator participant PP as Power Pack participant Pt as Test Piece participant MP as Magnetic Particles Op->>PP: Set current (e.g., 1500 A) Op->>Pt: Place prods 75–200 mm apart Op->>PP: Trigger SHOT (current pulse) PP-->>Pt: Current flows A → B through metal Pt-->>Pt: Circular magnetic field induced Op->>MP: Spray wet particles / dust dry powder MP->>Pt: Particles cluster at flux leakage Op->>Op: Interpret indications under UV / white light ``` --- # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between the Head Shot technique and the Coil Shot technique of magnetization.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Head Shot | Coil Shot (Cold Shot) | |---|---|---| | Current path | **Through** the workpiece | **Through** the external coil only | | Field geometry | Circular between prods | Longitudinal along axis | | Surface damage | Possible arc burns at prods | No contact damage | | Typical application | Local surface inspection | Long bars, shafts | | Current magnitude | 1000–4000 A | 500–2000 A (coil turns multiply) | --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the ASTM E709 lift test for a magnetic yoke? Why is it performed?** **Model Answer:** The yoke must lift a **4.5 kg (10 lb) ferrous block** across a **25 mm air gap** between its poles. This **performance verification** ensures the yoke generates sufficient flux to magnetize the test piece to near-saturation, **before** any inspection begins. It is performed at the start of every shift and after any demagnetization or mechanical disturbance. --- ## Part B Question (14 Marks) — Choose ONE ### ⬇️ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **Head Shot technique** of magnetization with a neat sketch. State **two advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **Central Conductor method** of magnetization. A steel tube of mean radius **60 mm** is inspected using a central conductor carrying **2000 A** looped **4 times**. Calculate the magnetizing field strength $H$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Definition:** Head Shot technique uses two prod-type electrodes pressed on the workpiece surface to pass magnetizing current directly through the part, generating a circular magnetic field between the contacts. **Sketch:** *(See Mermaid Diagram 4.2 – Head Shot Setup)* **Procedure:** 1. Clean contact points to ensure low resistance. 2. Place prods **75 – 200 mm** apart on either side of the suspected defect. 3. Switch on magnetizing current pulse. 4. Apply magnetic particles (dry or wet). 5. Inspect under appropriate lighting. **Advantages:** 1. Highly portable and suitable for **large stationary structures**. 2. Effective for **localized inspection** of welds and critical zones. **Limitations:** 1. Causes **arc burns and surface damage** at contact points. 2. Field strength **drops sharply** in the bulk — not suitable for sub-surface defects. #### Model Solution (b): **Central Conductor Setup Description:** A flexible cable or rigid copper bar is passed through the bore of a hollow component. Current through this central conductor induces a **circumferential magnetic field** inside the tube wall (Ampère's Law). Longitudinal cracks in the tube wall produce flux leakage detected by magnetic particles. **Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} H &= \frac{NI}{2\pi r_{\text{mean}}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{4 \times 2000}{2\pi \times 0.060} \\[6pt] &= \frac{8000}{0.3770} \\[6pt] &= 21{,}220 \ \text{A/m} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $H \approx 21.22 \ \text{kA/m}$ — sufficient to magnetize the mild-steel tube to near saturation. --- ### ⬇️ Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Describe the **Coil Shot technique** of magnetization. Explain the significance of the **L/D ratio** and how it is compensated when the ratio is low. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain magnetization using **permanent and electromagnetic yokes**. State the **lift-test criterion** and mention three industrial applications. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Coil Shot Technique:** The component is placed inside or alongside a multi-turn magnetizing coil. Pulsed current through the coil induces longitudinal flux along the part axis. No current passes through the part itself (hence "cold shot"). Suitable for detecting **transverse cracks**. **Significance of L/D Ratio:** $$ k = \frac{L}{D} $$ - For $k \geq 10$ → self-contained flux loop, magnetization is strong. - For $k < 10$ → flux leakage at ends reduces internal field. **Compensation when L/D < 10:** 1. Place **ferromagnetic pole pieces** at the part ends to channel flux back into the part. 2. Use **cross magnetization** (rotate part 90° and re-magnetize). 3. Add more **ampere-turns** using the empirical relation $NI = K/(L/D + 2)$. #### Model Solution (b): **Permanent Yoke:** Contains Al-Ni-Co or rare-earth (Nd-Fe-B) magnets between two soft-iron pole pieces. Flux is always present; no power supply needed. Portable, ideal for **field inspections**. **Electromagnetic Yoke:** Soft-iron core wound with a coil. Energized from 230 V mains or rectified DC. AC yoke → surface defects; DC yoke → sub-surface defects. **Lift Test (ASTM E709):** Yoke must lift **4.5 kg over a 25 mm gap**. **Industrial Applications:** 1. Inspection of **welded joints** in pressure vessels and bridges. 2. In-service inspection of **crane hooks and lifting gear**. 3. Detection of **fatigue cracks** at railway wheel seats. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse coil-shot current with workpiece current** — many students incorrectly write "current passes through the workpiece." It passes through the **external coil** only. > 2. **Always quote units** — a numerical answer of "21220" without "A/m" will lose 1 mark. > 3. **Draw the sketch even if the question says "with neat sketch"** — KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for a labelled diagram. > 4. **Mention the L/D rule** in any coil-shot answer — this is the *most-asked* sub-topic in KTU exams. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - 🔹 **Head Shot** → prods on surface → circular field → **local** surface cracks. - 🔹 **Coil Shot (Cold Shot)** → external coil → longitudinal field → **transverse** cracks. - 🔹 **Central Conductor** → cable through bore → circumferential field → **longitudinal** cracks in tubes. - 🔹 **Yoke** → portable flux across pole gap → **versatile** field inspection. - 🔹 **Master equation:** $H = NI / (2\pi r)$. - 🔹 **L/D rule:** $L/D \geq 10$ ideal; below 3 → use pole pieces or cross magnetization. - 🔹 **Lift test:** Yoke must lift **4.5 kg / 25 mm** gap per ASTM E709. - 🔹 **Prod spacing rule:** ≈ **4 × prod face diameter**. - 🔹 **Current selection:** AC = surface only; DC = sub-surface. - 🔹 **Fundamental constant:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. - 🔹 Always **demagnetize** the part after inspection if it will return to service in a magnetically sensitive environment. ---

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Magnetization" in NDT Context?

In **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** — one of the most widely deployed surface NDT methods — *magnetization* is the deliberate process of inducing a magnetic field inside a ferromagnetic test piece so that any surface or near-surface discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion, laps, porosity) distorts the otherwise uniform flux lines and reveals itself through indications formed by applied ferromagnetic particles.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMET724, Module 2):**  
> *Magnetization* is the process of establishing a magnetic flux of suitable strength and direction inside a ferromagnetic specimen such that leakage fields from flaws become detectable. The **two principal classifications** are **Direct Methods** (current passes *through* the test piece itself) and **Indirect Methods** (magnetic field is induced *into* the test piece from an external source without passing current through it).

## 1.2 The Core Principle — Right-Hand Rule and Flux Direction

The direction of magnetization is governed by **Ampère's Right-Hand Rule**: when the fingers of the right hand curl in the direction of current flow, the extended thumb points in the direction of the magnetic flux lines. This single rule dictates whether a defect will produce a visible indication — defects lying **perpendicular** to the flux produce the strongest leakage fields, while those **parallel** to the flux produce little or no indication.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose of Magnetism"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition for first-time learners:**  
> Imagine the magnetic flux inside a steel bar as water flowing through a smooth garden hose. When the hose is intact, water flows smoothly and leaks nowhere. Now imagine a small crack cuts across the hose (a transverse flaw) — water *leaks* out of the crack, and you can detect the leak from the outside. This "leakage" is exactly what happens to magnetic flux at a defect, and the magnetic particles we dust on the surface are like colored dye that reveals the leak location.  
>  
> **Direct magnetization** = pumping water *through* the hose itself.  
> **Indirect magnetization** = placing the hose inside a stronger magnetic field (e.g., near a giant bar magnet) that pulls the water along without any water ever touching the hose from the outside.

## 1.4 Why Two Methods? — Classification Rationale

| Criterion | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Current path** | Through the test piece | External source (magnet/yoke) |
| **Contact required?** | Yes (prods, clamps, leads) | No (for yokes); induced field for coils |
| **Typical use** | Large stationary parts | In-service, in-field inspections |
| **Risk of surface damage** | Prod burn marks possible | None — non-contact |
| **Field orientation** | Predominantly circumferential | Predominantly longitudinal (yokes/coils) |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
- **Coercivity** and **remanent flux density** are quoted in **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (G)**, where $1\ \text{T} = 10{,}000\ \text{G}$.
- **Magnetic field strength (H)** is in **Ampere-turns per metre (At/m)**.
- **Typical magnetizing current density in MPT:** $1200\text{–}4800\ \text{A/m}$ of specimen thickness (per ASTM E709).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method A — Direct Magnetization Techniques

In **direct magnetization**, the test piece itself becomes part of the electric circuit. The current flowing through the specimen generates a circumferential magnetic field around the current path, satisfying Ampère's Law.

### 2.1.1 Head Shot / Prod Method
- Two prods (prods = handheld copper/brass-tipped electrodes) are pressed onto the surface of the test piece.
- High-amperage low-voltage AC or half-wave DC current flows between the prods *through* the material.
- The flux lines run **circumferentially** around the axis joining the two prods.
- **Best for:** Localized inspection of welds, small castings, in-service equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Prod spacing is critical: minimum spacing $\geq$ **200 mm (8 in)** is recommended by ASTM E709 to prevent arc burns and ensure adequate field strength between the contacts.

### 2.1.2 Central Conductor Method
- A copper bar or cable is passed through a hollow cylindrical part (tubes, rings, bored castings).
- Current flows through the central conductor — *not* through the part — but **the test piece experiences a circumferential magnetic field** by induction.
- **Best for:** Inspection of tube interiors, ring-shaped forgings.

### 2.1.3 Clamp / Direct Contact Method
- Heavy-duty clamps make direct electrical contact at the ends of the part.
- Current passes lengthwise through the entire piece.
- **Best for:** Long shafts, bars, billets where uniform longitudinal fields are required.

## 2.2 Method B — Indirect Magnetization Techniques

In **indirect magnetization**, no current passes through the test piece. The magnetic field is either induced from a permanent magnet/electromagnet (yoke) or from a coil encircling the part.

### 2.2.1 Yoke Method (Permanent Magnet Yoke & Electromagnetic Yoke)
- A horseshoe-shaped magnet is placed across the surface.
- Flux enters one leg, travels *through* the test piece, and exits through the other leg.
- Produces a **longitudinal field** between the pole pieces.
- **Lifting Power Test:** A permanent magnet yoke must lift **$\geq$ 4.5 kg (10 lb)** at minimum pole spacing per ASTM E709.
- **Best for:** Welds, in-service field inspections, demagnetizable materials.

### 2.2.2 Coil Method (Encircling Coil)
- The part is placed inside (or inserted into) a multi-turn solenoid coil.
- AC or DC current in the coil produces a **longitudinal field** along the part's axis.
- **Best for:** Shafts, rods, long cylindrical components.

### 2.2.3 Threading Bar Method
- A flexible cable is wound *around* a fixed part (e.g., a long pipe in place) several times, simulating a coil when current flows.
- Combines flexibility of direct methods with longitudinal field orientation of indirect methods.
- **Best for:** Long pipe runs, fixed vessels.

## 2.3 The "5r Rule" for Encircling Coils — A KTU Favourite

For a circular magnetization (i.e., direct current flow through the part), the field strength at the surface is approximated by:

$$H \approx \frac{I}{5r}$$

where $r$ is the radius of the part. This empirical relation comes from the Biot–Savart Law applied to a long straight conductor where the surface is at distance $r$ and the effective integrating length is approximately $\pi D \approx 5r$ (since $D = 2r$ and $2\pi \approx 6.28$, the engineering approximation uses 5 for a 20% safety factor).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with the OEMET724 Module 2 syllabus.

| Phenomenon / Method | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field strength in solenoid coil | $H = \dfrac{NI}{L}$ | $N$ = turns, $I$ = current (A), $L$ = coil length (m) | Long solenoid ($L \gg D$) |
| Flux density in material | $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | $\mu_r$ = relative permeability | Inside linear material |
| Encircling-coil demag / magnetization rule | $H \approx \dfrac{NI}{5r}$ | $r$ = part radius (m) | When $L \approx 5r$ (length-to-diameter ratio ≈ 1.57) |
| Direct circular magnetization field | $H \approx \dfrac{I}{5r}$ | $I$ = current through part (A) | Long straight conductor approximation |
| Right-hand rule for direction | $\vec{B}$ curls around $I$ | — | Conventional current |
| Lifting-power criterion (yoke) | $F_L \geq 4.5\ \text{kg}$ (ASTM) | $F_L$ = pull-off force | Permanent magnet yokes only |
| Prod-spacing minimum | $s_{min} \geq 200\ \text{mm}$ | $s$ = prod spacing | ASTM E709 |
| Recommended magnetizing force for MT | $H \geq 36\ \text{At/cm}$ at surface (longitudinal); $H \geq 12\text{–}30\ \text{At/cm}$ for prod contact | — | Approximate guidelines |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (airframes):** Yoke + wet fluorescent MT is the dominant method for inspecting aluminium airframes? *No — for ferromagnetic components like landing gear, engine shafts, and rotor hubs, direct current magnetization with prods and encircling coils is standard.*
- **Pressure vessel / weld inspection:** Head-shot prods with dry powder — the most common field inspection.
- **Railway industry:** Axles and rails magnetized longitudinally by encircling coils during periodic overhaul.
- **Pipeline girth welds:** Threading bar / yoke method used in situ.
- **Power generation:** Generator rotor shafts inspected via central conductor / coil methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field Inside a Long Solenoid (Coil Method)

We begin with Ampère's Law in free space for a solenoid of $N$ turns, length $L$, carrying current $I$:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = NI_{enclosed}$$

For an ideal solenoid, $\vec{H}$ is uniform inside and parallel to the axis. Choosing the Amperian loop to coincide with the solenoid axis of length $L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} &= H \cdot L \\
H \cdot L &= N \cdot I \\
\therefore\ H &= \dfrac{NI}{L} \quad \left[\frac{\text{At}}{\text{m}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Reasoning logic:** The magnetic field strength inside a long solenoid depends *only* on the turn density (turns per unit length) and the current, not on the cross-sectional area — a direct consequence of Ampère's Law applied to the symmetry of the geometry.

**Resulting flux density:**

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= \mu_0 \mu_r H \\
B &= \mu_0 \mu_r \dfrac{NI}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Encircling Coil for Short Specimens (The "5r Rule")

A coil of $N$ turns wound around a short cylindrical specimen of radius $r$ acts as a *finite* solenoid. The effective magnetic path length is approximated as the mean circumference of the coil:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{eff} &\approx \pi D \\
D &= 2r \\
\therefore\ L_{eff} &= 2\pi r \approx 6.28\,r
\end{aligned}$$

However, empirical calibration studies in NDT practice apply a safety factor of ~20% (compensating for non-uniform flux near the coil ends), leading to the widely cited working approximation:

$$L_{eff} \approx 5r$$

Substituting back into the solenoid formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{NI}{L_{eff}} = \dfrac{NI}{5r} \quad \left[\frac{\text{At}}{\text{m}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conversion to field strength in At/cm (more common in MT practice):**  
> Since $1\ \text{m} = 100\ \text{cm}$: $\quad H\left[\frac{\text{At}}{\text{cm}}\right] = \dfrac{NI}{5r[\text{cm}]}$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Direct Circular Magnetization Field (Current Through the Part)

When current $I$ flows axially through a long cylindrical ferromagnetic rod of radius $r$, Ampère's Law gives the tangential $\vec{H}$ at the surface:

$$\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} &= I \\
H \cdot (2\pi r) &= I \\
H &= \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}
\end{aligned}$$

Because $2\pi \approx 6.28$, NDT practice uses the *empirical* simplified form (again with the 20% engineering safety factor):

$$\boxed{H \approx \dfrac{I}{5r}}$$

This is the *single most cited formula* in KTU Module 2 numericals for direct magnetization.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A solid cylindrical steel shaft of diameter **$50\ \text{mm}$** is to be magnetized using the *direct contact* (current-through-part) method. Calculate the magnetizing current required to produce a tangential field strength of **$2400\ \text{At/m}$** at the surface.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula and knowns:**
$$H = \dfrac{I}{5r}, \quad r = \dfrac{50}{2} = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}, \quad H = 2400\ \text{At/m}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $I$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I &= H \cdot 5r \\
I &= 2400 \times 5 \times 0.025 \\
I &= 2400 \times 0.125 \\
I &= 300\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check:** Doubling the radius would double the current — consistent with the physical intuition that a larger cross-section requires proportionally more current to achieve the same surface field.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Answer:** $I = 300\ \text{A}$.  
> Always remember to convert **mm → m** before substituting into SI formulas.

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method?

| Inspection Scenario | Recommended Method | Field Direction | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transverse surface crack in weld | Head shot (Prods) | Circumferential (between prods) | Flaw ⊥ flux = max leakage |
| Longitudinal crack in long shaft | Encircling coil | Longitudinal along shaft | Flaw ⊥ flux = max leakage |
| Crack orientation unknown | Two-pass inspection: prod then coil | Both orientations | Ensures detection regardless of crack orientation |
| In-service pipe weld (no cutting) | Yoke or threading bar | Longitudinal | Non-contact / minimal setup |
| Hollow cylinder bore | Central conductor | Circumferential | Uniform field around bore |
| Ferromagnetic ring | Central conductor | Circumferential | Field induced in ring walls |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the field *direction* of direct methods (circumferential) with indirect coil/yoke methods (longitudinal). Memorize: **Prods → circular field; Coils/yokes → linear field.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between direct and indirect methods of magnetization used in Magnetic Particle Testing. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
In **direct magnetization**, the magnetizing current is passed *through* the test piece itself, e.g., via prods (head shot), clamps, or central conductor. In **indirect magnetization**, the test piece is not part of the electrical circuit; instead, a magnetic field is induced *into* it from an external source such as a permanent magnet yoke, electromagnet yoke, or an encircling coil. Direct methods produce primarily **circumferential fields**, whereas indirect methods produce primarily **longitudinal fields**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the "5r rule" and explain its significance in magnetic particle testing.**

**Model Answer:**
The **5r rule** is an empirical engineering formula that relates the magnetic field strength $H$ produced in a cylindrical part to the magnetizing current $I$ and the radius $r$ of the part. For direct circular magnetization: $H \approx I / (5r)$. For encircling coil magnetization: $H \approx NI / (5r)$. Its significance is that it allows the inspector to **calculate the required magnetizing current** to achieve the recommended minimum field strength (typically 2400–4800 A/m) without resorting to complex Biot–Savart integrations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Students frequently lose marks by (a) writing field directions incorrectly (circumferential vs. longitudinal), (b) omitting the *right-hand rule application*, (c) failing to mention the **two-pass inspection technique** when crack orientation is unknown, and (d) forgetting to specify **ASTM E709** standards for current density and prod spacing. Always include units in numerical answers.

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **head shot (prod) method** and the **central conductor method** of magnetization. Indicate the direction of current flow and the resulting magnetic field in each case.

**Model Solution:**

**Head Shot (Prod) Method:**
- Two prods are pressed onto the cleaned surface of the ferromagnetic part, separated by a minimum of **200 mm** (per ASTM E709).
- A high-amperage low-voltage magnetizing current (AC, DC, or half-wave DC) is passed *between the prods* through the part itself.
- By the right-hand rule, the magnetic flux lines form **closed loops in planes perpendicular to the current axis**, i.e., a circumferential field.
- **Cracks lying perpendicular to the line joining the prods** produce maximum leakage flux and hence the strongest indications.
- **Limitations:** Prod contact marks, overheating, uneven field strength between prods.

**Central Conductor Method:**
- A copper bar or cable is inserted through the hollow part (tube, ring, bored forging).
- Current is passed through the *conductor*, not the part.
- The part is magnetized by the **circumferential field** surrounding the central conductor.
- **Best for:** detecting longitudinal cracks in tubes and ring-shaped components.
- **Limitation:** Requires a hollow part.

**Diagram (textual sketch):**
- *Prod method:* Draw two prod tips touching the surface with current $I$ flowing between them; show circular flux loops in the cross-sectional view.
- *Central conductor:* Draw a rod passing through a tube with arrows showing circumferential flux in the tube wall.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical steel bar of **diameter 80 mm** is to be magnetized using the **direct contact method**. Calculate the magnetizing current required to produce a tangential magnetic field strength of **3200 At/m** at the surface of the bar. What is the magnetic flux density if the relative permeability of the steel is **$\mu_r = 500$**?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the 5r formula:**
$$H = \dfrac{I}{5r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = H \cdot 5r$$

**Step 2 — Convert units:**
- Radius $r = \dfrac{80}{2} = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.04\ \text{m}$
- $H = 3200\ \text{At/m}$

**Step 3 — Calculate current:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 3200 \times 5 \times 0.04 \\
I &= 3200 \times 0.20 \\
I &= 640\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate flux density:**
$$\begin{aligned}
B &= \mu_0 \mu_r H \\
B &= (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times 3200 \\
B &= 6.2832 \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 3200 \\
B &= 6.2832 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.6 \times 10^{6} \\
B &= 1.005\ \text{T} \quad (\text{approximately})
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Required magnetizing current: **$I = 640\ \text{A}$**
- Magnetic flux density: **$B \approx 1.005\ \text{T}$**

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **yoke method** and the **encircling coil method** of indirect magnetization. Compare their suitability for field inspection of welded joints.

**Model Solution:**

**Yoke Method:**
- A horseshoe-shaped permanent magnet or electromagnet is placed across the surface to be inspected.
- Flux flows from the **North pole → through the test piece → into the South pole**, producing a **longitudinal field** in the region between the poles.
- For permanent magnet yokes, a **lifting power test** is mandatory: the yoke must lift **≥ 4.5 kg (10 lb)** at maximum pole spacing (ASTM E709).
- **Advantages:** No electrical contact → no surface damage; portable; ideal for in-service inspection.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited depth of magnetization (effective only near surface).

**Encircling Coil Method:**
- The part is placed inside a multi-turn solenoid coil carrying AC or DC current.
- Current produces a **longitudinal magnetic field** along the axis of the part.
- Effective for shafts, rods, and elongated components.

**Comparison for Field Inspection of Welded Joints:**

| Aspect | Yoke | Encircling Coil |
|---|---|---|
| Portability | Highly portable | Requires power source + coil |
| Surface damage | None | None (if coil does not touch) |
| Setup time | Minimal | Longer (coil sizing needed) |
| Best for | Local weld zones | Full-length longitudinal inspections |
| Power | Self (PM) or low (EM) | Mains/high-current needed |

**Verdict:** Yokes are **preferred for field inspection** due to portability and absence of contact marks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **5-turn encircling coil** is used to magnetize a steel rod of **diameter 30 mm**. The coil is energized with a current of **1500 A**. Calculate the magnetic field strength at the centre of the coil. Comment on whether this value is sufficient to produce a strong indication, given the minimum recommended field strength is **2400 At/m**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the encircling coil 5r formula:**
$$H = \dfrac{NI}{5r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
- $N = 5$
- $I = 1500\ \text{A}$
- $r = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15\ \text{mm} = 0.015\ \text{m}$

**Step 3 — Calculate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{5 \times 1500}{5 \times 0.015} \\
H &= \dfrac{7500}{0.075} \\
H &= 100{,}000\ \text{At/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with recommendation:**
- Recommended minimum = 2400 At/m
- Achieved = 100,000 At/m
- Achieved value is **~42× higher** than the minimum.

**Comment:**
This is more than sufficient to produce a strong indication. In fact, the field is excessively high — the inspector should consider reducing the current or using fewer turns to avoid over-saturation of the part (which can mask indications) and excessive heating of the coil.

**Adjusted suggestion:** A current of $I = 1500 \times \dfrac{2400}{100{,}000} \approx 36\ \text{A}$ would be sufficient. The coil of 5 turns × 1500 A is over-engineered for this small-diameter rod.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Summary — Print This Before the Exam:**

- **Magnetization = inducing flux inside a ferromagnetic specimen** to detect flaws via leakage-field indications.
- **Two classes:** Direct (current through part) vs. Indirect (field from external source).
- **Direct methods:** Head shot (prods), central conductor, clamps/contacts → **circumferential fields**.
- **Indirect methods:** Yoke (PM & EM), encircling coil, threading bar → **longitudinal fields**.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $H = NI/L$ (long solenoid)
  - $H = I / (5r)$ (direct, current through part)
  - $H = NI / (5r)$ (encircling coil, short specimen)
  - $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$
- **Right-hand rule:** Flux curls around current — always state this explicitly in KTU answers.
- **ASTM E709 standards:** Prod spacing ≥ 200 mm; yoke lift ≥ 4.5 kg; recommended $H$ ≈ 2400–4800 A/m.
- **Crack orientation rule:** Indications are strongest when the **crack is perpendicular** to the flux direction.
- **Two-pass inspection:** Always inspect twice with **perpendicular field orientations** if crack direction is unknown.
- **Largest pitfall:** Confusing circumferential vs. longitudinal field directions in direct vs. indirect methods.
- **Most cited KTU numerical:** Direct current magnetization using $H = I / (5r)$.
- **Permanent magnet yokes:** Use the **lifting power test** for periodic verification.
- **Demagnetization:** Should follow inspection if residual magnetization could interfere with service (e.g., in bearing races).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous and Residual Techniques of MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous and Residual Techniques of MPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI) – A Quick Recap

Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI) is a **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** method used to detect surface and near-surface discontinuities in **ferromagnetic materials** (materials that can be magnetized, such as iron, steel, nickel, cobalt, and their alloys). The principle relies on the fact that when a ferromagnetic component is magnetized, **magnetic flux lines** travel through the material. If a discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion) exists, the flux lines **leak out into the surrounding air**, creating a local **flux leakage field**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEMET724):**
> The choice between **Continuous** and **Residual** techniques is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU ESE Module 2. Students must understand the *timing relationship* between magnetizing current and particle application.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of the Two Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Technique of MPI:**
> A method in which the **magnetizing force is maintained (kept "ON") while the magnetic particles are being applied** to the surface of the test specimen, and continues until the particles have been attracted and aligned at the leakage field locations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Residual Technique of MPI:**
> A method in which the **magnetizing force is switched OFF first**, and the **magnetic particles are applied AFTER the magnetizing current has been cut off**. The detection depends entirely on the **residual (retained) magnetism** left in the part after magnetization.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Water Sponge Intuition"

Imagine a dry **sponge (residual magnetism)** sitting in a **puddle of water (continuous magnetizing current)**.

- **Continuous Technique** = You press the sponge onto a dusty surface **while the sponge is still in the puddle**. The sponge is actively pulling water, dust sticks instantly, and you see the pattern *in real time*. This is exactly how MPI works when current is still flowing — particles are pulled by the **live magnetic field**.

- **Residual Technique** = You take the sponge **out of the puddle first**, squeeze out the excess, and then press it onto the dusty surface. The sponge is no longer in the water, but it still has *retained moisture*. This moisture (residual magnetism) is enough to attract dust (magnetic particles) at crack locations. The field is "frozen in" because the material has **high retentivity** (like hard steel).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Soft steel forgings (low retentivity) work great with the *continuous* method. Hardened components like springs, crankshafts, and tool bits (high retentivity) are best inspected using the *residual* method.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$** |
| Relative permeability (steel) | $\mu_r$ | **100 – 5000** |
| Coercive force (soft steel) | $H_c$ | **< 400 A/m** |
| Coercive force (hard steel) | $H_c$ | **> 4000 A/m** |
| Residual flux density | $B_r$ | **0.5 – 1.4 Tesla** |
| Standard test sensitivity | AISI/ASTM | **Ketos ring or pie gauge** |
| Recommended magnetizing field | $H$ | **~ 2400 to 4800 A/m** for general MPI |

---

## 1.5 Callout Box – Why Two Techniques Exist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (Industrial Standard):**
> - **Soft ferromagnetic materials (low retentivity, low coercivity)** → **Continuous Technique ONLY** (residual magnetism is too weak to hold particles).
> - **Hard ferromagnetic materials (high retentivity, high coercivity)** → **Residual Technique is PREFERRED** (avoids arc burns, allows inspection of hollow bores, and permits free movement of operator).

---

## 1.6 Classification of Magnetization Techniques (Parent-Child View)

```text
Magnetization Techniques in MPI
│
├── 1. Continuous Technique (Current ON during particle application)
│   ├── Wet Horizontal
│   ├── Wet Mobile
│   └── Dry (with portable yokes)
│
├── 2. Residual Technique (Current OFF before particle application)
│   ├── Residual Magnetism Method
│   └── Induced (Solenoid) Method
│
└── 3. Pre-magnetization (variants)
```

This parent view helps you recall the entire taxonomy during the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hysteresis Loop Connection (Most Critical Concept)

The choice between **Continuous** and **Residual** techniques is **not arbitrary** — it is dictated by the **B-H hysteresis loop** of the test material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** When you magnetize a material and then switch OFF the magnetizing current, the magnetic flux density does **NOT** drop to zero immediately. It drops to a value called the **Residual Flux Density ($B_r$)**. This $B_r$ is what powers the Residual Technique.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Apply magnetizing force ($H$)**: Current passes through the specimen or a coil → magnetic domains align.
2. **Increase $H$** until saturation point → nearly all domains aligned.
3. **Reduce $H$ to zero** → domains partially return to random orientation, but a fraction remains aligned.
4. **The remaining alignment** = **Residual Magnetism ($B_r$)**.
5. **To demagnetize the part completely**, a reverse coercive force $H_c$ must be applied.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Working – Continuous Technique

### Step-by-Step Procedure:
1. **Pre-clean** the test surface (remove oil, paint, scale).
2. **Place the specimen** in the magnetizer (yoke, prods, coil, or central conductor).
3. **Apply magnetizing current**.
4. **While current is ON**, apply **dry powder** OR **wet suspension** of magnetic particles.
5. **Maintain current** for a brief dwell time (typically **0.5 – 2 seconds**) so particles migrate to leakage fields.
6. **Inspect under suitable lighting** (UV-A for fluorescent particles, white light ≥ 1000 lux for visible particles).
7. **Switch OFF current** only AFTER particles have been deposited and observed.
8. **Demagnetize** (mandatory for parts going into service to avoid attracting iron filings in operation).

### Why It Works:
The **active magnetizing field** overcomes the **coercive force** of soft materials easily. The live field is **strong and uniform**, so even very tight, tight cracks (with low leakage field) can be detected.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Working – Residual Technique

### Step-by-Step Procedure:
1. **Pre-clean** the test surface.
2. **Magnetize the part** by passing a high magnetizing current through it (or through a coil around it) for a short duration — typically a **shot (magnetizing pulse)** of **0.5 – 1 second**.
3. **Switch OFF the magnetizing current.**
4. **Without re-applying current**, spray or flow **wet particle suspension** over the surface (or apply dry powder gently).
5. **Observe the indications** — particles migrate to leakage fields **using only the residual magnetism**.
6. **Record, interpret, and demagnetize.**

### Why It Works:
After current is cut off, the part retains a **residual flux density $B_r$**. For hard steels (e.g., AISI 1095, tool steel, hardened alloy steels), $B_r$ is high enough to retain the indication pattern for **several minutes**, giving the operator plenty of time to inspect.

---

## 2.4 The Two Sub-Categories of Residual Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks the difference between *Residual Magnetism* and *Induced Magnetism* methods.

### (a) Residual Magnetism Method (Direct Magnetization)
- The current is passed **directly through the part** (using prods or head-shot current).
- The **longitudinal axis of the part itself** becomes the magnetic axis.
- The current is **shut off**, then particles are applied.
- Best for detecting **circumferential (transverse to length)** cracks.

### (b) Induced (Solenoid) Magnetism Method
- The part is placed **inside a solenoid coil** (or wrapped with cable).
- Magnetizing field is generated by **Ampere's law** in the coil.
- Current is shut off, part retains magnetism, particles are applied.
- Best for detecting **longitudinal (axial) cracks** in cylindrical parts.
- Also called **"coil shot" technique**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Module 2 – MPI Techniques**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Magnetic flux density in a medium | Tesla (T) |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | Magnetizing field intensity in a solenoid | A/m |
| 3 | $\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I$ | Ampere's Circuital Law (basis of coil magnetization) | A |
| 4 | $B_r$ | Residual flux density (after $H=0$) | Tesla |
| 5 | $H_c$ | Coercive force (reverse field to reduce $B=0$) | A/m |
| 6 | $F_m = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | Magnetic pulling force on a particle | Newton (N) |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{H \cdot D}{0.16}$ (prod spacing) | Current for prod method | Amperes |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{45000}{L \cdot D}$ (solenoid turns) | Turns required for circular magnetization | turns |
| 9 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$ | Inductance of a magnetizing coil | Henry (H) |
| 10 | $B_{leak} \propto \dfrac{\Delta \mu}{\mu} \cdot B_0$ | Flux leakage field (related to permeability contrast) | Tesla |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Markdown Note:** All absolute values and conditional bars in the table above are written as plain text or with $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside math mode. **Never** write `|x|` directly inside markdown table cells.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component Inspected | Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Landing gear, turbine shafts (hardened) | **Residual** |
| Automotive | Crankshafts, connecting rods, springs | **Residual** (coil shot) |
| Oil & Gas | Drill pipes, sucker rods | **Continuous** (mobile yokes) |
| Power Generation | Boiler tubes, weld seams | **Continuous** |
| Forging Industry | Large rough forgings | **Continuous** (wet) |
| Tool Manufacturing | Hardened tool bits, dies | **Residual** |
| Railway | Axles, rails (in-service) | **Continuous** (portable) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"Hard Holds, Soft Needs Help"** → Hard materials hold residual magnetism (use Residual); Soft materials need live field (use Continuous).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Magnetizing Current for a Solenoid (Residual Technique)

When a cylindrical ferromagnetic part is placed inside a magnetizing coil for the **Residual (Induced) Technique**, we need to determine the **ampere-turns required** to fully magnetize the part.

### Given:
- Length of part: $L$ (metres)
- Diameter of part: $D$ (metres)
- Number of coil turns: $N$
- Current: $I$ (amperes)
- Required magnetizing field: $H$ (A/m), typically **2400–4800 A/m** for general MPI.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Apply Ampere's Circuital Law** along the magnetic path inside the coil:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I
$$

**Step 2 — Assume uniform field** $H$ inside the coil of length $L$:

$$
H \cdot L = N I
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for current:**

$$
I = \frac{H \cdot L}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for turns** (more common in practice):

$$
N = \frac{H \cdot L}{I}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute typical values** for a **10 cm diameter, 30 cm long shaft** with required $H = 3600$ A/m and supply current $I = 1000$ A:

$$
N = \frac{3600 \times 0.30}{1000} = \frac{1080}{1000} = 1.08 \approx 2 \text{ turns}
$$

**Step 6 — Therefore**, only **~ 2 turns** of heavy cable are needed to magnetize the shaft by the induced (solenoid) method.

### KTU Examiner Note:
In exams, students often forget the unit conversion from cm to m. Always convert: **$L$ (in metres)** when using SI units.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Demagnetization Energy Required After Residual Inspection

After residual MPI, the part **must be demagnetized** before going back into service. The energy stored in the magnetic field of the part is:

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Energy density** stored in a magnetic field:

$$
w = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}
$$

(units: $\text{J/m}^3$)

**Step 2 — Total energy** in the part of volume $V$:

$$
E = w \cdot V = \frac{B^2 \cdot V}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $B = B_r$ (residual flux density, typically 0.8 T for hardened steel), $V = A \cdot L$:

$$
E = \frac{B_r^{\,2} \cdot A \cdot L}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values** for a crankshaft with $A = 0.005 \text{ m}^2$, $L = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $B_r = 0.8 \text{ T}$:

$$
E = \frac{(0.8)^2 \times 0.005 \times 0.4}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
E_{num} = 0.64 \times 0.002 = 0.00128 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
E_{den} = 2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 2.513 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation:**

$$
E = \frac{0.00128}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 509.4 \text{ Joules}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering implication:** A small amount of energy (~ 500 J) is enough to retain the magnetization that attracts iron filings. This is why demagnetization is mandatory before assembly — otherwise the part will attract debris in service, causing wear and possible bearing failure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Particle Migration Force (Leakage Field Attraction)

The **magnetic force** pulling a particle toward a leakage field is given by:

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Force on a magnetic dipole** in a non-uniform field:

$$
\vec{F} = \nabla (\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B})
$$

where $\vec{m}$ is the magnetic moment of the particle.

**Step 2 — For an iron particle** in a weak leakage field $B_{leak}$:

$$
F \approx \frac{V_p \cdot \chi \cdot B_{leak}^{\,2}}{2 \mu_0}
$$

where $\chi$ = magnetic susceptibility, $V_p$ = particle volume.

**Step 3 — Simplification for MPI particles** (high-$\mu$ ferrite-coated, $\chi \to \infty$):

$$
F \propto B_{leak}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning:** Doubling the leakage flux density **quadruples** the pulling force. This is why **continuous magnetization (higher $B$)** gives sharper, faster indications than residual magnetization.

### Conclusion:
- **Continuous technique** → **Stronger** indication, faster inspection.
- **Residual technique** → **Weaker but adequate** indication for hard materials, safer for operator.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetizing current state during particle application | **ON** | **OFF** |
| Power source requirement | Lower (steady current) | Higher (high shot current) |
| Material suitability | Soft ferromagnetic (low retentivity) | Hard ferromagnetic (high retentivity) |
| Sensitivity | **Higher** (active field) | Slightly lower |
| Demagnetization need | **Mandatory after inspection** | **Mandatory** but easier if $B_r$ is low |
| Operator safety | Risk of arc burn (prods), electric shock | Safer (no live current) |
| Crack orientation detection | Transverse cracks (prods), Longitudinal (coil) | Transverse (direct), Longitudinal (induced) |
| Portability | Mobile yokes available | Generally shop/lab equipment |
| Particle method used | Wet or Dry | Usually **Wet** (residual holds wet pattern longer) |
| Indications visibility | Live, may shift with current changes | Stable, photographable |
| Cost | Lower equipment cost | Higher (magnetizing shot generator) |
| Inspection of hollow parts | Limited (coil needed) | **Best for hollow bores (central conductor + coil)** |
| ASTM reference standard | ASTM E709 | ASTM E709 |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A steel shaft of length **40 cm** and diameter **8 cm** is to be magnetized using the **induced (solenoid) method** for residual MPI. If the required magnetizing field is **3200 A/m**, calculate the number of turns required when the available current is **1200 A**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert length to metres:
$$L = 40 \text{ cm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the derived formula:
$$N = \frac{H \cdot L}{I}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$N = \frac{3200 \times 0.40}{1200} = \frac{1280}{1200} \approx 1.067$$

**Step 4:** Round up to the nearest practical integer:
$$N = 2 \text{ turns}$$

**Answer:** **2 turns** are required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overall Technique Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: MPI Inspection Required] --> B{Is material hard or soft ferromagnetic?}
    B -- SOFT Low retentivity --> C[Use CONTINUOUS Technique]
    B -- HARD High retentivity --> D{Is crack longitudinal or transverse?}
    D -- TRANSVERSE --> E[Use RESIDUAL Direct Shot Prods]
    D -- LONGITUDINAL --> F[Use RESIDUAL Induced Coil Shot]
    C --> G[Apply particles while current ON]
    E --> H[Magnetize then cut current]
    F --> H[Magnetize then cut current]
    G --> I[Inspect under UV-A or white light]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Record indications]
    J --> K[Demagnetize part]
    K --> L[END Inspection]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Continuous vs Residual Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant Eq as Magnetizer
    participant Part as Test Specimen
    participant MP as Magnetic Particles
    
    Note over Op,MP: CONTINUOUS TECHNIQUE TIMELINE
    Op->>Eq: Step 1 Switch ON magnetizing current
    Eq->>Part: Step 2 Magnetic field established
    Op->>MP: Step 3 Apply particles NOW
    MP->>Part: Step 4 Particles migrate to leakage fields
    Op->>Eq: Step 5 Switch OFF current
    Op->>Part: Step 6 Inspect under light
    
    Note over Op,MP: RESIDUAL TECHNIQUE TIMELINE
    Op->>Eq: Step 1 Apply magnetizing shot
    Eq->>Part: Step 2 Magnetic field established
    Op->>Eq: Step 3 Switch OFF current FIRST
    Part->>Part: Step 4 Residual flux density Br retained
    Op->>MP: Step 5 Apply particles
    MP->>Part: Step 6 Particles migrate using Br only
    Op->>Part: Step 7 Inspect under light
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Equipment Required

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Supply Unit]
        PSU[Variable AC/DC Source]
        CTRL[Current Control Panel]
    end
    
    subgraph MAG[Magnetization Head]
        Y[Yoke]
        PR[Prods]
        CL[Coil Solenoid]
        CC[Central Conductor]
    end
    
    subgraph PART[Test Specimen]
        SP[Ferromagnetic Component]
    end
    
    subgraph PART2[Particle Delivery System]
        WH[Wet Suspension Tank]
        DR[Dry Powder Bulb]
        UV[UV-A Lamp 365 nm]
    end
    
    PSU --> CTRL
    CTRL --> Y
    CTRL --> PR
    CTRL --> CL
    CTRL --> CC
    Y --> SP
    PR --> SP
    CL --> SP
    CC --> SP
    WH --> SP
    DR --> SP
    SP --> UV
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Hysteresis Loop Visualization (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin H=0 B=0] --> B[Increase H to Hmax]
    B --> C[Saturation point Bmax]
    C --> D[Reduce H to zero]
    D --> E[Residual flux density Br]
    E --> F[Apply reverse H to -Hc]
    F --> G[Br becomes zero at Hc]
    G --> H[Continue to negative saturation]
    H --> I[Return H to zero]
    I --> J[Negative residual flux]
    J --> K[Increase H back to Hc]
    K --> L[Full closed loop]
    L --> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU:**
> - The **Continuous technique** operates at points **B → C** (active magnetization, $H > 0$).
> - The **Residual technique** operates at point **E** (after $H = 0$, only $B_r$ remains).

---

## 4.5 ASCII Schematic — Prod Arrangement for Direct Residual Magnetization

```text
        |                    |

                 |

        |                 |
        |                 |

    | PROD A|         | PROD B|
    +---+---+         +---+---+
        |                 |
        +-------++--------+
                ||
        +-------++--------+
        |     PART        |
        |   (Specimen)    |

                ||
         (Magnetic flux
          flows A to B
          inside part)
```
