# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Residual Technique of Magnetic Particle Inspection. State one material where it is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Residual Technique of MPI is a method of magnetic particle inspection in which the **magnetizing current is switched OFF first**, and the **magnetic particles are applied afterwards**. The detection of discontinuities depends on the **residual flux density ($B_r$)** retained in the ferromagnetic part after the magnetizing force is removed.
>
> **Preferred material (1 mark):** It is **preferred for hardened ferromagnetic materials** (e.g., tool steel, springs, crankshafts, AISI 1095 steel) because they have **high retentivity and high coercivity**, retaining enough magnetism to attract particles even after the current is switched off.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the Continuous Technique not suitable for inspecting soft ferromagnetic materials using residual methods?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(1 mark)** Soft ferromagnetic materials have **low retentivity** and **low coercivity**.
>
> **(1 mark)** When the magnetizing current is switched off, the magnetic domains in soft materials quickly randomize, and the **residual flux density $B_r$ becomes very small** (often negligible).
>
> **(1 mark)** This weak residual field is insufficient to attract and hold magnetic particles at the leakage field of discontinuities, so **continuous magnetization (current kept ON)** is required to provide the active field needed to detect defects.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Marks are **deducted** if students confuse **"Residual Magnetism Method"** with **"Induced Magnetism Method"** — they are *sub-categories* of the residual technique, not separate.
> - Always mention the **role of $B_r$ (residual flux density)** and the **hysteresis loop** when answering residual technique questions.
> - For numericals, students often **forget to convert cm to m** — losing 1–2 marks easily.
> - Never write "magnetization" when you mean "magnetizing force $H$" — they are different quantities.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Continuous vs Residual Techniques**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Continuous Technique of Magnetic Particle Inspection** with a neat labelled diagram. List any **four advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Residual Technique of MPI**. Compare it with the Continuous Technique in a tabular form (any **six points**). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Continuous Technique

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
In the Continuous Technique, the magnetizing current is **maintained (kept ON)** while the magnetic particles are being applied to the test specimen. The inspection is performed with the magnetizing force still active.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Clean the surface to be inspected (free from oil, paint, rust).
2. Magnetize the part using prods, yoke, coil, or central conductor.
3. Apply magnetic particles (wet suspension or dry powder) on the surface.
4. Keep current ON while particles are applied (continuous for ~ 1–2 seconds).
5. View the surface under suitable illumination (UV-A 1000 µW/cm² for fluorescent; ≥ 1000 lux white light for visible particles).
6. Interpret the indications.
7. Demagnetize the part before returning to service.

**Diagram (1.5 marks):** (Use the prod arrangement ASCII or a hand-drawn equivalent showing source, prods, part, and particle application zone.)

**Advantages (1 mark, any 4):**
- Works on **soft ferromagnetic materials** (low retentivity).
- **Higher sensitivity** — live field can detect very fine, tight cracks.
- Suitable for **large rough forgings** (forging industry).
- **Wet method** allows particles to flow into tight cracks via capillary action.
- Real-time observation of indications.

**Limitations (1 mark, any 2):**
- Risk of **arc burns** at prod contact points.
- Cannot inspect **hollow bores** easily (live current creates heat).
- Higher **operator hazard** due to live current.

---

### Part (b) — Residual Technique

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
In the Residual Technique, the magnetizing current is **switched OFF first**, and the magnetic particles are **applied afterwards**. Detection depends entirely on the **retained magnetism ($B_r$)** in the part.

**Sub-categories (1 mark):**
1. **Residual Magnetism Method** — current passed directly through the part (using prods or "head-shot").
2. **Induced (Solenoid) Method** — current passed through a coil surrounding the part.

**Working (1.5 marks):**
After current is cut, the part retains a residual flux density $B_r$. For hardened steels, this $B_r$ is significant (0.8–1.4 T). Magnetic particles are then applied; they migrate to leakage fields around discontinuities and form visible indications.

**Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Continuous | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| Current during particle application | ON | OFF |
| Material suitability | Soft ferromagnetic | Hard ferromagnetic |
| Sensitivity | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Operator safety | Lower (live current) | Higher |
| Demagnetization need | Mandatory | Mandatory but $B_r$ low for hard steel |
| Indication stability | May shift when current removed | Stable after current removed |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Residual Technique with Numerical**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **Induced (Solenoid) Method** of residual magnetization. Derive the expression for the number of turns required in the magnetizing coil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft of length **50 cm** and diameter **10 cm** is to be magnetized by the **induced method**. If the required magnetizing field is **3600 A/m** and the magnetizing current available is **1500 A**, find the **number of turns** required. State whether this shaft is suitable for residual or continuous inspection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Induced (Solenoid) Method

**Sketch / Setup (2 marks):** The cylindrical part is placed **inside a coil** wound with heavy insulated copper cable. Current from an AC or DC source is passed through the coil, generating a longitudinal magnetic field inside the part (parallel to the part's axis). This longitudinal field detects **transverse cracks**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Using **Ampere's Circuital Law**:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I
$$

For a uniform field along length $L$:

$$
H \cdot L = N I
$$

Rearranging for **number of turns**:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{H \cdot L}{I}}
$$

Where:
- $H$ = required magnetizing field intensity (A/m)
- $L$ = length of the coil/part (m)
- $I$ = magnetizing current (A)

**Practical Note (1 mark):** Only a **few turns** of heavy cable are needed because the required $H$ (a few thousand A/m) is much smaller than typical $I$ values (hundreds to thousands of amperes).

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Given Data (1 mark):**
- $L = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- $D = 10 \text{ cm}$ (not needed for turn calculation)
- $H = 3600 \text{ A/m}$
- $I = 1500 \text{ A}$

**Step 2 — Apply the Formula (1 mark):**

$$
N = \frac{H \cdot L}{I} = \frac{3600 \times 0.50}{1500}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator (1 mark):**

$$
3600 \times 0.50 = 1800
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Final Result (1 mark):**

$$
N = \frac{1800}{1500} = 1.2 \approx 2 \text{ turns (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- The shaft is **steel** (ferromagnetic).
- For **residual MPI** by the **induced method**, the shaft must retain magnetism after current cutoff. This works well only if the steel is **hardened** (high retentivity).
- If the shaft is **hardened alloy steel (e.g., AISI 4140, EN24)**, it is **suitable for RESIDUAL inspection**.
- If the shaft is **mild steel (low retentivity)**, **CONTINUOUS inspection** is recommended.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Assuming the shaft is hardened steel, **2 turns** are required and the **Residual Technique (Induced/Solenoid Method)** is the most suitable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU OEMET724 – Module 2 | Continuous & Residual Techniques of MPI – Cheat Sheet**

- [ ] **Continuous Technique** = Current **ON** during particle application.
- [ ] **Residual Technique** = Current **OFF** before particle application.
- [ ] **Soft steel (low $B_r$)** → **Continuous** only.
- [ ] **Hard steel (high $B_r$)** → **Residual** preferred.
- [ ] **Residual Magnetism Method** = current passed **through the part** (prods/head-shot).
- [ ] **Induced (Solenoid) Method** = current passed **through a coil** around the part.
- [ ] **Hysteresis loop key points:** $B_r$ (remanence) and $H_c$ (coercivity).
- [ ] **Particle force equation:** $F \propto B_{leak}^{\,2}$ (square law — residual gives ~25–50% the force of continuous).
- [ ] **Solenoid turn formula:** $N = H \cdot L / I$.
- [ ] **Always demagnetize** after MPI to avoid iron-filing attraction in service.
- [ ] **Wet method** is preferred for both techniques when **high sensitivity** is needed.
- [ ] **ASTM E709** is the governing standard for MPI.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** *"Hard Holds, Soft Needs Help"* (Hard → Residual; Soft → Continuous).
- [ ] **Transverse cracks** detected by **longitudinal magnetization** (coil shot or head-shot).
- [ ] **Longitudinal cracks** detected by **transverse magnetization** (prod or central conductor).
- [ ] **Demagnetization energy:** $E = B_r^{\,2} V / (2 \mu_0)$.
- [ ] **Residual indications are stable** (good for photography); **continuous indications** may shift when current is removed.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Continuous and Residual Techniques of MPI**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET724 — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned*

topics.title: System Sensitivity and Checking Devices in MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Sensitivity and Checking Devices in MPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)**, **System Sensitivity** is defined as the *minimum size of discontinuity (defect) that the entire NDT system — magnetizing equipment, particles, lighting, demagnetizer, surface preparation, and operator skill — can reliably detect under standardized test conditions*. 

Mathematically and operationally, system sensitivity is the combined response of the *magnetic circuit* and the *inspection medium* to a flaw. It is not the same as the sensitivity of any single component, but a holistic performance parameter governed by the relevant **ASME Section V, ASTM E709, ASTM E1444, ISO 9934**, and **EN 1369** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEMET724 / Module 2):**
> System Sensitivity is the *operational benchmark* used to verify that an MPI set-up is performing correctly **before, during, and after** every inspection. Without periodic sensitivity checks, an MPI result is legally and technically invalid under KTU-referenced ASME/ASTM codes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are using a metal detector on a beach to find coins.

* If the detector is set to low sensitivity → it only finds large coins, missing small ones.
* If the sensitivity is too high → it beeps for every bottle cap, giving false positives.
* You also need clean sand (clean surface), proper coil orientation (magnetization direction), and good lighting (UV-A for fluorescent MPI) to actually *see* what you found.

The **metal detector's setting** = magnetizing current strength.
The **coins of different sizes** = defects of varying sizes.
The **clean sand and lighting** = surface preparation and inspection medium.
The **overall ability to find and confirm a coin** = **System Sensitivity**.

In MPI, we cannot simply turn a "sensitivity knob." Instead, we use **sensitivity test blocks, test pieces, and field indicators** to *prove* the system can detect a discontinuity of known, certified size. This proof is the **sensitivity check**.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value |
|---|---|
| Minimum detectable artificial defect width | **0.25 mm to 0.5 mm** (for high sensitivity) |
| Tangential field strength requirement (ASTM E709) | **30 to 60 A/m** at the surface |
| Fluorescent UV-A irradiance | **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** at 380–400 nm peak |
| White light intensity (visible MPI) | **≥ 1000 lux** at the inspection surface |
| Magnetizing current adequacy check | **Hi-lo field indicator** (≥ 30 A/m, ≤ 120 A/m) |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 OEMET724 syllabus uses these metrics directly in the **Cognitive Levels L1 (Remember) and L2 (Understand)** of Module 2 outcomes. Numerical values like **30 A/m** and **1000 µW/cm²** appear as 3-mark Part A questions in KTU university exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Governing MPI System Sensitivity (Step-by-Step Logic)

The sensitivity of the MPI system is the *product* of the following independent factors. If **any one** factor fails, the system sensitivity collapses. The order below reflects the KTU Module 2 critical-path:

1. **Magnetizing Force Adequacy**
   * The applied magnetic field $H$ must be strong enough to drive the flux out of the surface and into the air at the discontinuity, producing a **leakage field** $H_L$ that exceeds the **coercive force** of the magnetic particles.
   * Inadequate $H$ → no leakage flux → no particle accumulation → **false negative**.

2. **Field Direction Relative to the Discontinuity**
   * The leakage field forms only when the discontinuity plane is **perpendicular** (within ±15°) to the local magnetic flux lines.
   * If the field is parallel to the crack, the crack is **invisible** to MPI.
   * Hence, two orthogonal magnetizations (circular + longitudinal) are mandatory.

3. **Particle Characteristics**
   * Particle **size** (typically 1–10 µm for fluorescent, 10–100 µm for visible).
   * **Coercivity** $H_c$ of the particles must be less than the leakage field strength.
   * **Magnetization state** of wet vs. dry particles — fluorescent wet suspensions have the highest sensitivity.

4. **Surface Condition**
   * Paint, oil, scale, rust, and coatings reduce particle mobility and contact.
   * Surface roughness Ra should be ≤ **25 µm** for high-sensitivity inspection.

5. **Demagnetization State (Residual Magnetism)**
   * Residual field $B_r$ must be < **3 Gauss (0.3 mT)** after inspection to avoid post-service interference.
   * Improper demagnetization causes service damage in bearings and rotating components.

6. **Lighting Conditions**
   * Visible MPI: white light ≥ **1000 lux**.
   * Fluorescent MPI: UV-A irradiance ≥ **1000 µW/cm²** at the part.

7. **Operator Skill and Viewing Conditions**
   * Pre-cleaning, particle application timing, washing, and interpretation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe symbol `|` is rendered as `\vert` below to keep the markdown table valid.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Physical Meaning | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{t} = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{2 \pi r}$ | Tangential field strength at the surface of a cylindrical part for **circular magnetization** (head-shot/prod) | ASTM E709 |
| 2 | $H_{t} = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | Longitudinal field for **solenoid magnetization** | ASTM E709 |
| 3 | $H_{t} = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{D}$ | Longitudinal field for **encircling coil** (approx. for short solenoids) | ASTM E709 |
| 4 | $B = \mu_0 (H + M)$ | Flux density, where $M$ is magnetization | Fundamental |
| 5 | $H_{L} \propto \dfrac{B \cdot d}{r}$ | Leakage field $\propto$ flux density, defect depth $d$, $\propto 1/r$ (defect sharpness) | Maxwell's equations |
| 6 | $H_{c,\text{particle}} < H_{L}$ | Particle coercivity must be less than leakage field for attraction | ASTM E1444 |
| 7 | $B_{r,\text{residual}} \le 3 \text{ Gauss} = 0.3 \text{ mT}$ | Post-inspection residual field limit | ASTM E709 |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = f(H,\theta,\phi_{\text{particle}},\text{Light},\text{Operator})$ | Holistic dependency | System parameter |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace (DGQA / AS9100 audits)**: Mandatory sensitivity check on each shift using a **Berthold test bar** or **pie gage**. Without it, an aircraft component is grounded.
* **Nuclear (ASME Section XI)**: In-service inspection of reactor coolant pump shafts requires **Ketos test rings** for calibrated sensitivity.
* **Defence (Gun barrels, missile casings)**: Sensitivity verification using **MTU-2 or IOW test blocks**.
* **Power Generation (Steam turbine rotor discs)**: Atlantic test rings for residual magnetism verification before re-coating.
* **Automotive crankshafts and railway axles**: PIRT-based sensitivity check at every shift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **KTU 2024 OEMET724 Part B (14-mark) questions**, examiners frequently ask: *"Discuss the system sensitivity in MPI and explain any two sensitivity test devices with neat diagrams."* (7 marks each sub-part).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tangential Field Strength for Circular Magnetization

**Background:** A current-carrying conductor passing through a cylindrical ferromagnetic part produces a circumferential (circular) magnetic field around the current path. This is the basis of *head-shot* and *prod* magnetization in MPI.

**Step 1 — Ampere's Law**

We start from Ampere's circuital law, which is one of Maxwell's four equations:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enclosed}}
$$

**Step 2 — Symmetry Argument**

For a long cylindrical part of radius $r$ with a central axial current $I$, the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is purely tangential and uniform on a circular path of radius $r$ around the conductor. Therefore $H_t$ is constant along the contour, and the dot product reduces to:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H_t \cdot (2 \pi r)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve**

Substituting into Ampere's law:

$$
H_t \cdot (2 \pi r) = N \cdot I
$$

where $N$ is the number of turns (for a single conductor, $N=1$; for an encircling head-shot coil, $N$ may be the effective conductor count). Solving for $H_t$:

$$
H_t = \frac{N \cdot I}{2 \pi r}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU standard example)**

> **Example:** A shaft of diameter $d = 100$ mm ($r = 0.05$ m) is magnetized by passing a current of $I = 800$ A through it (single conductor, $N=1$). Find the tangential field strength at the surface.

$$
H_t = \frac{(1) \cdot 800}{2 \pi \cdot 0.05} = \frac{800}{0.3142} \approx 2548 \text{ A/m}
$$

This is well above the **ASTM E709 minimum of 30 A/m**, confirming adequate magnetization.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme frequently includes this as a numerical sub-part. Students must convert **mm → m** before substituting to avoid dimensional error. Common pitfall: forgetting the factor of 2π.

### 3.2 Derivation of Encircling-Coil Longitudinal Field

**Step 1 — Setup**

For a solenoid of $N$ turns, length $L$ (in meters), carrying current $I$:

$$
H_{\text{solenoid}} = \frac{N \cdot I}{L} \quad \text{[A/m]}
$$

**Step 2 — Short-Coil Correction (Boeré Equation)**

For a short coil where the length is comparable to the diameter $D$, the field at the centre is approximated by the *Boeré* formula:

$$
H_t = \frac{N \cdot I}{\sqrt{L^2 + D^2}} \quad \text{[A/m]}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Use**

This is used when a long shaft is magnetized longitudinally by passing it through an encircling coil. KTU Module 2 emphasises that the **ASTM E709 minimum field of 30 A/m** at the surface must be achieved for *every* point on the part.

### 3.3 Derivation of Leakage Field Approximation

For a surface-breaking crack of depth $d$ and width $w$ in a magnetized ferromagnetic medium of flux density $B$:

$$
H_L \approx \frac{2 \cdot B \cdot d}{\pi \cdot w} \quad \text{(for a sharp slot, idealised)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Smaller defect width $w$ and larger depth $d$ both increase $H_L$. This is why **tight, deep cracks** (e.g. fatigue cracks) are easier to detect than **wide, shallow laps** in MPI.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensitivity Test Devices

| Device | Standard | Primary Use | What It Detects | Sensitivity Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Berthold Test Bar** | DIN 54130 | Calibration of yokes and bench units | Surface defects on small parts | Detects 0.25 mm wide slots |
| **Ketos Test Ring** | ASTM E709 Annex | Calibration of prod systems on large shafts | Circular magnetization adequacy | 3 artificial slots of graded depth |
| **IOW Test Block** | ASME / NAVSHIPS | Sensitivity check on production floor | Combined longitudinal + circular | Multiple geometric flaws |
| **Atlantic Test Ring** | ASTM E709 | Calibration of magnetizing current on rings | Residual magnetism measurement | 3, 5, 10 mil slot depths |
| **Pie Gage / Quantimeter** | ASTM E1444 | Direct field strength measurement at surface | Tangential $H$ in A/m | 0–1000 A/m range |
| **PIRT (Portable Indicator of Residual magnetism Test)** | ASTM E709 | Post-demagnetization check | Residual flux $B_r$ in Gauss | 0–20 Gauss |
| **Hi-Lo Field Indicator** | ASTM E1444 | Both minimum and maximum field check | Field strength bounds | 30 A/m and 120 A/m markers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common 2-mark deduction occurs when students confuse **PIRT** (measures residual $B$ in Gauss) with **Pie Gage** (measures tangential $H$ in A/m). They are *not interchangeable*. The Pie gage is used **during** magnetization; the PIRT is used **after** demagnetization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MPI Sensitivity Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Initiates MPI Setup] --> B[Pre-Inspection System Sensitivity Check]
    B --> C{Select Test Device}
    C -->|Current Adequacy| D[Ketos Test Ring]
    C -->|Field Strength| E[Pie Gage or Hi-Lo Indicator]
    C -->|Residual Check| F[PIRT Meter]
    C -->|Material Calibration| G[Berthold or IOW Test Block]
    D --> H{Field Within ASTM E709 Bounds}
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[Proceed to Inspection]
    H -->|No| J[Adjust Current or Demagnetizer]
    J --> C
    I --> K[Apply Particles Wet/Dry]
    K --> L[Inspect Under UV or White Light]
    L --> M[Mark Defects]
    M --> N[Demagnetize Part]
    N --> O[Post-Inspection PIRT Check]
    O --> P{B_r ≤ 3 Gauss}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Accept and Sign Off]
    P -->|No| R[Redo Demagnetization]
    R --> O
```

### 4.2 Sensitivity Test Device Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MPI Sensitivity Checking Devices] --> B[Field Strength Indicators]
    A --> C[Test Pieces with Artificial Flaws]
    A --> D[Residual Field Indicators]
    B --> B1[Pie Gage]
    B --> B2[Quantimeter]
    B --> B3[Hi-Lo Field Indicator]
    C --> C1[Berthold Test Bar]
    C --> C2[Ketos Test Ring]
    C --> C3[IOW Test Block]
    C --> C4[Atlantic Test Ring]
    D --> D1[PIRT Meter]
    D --> D2[Compass Residual Probe]
```

### 4.3 Functional Diagram — Berthold Test Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Berthold Test Bar Schematic] --> Info1[Two Steel Plates Joined Mechanically]
    Info1 --> Info2[Top Plate Contains Three Artificial Slots]
    Info2 --> Slot1[Slot 1: 0.25 mm wide x 5 mm deep]
    Info2 --> Slot2[Slot 2: 0.50 mm wide x 5 mm deep]
    Info2 --> Slot3[Slot 3: 1.00 mm wide x 5 mm deep]
    Start --> Use[Used Between Yoke Poles]
    Use --> Step1[Place Bar Across Yoke Poles]
    Step1 --> Step2[Apply Magnetic Particles]
    Step2 --> Step3[All Three Slots Must Show Indications]
    Step3 --> Pass[System Sensitivity ACCEPTED]
    Step3 --> Fail[If Slot 3 Fails: Increase Current or Realign Yoke]
```

### 4.4 Functional Diagram — Ketos Test Ring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ketos Test Ring] --> B[Bronze Ring with 12 Holes]
    B --> C[Center Hole for Conductor]
    C --> D[3 Artificial Slots Drilled in Outer Surface]
    D --> D1[Slot A: 1.0 mm deep]
    D --> D2[Slot B: 2.0 mm deep]
    D --> D3[Slot C: 3.0 mm deep]
    A --> E[Placed on Test Shaft or Bench]
    E --> F[Pass Current Through Center Conductor]
    F --> G{All Three Slots Produce Indication?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Sensitivity CONFIRMED]
    G -->|No| I[Increase Current to Minimum for Slot A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** The above Mermaid blocks are *functional architecture diagrams* representing the operational logic of each sensitivity device. They replace the physical engineering sketches of the Berthold, Ketos, and PIRT devices, which are difficult to render in plain Mermaid. The student should refer to **ASTM E709 Annex A1–A4** and **ASTM E1444** for the actual engineering drawings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMET724]**

**Q1. Define system sensitivity in Magnetic Particle Inspection. List any four factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

System sensitivity in MPI is defined as the *minimum size of a discontinuity that the complete magnetic particle testing system can detect reliably under specified test conditions*. It is a holistic parameter that depends on:

1. Adequacy of the magnetizing force (magnitude and direction).
2. Type, size, and magnetic properties of the particles used.
3. Surface condition and preparation of the part.
4. Intensity and type of lighting (UV-A or white light).

*(Each point ~0.5 mark, definition 1 mark = 3 marks total)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMET724]**

**Q2. What is a Pie gage? State its application in MPI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Pie gage (also called a Quantimeter) is a precision instrument used in MPI to measure the **tangential magnetic field strength** $H_t$ at the surface of a magnetized part, expressed in **A/m** (or Oersted in CGS). It contains a coil wound on a calibrated former; when placed on the surface, the induced EMF is proportional to the field strength. **Application:** It is used during the *system sensitivity check* to verify that the magnetizing current produces a field of at least **30 A/m** (ASTM E709) and not more than **120 A/m** at the inspection surface, ensuring the field is neither too weak nor causing magnetic saturation or particle piling.

*(Definition 1.5 marks + Application 1.5 marks = 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / OEMET724 Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B**

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the concept of system sensitivity in MPI. Explain the factors affecting system sensitivity with reference to ASTM E709 standards. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** With a neat schematic, describe the **Berthold Test Bar** and the **Ketos Test Ring** as sensitivity test devices. Compare their applications. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — System Sensitivity in MPI (7 marks)**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** System sensitivity is the minimum size of discontinuity that the entire MPI system — equipment, particles, lighting, operator — can detect under standard conditions. It is governed by **ASTM E709** and **ASTM E1444**.

2. **Factor 1 – Magnetizing Force Adequacy (1.5 marks):** The tangential field $H_t$ at the surface must satisfy $30 \le H_t \le 120$ A/m. For a circularly magnetized cylinder:
$$
H_t = \frac{N I}{2 \pi r}
$$
Below 30 A/m, leakage fields are too weak; above 120 A/m, particles pile non-specifically (false indications).

3. **Factor 2 – Field Direction (1 mark):** Discontinuity must be perpendicular (within ±15°) to the local flux. Two orthogonal magnetizations are required.

4. **Factor 3 – Particle Quality (1 mark):** Wet fluorescent particles with coercivity $< H_L$ of leakage fields give the highest sensitivity. Particle size 1–10 µm for fluorescent; 10–100 µm for visible.

5. **Factor 4 – Surface Condition & Lighting (1.5 marks):** Surface Ra ≤ 25 µm. UV-A irradiance ≥ 1000 µW/cm² for fluorescent. White light ≥ 1000 lux for visible.

6. **Factor 5 – Demagnetization (1 mark):** Post-inspection residual flux $B_r \le 3$ Gauss (0.3 mT), verified using PIRT.

---

**Part (b) — Berthold Test Bar & Ketos Test Ring (7 marks)**

**Berthold Test Bar (3.5 marks):**
* Constructed of two steel plates clamped together.
* Top plate has three artificially EDM-machined slots of widths **0.25 mm, 0.50 mm, 1.00 mm**, all of depth 5 mm.
* Placed across the poles of a **magnetizing yoke**.
* Apply particles; **all three slots must produce visible indications** to confirm system sensitivity.
* Used for small ferromagnetic parts in aerospace and automotive.

**Ketos Test Ring (3.5 marks):**
* Bronze ring with **12 peripheral holes** for mounting on a test shaft.
* **Three artificial slots** machined in the outer circumference, depths 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 mm.
* A central hole accepts a current-carrying conductor for **circular magnetization**.
* Verifies both *current adequacy* and *circular field uniformity* in prod/head-shot systems.
* Used on large shafts in power and aerospace industry per ASTM E709 Annex.

| Comparison Aspect | Berthold Test Bar | Ketos Test Ring |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetization type | Longitudinal (yoke) | Circular (prod/head-shot) |
| Sensitivity marker | Three slot widths | Three slot depths |
| Part size | Small components | Large shafts |
| Standard | DIN 54130 | ASTM E709 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle and construction of the **PIRT (Portable Indicator of Residual magnetism Test)** device. Why is it important after MPI? *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A shaft of diameter **80 mm** is magnetized using a head-shot current of **600 A** through a single axial conductor. Calculate the tangential field strength at the surface and comment on whether the magnetization is adequate as per ASTM E709. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PIRT Device (7 marks)**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** PIRT is a portable, hand-held instrument used to measure the **residual magnetic flux density** $B_r$ in a ferromagnetic part **after** demagnetization, in units of **Gauss** (or milliTesla).

2. **Construction (2 marks):** Contains a Hall-effect sensor or a moving-coil probe inside a calibrated housing with a digital/analog display. Switchable ranges (0–5 G, 0–20 G typical). A push-button zero-adjust.

3. **Working Principle (2 marks):** When the probe tip is placed on a magnetized surface, the **Hall voltage** $V_H = R_H \cdot I_x \cdot B / t$ is generated, where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I_x$ is the bias current, $B$ is the normal component of residual flux, and $t$ is the sensor thickness. The instrument converts this voltage to Gauss on the display.

4. **Importance After MPI (2 marks):**
   * Prevents *magnetic lock-up* of bearings and rotating assemblies.
   * Prevents attraction of ferrous debris in fuel systems and hydraulic lines.
   * Required by ASTM E709 and ASME V to certify residual flux $B_r \le 3$ Gauss.
   * Mandatory for aircraft, defence, and nuclear components.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
* Diameter $d = 80$ mm → radius $r = 0.040$ m
* Current $I = 600$ A
* Number of conductors $N = 1$ (single axial conductor)

**Step 1 — Write the formula (1 mark):**
$$
H_t = \frac{N I}{2 \pi r}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark):**
$$
H_t = \frac{1 \times 600}{2 \pi \times 0.040}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator (1 mark):**
$$
2 \pi \times 0.040 = 0.2513 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute final $H_t$ (2 marks):**
$$
H_t = \frac{600}{0.2513} \approx 2387 \text{ A/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on adequacy (2 marks):**
The ASTM E709 minimum required field is **30 A/m** and the upper safe limit is **120 A/m**. The computed $H_t = 2387$ A/m is **20× the minimum** but **≈ 20× the upper safe limit**. Therefore, the magnetization is **grossly excessive**, and the part must be **re-magnetized at a lower current** to avoid particle piling, false indications, and possible magnetic saturation of the part. A safer current would be:
$$
I_{\text{recommended}} = \frac{30 \times 2 \pi \times 0.040}{1} \approx 7.5 \text{ A (minimum)}
$$
$$
I_{\text{maximum}} = \frac{120 \times 2 \pi \times 0.040}{1} \approx 30.2 \text{ A}
$$
Hence, an acceptable current range is **7.5 A to 30.2 A**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 cause of lost marks.** A radius of 80 mm must be converted to **0.040 m** before substitution. Students who use 80 directly in the denominator lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $H_t$ (A/m) with $B$ (Tesla/Gauss).** $H_t$ is field strength; $B = \mu_0 (H + M)$ is flux density. The ASTM E709 limit of 30 A/m is on $H_t$, not on $B$.
> 3. **Confusing Pie gage (A/m, during magnetization) with PIRT (Gauss, after demagnetization).** Many students write PIRT when the question asks for a *during-magnetization* sensitivity check.
> 4. **Forgetting the upper limit of 120 A/m.** A common omission is to check only the lower 30 A/m bound. Excess field causes *particle piling* and *burning*, both of which are KTU-recognised defects in the marking scheme.
> 5. **In Part B diagram questions**, examiners expect the test device's *functional operation* (what slots, where placed, what current) — not just a labelled box. Diagrams without functional annotation lose 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **System Sensitivity** = overall ability of the complete MPI set-up to detect a minimum-size defect, governed by ASTM E709 / E1444.
* **ASTM E709 required tangential field**: **30 A/m ≤ $H_t$ ≤ 120 A/m** at the inspection surface.
* **Formula for circular magnetization**: $H_t = N I / (2 \pi r)$.
* **Formula for solenoid**: $H_t = N I / L$. **Boeré's short-coil correction**: $H_t = N I / \sqrt{L^2 + D^2}$.
* **Leakage field** $\propto$ flux density $B$, defect depth $d$, and $\propto 1/w$ (defect width).
* **Test pieces (artificial flaws):** Berthold Test Bar, Ketos Test Ring, IOW Test Block, Atlantic Test Ring.
* **Field indicators:** Pie Gage (Quantimeter), Hi-Lo Field Indicator.
* **Residual field indicator:** **PIRT** — must read $B_r \le 3$ Gauss (= **0.3 mT**) after demagnetization.
* **Lighting:** White light $\ge 1000$ lux; UV-A $\ge 1000$ µW/cm² at 365 nm.
* **Surface preparation:** Clean, dry, Ra $\le 25$ µm; coatings removed.
* **Defect orientation:** Must be **perpendicular** (within ±15°) to flux lines; hence two orthogonal magnetizations.
* **Wet fluorescent** particles give the highest sensitivity; **dry particles** for field/site use.
* **Coercivity rule:** $H_{c,\text{particle}} < H_L$ (leakage field) for particle attraction.
* **Standard demagnetization:** Either thermal above Curie point (770°C for Fe) or AC coil with decaying amplitude.
* **Sensitivity check is mandatory** before, during (every shift), and after MPI per ASME V Article 7 and ASTM E1444.
* **KTU 2024 Module 2 expected outcomes** map this topic to **CO2** with Bloom's levels **L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse)**. Numerical problems on $H_t = N I / 2\pi r$ are a guaranteed 7-mark Part B sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eddy Current Testing: Physical Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Eddy Current Testing: Physical Aspects # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** is a non-contact electromagnetic NDT method that uses the principles of **electromagnetic induction** to inspect the surface and sub-surface integrity of electrically conductive materials. When an alternating current (AC) carrying coil (probe) is brought near a conductive test piece, **time-varying magnetic flux lines** cut through the test material, inducing circulating electrical currents — known as **eddy currents (Foucault currents)** — in closed loops within the test piece. Any discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion, corrosion) or material property variation alters the magnitude and distribution of these eddy currents, which in turn modifies the **impedance of the exciting coil**, enabling defect detection and material characterization. **Standard Test Parameters (KTU 2024 Scheme):** - **Frequency Range:** Typically **10 Hz to 10 MHz** (most common industrial range: 100 kHz – 5 MHz) - **Standard Depth of Penetration (δ):** Defined as the depth at which the eddy current density falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value. - **Magnetic Permeability of free space (μ₀):** **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** - **Conductivity (σ):** Expressed in **% IACS** (International Annealed Copper Standard), where pure copper = **100 % IACS ≈ 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Whirlpool in a Bathtub":** > Imagine you are stirring water in a bathtub with your hand. The hand acts as the **exciting coil**, and the swirling water vortex is the **eddy current**. Now, drop a stone (defect) into the water — the whirlpool pattern changes instantly, and the force on your hand feels different. The change in force on your hand is exactly analogous to the change in **impedance** measured at the probe coil in ECT. > > **A second analogy — "The Transformer Model":** > The probe coil and the test piece behave like a **two-winding transformer**: the probe is the primary (energized by AC) and the test piece is a single-turn short-circuited secondary. The "load" on the primary (impedance) changes as the secondary current (eddy current) is disturbed by defects. --- ## 1.3 Physical Foundation – The Three Governing Laws | Law | Statement | Role in ECT | |------|-----------|-------------| | **Faraday's Law** | Induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux | Generates eddy currents in the test piece | | **Lenz's Law** | Induced current opposes the cause producing it | Determines direction of eddy current flow | | **Skin Effect (Larmor)** | AC current density decreases exponentially with depth | Limits inspection depth, sets frequency choice | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Favourite Line:** *"ECT is governed by three inter-dependent variables — frequency (f), permeability (μ), and conductivity (σ)."* Memorize this trio — it is the key to nearly every theory question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Eddy Current Generation — Step-by-Step 1. **Excitation:** A high-frequency AC source drives current through the probe coil, producing a **primary alternating magnetic field (H_p)**. 2. **Coupling:** When the probe approaches a conductive test piece, the time-varying magnetic flux links with the test piece. 3. **Induction:** By **Faraday's law**, an EMF is induced in the test piece. 4. **Circulation:** Because the test piece is a closed conducting path, the EMF drives **eddy currents (I_e)** in closed loops, whose plane is perpendicular to the flux lines. 5. **Reaction Field:** The eddy currents produce their own **secondary magnetic field (H_s)**, which by Lenz's law **opposes** the primary field. 6. **Net Field:** The resultant field in the probe region is **H_net = H_p − H_s**. 7. **Impedance Change:** This net field alters the probe coil's self-inductance and hence its **impedance (Z)**. A defect disrupts the eddy current path → changes H_s → changes Z. 8. **Signal Output:** The impedance change is converted to a voltage signal, displayed on a **cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO)** or impedance plane plotter. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Every formula here is **derivable and examinable**. | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law of Induction | V (Volts) | | 2 | $\Phi = B \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta$ | Magnetic flux linkage | Wb (Weber) | | 3 | $B = \mu H$ | Flux density relation | T (Tesla) | | 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | **Standard Depth of Penetration** | m (metres) | | 5 | $J(x) = J_{0}\, e^{-x/\delta}$ | Eddy current density at depth *x* | A/m² | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{x}{\delta}$ | Normalized depth (dimensionless) | — | | 7 | $f_{std} = \dfrac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^{2}}$ | Frequency for required penetration δ | Hz | | 8 | $Z = \sqrt{R^{2} + X_{L}^{2}}$ | Coil impedance | Ω (Ohms) | | 9 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{X_{L}}{R}$ | Phase angle of coil | radians / degrees | | 10 | $X_{L} = 2\pi f L$ | Inductive reactance | Ω | | 11 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Conductivity–resistivity relation | S/m | | 12 | $\mu = \mu_{0} \mu_{r}$ | Absolute permeability | H/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary & Approximation Notes:** > - For **non-ferrous materials** (Al, Cu, Brass): $\mu \approx \mu_{0}$ (since $\mu_{r} \approx 1$). > - For **ferromagnetic materials** (Steel, Iron): $\mu$ is **non-linear and field-dependent** → makes analysis complex. > - Standard depth = **37 %** of surface value; effective depth for practical inspection is often taken at **2.6 δ** (≈ 7 %). --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility ECT is used in industry for: - **Surface and sub-surface crack detection** in aircraft fuselage, turbine blades, and heat exchanger tubes. - **Material sorting** (alloy identification) based on conductivity mapping. - **Coating thickness measurement** (using lift-off effect on non-conductive coatings over conductive substrate). - **Weld inspection** in stainless steel pipes and nuclear fuel rods. - **Heat damage detection** in ferromagnetic components (via permeability changes). > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** ECT requires **no couplant** (unlike UT), can be performed at high temperatures, and is excellent for tubular inspections using **bobbin probes** — making it the preferred NDT for in-service inspection of **condenser and boiler tubes** in thermal power plants. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Standard Depth of Penetration (δ) **Starting Premise (Maxwell's Equation in 1-D):** For a semi-infinite conductor with a sinusoidal primary field applied at the surface (x = 0), the magnetic field intensity must satisfy the **Helmholtz diffusion equation**: $$ \frac{\partial^{2} H}{\partial x^{2}} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial H}{\partial t} $$ **Step 1 — Convert to Phasor Form (Time-Harmonic Excitation):** Let $H(x,t) = \text{Re}\bigl[\tilde{H}(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\bigr]$. Substituting, $\partial / \partial t$ becomes $j\omega$: $$ \frac{d^{2} \tilde{H}}{d x^{2}} = j\omega \mu \sigma\, \tilde{H} $$ **Step 2 — Rewrite the Complex Coefficient:** $$ j\omega\mu\sigma = j(2\pi f)\mu\sigma $$ Since $j = e^{j\pi/2} = \cos(\pi/2) + j\sin(\pi/2)$, the square root operator gives: $$ \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma}\, e^{j\pi/4} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Define the Propagation Constant:** $$ \gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \frac{1+j}{\delta} $$ where the **standard depth of penetration** is defined as: $$ \boxed{\;\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Solve the ODE:** The general solution of the second-order linear ODE is: $$ \tilde{H}(x) = A\, e^{-\gamma x} + B\, e^{+\gamma x} $$ For a **semi-infinite conductor** (field must vanish as $x \to \infty$), we set $B = 0$: $$ \tilde{H}(x) = A\, e^{-x/\delta}\, e^{-j x/\delta} $$ **Step 5 — Recover Real-Time Expression:** The physical field is the real part: $$ H(x,t) = H_{0}\, e^{-x/\delta}\, \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{x}{\delta}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Interpret the Result — Two Physical Effects:** | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | $e^{-x/\delta}$ | **Amplitude decay** → eddy current density (and field) decrease exponentially with depth. | | $\cos(\omega t - x/\delta)$ | **Phase lag** → the field at depth *x* lags the surface field by angle $x/\delta$ radians. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** At depth $x = \delta$, the amplitude drops to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of the surface value. At $x = 2.6\delta$, it falls to $\approx 7\%$ — the practical limit of detection. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency Required for a Given Penetration Starting from the standard depth relation: $$ \delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} $$ Square both sides: $$ \delta^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma} $$ Rearrange to isolate **f**: $$ \boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^{2}}\;} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** To inspect a deep defect, use **low frequency**; to inspect a surface defect, use **high frequency**. This is the basis of **multi-frequency ECT** used in modern aerospace NDT. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Impedance Phase Angle For a probe coil of resistance *R* and inductance *L* in AC excitation: $$ V(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t) $$ The current response is: $$ I(t) = I_{0}\sin(\omega t - \phi) $$ The complex impedance is: $$ Z = R + jX_{L} = R + j(2\pi f L) $$ Magnitude: $$ \vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^{2} + (2\pi f L)^{2}} $$ Phase angle: $$ \boxed{\;\phi \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{2\pi f L}{R}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_{L}}{R}\right)\;} $$ **Why this matters in ECT:** A defect changes both *L* and *R* of the coil, causing $\phi$ to shift. By plotting the impedance vector tip on a **2-D impedance plane diagram** (X-axis = R, Y-axis = X_L), defects produce **characteristic loops** unique to their type. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Eddy Current Generation | Factor | Symbol | Effect on Eddy Current | Effect on Depth δ | |--------|--------|------------------------|---------------------| | Test Frequency | $f \uparrow$ | Current tends to flow near surface | $\delta \downarrow$ (shallower) | | Electrical Conductivity | $\sigma \uparrow$ | Stronger induced currents | $\delta \downarrow$ (shallower) | | Magnetic Permeability | $\mu \uparrow$ | Stronger field coupling | $\delta \downarrow$ (shallower) | | Coil-to-Surface Gap (Lift-off) | $d \uparrow$ | Weaker coupling, lower $I_e$ | Apparent shift only | | Defect Geometry | — | Disturbs local current path | Local disturbance | | Material Temperature | $T \uparrow$ | $\sigma$ changes (metal-specific) | $\delta$ changes | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Mnemonic: "F-C-μ" → Frequency, Conductivity, Permeability.** All three are in the denominator-root of the δ equation; increasing any one **decreases** δ. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Eddy Current Testing System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oscillator
10 Hz to 10 MHz AC Source] --> B[Probe Coil
Primary Winding] B -->|Primary Magnetic Field Hp| C[Test Piece
Conductive Material] C -->|Induced Eddy Currents Ie| D[Reaction Field Hs
Opposes Hp] D --> E[Impedance Change
Delta Z in Probe] E --> F[Signal Conditioner
Amplifier and Filter] F --> G[Display Unit
CRO or Impedance Plane] G --> H[Operator Interpretation
Defect Signal Output] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Physical Generation of Eddy Currents ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: AC Current Flows in Probe Coil] --> P2[Step 2: Alternating Magnetic Field Hp is Generated] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Test Piece Placed in Proximity] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Time Varying Flux Cuts the Conductor] P4 --> P5[Step 5: EMF Induced by Faraday's Law] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Eddy Currents Ie Flow in Closed Loops] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Secondary Magnetic Field Hs Created] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Hs Opposes Hp by Lenz's Law] P8 --> P9[Step 9: Net Field Alters Coil Impedance] P9 --> P10[Step 10: Impedance Change is Measured and Displayed] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Coupling Between Probe and Test Piece ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ProbeCircuit[Probe Side Primary] AC[AC Excitation Source] --> CPC[Probe Coil Lp and Rp] end subgraph TestPieceSide[Test Piece Side Secondary] EC[Eddy Current Loops
Modelled as Ls and Rs] end CPC -->|Mutual Inductance M| EC EC -->|Reflected Impedance Zr| CPC CPC --> OUT[Impedance Measurement Unit] style ProbeCircuit fill:#E0F2FE style TestPieceSide fill:#FEF3C7 ``` ## 4.4 Impedance Plane Diagram Concept (Locus Plot) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Balanced Coil
No Defect] -->|Lift-off Trajectory| B[Lift-off Curve
Arc Shifting Left] A -->|Defect Trajectory| C[Defect Loop
Curved Locus] A -->|Edge Effect Trajectory| D[Edge Curve
Shifted Pattern] A -->|Conductivity Change| E[Conductivity Arc] style A fill:#D1FAE5 style B fill:#FECACA style C fill:#FED7AA style D fill:#FBCFE8 style E fill:#DDD6FE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Figure 4.1 Explanation:** The impedance plane diagram plots the coil's resistive (R) and reactive (X_L) components. With no defect, the coil sits at a balance point. As the probe moves over a defect, the impedance tip traces a **characteristic loop** (called a "defect signature") whose **shape, size, and orientation** identify the type and severity of the discontinuity. This is the graphical heart of modern ECT. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define Eddy Current Testing. List any two advantages of ECT over other NDT methods.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Eddy Current Testing is a non-destructive testing technique based on the principles of **electromagnetic induction**, in which an alternating current carrying coil induces circulating electrical currents (called **eddy currents** or **Foucault currents**) in a nearby conductive test piece. Defects or material variations alter the flow of these currents, producing measurable changes in the **impedance of the probe coil**, which are used to characterize the test piece. **Advantages (1 mark — any two):** 1. **No couplant required** — works at a stand-off distance; suitable for hot, rough, or coated surfaces. 2. **Fast and sensitive to surface defects** — detects cracks as small as 0.5 mm on metal surfaces. 3. **Can be used to measure coating thickness, conductivity, and permeability** — multi-parameter inspection. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is meant by "skin effect" in the context of Eddy Current Testing? State the standard depth of penetration formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Skin Effect (2 marks):** The **skin effect** is the phenomenon by which alternating current (and the associated magnetic field) in a conductor does not distribute uniformly across its cross-section but is **confined to a thin layer near the surface**, with the current density decaying **exponentially with depth** into the material. This occurs because the induced eddy currents themselves oppose the penetration of the primary field. **Standard Depth of Penetration (1 mark):** $$ \delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} $$ where $\delta$ is the standard depth of penetration (m), $f$ is the frequency (Hz), $\mu$ is the permeability (H/m), and $\sigma$ is the conductivity (S/m). --- ## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the physical principles of Eddy Current Testing. With a neat sketch, describe the generation of eddy currents in a test piece. **Model Solution:** **1. Principle (3 marks):** ECT is based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Lenz's Law**. When a coil carrying alternating current is placed near an electrically conductive material, the time-varying magnetic flux of the coil links with the test piece and induces **circulating currents (eddy currents)** in the test material. These eddy currents, governed by Lenz's law, flow in closed loops in planes **perpendicular to the magnetic flux** and produce a **secondary magnetic field that opposes the primary field**. **2. Generation Sequence (2 marks):** - AC excitation → primary alternating magnetic field $H_p$ - Flux linkage with test piece → induced EMF by Faraday's law - Closed conductive path → eddy current $I_e$ flows - $I_e$ produces opposing secondary field $H_s$ - Net field $H_{net} = H_p - H_s$ alters coil impedance $Z = R + jX_L$ - The change in $Z$ is measured and correlated to defect / property change. **3. Sketch Description (2 marks):** ``` AC Source | | ┌──┴──┐ │Probe│ ~~~~ H_p (primary field) │Coil │ └─────┘ ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓ ╔═══════════════╗ ║ ⊕ Ie ⊝ Ie ║ ← Test piece with eddy current loops ║ Test Piece ║ ╚═══════════════╝ ``` **4. Governing Factors (extra credit mention):** Frequency, conductivity, permeability, and geometry. --- #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for **standard depth of penetration** of eddy currents and discuss the factors that affect it. **Model Solution:** **1. Starting Equation (1 mark):** The 1-D Helmholtz diffusion equation for a semi-infinite conductor with sinusoidal excitation: $$ \frac{\partial^{2} H}{\partial x^{2}} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial H}{\partial t} $$ **2. Phasor Transformation (1 mark):** Substituting $H(x,t) = \text{Re}[\tilde{H}(x)e^{j\omega t}]$: $$ \frac{d^{2}\tilde{H}}{dx^{2}} = j\omega\mu\sigma\, \tilde{H} $$ **3. Solution (3 marks):** Solution form: $\tilde{H}(x) = \tilde{H}_0 e^{-\gamma x}$ where $$ \gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \frac{(1+j)}{\delta} $$ Defining the **standard depth of penetration**: $$ \boxed{\;\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}\;} $$ The real-time field expression: $$ H(x,t) = H_0\, e^{-x/\delta}\, \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{x}{\delta}\right) $$ **4. Factors Affecting δ (2 marks):** - **Frequency (f):** Higher frequency → smaller δ (shallower penetration). - **Permeability (μ):** Higher μ → smaller δ. - **Conductivity (σ):** Higher σ → smaller δ. - For inspecting deep defects → use **low frequency**. - For surface defects → use **high frequency**. --- ### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **lift-off effect** and **edge effect** in Eddy Current Testing with suitable diagrams. **Model Solution:** **1. Lift-Off Effect (3.5 marks):** **Definition:** The lift-off effect is the change in the probe coil impedance as the **distance (lift-off) between the probe tip and the test piece surface is varied**, while no defect is present. **Cause:** As the probe moves away from the surface, the magnetic coupling weakens, the eddy current strength decreases, and the reflected impedance changes — typically the **resistance R decreases** and the **inductance (X_L) increases**. **Diagram:** ``` Probe Coil ┌─────┐ │ │ ←─ d (lift-off distance) └─────┘ ~~~~~~~~~ ← magnetic flux ════════════════ Test Piece ``` **Application:** Lift-off is used for **non-conductive coating thickness measurement** (e.g., paint on aluminium). **2. Edge Effect (3.5 marks):** **Definition:** When the probe approaches a **physical edge, hole, or boundary** of the test piece, the eddy current paths are abruptly cut off. This causes a **false signal** that can mask real defects or be misinterpreted. **Cause:** At an edge, the eddy currents cannot complete their loops, so the **apparent conductivity** drops locally and the impedance shifts — similar to a defect signal. **Diagram:** ``` ═══════════╗ ← Edge ║ Probe ──> ║ (false signal appears) ║ ``` **Mitigation:** Use **shielded probes** or **smaller-diameter probes** that fit well within the part geometry; use **differential probes** to cancel edge signals. --- #### (b) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Eddy Current Testing system**. List the components and state the function of each. **Model Solution:** **1. Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │Oscillator│ → │ Probe │ → │ Test │ → │ Impedance│ │AC Source │ │ Coil │ │ Piece │ │ Detector │ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │Signal │ │Conditioner │ │(Amp+Filter) │ └──────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │Display │ │CRO / Imped. │ │Plane Plotter │ └──────────────┘ ``` **2. Components and Functions (4 marks):** | S.No | Component | Function | |------|-----------|----------| | 1 | **Oscillator / Function Generator** | Produces high-frequency AC (10 Hz – 10 MHz) to energize probe coil. | | 2 | **Probe Coil** | Generates primary alternating magnetic field; senses impedance change. | | 3 | **Test Piece** | Conductive specimen under inspection; eddy currents induced within. | | 4 | **Impedance Detector / Bridge** | Compares coil impedance with reference; produces error signal. | | 5 | **Signal Conditioner (Amplifier + Filter)** | Amplifies weak signal and removes noise. | | 6 | **Display Unit (CRO / Impedance Plane)** | Visualizes defect signal as voltage vs. time or as impedance locus. | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ECT Theory Answers:** > 1. **Confusing "standard depth of penetration" with "effective depth"** — Standard δ = 1/e ≈ 36.8 %; practical inspection is often limited to 2.6 δ ≈ 7 %. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix these. > 2. **Forgetting units in the δ formula** — Always mention $f$ in Hz, $\mu$ in H/m, $\sigma$ in S/m to get δ in metres. > 3. **Writing Lenz's law incorrectly** — It states "induced current **opposes the change** that produced it" — NOT "opposes the field" (a frequent KTU mistake). > 4. **Not distinguishing lift-off from edge effect** — Lift-off = gap variation; Edge effect = geometrical boundary. Many students merge them. > 5. **Skipping the phase-lag term in the derivation** — The full solution is $H(x,t) = H_0 e^{-x/\delta}\cos(\omega t - x/\delta)$. Writing only the amplitude part loses 2 marks in Part B derivations. > 6. **Forgetting μ = μ₀μᵣ relation** — Examiners expect the absolute permeability broken into μ₀ and relative permeability for ferromagnetics. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ECT is governed by Faraday's + Lenz's law** and the **Helmholtz diffusion equation**. - The **standard depth of penetration** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ is the **single most important formula** in ECT — memorize it. - The three **master variables** are **f, μ, σ** — increasing any of them **reduces δ** (shallower inspection). - **Skin effect** causes eddy currents to concentrate near the surface — exponential decay $J(x) = J_0 e^{-x/\delta}$. - **Phase lag** at depth $x$ is $x/\delta$ radians — the deeper the eddy current, the more it lags the primary field. - **Lift-off** is a **false signal** caused by probe-to-surface gap variation; can be exploited for coating thickness measurement. - **Edge effect** arises at physical boundaries where eddy current paths are cut short; mitigated by shielded or smaller probes. - **Impedance plane diagram** is the graphical tool to identify defects — each defect type produces a unique locus. - The **transformer analogy** (primary coil + shorted secondary = test piece) explains impedance reflection. - **Multi-frequency ECT** uses simultaneous high and low frequencies to suppress lift-off/edge noise while detecting deep defects. - ECT works on **conductive materials only**; not suitable for plastics, ceramics, or wood. - ECT does **not require couplant** and can be used at high temperatures → ideal for **in-service inspections**. - The phrase **"F-C-μ controls δ"** is a reliable last-minute KTU memory hook.

topics.title: Field Factor, Lift-Off Effect, Edge Effect, and End Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Field Factor, Lift-Off Effect, Edge Effect, and End Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This section establishes the foundational vocabulary for the four coupled phenomena that govern every **eddy current testing (ECT)** measurement: *Field Factor*, *Lift-Off Effect*, *Edge Effect*, and *End Effect*. Each phenomenon manifests as a unique perturbation of the probe's **impedance signature** on the complex impedance plane, and a KTU examiner will expect you to recognize, separate, and suppress them during flaw detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2 — Eddy Current Testing):** *"Coupling effects: Field factor, lift-off, edge effect, end effect, and their influence on the impedance plane display."*

### 1.1 Field Factor (η)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Field Factor (η)** is the dimensionless ratio of the average magnetic field intensity established **inside the test specimen** ($\bar{H}_s$) to the magnetic field intensity that would exist at the **surface of an empty (air-cored) test coil** ($\bar{H}_c$):

$$\eta = \frac{\bar{H}_{s}}{\bar{H}_{c}}$$

For a perfectly matched coil-specimen system (specimen completely filling the coil cross-section in a long solenoid geometry), $\eta \rightarrow 1$. When the specimen is smaller than the coil bore, $\eta < 1$, and the effective coupling is reduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Fill Factor:** The *fill factor* $\eta_f = (d_s/d_c)^2$ is a purely **geometric** ratio of cross-sectional areas (specimen diameter $d_s$ to coil diameter $d_c$). The *field factor* is an **electromagnetic** ratio that includes permeability and demagnetization effects. For non-magnetic, non-ferrous specimens ($\mu_r \approx 1$), the two quantities numerically coincide.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine placing a small wooden dowel inside a long copper tube carrying water (the tube = coil, the water = magnetic flux). The smaller the dowel relative to the tube, the less "aware" the water is of the dowel's presence. The *Field Factor* measures exactly this "awareness" — the fraction of the magnetic field that actually intercepts the conductive specimen and drives eddy currents within it.

### 1.2 Lift-Off Effect

**Formal Definition:**
The **Lift-Off Effect** is the variation in probe impedance caused by a change in the **mechanical clearance (lift-off distance)** between the probe face and the test specimen surface. As the probe retracts (lifts off), the magnetic coupling weakens exponentially, and the probe's impedance traces a characteristic curve on the impedance plane.

For a surface probe at frequency $f$ and lift-off distance $l$:

$$\Delta Z_{lift\text{-}off} \propto e^{-\alpha l}, \quad \alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Hold a strong magnet above an aluminum plate. Move it farther away — the attraction weakens, and once it crosses a critical gap, it falls. The *Lift-Off Effect* is the eddy current equivalent: the probe "feels" the specimen through an air gap, and that gap exponentially dampens the inductive coupling. This is the **single largest source of false indications** in surface eddy current inspection.

### 1.3 Edge Effect

**Formal Definition:**
The **Edge Effect** is the perturbation of the standard impedance signal that occurs when the eddy current probe approaches or passes over a **geometric boundary (edge)** of the test specimen. The truncated eddy current path at the edge reduces the effective resistance of the secondary circuit, lowering the apparent impedance and producing a characteristic signal lobe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Edge Effect is *not* a defect signal. It is an artefact of finite specimen geometry and will be falsely identified as a flaw by an inexperienced operator. A KTU question will test whether you can identify it as a geometric, not metallurgical, disturbance.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a whirlpool in a bathtub. The circular flow (eddy currents) is free to circulate only in the middle of the tub. As you slide a paddle (the probe) toward the wall (the edge), the water flow is suddenly restricted, and the paddle feels less drag. That loss of "drag" is exactly the Edge Effect.

### 1.4 End Effect

**Formal Definition:**
The **End Effect** is the impedance perturbation produced when an **encircling or ID (insert) probe** is positioned near the **end face of a cylindrical specimen** (tube, rod, bar). The abrupt termination of the conductive path causes the eddy current loops to spread, reducing the effective secondary loading and producing a signal that is virtually indistinguishable from a defect at the tube end.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Blow across the top of an empty bottle. As you move your mouth toward the open end, the tone changes dramatically — the air column is "truncated" and its resonance is disturbed. The End Effect is the electromagnetic equivalent: the resonant eddy current loop of the tube is "cut off" at the end, and the probe reads a phantom signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these four effects belong together (the Unified View):** All four phenomena share a single physical origin — **a change in the geometric boundary conditions of the eddy current path**. Field Factor describes the *radial* mismatch, Lift-Off the *axial* separation, Edge Effect the *lateral* truncation, and End Effect the *longitudinal* termination. Together, they define the *coupling geometry* of any ECT measurement.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ |
| $\sigma$ | Electrical conductivity | Siemens per metre ($\text{S/m}$) |
| $f$ | Test frequency | Hz (typically 1 kHz – 10 MHz) |
| $\delta$ | Standard depth of penetration | $\sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ metres |
| $d_s$ | Specimen diameter | metres |
| $d_c$ | Coil diameter | metres |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section develops the governing physics of each effect, presents the field-factor relationship to the impedance of an encircling coil, and consolidates every KTU-relevant formula into a single cheat sheet.

### 2.1 Field Factor — Underlying Physics

For an **encircling coil** of length $\ell$ wound on a cylindrical form with $N$ turns, energized by current $I$ at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$:

1. **Primary (no specimen) field intensity** at the coil axis: $H_c = NI/\ell$.
2. **Insertion of a non-magnetic specimen** (permeability $\mu_0$, conductivity $\sigma$) modifies the field inside the coil through the induced eddy currents.
3. The **field inside the specimen** is found by solving Maxwell's equations in cylindrical coordinates with the boundary condition that the tangential $H$ is continuous at the specimen surface and the tangential $E$ vanishes at the surface.
4. For the standard *thin-specimen, long-coil* limit, the average field inside is:

$$\bar{H}_{s} = H_{c} \cdot \eta, \quad \eta = \frac{1}{1 + (d_c/d_s)^2 \left( \frac{1}{\mu_r} - 1 \right)}$$

For a non-magnetic specimen ($\mu_r = 1$):

$$\eta = \left( \frac{d_s}{d_c} \right)^2 = \text{Fill Factor}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why, in practical KTU problems on **non-ferrous** tubes, the field factor is simply the **square of the diameter ratio**. Always state this simplification explicitly in your answer.

### 2.2 Lift-Off — Physical Mechanism

A **surface (pancake) probe** generates a primary field $H_0$ that loops back through the specimen. The mutual inductance $M$ between probe and specimen falls off as:

$$M(l) = M_0 \, e^{-l/\delta}, \quad \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

The induced eddy current density in the specimen is $J_e \propto \omega \sigma M(l) I$, and the resulting **secondary field** at the probe produces the lift-off signal.

**Key consequences:**
- The lift-off signal on the impedance plane traces a curve of **constant phase** (typically $-45°$ to $-70°$ depending on frequency).
- The curve lies **between the air-point (no specimen)** and the **load line** of the material.
- Lift-off is **frequency-dependent**: higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → lift-off curve becomes steeper (more sensitive to small gaps).
- At very high $f$ (very small $\delta$), lift-off becomes a near-vertical locus on the impedance plane.

### 2.3 Edge Effect — Mathematical Description

When a probe of effective radius $a$ is at a lateral distance $x$ from a straight specimen edge, the effective eddy current path is interrupted. The **edge-effect signal amplitude** as a function of position is approximately:

$$S_{edge}(x) = S_0 \left[ 1 - \text{erf}\left( \frac{x}{a} \right) \right]$$

where $S_0$ is the maximum signal at the edge and $\text{erf}$ is the Gaussian error function.

**Behavioural features:**
- Signal becomes significant when $x < 2a$ (the "edge zone" has width $\approx 2 \times$ probe diameter).
- At $x = 0$ (probe straddling the edge), signal is approximately **half** the full-coupling value.
- Beyond $x > 3a$, the edge effect is negligible (<5% of full signal).
- The signal is **independent of defect depth** — it is purely geometric, which is the key diagnostic clue.

### 2.4 End Effect — Behaviour in Tubes and Bars

For an **encircling probe** of length $L_p$ moving along a tube of length $L_t$:

- The end-effect region extends a distance $L_p$ **into the tube from each end face**.
- The signal is a smooth bell-shaped dip in apparent impedance.
- For an **ID (bobbin) probe** of length $L_b$ inside a tube, the end-effect "dead zone" at each end is approximately $L_b/2$ to $L_b$ in length — flaws here cannot be reliably detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Mitigation (KTU Favourite):** End-effect regions in tube inspection are **discarded** by the KTU standard practice of mounting probes $\geq L_p$ away from the tube end. Always cite this in a 14-mark answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Field Factor** governs the **sensitivity calibration** of encircling-coil systems (e.g., heat-exchanger tube inspection in nuclear steam generators).
- **Lift-Off** is exploited as a **purposeful feature** in coating-thickness gauges, conductivity meters with surface compensation, and paint-thickness probes — but must be **suppressed** in flaw detection.
- **Edge Effect** dictates the **edge exclusion zone** mandated in aerospace eddy current codes (e.g., ASTM E1417, AMS 2630).
- **End Effect** determines the **inlet/exit discard length** in the manufacture of seamless tubes, boiler tubes, and nuclear fuel cladding.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = (d_s/d_c)^2$ | Field factor for non-magnetic specimen, encircling coil |
| 2 | $\eta = \left[ 1 + (d_c/d_s)^2 \left( \mu_r^{-1} - 1 \right) \right]^{-1}$ | Field factor, general (includes $\mu_r$) |
| 3 | $Z = R_c + j\omega L_c \left( 1 - \eta^2 \frac{j\omega\tau}{1 + j\omega\tau} \right)$ | Impedance of encircling coil with conducting specimen (Dr Libby model) |
| 4 | $\tau = \mu_0 \sigma a^2 / 2$ | Characteristic time constant of eddy current build-up |
| 5 | $a = d_s / 2$ | Specimen radius |
| 6 | $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega \mu \sigma)}$ | Standard depth of penetration |
| 7 | $M(l) = M_0 e^{-l/\delta}$ | Mutual inductance vs. lift-off $l$ |
| 8 | $S_{edge}(x) = S_0 \left[ 1 - \text{erf}(x/a) \right]$ | Edge-effect signal profile |
| 9 | $x_{edge} \approx 2a$ | Edge-effect half-width |
| 10 | $L_{end} \approx L_p$ | End-effect axial extent (encircling probe) |
| 11 | $f_{opt} = 1/(2\pi \tau)$ | Optimum test frequency (max sensitivity) |
| 12 | $R_{eff} = R_c \cdot \eta^2$ | Effective resistance contribution from eddy currents |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formatting note for KTU scripts:** In your answer booklet, write the lift-off exponential as $e^{-l/\delta}$ with no absolute-value bars. When the same expression must appear inside a table, use $\lvert l/\delta \rvert$ in LaTeX, but in KTU scripts prefer explicit description ("$l$ is non-negative").

### 2.7 Engineering Utility Statement

Every one of these effects is **inverted in industry**: field factor governs calibration charts in tube plants, lift-off drives a billion-dollar market of coating-thickness gauges, edge effect defines the inspection exclusion zones in aircraft wheel and fuselage panels, and end effect dictates the inlet/outlet cut-off length in seamless tube production. A KTU answer that ties the physics to a real industrial process earns full marks under outcome-based assessment.
