The ECT system is governed by **electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law) and operates within a typical frequency range of **10 Hz to 10 MHz**, with surface inspection depths generally limited to the **skin depth (δ)** of the test material.

The equipment chain can be broadly classified into **four functional blocks**:

| Block | Primary Function | Typical Components |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Excitation & Signal Generator** | Produces high-frequency AC current | Oscillator, Function generator |
| **Probe / Test Coil Assembly** | Induces and senses eddy currents | Surface probe, Encircling coil, Bobbin probe |
| **Signal Processing Unit** | Amplifies, filters, and analyzes response | Preamplifier, Phase-sensitive detector, Display |
| **Calibration & Accessories** | Reference response, coupling, marking | Test blocks, demagnetizer, comparator |

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The Metal Detector at the Beach

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are walking on a beach holding a **metal detector**. The detector sends an invisible magnetic "buzz" into the sand. When this buzz hits a buried coin, it bounces back changed, and the detector beeps. **Eddy Current Testing works on the exact same principle**, except the "buzz" is a high-frequency alternating magnetic field, the "sand" is the conductive metal component, and the "coin" is any flaw, crack, or change in material property. The **beep** in ECT is the change in **impedance (Z)** of the test coil, which is plotted on a **phase-plane (impedance) diagram**.

## 1.2 Engineering Significance

ECT is one of the most versatile NDT methods because it can detect:
- **Surface and sub-surface cracks** (up to skin depth)
- **Corrosion and wall-thinning** in tubes
- **Conductivity variations** (alloy sorting, heat treatment verification)
- **Permeability changes** (ferromagnetic material sorting)

For this to work reliably, the engineer must understand the **hardware** that delivers, receives, and interprets the signal — hence the study of ECT equipment and accessories.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Building Blocks of an ECT System

## 2.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Modern ECT Instrument

A modern digital ECT flaw detector is essentially a **narrow-band AC bridge instrument**. Its functional architecture is as follows:

> 1. **Oscillator** → 2. **Power Amplifier** → 3. **Test Coil (Probe)** → 4. **Bridge / Balancing Network** → 5. **Preamplifier** → 6. **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** → 7. **Signal Conditioning (Filter + Gain)** → 8. **Display (CRT / LCD Impedance Plane)** → 9. **Recorder / Data Storage**

### 2.1.1 Detailed Component-Level Analysis

**A. Oscillator**
- Generates a stable sinusoidal AC signal at the chosen test frequency.
- Frequency selection is critical: **lower frequencies (≈ 1–10 kHz)** for sub-surface flaws, **higher frequencies (≈ 100 kHz – 2 MHz)** for surface flaws.
- Modern instruments use **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** for ±0.01 % frequency stability.

**B. Power Amplifier**
- Boosts the oscillator signal to a level sufficient to drive the test coil (typically 1–10 V into 50 Ω).
- Maintains low harmonic distortion (< 0.5 %) to avoid spurious indications.

**C. Test Coil (Probe) — see Section 2.2**

**D. Bridge / Balancing Network**
- The test coil is one arm of an **AC Wheatstone bridge** (or an equivalent differential circuit).
- A **reference coil** or active balancing circuit cancels the large "air" signal so the small flaw signal can be amplified.
- Manual and **electronic auto-balance** modes are standard.

**E. Preamplifier**
- Low-noise, high-gain (typically **60–80 dB**) amplifier immediately after the bridge.
- Located close to the probe to minimize cable noise.

**F. Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**
- A *lock-in* type detector that resolves the signal into **X (real, resistive)** and **Y (imaginary, inductive)** components relative to a reference signal at the same frequency.
- This enables the classic **impedance-plane (Lissajous) display**.

**G. Signal Conditioning**
- **High-pass, low-pass, and band-pass filters** remove drift (1/f noise) and high-frequency interference.
- Adjustable gain (0–60 dB) sets display sensitivity.

**H. Display**
- CRT or LCD showing the **impedance plane diagram** (X-axis = resistance, Y-axis = inductance).
- Flaw signals appear as **lift-off lobes**, **phase shifts**, and **amplitude peaks** depending on the flaw type.

**I. Data Recording**
- XY recorders historically, modern **digital storage + USB/Ethernet** export.

### 2.2 Test Coils / Probes — The Heart of ECT

The probe is the transducer that converts electrical energy into a magnetic field and back. There are **three fundamental modes** of operation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 — Absolute Coil:** Single coil measures *total* impedance change. Sensitive to lift-off, but excellent for conductivity/thickness measurements.
> **Mode 2 — Differential Coil:** Two identical coils in series-opposition. Output is *zero* for uniform material; defects passing under one coil produce a clean signal — **insensitive to lift-off and temperature drift**.
> **Mode 3 — Reflection / Send-Receive:** Separate driver and pickup coils; used for high-frequency surface inspection and fastener hole scans.

#### 2.2.1 Probe Construction

| Probe Part | Material / Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Coil Former** | Non-conductive, low-loss plastic (Delrin, PTFE) to avoid parasitic currents |
| **Wire** | Fine Litz wire (50/44 AWG) to reduce skin-effect losses at high frequency |
| **Core** | Ferrite (MnZn) for low-frequency; air core for high-frequency to avoid saturation |
| **Shield (Case)** | Mu-metal or ferrite shield to confine field and reduce external interference |
| **Connector** | Lemo 00 series or BNC for high-frequency integrity |
| **Wear Face** | Alumina or sapphire cap for surface probes to resist abrasion |

### 2.3 Common Probe Geometries (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask the student to *draw and label* the following standard probe types. Memorize the construction and the **defect-direction sensitivity rule**.

**1. Surface Probe (Pencil / Pancake)**
- Flat or right-angled coil, 3–10 mm diameter, used for **manual scanning** of flat or curved surfaces.
- Sensitive to defects oriented **perpendicular to the coil's magnetic field** (i.e., perpendicular to the surface for a pancake coil).

**2. Encircling Coil (Annular / O.D. Probe)**
- The test piece (typically tubing, rod, wire) passes **through** the coil.
- Used in **production-line inspection** of bars, billets, tubes.
- Detects **transverse** flaws (those with circumference around the part).

**3. Bobbin / ID Probe (Insertion Probe)**
- A small coil mounted on a flexible stem; inserted **inside** a tube.
- Used to inspect **ferrule tubes, heat-exchanger U-tubes, condenser tubes**.
- Detects both **transverse and longitudinal** flaws.

**4. Rotating / Spin Probe**
- A probe head rotates at high speed (typically **600–3000 RPM**) inside a tube.
- Provides **100 % circumferential coverage** at high inspection speeds.
- Common in **in-service inspection** of boiler tubes and ferritic steam-generator tubes.

**5. Sliding Probe**
- Mounted on a fixture that maintains a **fixed lift-off** during automated scanning of plates.

**6. ID-Rotating + Axial-Feed Scanner**
- Combines rotation and pull-through for full-volume scanning.

**7. Special Probes**
- **Bolt-hole probes** (split-D, plus-point) for fastener-hole inspection in aerospace.
- **Spring-loaded probes** for irregular contours.
- **Saturating probes** (with DC-biased ferrite core) to suppress permeability noise in ferromagnetic materials.

### 2.4 Calibration Standards (Reference Test Blocks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without a calibration standard, an ECT indication is meaningless.** Every ECT procedure requires a **Reference Standard (Calibration Block)** containing *known* artificial defects in a material *identical* to the test piece.

| Standard | Geometry | Typical Artificial Flaws |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ASME / ASTM Calibration Block** | Flat plate, same alloy as test | EDM notches (0.2, 0.4, 1.0 mm deep), through-holes (0.8, 1.6, 3.2 mm) |
| **Tube Standard** | Short length of identical tube | Transverse & longitudinal EDM notches; through-holes |
| **Conductivity Standard** | Reference alloy coupons of known % IACS | None (used for conductivity meter calibration) |
| **Reference Block for Lift-off** | Stepped wedge (3–5 thickness steps) | Used to set lift-off curve |

**Key point:** Notches are usually **Electric Discharge Machined (EDM)** — they are sharp, repeatable, and dimensionally accurate, in contrast to drilled holes which give a different signal phase.

### 2.5 Supporting Accessories

| Accessory | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Demagnetizer** | Removes residual magnetism after DC-magnetization-based tests; required for ferromagnetic parts to prevent magnetic particle retention. |
| **Magnetic Saturator (Yoke Probe)** | DC electromagnet saturates the test material so that permeability variations are eliminated → effective ECT on ferromagnetic surfaces. |
| **Couplant / Conductive Gel** | Not required for ECT (unlike UT), but conductive paste may be used for impedance matching on rough surfaces. |
| **Probe Cables** | Lemo/BNC coaxial, double-shielded, length matched to avoid capacitive imbalance. |
| **Scanner / Manipulator** | Motorized X-Y, R-θ, or rotary stage for automated inspection; encoder for C-scan mapping. |
| **Marking Device** | Pen marker / paint pen integrated with probe to mark defect location. |
| **Cleaning Materials** | Solvents, lint-free wipes, degreasers — surface cleanliness is **critical** for repeatable lift-off. |
| **Adapters** | Test-piece holders, centering fixtures for tubes, lift-off gauges (precision 0.1 mm shims). |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Skin Depth (δ)** | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = 503\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | m (or mm) | ρ in Ω·m, f in Hz, μᵣ relative |
| **Impedance of test coil (Z)** | $Z = R + jX_L = R + j\omega L$ | Ω | R = resistance, X = inductive reactance |
| **Inductive Reactance** | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ | Ω | L is the coil's inductance |
| **Test Frequency (Standard depth)** | $f \approx \dfrac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^2}$ | Hz | Solve for f given desired depth δ |
| **% IACS Conductivity** | $\%IACS = \dfrac{\sigma_{sample}}{5.8 \times 10^7} \times 100$ | % | 5.8×10⁷ S/m is annealed copper |
| **Inspection Frequency for surface flaw** | $f \ge \dfrac{1}{\delta^2 \pi \mu \sigma}$ | Hz | For a target depth of ≤ 1 mm |
| **Frequency Ratio (Depth Selection)** | $f_2 = f_1 \left(\dfrac{\delta_1}{\delta_2}\right)^2$ | Hz | Used in dual-frequency ECT |
| **Liftoff Noise Suppression** | Higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → less liftoff noise | — | Empirical rule of thumb |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal skin-depth constant:** $503$ comes from $1 / \sqrt{\pi^2 \cdot 4 \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot 10^7} = 503$ when ρ is in Ω·m and f in Hz, and μᵣ = 1. Always check your textbook's exact coefficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Skin Depth (δ) — The Most Important ECT Equation

The skin depth governs **how deep** the eddy currents (and therefore the inspection) can penetrate. It is derived from the wave equation in a conducting medium.

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equation (Faraday's Law in differential form):**
$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 1.** Assume a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in the +x direction into a conductor with conductivity σ and permeability μ.

**Step 2.** The wave equation in the conductor reduces to:
$$\frac{d^2 E}{dx^2} = \mu \sigma \frac{dE}{dt}$$

**Step 3.** For a sinusoidal excitation of angular frequency ω, replace $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ with $j\omega$:

$$\frac{d^2 E}{dx^2} = j\omega \mu \sigma \, E$$

**Step 4.** Assume a solution of the form $E(x) = E_0 \, e^{-\gamma x}$, where γ is the propagation constant.

**Step 5.** Substitute and find γ:

$$\gamma^2 = j\omega \mu \sigma$$

**Step 6.** Take the complex square root. Note that $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j \omega \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} \, (1 + j)$$

**Step 7.** The amplitude decays as $e^{-x/\delta}$ where $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\text{Real}(\gamma)}$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} \quad \text{(SI units: m)}$$

**Step 8.** Express in terms of frequency f (Hz) and resistivity ρ = 1/σ:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

**Step 9.** For a non-magnetic conductor with μᵣ = 1, μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m, and ρ in Ω·m:

$$\delta = 503 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{f}} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

This is the **standard practical form**.

### Worked Example 1 — Skin Depth of Aluminum

> Given: Aluminum, ρ = 2.82 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m, μᵣ = 1, test frequency f = 100 kHz.

**Step 1.** Compute $\delta$:
$$\delta = 503 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{100 \times 10^{3}}}$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate the argument of the square root:
$$\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{5}} = 2.82 \times 10^{-13}$$

**Step 3.** Take the square root:
$$\sqrt{2.82 \times 10^{-13}} = 5.31 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4.** Multiply by 503:
$$\delta = 503 \times 5.31 \times 10^{-7} = 2.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.267 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** At 100 kHz, eddy currents penetrate only **0.267 mm** into aluminum. To detect sub-surface flaws at 1 mm depth, frequency must be reduced.

### Worked Example 2 — Frequency Selection for Given Depth

> **Problem:** Inspect a stainless-steel tube (ρ = 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m, μᵣ = 1) for flaws at depth 2 mm. What frequency gives δ ≈ 2 mm?

**Step 1.** Solve the practical formula for f:
$$f = \dfrac{503^2 \cdot \rho}{\delta^2}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute δ = 2 × 10⁻³ m, ρ = 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m:
$$f = \dfrac{253009 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-7}}{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

**Step 3.** Numerator:
$$253009 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-7} = 1.771 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 4.** Denominator:
$$(2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5.** Divide:
$$f = \dfrac{1.771 \times 10^{-1}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.43 \times 10^{4} \text{ Hz} \approx 44.3 \text{ kHz}$$

**Answer:** Use a test frequency of approximately **44 kHz** to achieve a 2 mm skin depth in this stainless steel.

### Worked Example 3 — % IACS Calculation

> **Problem:** A non-magnetic sample has measured conductivity σ = 3.6 × 10⁷ S/m. Compute % IACS.

**Step 1.** Recall IACS reference: σ_cu = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m.

**Step 2.** Compute ratio:
$$\%IACS = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{7}}{5.8 \times 10^{7}} \times 100$$

**Step 3.** Numerator / Denominator:
$$3.6 / 5.8 = 0.6207$$

**Step 4.** Multiply by 100:
$$\%IACS = 62.07 \%$$

**Interpretation:** This corresponds approximately to **aluminum alloy 2024-T3** (~30–50 % IACS range, so this is a slightly more conductive grade, perhaps pure aluminum 1100 or Al-Mg alloy).

### 3.2 KTU Exam-Style Numerical: Choosing Frequency for Two Zones (Dual-Frequency ECT)

> **Problem:** A dual-frequency ECT system uses f₁ = 100 kHz (for surface flaws, δ₁ ≈ 0.1 mm) and f₂ for a deeper zone. If the second zone requires δ₂ = 0.3 mm in the same material, compute f₂.

**Step 1.** Use the frequency–depth relationship:
$$\dfrac{f_2}{f_1} = \left(\dfrac{\delta_1}{\delta_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2.** Substitute values:
$$\dfrac{f_2}{100 \text{ kHz}} = \left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.3}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{9}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for f₂:
$$f_2 = \dfrac{100 \text{ kHz}}{9} = 11.11 \text{ kHz}$$

**Interpretation:** The lower frequency 11.1 kHz is used to probe the deeper zone, while 100 kHz probes the surface — the two signals are mixed in the phase-sensitive detector to suppress lift-off noise.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Probe Type vs. Application

| Probe Type | Test-piece Geometry | Typical Frequency | Defect Type Detected | Lift-off Sensitivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Surface (Pancake)** | Flat/curved surface | 100 kHz – 2 MHz | Surface cracks | High |
| **Encircling (OD)** | Bars, rods, tubes | 1 kHz – 100 kHz | Transverse defects, wall thinning | Low (concentric) |
| **Bobbin (ID)** | Tube interiors | 2 kHz – 500 kHz | All orientations | Low |
| **Rotating** | Tube interiors | 10 kHz – 1 MHz | OD/ID surface cracks | Medium |
| **Bolt-hole (Plus-point)** | Fastener holes | 200 kHz – 2 MHz | Cracks radiating from hole | Medium |
| **Saturating Probe** | Ferromagnetic surfaces | 1 kHz – 50 kHz | Surface cracks (with DC bias) | High (suppressed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**List any three types of probes used in Eddy Current Testing and state the typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per probe):**

1. **Surface (Pancake) Probe** — Used for manual scanning of flat or curved surfaces to detect surface-breaking cracks.
2. **Encircling (Annular) Probe** — Used in production-line inspection of rods, bars, and tubes; the part passes *through* the coil.
3. **Bobbin (ID) Probe** — Inserted *inside* a tube (heat-exchanger, condenser) to detect wall-thinning and cracks on the inner/outer surfaces.

> (Any other valid probe — *rotating, bolt-hole, sliding* — is also acceptable, provided the application is correctly matched.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of a calibration standard in ECT? Mention one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **calibration standard** (or *reference test block*) is a specimen of the *same material, geometry, and surface finish* as the test piece, containing *known artificial defects* (e.g., EDM notches of fixed depth, through-holes of specific diameter). **[2 marks]**
- Its purpose is to **verify the ECT system's response**, set the **sensitivity / phase angle**, and establish an **acceptance/rejection threshold** for the production inspection. **[1 mark]**
- **Example:** A flat aluminum plate with three EDM notches of 0.2 mm, 0.4 mm, and 1.0 mm depth used to set the alarm threshold for surface-crack inspection of an aircraft skin panel.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Both sub-parts are **compulsory** within the chosen question.

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional block diagram of a modern ECT flaw detector**. Explain the function of any **four** major blocks.

**(b) [7 marks)** Explain the construction and working of an **encircling coil probe** and a **bobbin (ID) probe**. Compare their applications.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram of ECT Flaw Detector [7 marks]

**Block diagram (4 marks):**

```
Oscillator → Power Amplifier → AC Bridge → Test Coil (Probe) → Preamplifier
   → Phase-Sensitive Detector → Filter & Gain → Impedance-Plane Display
   → Recorder / Marker
```

**Function of four major blocks (3 marks — ½ mark each well-explained point):**

1. **Oscillator:** Generates a stable sinusoidal AC voltage at the selected test frequency (1 kHz – 10 MHz). Modern instruments use DDS for frequency stability of ±0.01 %. Frequency is chosen based on the desired skin depth.

2. **AC Bridge:** The test coil forms one arm; reference coil forms the opposite arm. Balances out the large "air" signal so the small flaw-induced change in impedance can be measured. Both manual and auto-balance modes are used.

3. **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD):** A lock-in type detector that resolves the coil's voltage into two orthogonal components:
   - **X-component** proportional to resistance change (R) — sensitive to cracks and permeability.
   - **Y-component** proportional to inductive reactance change (X_L) — sensitive to conductivity and lift-off.
   These are displayed on the **impedance plane (Lissajous) display**, allowing the operator to *separate* defect signals from lift-off by phase angle.

4. **Preamplifier:** A low-noise, high-gain (60–80 dB) amplifier located *close to the probe* to boost the small flaw signal (often microvolts) above the noise floor of the connecting cable.

#### Part (b) — Encircling vs. Bobbin Probes [7 marks]

**Encircling Coil Probe (3 marks):**

- **Construction:** A multi-turn solenoid coil wound on a non-conductive former (Delrin/PTFE), surrounded by a mu-metal or ferrite shield to confine the field. The coil former has an internal bore slightly larger than the test piece.
- **Working:** The test piece (rod, bar, tube) is fed continuously through the energized coil. Any local change in conductivity, permeability, or geometry (crack, pit, wall thinning) disturbs the eddy-current flow and changes the coil's impedance. Output is recorded as a continuous time-signal; a defect produces a peak.
- **Sensitivity:** Detects **transverse defects** (those whose major axis is along the circumference) most sensitively, and also detects **wall-thinning** as a continuous change in signal.

**Bobbin (ID) Probe (3 marks):**

- **Construction:** A small pancake or solenoid coil mounted on the tip of a flexible stem, often with spring-loaded centering fins. Coaxial cable runs through the stem.
- **Working:** Inserted into the tube, pulled through at a controlled speed (often via a winch). The coil's field penetrates the tube wall from inside. As it moves, it scans the entire circumference; rotating bobbin probes also give **full circumferential coverage**.
- **Sensitivity:** Detects both **transverse and longitudinal** flaws; widely used for **in-service inspection of heat-exchanger and condenser tubes** in power plants, refineries, and petrochemical plants.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Encircling | Bobbin (ID) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Probe location** | Outside the part | Inside the part |
| **Test piece** | Bars, rods, tubes (in-line) | Tubes with accessible bore |
| **Best for defect type** | Transverse, wall-thinning | Transverse + longitudinal |
| **Coupling stability** | Stable (concentric) | Stable with centering fins |

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for **skin depth (δ)** of an electromagnetic wave in a conducting medium. State the practical formula and explain its significance in ECT.

**(b) [7 marks)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **differential probe** mode of operation. Why is it relatively insensitive to **lift-off variations**?

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Skin Depth [7 marks]

**Statement of wave equation in a conductor (2 marks):**

$$\frac{d^2 E}{dx^2} = \mu \sigma \frac{dE}{dt}$$

**Assume sinusoidal excitation** $E \propto e^{j\omega t}$ and a plane-wave solution of the form $E(x) = E_0 e^{-\gamma x}$. Substitute and obtain: **(1 mark)**

$$\gamma^2 = j\omega \mu \sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

**Identify skin depth as the reciprocal of the real part of γ (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\text{Re}(\gamma)} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

**Convert to frequency form using ω = 2πf and ρ = 1/σ (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

**Practical formula for non-magnetic conductor (μᵣ = 1, μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷), with ρ in Ω·m and f in Hz (1 mark):**

$$\delta = 503 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{f}} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

**Significance in ECT (1 mark):**
- δ sets the **maximum depth of flaw detection** — typically up to 1δ to 3δ depending on sensitivity.
- By **choosing the test frequency f**, the inspector controls the inspection depth:
  - **Higher f** → smaller δ → surface-only inspection
  - **Lower f** → larger δ → sub-surface inspection
- For aluminum (ρ = 2.82 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m) at 100 kHz, δ ≈ 0.27 mm; at 1 kHz, δ ≈ 8.5 mm.

#### Part (b) — Differential Probe [7 marks]

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
                ┌─── Coil A ───┐
AC Source ──┤ ├─── Coil B ───┤─→ Detector
                └─── Equal & Opposite ───┘
                       │
                  Test Piece
              (moves left → right)
```

**Working (3 marks):**

- A **differential probe** consists of **two identical coils**, electrically connected in **series opposition** (i.e., the induced voltages are subtracted).
- When the probe is over a **uniform region** of the test piece, both coils "see" the same material and the output is **zero** (balanced).
- When the probe moves over a **defect** that lies *under one of the coils only*, the balance is disturbed and a **peak signal** appears.
- As the defect passes *from Coil A into Coil B*, the output **reverses polarity** (a characteristic "figure-8" or sinusoidal response).
- The **midpoint** between the two coils gives a *null* even in the presence of a defect — useful for **edge detection** and for separating defects from *gradual* changes (e.g., uniform wall thinning produces no differential signal, but a localized pit does).

**Why insensitive to lift-off? (1 mark):**
- A change in lift-off affects **both** coils **equally and simultaneously**. Since the coils are in series opposition, the equal lift-off signals **cancel** in the output. Only *differential* changes (such as a defect under one coil) appear in the output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Wrong skin-depth constant** — many students write $δ = \sqrt{\rho / \pi f \mu}$ but then **forget to substitute μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** to get the numerical constant 503. Without this, you get 0 out of 2 in derivation. **Always show the substitution step.**
> 2. **Confusing absolute and differential probes** — stating that "differential probes measure the total impedance" loses 1 mark. They measure the **difference** in impedance between two adjacent coils.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — skin depth is **not dimensionless**. Always carry units (m or mm) in your final answer.
> 4. **Lift-off vs. Probe tilt** — students often use these interchangeably. *Lift-off* is the gap between probe and surface; *tilt* is angular misalignment. Only the former matters in ECT lift-off-noise discussions.
> 5. **Calibration block material mismatch** — using a *ferrous* standard to calibrate a *non-ferrous* test piece. The standard **must be the same alloy**; otherwise conductivity mismatch shifts the impedance response.
> 6. **Frequency-depth confusion** — students often say "high frequency gives deeper penetration." It is the **opposite**: high frequency → small skin depth → shallow inspection.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before the Exam!**

- ✅ ECT is governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**; operating frequencies range from **1 kHz to 10 MHz**.
- ✅ **Skin depth (δ)** is the depth at which eddy current density falls to **1/e ≈ 36.8 %** of its surface value.
- ✅ Practical skin-depth formula: $\delta = 503\sqrt{\rho / f}$ (meters, for μᵣ = 1).
- ✅ **Higher frequency → smaller δ → surface inspection only.**
- ✅ **Lower frequency → larger δ → sub-surface inspection** (limited to 1δ to 3δ).
- ✅ **Three probe modes:** Absolute, Differential, Reflection (Send-Receive).
- ✅ **Differential probe is lift-off insensitive** but cannot detect uniform wall thinning.
- ✅ **Encircling coil** = production-line inspection of rods/tubes; **Bobbin (ID) probe** = inspection of tube interiors.
- ✅ **Rotating probe** = 100 % circumferential coverage of tube ID, used in service inspection of heat-exchanger tubes.
- ✅ **Calibration standards** must be of the *same material and geometry* as the test piece, with *known* artificial defects (EDM notches preferred).
- ✅ **% IACS reference:** Annealed copper = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m = 100 % IACS.
- ✅ **Accessories:** Demagnetizer, Magnetic Saturator (yoke), Probe Cables (matched length), Scanner/Manipulator, Centering Fixtures.
- ✅ **Demagnetization** is required after any DC-magnetization step to prevent magnetic-particle retention.
- ✅ **Lift-off is the dominant noise source** in surface ECT; use differential coils, dual-frequency mixing, or phase-angle gating to suppress it.
- ✅ In an **AC bridge**, the test coil and a reference coil form two arms; balance is the *zero-signal* condition.
- ✅ **Phase-sensitive detector** resolves signal into **X (resistive)** and **Y (inductive)** components, displayed on the **impedance plane**.
- ✅ **Tube inspection** rule: use **low frequency** for ferromagnetic tubes (low conductivity) and **higher frequency** for non-ferrous tubes (high conductivity) to achieve comparable skin depth.
- ✅ **Probes are consumables** — they wear out, drift, and need periodic replacement; the standard re-scan every 4 hours is mandatory under most KTU-lab and industrial procedures.
- ✅ **Signal phase angle** uniquely identifies the defect type: lift-off ≈ 90° (purely inductive), cracks ≈ 45°–70°, conductivity ≈ 0°–30°, permeability ≈ 45° (ferromagnetic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of ECT: Conductivity Measurement, Hardness Measurement, Defect Detection, and Coating Thickness Measurement
UT Testing Methods: Contact Testing and Immersion Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** UT Testing Methods: Contact Testing and Immersion Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Ultrasonic Testing (UT), **UT Testing Methods** refer to the standardized physical configurations used to inject, propagate, and receive ultrasonic energy into a test specimen. The two principal industrial methods covered under KTU Module 3 are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contact Testing (also called "Hand/Manual Contact Testing" or "Squirt Testing")**: A UT method in which the ultrasonic probe is placed **in direct mechanical contact** with the test surface, coupled acoustically through a thin layer of liquid couplant (typically 0.025 mm – 0.25 mm thick).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Immersion Testing (also called "Water-Bath Immersion Testing")**: A UT method in which both the ultrasonic probe and the test specimen are **fully submerged in a liquid medium (water)**, with the water column itself acting as the acoustic couplant between the probe face and the part surface.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🍎 The Apple-in-Pool Analogy

Imagine you want to hear the echo from a sunken apple in a pond:

- **Contact Testing** → You dip your fingertip just **touching** the water surface and tap; you hear the echo only where your finger is. To scan the entire pond, you must drag your finger everywhere — slow and tiring. This is exactly how a contact probe works: the probe "touches" the part through a thin oil/glycerin film.
- **Immersion Testing** → You stand on the dock, hold a microphone **fully underwater**, and the sound travels through water to hit the apple. The microphone never touches the apple; you can move the microphone (or the apple) mechanically in 3D — fast, repeatable, and automated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway**: Contact testing = probe touches part (manual, field inspection). Immersion testing = probe floats in water bath (mechanized, lab/production inspection).

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standards

The following standard reference values are used throughout KTU UT evaluations:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (in Steel) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal velocity (steel) | $c_L$ | **5920** | m/s |
| Shear velocity (steel) | $c_S$ | **3255** | m/s |
| Longitudinal velocity (water @ 25°C) | $c_W$ | **1497** | m/s |
| Acoustic impedance (steel, longitudinal) | $Z_{steel}$ | **46 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Acoustic impedance (water) | $Z_W$ | **1.5 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Standard reference probe frequencies | $f$ | **1, 2.25, 5, 10, 15, 25** | MHz |
| Typical couplant (gel) — velocity | $c_{gel}$ | **1500 – 1700** | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why water works as a couplant**: Water's acoustic impedance ($Z_W = 1.5 \times 10^6$) is reasonably matched to common couplant gels and the test piece, and its longitudinal velocity ($c_W = 1497$ m/s) is close to many plastics — making it ideal for longitudinal wave generation. Steel's velocity (5920 m/s) is roughly **4× water's velocity** — this velocity mismatch is critical for refraction-angle calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Method 1: Contact Testing (Detailed Mechanics)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

In contact testing, the probe (transducer + damping block + wear plate) is **hand-held or fixture-mounted** directly against the test surface. A couplant film (oil, glycerin, paste, or cellulose gel) of thickness $t_c \le 0.25$ mm is used to displace air, since air's acoustic impedance ($Z_{air} \approx 430$ kg/(m²·s)) is so low that ~99.9% of ultrasound is reflected at any air gap.

### 2.1.2 Why a Couplant is Mandatory — The Transmission Coefficient

The pressure transmission coefficient at the couplant-to-steel interface:

$$T = \frac{2 Z_{steel}}{Z_{steel} + Z_{couplant}}$$

For steel–oil interface:

$$T = \frac{2 \times 46 \times 10^6}{(46 + 1.2) \times 10^6} = 0.974$$

So ~**97.4% energy transmits** with oil couplant vs ~**0.001% with air** — a 100,000× amplification of transmitted signal.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Contact Pulse-Echo Testing

- **Step 1 — Excitation**: The pulser sends a high-voltage spike (typically 100–400 V, rise time < 10 ns) to the piezoelectric crystal.
- **Step 2 — Couplant Bridge**: The couplant layer transmits > 97% of acoustic energy into the part.
- **Step 3 — Initial Pulse (IP)**: The first signal returned is the **Interface Echo** from the front surface of the part.
- **Step 4 — Bulk Wave Propagation**: The remainder of the wave travels through the part at longitudinal velocity $c_L$.
- **Step 5 — Backwall Echo (BWE)**: At the back surface, part of the wave reflects and returns to the probe (acting as receiver in pulse-echo mode).
- **Step 6 — Flaw Echo**: A discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion) reflects energy earlier than the back wall, producing an intermediate echo.
- **Step 7 — Display**: The CRT/oscilloscope/A-scan displays Time vs Amplitude.

### 2.1.4 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages**:
- Portable and field-deployable (no water tank needed)
- Low capital cost
- Suitable for large, immobile structures (bridges, pipelines, pressure vessels)
- Quick setup

**Limitations**:
- Surface must be reasonably smooth (Ra ≤ 25 µm typical)
- Couplant must be reapplied frequently
- Manual scanning → operator-dependent results
- Probe wear → frequency drift over time
- Lower near-surface resolution (because of dead-zone from ringing)
- Slower scanning rate (manual vs mechanized)

## 2.2 Sub-Method 2: Immersion Testing (Detailed Mechanics)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

In immersion testing, the part is **fully submerged** in a water tank (typically 300–600 mm deep, made of stainless steel to minimize corrosion). The probe is positioned below the water surface, typically 25–150 mm above the test piece. The water column itself is the couplant.

### 2.2.2 Why Immersion Testing Provides Better Resolution

Because the probe is **farther from the part** (water path distance, $w$), the transducer ringing (dead-zone) decays before the ultrasound reaches the test surface. This means **flaws just below the surface can be detected** — a major advantage for near-surface defect detection.

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Immersion Testing

- **Step 1 — Bath Preparation**: Tank is filled with degassed, deionized water (degassed to prevent cavitation noise).
- **Step 2 — Part Immersion**: The test piece is clamped in a fixture and lowered into the water.
- **Step 3 — Probe Positioning**: The probe is mounted on a motorized scanner (X-Y-Z gantry or rotary turntable).
- **Step 4 — Water Path Calibration**: The water path distance $w$ is set so that the front-surface reflection occurs at the correct time base position.
- **Step 5 — Scanning**: The motorized scanner moves the probe (or the part) in a programmed raster or polar pattern.
- **Step 6 — Data Acquisition**: A-scan, B-scan, or C-scan images are recorded electronically.
- **Step 7 — Evaluation**: Flaw indications are sized by amplitude, time-of-flight, and pattern recognition.

### 2.2.4 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages**:
- **Consistent, repeatable coupling** → not operator-dependent
- Excellent **near-surface flaw detection** (no dead-zone problem)
- Suitable for **complex geometries** (small parts, curved surfaces, irregular shapes)
- Enables **automated, high-speed scanning** for production lines
- Probe life is longer (no wear plate abrasion)
- Multi-axis scanning possible (immersion scanners have 4–6 axes)

**Limitations**:
- Requires the part to fit in a water tank
- Part must be **water-compatible** (or use special couplants like oil)
- Higher capital cost (tank + scanner + software)
- Slower for very large structures
- Setup time longer (water degassing, alignment)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Examination**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Pressure transmission coefficient (normal incidence) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Pressure reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_t} = \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ | **Snell's Law** (refraction) | degrees |
| 5 | $T_c = \dfrac{2 t_c}{c_c}$ | Time delay in couplant layer | seconds |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c}$ | **Near-field length** (Fresnel zone) | meters |
| 7 | $\sin \theta_B = \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ | **First critical angle** (longitudinal → shear) | degrees |
| 8 | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Beam divergence half-angle | radians |
| 9 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Flaw depth (pulse-echo) | meters |
| 10 | $\alpha_dB = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_x}\right)$ | Attenuation in decibels | dB |
| 11 | $w_{path} = t_W \cdot c_W$ | Water path time delay | seconds |
| 12 | $R_{focal} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)$ | Focal length of focused immersion probe | meters |
| 13 | $\text{PRF}_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{max}}$ | Maximum pulse repetition frequency | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **In LaTeX table cells, never use the vertical bar `|` symbol** for absolute value. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`. KTU examiners have flagged broken-table answers as incomplete.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: Immersion testing is the **gold standard** for turbine blade, composite panel, and honeycomb structure inspection (Boeing, Airbus production lines use water-bath C-scan systems).
- **Nuclear Power**: Contact UT is used for **in-service inspection** of reactor pressure vessels and steam generator tubes (manual phased-array probes).
- **Railways**: Contact UT is used for **rail-wheel and axle inspection** on tracks and at depots.
- **Automotive**: Immersion UT is used for **forged/cast component inspection** (crankshafts, connecting rods) at 100% production rate.
- **Oil & Gas Pipeline**: Contact UT crawlers perform **long-range pipeline girth weld inspection** during construction.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Near-Field Length and Its Role in Testing-Method Selection

The **near-field length** $N$ is a critical parameter that determines the **optimal standoff distance** for immersion probes and the **minimum detectable flaw depth** for contact probes.

### Step 1: Starting from Huygens-Fresnel Principle

A circular piezoelectric transducer of diameter $D$ vibrating at frequency $f$ in a medium of velocity $c$ can be modeled as a piston source. The on-axis pressure amplitude $P$ at a distance $x$ from the transducer is given by:

$$P(x) = 2 P_0 \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi}{\lambda} \left[ \sqrt{D^2/4 + x^2} - x \right] \right)$$

### Step 2: Identifying the Last Axial Maximum

The last maximum on-axis pressure occurs when the bracketed quantity equals $\lambda/2$. Setting:

$$\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} - N = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

### Step 3: Solving for $N$

$$\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} = N + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$D^2/4 + N^2 = N^2 + N\lambda + \lambda^2/4$$

The $N^2$ terms cancel:

$$D^2/4 = N\lambda + \lambda^2/4$$

For $D \gg \lambda$ (typical industrial probe), $\lambda^2/4$ is negligible:

$$D^2/4 \approx N\lambda$$

Solving:

$$N \approx \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$$

### Step 4: Expressing in terms of Frequency

Since $\lambda = c/f$:

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example (Steel, 5 MHz, 19 mm probe)

$$N = \frac{(0.019)^2 \times 5 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5920}$$

Computing numerator:
- $(0.019)^2 = 0.000361$ m²
- $0.000361 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 1805$ m·Hz

$$N = \frac{1805}{4 \times 5920} = \frac{1805}{23680} = 0.0762 \text{ m} = 76.2 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: For a 19 mm, 5 MHz probe in steel, the near field is ~76 mm long. In **contact testing**, the part begins at $x = 0$, so the inspection zone falls within the complex near-field pattern → causing **signal-to-noise issues and near-surface dead-zone** problems. In **immersion testing**, the probe is set at $x \approx N$ in water → wave enters part already in the far-field → **clean, interpretable signal**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Water-Path Refraction Angle for Immersion Testing

### Step 1: Geometry of an Immersion Setup

When a longitudinal wave travels from water (velocity $c_W$) into steel (velocity $c_L$) at normal probe-face incidence, it refracts according to Snell's law. The probe axis is vertical; the part is horizontal below the probe at water path distance $w$.

### Step 2: Apply Snell's Law

$$\frac{\sin \theta_W}{\sin \theta_L} = \frac{c_W}{c_L}$$

For a normal-incidence immersion probe ($\theta_W = 0$): $\theta_L = 0$ → straight-line transmission.

### Step 3: For an Angle Beam in Immersion (e.g., 45° Shear Wave in Steel)

A special immersion probe with a built-in wedge generates an angle $\theta_W$ in water. To get $\theta_L = 45°$ in steel:

$$\sin \theta_W = \frac{c_W}{c_L} \sin 45° = \frac{1497}{5920} \times 0.7071 = 0.1788$$

$$\theta_W = \arcsin(0.1788) = 10.3°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: To get a 45° shear wave in steel, the water-angle only needs to be ~10° → this is the principle behind **immersion angle-beam probes**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Detectable Flaw Size (Contact vs Immersion)

The minimum detectable flaw size $a_{min}$ depends on signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and the acoustic pressure reflected from the flaw. For a flaw of area $A_f$ in a far-field beam of cross-section $A_b$:

$$\frac{P_{flaw}}{P_{incident}} = \Gamma \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_b}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the reflection coefficient of the flaw interface. For SNR ≥ 3 (minimum detectable):

$$a_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot A_b}{\Gamma}}$$

**Comparison**:
- **Contact testing**: $A_b$ is small (focused beam), but SNR is lower → larger $a_{min}$
- **Immersion testing**: Water path allows **focused immersion probes** with very tight $A_b$ at the focal point → **smaller $a_{min}$ possible** (down to 50 µm flaw in some aerospace applications)

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Parameter | Contact Testing | Immersion Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Setup time | **Low** (1–2 min) | High (15–60 min) |
| Operator skill required | High | Moderate |
| Throughput | Low (manual) | High (automated) |
| Capital cost | Low (< ₹ 5 lakh) | High (> ₹ 50 lakh) |
| Portability | Excellent | Poor |
| Near-surface flaw detection | Poor (dead-zone) | Excellent |
| Couplant consistency | Variable | Excellent (water bath) |
| Complex geometry | Difficult | Easy (manipulators) |
| Couplant cost | Moderate (gel/oil) | Low (water) |
| Probe wear | High (replaced frequently) | Low |
| Part size limit | Unlimited (large parts) | Limited by tank size |
| Production-line integration | Difficult | Excellent |
| Field inspection | Excellent | Not practical |
| Best suited for | Welds, in-service parts | Lab, production, aerospace |
| Typical frequency | 2–5 MHz | 5–25 MHz |
| Typical probe diameter | 6–25 mm | 6–19 mm |

## 3.5 Squirt Testing (Bridge Method)

A hybrid called **"Squirt Testing"** or **"Bubbler Testing"** uses a localized water jet (or water column) between the probe and the part. This combines the **portability of contact testing** with the **coupling consistency of immersion testing**.

**Operating principle**:
- A small nozzle creates a standing water column (typically 25–100 mm long, 5–10 mm diameter) between probe and part.
- The probe is held above the part; water flows continuously through the column.
- Advantages: no tank required, less sensitive to surface roughness than contact, consistent coupling.
- Used for **online inspection of large cylindrical parts** (e.g., engine bores, pipe ID).
