# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define contact testing in ultrasonic NDT. What is the role of the couplant?

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

Contact testing is an ultrasonic testing method in which the probe is placed **directly on the test surface** through a thin film of liquid couplant (typically oil, glycerin, or cellulose gel, 0.025–0.25 mm thick).

The role of the couplant is to **eliminate the air gap** between the probe face and the test surface. Since air has a very low acoustic impedance (Z ≈ 430 kg/m²·s), over 99.9% of ultrasonic energy is reflected at any probe–air interface. The couplant's impedance (Z ≈ 1.2–1.5 × 10⁶ kg/m²·s) is much closer to steel (Z ≈ 46 × 10⁶ kg/m²·s), enabling ~97% acoustic transmission into the part.

**[Mark Distribution: Definition 1 + Couplant role 1 + Quantitative justification 1]**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any four advantages of immersion ultrasonic testing over contact testing.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

1. **Consistent coupling**: Water bath provides uniform, repeatable coupling independent of operator skill.
2. **Better near-surface flaw detection**: Water path allows transducer ringing to decay before the wave enters the part, eliminating the dead-zone problem.
3. **Automation**: Mechanized X-Y-Z scanners enable high-speed, repeatable C-scan imaging suitable for 100% production inspection.
4. **Longer probe life**: No abrasive contact → wear plate is not consumed → probe parameters remain stable over time.

(Any four valid points, 0.75 marks each.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the contact testing method of ultrasonic testing with a neat block diagram. Discuss the advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 19 mm diameter, 5 MHz longitudinal probe is used in pulse-echo contact testing of a steel plate (c_L = 5920 m/s). Calculate: (i) the near-field length, (ii) the wavelength, (iii) the time taken for the backwall echo to return from a 50 mm thick plate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a): Contact Testing Method (7 marks)

**Definition** [1 mark]: Contact testing is a UT method where the probe is hand-held or fixture-mounted directly on the test surface, using a thin liquid couplant layer (0.025–0.25 mm) to transmit ultrasound into the part.

**Block Diagram** [2 marks]:

```
Pulser → Probe → Couplant → Specimen → Reflected Echo → Probe → Receiver → Display
       (Transmit)           (Front surface, Flaw, Backwall)        (A-scan CRT)
```

(Describe as: Pulser excites piezo crystal → probe vibrates → couplant transmits wave → wave reflects from front surface, internal flaws, and backwall → echoes return through couplant to probe → receiver amplifies → A-scan display shows Time vs Amplitude.)

**Pulse-Echo vs Through-Transmission** [1 mark]:
- **Pulse-Echo (PE)**: Single probe acts as transmitter and receiver. Most common.
- **Through-Transmission (TT)**: Two probes, one on each side of the part. Better for thin sheets and attenuation measurement.

**Couplant** [1 mark]: Oil, glycerin, water, cellulose gel. Must have low viscosity and acoustic impedance close to the test material.

**Advantages** [1 mark]:
- Portable, field-deployable
- Low cost
- Quick setup
- Suitable for large/immobile structures

**Limitations** [1 mark]:
- Surface must be smooth
- Operator-dependent
- Dead-zone problem near front surface
- Probe wear

**Applications**: Welds, in-service pressure vessels, rail axles, pipelines, bridge girders.

---

#### Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given**:
- Probe diameter $D = 19$ mm $= 0.019$ m
- Frequency $f = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Longitudinal velocity in steel $c_L = 5920$ m/s
- Plate thickness $T = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m

**(i) Wavelength** [2 marks]:

$$\lambda = \frac{c_L}{f} = \frac{5920}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.184 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Near-Field Length** [2 marks]:

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c_L} = \frac{(0.019)^2 \times 5 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5920}$$

Numerator: $(0.019)^2 = 3.61 \times 10^{-4}$ m²; $\times 5 \times 10^6 = 1805$

$$N = \frac{1805}{23680} = 0.0762 \text{ m} = 76.2 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Time for Backwall Echo** [3 marks]:

Round-trip distance $= 2T = 2 \times 0.050 = 0.100$ m

$$\Delta t = \frac{2T}{c_L} = \frac{0.100}{5920} = 1.689 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 16.89 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Final Answers**: $\lambda = 1.184$ mm; $N = 76.2$ mm; $\Delta t = 16.89$ μs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the principle and procedure of immersion ultrasonic testing. With a neat diagram, describe the components of an immersion UT system. List four advantages of immersion testing. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An immersion probe of 12.7 mm diameter and 10 MHz frequency is used to test an aluminum plate (c_L = 6320 m/s) submerged in water (c_W = 1497 m/s). The water path is 75 mm. Calculate: (i) the near-field length in water, (ii) the wavelength in water and in aluminum, (iii) the time-of-flight for a flaw echo from a depth of 8 mm inside the aluminum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a): Immersion Testing (7 marks)

**Principle** [1 mark]: Both probe and specimen are submerged in a water bath. Water acts as the couplant. Probe is held at a fixed standoff distance (water path) above the part.

**Procedure** [2 marks]:
1. Fill tank with degassed, deionized water.
2. Mount specimen on fixture, submerge.
3. Mount probe on X-Y-Z manipulator.
4. Set water path distance so probe operates in far field.
5. Pulse probe and observe A-scan for front-surface echo.
6. Mechanically scan in raster pattern.
7. Record A-scan / B-scan / C-scan data.
8. Interpret and report flaws.

**Block Diagram** [2 marks]:

```
Water Tank (Degassed Water)
   |
   v
Immersion Probe ← Pulser-Receiver
   |
   v
Water Path → Specimen → Flaw → Reflection → Probe → Receiver
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                            Display / DAQ
```

**Components**: Water tank, immersion probe (often focused), manipulator (X-Y-Z or rotary), pulser-receiver, data acquisition system, display, water treatment system.

**Advantages** [2 marks]:
- Consistent, operator-independent coupling.
- Excellent near-surface flaw detection (no dead zone).
- Automation possible → high throughput.
- Complex geometries inspectable.
- Longer probe life.

---

#### Solution to B(b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given**:
- $D = 12.7$ mm $= 0.0127$ m
- $f = 10$ MHz
- $c_W = 1497$ m/s
- $c_{Al} = 6320$ m/s
- Water path $w = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- Flaw depth $d = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m

**(i) Near-field length in water** [2 marks]:

$$N_W = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c_W} = \frac{(0.0127)^2 \times 10 \times 10^6}{4 \times 1497}$$

Numerator: $(0.0127)^2 = 1.6129 \times 10^{-4}$ m²; $\times 10^7 = 1612.9$

$$N_W = \frac{1612.9}{5988} = 0.2693 \text{ m} = 269.3 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Wavelengths** [2 marks]:

In water:
$$\lambda_W = \frac{c_W}{f} = \frac{1497}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.497 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1497 \text{ mm}$$

In aluminum:
$$\lambda_{Al} = \frac{c_{Al}}{f} = \frac{6320}{10 \times 10^6} = 6.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.632 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Time-of-flight for flaw echo** [3 marks]:

Round-trip distance = $2 w + 2 d$ (down and back through water + down and back through aluminum to flaw):

$$\text{Total distance} = 2 \times 0.075 + 2 \times 0.008 = 0.150 + 0.016 = 0.166 \text{ m}$$

Time through water:
$$t_W = \frac{2 w}{c_W} = \frac{0.150}{1497} = 1.002 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100.2 \text{ \mu s}$$

Time through aluminum (to flaw and back):
$$t_{Al} = \frac{2 d}{c_{Al}} = \frac{0.016}{6320} = 2.532 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.532 \text{ \mu s}$$

Total time-of-flight:
$$t_{total} = 100.2 + 2.532 = 102.73 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Final Answers**: $N_W = 269.3$ mm; $\lambda_W = 0.1497$ mm; $\lambda_{Al} = 0.632$ mm; $t_{total} = 102.73$ μs.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in UT Method Questions**

1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in pulse-echo depth calculation**: Flaw depth $d = (c \cdot \Delta t)/2$, NOT $c \cdot \Delta t$. Examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for this.
2. **Confusing "near-field in water" with "near-field in steel"**: The same probe has DIFFERENT near-field lengths in different media because $c$ differs. Always recompute $N$ using the medium's velocity.
3. **Unit inconsistency**: Use SI units (m, m/s, Hz) consistently. Mixing mm with m/s causes numerical disasters.
4. **Not mentioning the couplant's role**: A contact-testing answer without explicitly stating "couplant eliminates air gap, enabling acoustic energy transfer" is incomplete. Loss: 1 mark.
5. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows or labels**: Unlabeled diagrams fetch 0 marks out of 2.
6. **Confusing Pulse-Echo with Through-Transmission**: PE uses ONE probe (transmit + receive); TT uses TWO probes. This is a frequent 7-mark question pitfall.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Core Concepts

- [x] **Contact testing** = probe on part + couplant; manual, portable, field use.
- [x] **Immersion testing** = probe and part in water bath; mechanized, lab/production use.
- [x] **Couplant is mandatory** because air's acoustic impedance is 100,000× too low.
- [x] **Near-field length** $N = D^2 f / (4c)$ — critical for transducer placement.
- [x] **Dead zone** in contact testing is the near-surface region (typically 3–10 mm) where flaws are masked by transducer ringing.
- [x] **Immersion solves the dead-zone problem** by providing a water path standoff.
- [x] **Snell's law** governs refraction at water-steel interface for angle-beam immersion.

### ✅ Numerical Must-Knows

- [x] $c_L$ (steel) = 5920 m/s; $c_L$ (aluminum) = 6320 m/s; $c_W$ = 1497 m/s.
- [x] Round-trip time = $2 \times$ (one-way path).
- [x] Near-field depends on $D^2$, $f$, and inversely on $c$.
- [x] Higher $f$ → smaller $\lambda$ → better resolution but higher attenuation.

### ✅ Method-Selection Quick Rules

- [x] **Field / large / irregular → Contact**
- [x] **Production / lab / small / complex → Immersion**
- [x] **Near-surface flaw critical → Immersion**
- [x] **Welds / in-service → Contact (manual) or Phased Array**
- [x] **Aerospace composite C-scan → Immersion (mandatory)**

### ✅ Top 5 KTU-Asked Concepts

1. Couplant role + transmission coefficient derivation.
2. Near-field length formula + numerical (always asked).
3. Pulse-echo vs Through-transmission comparison.
4. Contact vs Immersion advantages/limitations.
5. Snell's law application in immersion angle-beam probes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "CONTACT"**:
> **C**ouplant mandatory, **O**n-site/field, **N**ear-surface dead-zone, **T**ransducer wears, **A**utomation not possible, **C**heap & portable, **T**hrough-transmission alternate mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "IMMERSION"**:
> **I**n water bath, **M**echanized scanning, **M**ulti-axis capable, **E**xcellent near-surface, **R**obust C-scan, **S**ensitive to small flaws, **I**deal for aerospace, **O**nline production, **N**o dead zone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Beam, Straight Beam, and Angle Beam Testing
Dual Crystal Probe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dual Crystal Probe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dual Crystal Probe** (also widely known as a **Twin Crystal Probe**, **TR (Transmit–Receive) Probe**, or **SE (Separate Exciter and Sensor) Probe**) is a specialized ultrasonic transducer assembly that houses **two independent piezoelectric crystals** within a single housing — one dedicated to **transmitting** the ultrasonic pulse into the test piece, and the other dedicated to **receiving** the reflected echoes from internal flaws or the back-wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dual crystal probes are explicitly listed under the ultrasonic testing (UT) module of OEMET724 because they solve the **"dead zone"** problem that plagues conventional single-element normal probes. They are the industry-standard tool for **near-surface flaw detection** and **corrosion thickness mapping**.

The two crystals are physically separated by an **acoustic barrier** (a sound-absorbing wedge or damping block), and their axes are tilted slightly toward each other so that their sound beams **intersect at a predetermined focal zone** inside the test material.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a foggy swimming pool, trying to locate a coin dropped at the bottom.

- **Single Crystal Probe (Mono-element):** You use *one* person who shouts "Marco!" and then immediately cups his ears to listen for "Polo!". Because his own shout is deafening, he cannot hear any echoes that return during the first second — he is "deaf" to anything very close to the surface.
- **Dual Crystal Probe:** You use *two* people — one dedicated **shouter** and one dedicated **listener**, standing shoulder-to-shoulder but facing slightly toward each other. The shouter yells continuously, while the listener (shielded by a sound blanket) focuses entirely on hearing the faint echo. Together, they can hear coins that are barely an inch below the water — including coins right at the surface.

This is exactly the operating principle of the dual crystal probe: **one crystal sends, the other crystal receives**, with physical isolation preventing electrical cross-talk and acoustic shielding preventing direct sound leakage.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Operational Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | **2 MHz to 25 MHz** (most common: **5 MHz**) |
| Crystal Diameter | 6 mm – 25 mm (typical **10 mm – 20 mm**) |
| Acoustic Barrier Material | Cork, rubber, tungsten-loaded epoxy, or air gap |
| Wear Plate (Delay Medium) | Cross-linked polystyrene, Rexolite, or thin stainless steel |
| Focal Zone Depth (typical) | Adjustable from **5 mm to 50 mm** depending on wedge angle |
| Beam Angle in Steel | Typically **0° to 15°** (often near-normal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two crystals, not one with a switcher?** A single crystal can technically be switched between transmit and receive modes (pulse-echo mode), but it suffers from **ringdown** — the crystal keeps vibrating for a few microseconds after the transmit pulse, masking any near-surface echoes. The dual crystal design *physically* and *electrically* isolates the receiver from this ringdown noise.

## 1.4 Two Principal Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variant 1 — Contact Type Dual Crystal Probe:** Both crystals are in direct contact (through a thin wear plate) with the test surface. Used for **thickness gauging and corrosion surveys** at frequencies 2–10 MHz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variant 2 — Delay Line Type Dual Crystal Probe:** Each crystal has its own replaceable plastic or liquid delay line (sound column) before contacting the test piece. Used for **high-resolution near-surface flaw detection** at frequencies 5–25 MHz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Step-by-Step Logic)

The dual crystal probe is built from the following components, each solving a specific problem:

1. **Transmitting Crystal (T):** Driven by a high-voltage spike (typically **100–400 V**) from the pulser. It converts electrical energy into a longitudinal ultrasonic wave.
2. **Receiving Crystal (R):** A separate piezo-element tuned to the same resonance frequency as T. It converts returning mechanical echoes back into electrical signals fed to the amplifier/display.
3. **Acoustic Barrier / Sound Absorber:** A wedge of damping material (cork, rubber, or composite) placed *between* T and R. It performs two roles:
   - Prevents the transmit pulse from leaking directly into the receive crystal (electromagnetic and acoustic isolation).
   - Mechanically damps back vibrations, shortening ringdown time.
4. **Wedge Angles (α_T and α_R):** The transmitting and receiving wedges are cut at specific angles so that the sound beams **converge** at a defined depth in the test piece. This creates a **focal zone** where sensitivity is maximum.
5. **Wear Face / Delay Block:** The bottom protective layer that contacts the test piece. It may be a thin hard plastic (contact type) or a longer replaceable delay line (delay line type).
6. **Common Housing:** Aluminum or polymer casing holding all components in rigid geometric alignment.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "V-Path" Sound Field

When the pulser fires crystal T, the ultrasonic beam travels through the transmitting wedge, refracts into the test material, and travels downward. Simultaneously, the receiving crystal R is "armed" and listening.

Any reflector (flaw, back-wall, lamination) within the **intersection zone** of the two beams sends an echo back. This echo travels upward, refracts into the receiving wedge, and strikes crystal R, producing the signal on the CRT/LCD.

The beams form a characteristic **"V" or "X" pattern** in the material. The probe is **most sensitive** at the point where the two beams cross — this is the **focal point (F)**.

### Why the V-Path Eliminates the Dead Zone

In a single-crystal probe, the dead zone arises because the same crystal must recover from its own transmit pulse before it can detect echoes. During recovery (ringdown, typically 1–5 µs), any echo arriving from very shallow depths is masked.

With the dual crystal design:

$$\text{Echo Path Length}_{\text{Dual}} = \text{Path in T-wedge} + \text{Path in material} + \text{Path in R-wedge}$$

$$\text{Delay introduced} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{wedge}}{v_{wedge}} + \frac{2 \cdot t_{couplant}}{v_{couplant}}$$

Because the echo must travel **up through the receiving wedge** before reaching the receive crystal, the receive crystal is electrically and acoustically **isolated from the transmit event** by both the acoustic barrier and the geometric separation. Near-surface flaws located within millimeters of the surface can therefore be resolved without being masked by ringdown.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The acoustic barrier does NOT stop the test signal from reaching the receiver — it only stops the *transmit pulse vibration* from leaking. The actual echoes still travel via the material path.

## 2.3 Sound Field Geometry and the Focal Zone

For a dual crystal probe with wedge angles α_T and α_R (measured from vertical), the **beam intersection depth (f)** below the contact surface is given by:

$$f = \frac{s}{\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R}$$

where **s** is the horizontal separation between the centers of T and R at the contact surface.

The **focal zone length (depth of field)** depends on the beam diameters and convergence geometry. A small s produces a shallow, narrow focal zone (good for thin materials); a larger s produces a deeper, broader focal zone.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave velocity = frequency × wavelength | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $\frac{\sin \alpha_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \alpha_2}{v_2}$ | Snell's Law (refraction between wedge and test material) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_{steel} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_{steel}}{v_{wedge}} \sin \alpha\right)$ | Refracted beam angle inside steel | degrees |
| 4 | $f_{depth} = \dfrac{s}{\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R}$ | Focal depth of dual crystal probe | mm |
| 5 | $DZ_{mono} \approx \frac{v \cdot \tau_{ringdown}}{2}$ | Dead zone of single-crystal probe | mm |
| 6 | $DZ_{dual} \approx \frac{v_{steel} \cdot t_{prop,wedge}}{2}$ | Effective dead zone of dual probe (greatly reduced) | mm |
| 7 | $R_{resolution} = \dfrac{v}{2 \cdot f_{center}}$ | Theoretical axial resolution | mm |
| 8 | $P_{beam}(z) = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 + (\lambda z / a)^2}$ | On-axis pressure decay (approximate) | Pa |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{reflected}}{P_{incident}}$ | Reflection coefficient at flaw | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{d_{delay}}{v_{delay}}$ | Time delay introduced by delay line | µs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** All angles in the formula sheet must be in **radians** when used inside trigonometric functions in derivations. The refraction equation **only applies when the wave crosses a medium boundary** — not within a single medium.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical** | Corrosion-thickness mapping of storage tank floors and pipe walls |
| **Aerospace** | Near-surface fatigue crack detection in aluminum airframes |
| **Nuclear** | Inspection of reactor pressure vessel cladding (3–6 mm) |
| **Marine** | Hull plate thickness surveys without grit blasting |
| **Railway** | Wheel tread and axle inspection at high speeds |
| **Power Generation** | Boiler tube wall thickness monitoring (online) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Beam Intersection Depth (Focal Point)

**Given:** Two identical piezo-crystals T and R, separated by a horizontal distance $s$ at the wedge bottom surface. Wedge half-angles $\alpha_T$ and $\alpha_R$ measured from the probe's central vertical axis.

**To Find:** The depth $f$ below the contact face at which the two refracted beams intersect.

**Step 1 — Define coordinate system.**
Place the origin at the midpoint between the centers of T and R on the contact surface. The center of T is at $x = -s/2$ and R is at $x = +s/2$. The downward direction is $+z$.

**Step 2 — Equation of the transmitted beam axis.**
The transmit beam travels downward at angle $\alpha_T$ from vertical, originating at $x = -s/2$. Its trajectory in the $xz$-plane is:

$$\text{Beam T: } x = -\frac{s}{2} + z \tan \alpha_T$$

**Step 3 — Equation of the received beam axis (when traced backward from the receiver).**
The receive beam converges toward the focal point at angle $\alpha_R$ from vertical, arriving at $x = +s/2$ on the surface:

$$\text{Beam R: } x = \frac{s}{2} - z \tan \alpha_R$$

**Step 4 — Find the intersection by equating the two equations.**

$$-\frac{s}{2} + z \tan \alpha_T = \frac{s}{2} - z \tan \alpha_R$$

**Step 5 — Collect terms containing $z$ on one side.**

$$z \tan \alpha_T + z \tan \alpha_R = \frac{s}{2} + \frac{s}{2}$$

$$z (\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R) = s$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the focal depth $z \equiv f$.**

$$\boxed{f = \frac{s}{\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R}}$$

**Interpretation:** When $\alpha_T = \alpha_R = \alpha$ (a common symmetric design):

$$f_{sym} = \frac{s}{2 \tan \alpha}$$

The deeper the focal point needed, the larger the wedge angle $\alpha$ must be (or the smaller the horizontal separation $s$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Refracted Beam Angle Inside Steel

**Given:** A longitudinal wave traveling in a Perspex (plexiglass) wedge at longitudinal velocity $v_{p} = 2730$ m/s, striking the steel interface at an incident angle $\alpha_{wedge}$ from normal. Steel longitudinal velocity is $v_{s} = 5920$ m/s.

**Step 1 — State Snell's Law for two media.**

$$\frac{\sin \alpha_{wedge}}{v_{p}} = \frac{\sin \theta_{steel}}{v_{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the refracted angle.**

$$\sin \theta_{steel} = \frac{v_{s}}{v_{p}} \sin \alpha_{wedge}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.** Take $\alpha_{wedge} = 18°$:

$$\sin \theta_{steel} = \frac{5920}{2730} \times \sin 18°$$

$$\sin \theta_{steel} = 2.169 \times 0.309 = 0.670$$

$$\theta_{steel} = \sin^{-1}(0.670) \approx 42.1°$$

**Step 4 — Observation.** Because $v_s > v_p$, the refracted angle is **larger** than the incident angle. For typical dual-crystal probe designs, wedge angles are chosen such that the steel-side refraction angle is between **35° and 50°** in compression-wave mode, creating a controlled V-path.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Effective Dead Zone Reduction

**Given:** A 5 MHz single-crystal probe has a ringdown time of approximately $\tau_{ring} = 2.5 \; \mu s$ in contact mode.

**Step 1 — Dead zone for single crystal probe:**

$$DZ_{single} = \frac{v_{steel} \cdot \tau_{ring}}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$DZ_{single} = \frac{0.0148}{2} = 7.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Dead zone for dual crystal probe with 12 mm Perspex delay wedges:**

$$t_{prop,wedge} = \frac{d_{wedge}}{v_{wedge}} = \frac{0.012}{2730} = 4.4 \; \mu s$$

The round-trip through the delay wedge plus the acoustic barrier isolation effectively reduces the dead zone to roughly:

$$DZ_{dual} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and interpretation.**

$$\frac{DZ_{single}}{DZ_{dual}} \approx \frac{7.4}{0.8} \approx 9.25$$

The dual crystal probe reduces the dead zone by approximately **one order of magnitude**, which is the entire reason this probe geometry exists.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Single vs. Dual Crystal Probe

| Performance Criterion | Single Crystal (Mono) | Dual Crystal (Twin/TR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead Zone** | Large (5–15 mm typical) | Very small (0.5–2 mm) |
| **Near-Surface Resolution** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Beam Path in Material** | Single straight or angled path | V-shaped intersection path |
| **Focal Zone** | Fixed by crystal diameter & freq. | Adjustable by wedge angle design |
| **Electrical Isolation** | Switched (pulser/receiver) | Naturally isolated (separate crystals) |
| **Sensitivity to Small Flaws** | Good at depth | Excellent at focal depth, weaker elsewhere |
| **Penetration Depth** | Higher (single crystal at resonance) | Limited (V-path loses energy) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cable Configuration** | Single coaxial | Dual coaxial (or special 4-wire) |
| **Typical Application** | General UT, weld inspection | Corrosion, near-surface flaw, thin sections |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Construction of a Dual Crystal Probe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PULSER UNIT"] -->|"High voltage spike 100 to 400 V"| B["TRANSMIT CRYSTAL T"]
    B -->|"Piezoelectric conversion"| C["ACOUSTIC WEDGE T alpha_T"]
    C -->|"Refracted longitudinal wave"| D["TEST MATERIAL STEEL"]
    D -->|"Reflected echo from flaw or backwall"| E["ACOUSTIC WEDGE R alpha_R"]
    E -->|"Mechanical echo"| F["RECEIVE CRYSTAL R"]
    F -->|"Electrical signal"| G["AMPLIFIER AND DISPLAY"]
    H["ACOUSTIC BARRIER Cork or Rubber"] -.->|"Isolates T from R"| B
    H -.->|"Isolates T from R"| F
    I["WEAR FACE Contact surface"] --> D
    J["COMMON HOUSING Aluminum"] --- A
    J --- B
    J --- H
    J --- F
    J --- G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — V-Path Sound Field Inside Test Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Surface of Test Piece"] --- B["Beam T axis at alpha_T from vertical"]
    A --- C["Beam R axis at alpha_R from vertical"]
    B -->|"Down and inward"| D["FOCAL POINT F at depth f"]
    C -->|"Down and inward"| D
    D -->|"Maximum sensitivity zone"| E["REFLECTOR flaw or backwall"]
    E -.->|"Echo returns upward"| C
    E -.->|"Echo returns upward"| B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operational Flow Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1 Apply couplant and place probe on test surface"] --> S2["Step 2 Pulser fires high voltage spike"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Transmit crystal T launches ultrasonic pulse"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Pulse refracts into test material at angle"]
    S4 --> S5{"Step 5 Does pulse encounter a reflector inside the V zone?"}
    S5 -->|"No"| S6["Step 6 No echo received Signal stays at baseline"]
    S5 -->|"Yes"| S7["Step 7 Echo travels upward into receive wedge R"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 Receive crystal R converts echo to voltage"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9 Amplifier boosts signal and displays peak on screen"]
    S6 --> S10["Step 10 Inspector evaluates amplitude and time of flight"]
    S9 --> S10
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison Block: Contact vs Delay Line Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DUAL CRYSTAL PROBE TYPES"] --> B["CONTACT TYPE"]
    A --> C["DELAY LINE TYPE"]
    B --> B1["Thin wear face 0.5 to 1 mm"]
    B1 --> B2["Frequency 2 to 10 MHz"]
    B2 --> B3["Used for corrosion thickness 3 to 50 mm"]
    C --> C1["Replaceable plastic column 10 to 25 mm long"]
    C1 --> C2["Frequency 5 to 25 MHz"]
    C2 --> C3["Used for near surface flaw detection within 0 to 5 mm"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Visual Tip:** When asked to "draw" or "sketch" a dual crystal probe in the exam, always include the **acoustic barrier wedge between T and R** and label the **wedge angles** α_T and α_R. These two features distinguish the diagram from a single crystal probe drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a dual crystal probe. Mention any two of its advantages over a single crystal probe.**

**Model Answer:**
A dual crystal probe is an ultrasonic transducer that contains two separate piezoelectric elements within the same housing — one for transmitting and one for receiving ultrasonic waves — separated by an acoustic isolating barrier.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Near-zero dead zone:** Because the receive crystal is electrically and acoustically isolated from the transmit pulse, near-surface flaws (within 0.5–2 mm of the surface) can be detected without being masked by ringdown noise.
2. **Adjustable focal zone:** By choosing appropriate wedge angles, the V-shaped beam intersection (focal point) can be positioned at a specific depth, providing maximum sensitivity at the depth of interest — particularly useful in corrosion thickness gauging.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the function of the acoustic barrier in a dual crystal probe?**

**Model Answer:**
The acoustic barrier is a wedge-shaped piece of damping material (cork, rubber, or composite) placed between the transmitting and receiving crystals.

Its functions are:
1. To **prevent direct acoustic leakage** of the high-energy transmit pulse from crystal T into crystal R, which would otherwise saturate the receiver.
2. To provide **mechanical damping** of the transmitting crystal's rear face, reducing ringdown time and thereby enabling detection of echoes from very shallow defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a dual crystal ultrasonic probe. Mention its applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A dual crystal probe consists of two independent piezoelectric crystals (T and R), each mounted on its own damping wedge. The two wedges are cut at angles α_T and α_R from the vertical and converge so that their sound beams intersect inside the test material. A sound-absorbing acoustic barrier (cork or rubber) is placed between the two crystals to isolate them. Both crystals are housed in a single aluminum casing with a common wear face at the bottom. Two coaxial cables (or a special 4-wire cable) connect T to the pulser and R to the receiver/amplifier.

**Working:**
1. The pulser delivers a short high-voltage electrical pulse to crystal T.
2. Crystal T converts this pulse into a mechanical ultrasonic wave, which travels through the T-wedge, refracts into the test material, and propagates at an angle defined by Snell's law.
3. Meanwhile, crystal R is electrically and acoustically isolated and remains in a "listening" state.
4. If the transmit beam encounters a flaw or the back-wall within the V-shaped intersection zone, an echo is generated.
5. This echo travels upward, refracts into the R-wedge, and strikes crystal R.
6. Crystal R converts the mechanical echo into a small electrical signal, which is amplified and displayed on the CRT.
7. The inspector interprets the amplitude (for flaw size) and time-of-flight (for depth) on the screen.

**Applications:**
- Corrosion-thickness mapping of pipelines and storage tanks.
- Near-surface flaw detection in aircraft components.
- Online thickness monitoring of boiler tubes.
- Inspection of thin sheets, tubes, and plates.
- Weld overlay and clad material inspection.

---

**(b)** A dual crystal probe has its transmitting and receiving crystals separated horizontally by a center-to-center distance of **s = 20 mm**. The wedge angles are α_T = 8° and α_R = 7°. Calculate the depth of the focal point below the contact surface. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the focal depth formula:**

$$f = \frac{s}{\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$\tan 8° = 0.1405$$
$$\tan 7° = 0.1228$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$\tan \alpha_T + \tan \alpha_R = 0.1405 + 0.1228 = 0.2633$$

**Step 4 — Compute the focal depth.**

$$f = \frac{20}{0.2633}$$

$$\boxed{f = 75.96 \text{ mm} \approx 76 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The maximum sensitivity zone lies about **76 mm** below the contact surface. For flaw detection in components thinner than ~30 mm, this probe would not be appropriate; a dual crystal probe with smaller wedge angles or smaller s should be selected.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of dead zone in ultrasonic testing. How does the dual crystal probe overcome the dead-zone limitation of a single crystal probe? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Dead Zone:**
The dead zone is the region immediately beneath the transducer surface from which echoes **cannot be detected** because they arrive at the crystal while it is still reverberating (ringing) from the transmit pulse. The dead zone depth is given by:

$$DZ = \frac{v_{steel} \cdot \tau_{ringdown}}{2}$$

For a typical 5 MHz contact probe with 2.5 µs ringdown, the dead zone in steel is approximately **7.4 mm** — meaning no flaw in the first 7–8 mm below the surface can be reliably detected.

**How the Dual Crystal Probe Overcomes Dead Zone:**

1. **Physical Separation:** The transmit and receive crystals are physically separated by an acoustic barrier. The receive crystal never sees the transmit pulse directly.
2. **Dedicated Listening:** The receive crystal is not pulsing; it remains in a high-gain listening mode throughout, so it does not experience ringdown from a transmit event.
3. **Delay Wedge Path:** Any echo that returns from the test material must travel back up through the receiving wedge (10–25 mm of plastic). This added travel time means the echo arrives at the receive crystal *after* any transient switching noise has decayed.
4. **Result:** The effective dead zone is reduced from ~7 mm to **0.5–2 mm**, allowing detection of flaws right at the surface.

---

**(b)** An ultrasonic longitudinal wave travels through a Perspex wedge (v = 2730 m/s) and strikes a steel test piece (v = 5920 m/s). The angle of incidence in the Perspex is **20°** from normal. Calculate (i) the refracted angle in steel and (ii) the wavelength in steel if the operating frequency is **5 MHz**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law.**

$$\frac{\sin \alpha_{wedge}}{v_{wedge}} = \frac{\sin \theta_{steel}}{v_{steel}}$$

$$\sin \theta_{steel} = \frac{v_{steel}}{v_{wedge}} \cdot \sin \alpha_{wedge} = \frac{5920}{2730} \cdot \sin 20°$$

**Step 2 — Compute.**

$$\sin \theta_{steel} = 2.169 \times 0.342 = 0.742$$

$$\theta_{steel} = \sin^{-1}(0.742) = 47.9° \approx 48°$$

**(i) Refracted angle in steel ≈ 48° from normal.**

**Step 3 — Wavelength in steel.**

$$\lambda_{steel} = \frac{v_{steel}}{f} = \frac{5920}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\lambda_{steel} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{steel} \approx 1.18 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use Snell's Law with velocities**, not refractive indices. Many students write $\sin \alpha_1 = \sin \alpha_2$ which is **wrong** — that would be true only for identical media.
> 2. **Mixing up angles** — "angle of incidence" is always measured **from normal**, not from the surface. KTU expects this convention.
> 3. **Confusing the two wedge angles** in the focal depth formula. If only one angle is given and the probe is symmetric, use $f = s/(2\tan\alpha)$, **not** $f = s/\tan\alpha$.
> 4. **Drawing single-crystal probe instead of dual-crystal** in construction questions — students lose 2–3 marks immediately. Always draw **two crystals**, label them T and R, and show the **acoustic barrier** between them.
> 5. **Unit mistakes** in wavelength/focal-depth answers — always include units (mm or m).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dual Crystal Probe:**

- ☐ A dual crystal probe contains **two separate piezo-crystals** — one transmitter (T) and one receiver (R).
- ☐ An **acoustic barrier** (cork/rubber wedge) between T and R isolates them acoustically and electrically.
- ☐ The beams form a **V-shaped intersection** in the test material.
- ☐ The **focal depth formula** is $f = \dfrac{s}{\tan\alpha_T + \tan\alpha_R}$.
- ☐ For symmetric probes: $f_{sym} = \dfrac{s}{2\tan\alpha}$.
- ☐ **Primary advantage:** greatly reduced dead zone (0.5–2 mm vs. 5–15 mm for mono probes).
- ☐ **Primary application:** near-surface flaw detection and corrosion thickness measurement.
- ☐ **Types:** Contact type (low–medium frequency, 2–10 MHz) and Delay-line type (high frequency, 5–25 MHz).
- ☐ **Snell's Law:** $\dfrac{\sin\alpha_1}{v_1} = \dfrac{\sin\alpha_2}{v_2}$ — must be used with **velocities**.
- ☐ **Wavelength in test material:** $\lambda = v/f$.
- ☐ **Two coaxial cables** (or 4-wire) connect T to pulser and R to receiver.
- ☐ Higher frequency ⇒ better resolution but lower penetration depth.
- ☐ The dual crystal probe **does not require an external TX/RX switch** because the crystals are already physically separate.
- ☐ Limitation: lower maximum penetration depth than single crystal probes due to V-path energy loss.
- ☐ Inspectors must ensure proper **couplant application** at the wear face for both transmit and receive paths.
- ☐ When drawing in exams: **always label** T, R, acoustic barrier, wedge angles α_T, α_R, focal point F, and the V-path inside the material.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Testing Techniques: Resonance Testing, Through Transmission Technique, and Pulse Echo Testing Technique

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Testing Techniques: Resonance Testing, Through Transmission Technique, and Pulse Echo Testing Technique # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — The Three Principal Techniques **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** is a volumetric, non-destructive evaluation method that uses high-frequency mechanical vibrations (typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**, beyond human hearing range of 20 kHz) propagated through a material to detect surface and sub-surface defects, measure thickness, and characterize material properties. Among the many ultrasonic techniques, **Module 3** focuses on the three foundational inspection philosophies used universally in industry: | # | Technique | Signal Strategy | # of Transducers | |---|-----------|----------------|------------------| | 1 | **Resonance Testing** | Continuous wave, frequency-swept | 1 or 2 | | 2 | **Through Transmission** | Continuous / pulsed wave sent across | 2 (separate T & R) | | 3 | **Pulse Echo Testing** | Short pulse, listens for reflections | 1 (dual-role) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a high-weightage, frequently asked module because it forms the **theoretical backbone** of every commercial flaw detector used in aerospace, petrochemical, power, and ship-building industries. --- ## 1.2 Resonance Testing — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Resonance Testing is an ultrasonic NDT technique in which the test object is excited by a continuous-wave (CW) ultrasonic signal of variable frequency, and the thickness of the specimen is determined by identifying the discrete frequencies at which standing-wave resonance is established within the material.* **Intuitive Analogy — "The Drum Analogy":** Imagine tapping a metal drum at different speeds. At certain specific tap rates, the drum "rings" loudly — these are its natural frequencies. If you know the speed (frequency) at which it rings loudest, and you know the wave speed in the material, you can calculate the drum's depth (thickness). The material literally "sings" only when its physical thickness accommodates a whole number of half-wavelengths. **Key Physical Constant:** - The minimum measurable thickness in resonance is governed by the **half-wavelength rule**: $t_{min} = \lambda/2$, where $\lambda = v/f$. --- ## 1.3 Through Transmission Technique — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Through Transmission (or "Pitch-Catch") is an ultrasonic NDT method in which ultrasonic energy emitted by a transmitting probe on one side of the test piece is received by a separate receiving probe on the opposite side; the presence, size, and location of a defect is inferred from the attenuation or interruption of the received signal amplitude.* **Intuitive Analogy — "The Flashlight and Wall Analogy":** Picture shining a flashlight through a glass window onto the opposite wall. If the glass is clean, the wall lights up brightly. If someone places their hand inside the glass, the light on the wall dims or disappears. In Through Transmission, the flashlight is the **transmitter probe**, the wall is the **receiver probe**, and the hand inside is the **defect**. The brightness drop tells you *something is in the way* — but not exactly *where* or *how deep*. --- ## 1.4 Pulse Echo Testing — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Pulse Echo Testing is an ultrasonic NDT technique in which short-duration ultrasonic pulses are transmitted into the test material by a probe that also functions as the receiver, and the echoes reflected from the rear surface, front surface, or any internal discontinuity (defect) are displayed on a time-base to determine the location and size of the flaw.* **Intuitive Analogy — "The Bat Echolocation Analogy":** A bat in a dark cave screams a tiny "ping" (pulse) and listens for the echo. The time it takes for the echo to come back tells the bat how far the wall is. Pulse Echo UT does exactly the same thing — the probe sends a **microsecond pulse**, the **microsecond timer** measures the round-trip, and the screen plots the **echo as a peak** on a time line. A hidden defect in the material shows up as an *extra echo* appearing between the initial pulse and the back-wall echo. > [!TIP] > **Why this technique dominates industry:** Pulse Echo requires access to **only one side** of the component, which is critical for inspecting pressure vessels, storage tanks, and in-service pipelines where the back side is inaccessible. --- ## 1.5 The Couplant — Common to All Three Techniques | Parameter | Typical Value | |-----------|---------------| | Acoustic Impedance of Steel | $\approx 46 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | | Acoustic Impedance of Air | $\approx 0.0004 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | Because air has an acoustic impedance **115,000 times lower** than steel, ultrasound cannot cross the air gap. A **couplant** (gel, oil, water, glycerin) with impedance between the probe and test piece is mandatory. This is the "**gel on a doctor's ultrasound probe**" principle. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Resonance Testing — Theoretical Operation The principle rests on the **standing wave condition**. For continuous-wave ultrasound of frequency $f$ and wave speed $v$ in the material, the test piece behaves as a resonant cavity. A standing wave is produced only when the thickness $t$ is an integer multiple of half-wavelengths. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Sweep Generation:** The instrument sweeps the transmitter frequency from a low value to a high value (e.g., 1 MHz → 10 MHz). - **Step 2 — Standing Wave Build-up:** At specific frequencies, the incident and reflected waves superpose to form a stationary pattern (antinodes at the surfaces, nodes at half-thickness for odd harmonics). - **Step 3 — Resonance Detection:** The receiver amplitude shows sharp peaks on the CRT/screen at these specific frequencies. - **Step 4 — Thickness Computation:** Using the relation $t = nv/(2f)$, the thickness is calculated knowing $v$, the resonance number $n$, and the resonance frequency $f$. - **Step 5 — Validation:** Sweeping yields multiple resonances ($f_1$, $f_2$, $f_3$, ...). The ratios $f_2/f_1 = 2$, $f_3/f_1 = 3$ must hold — a powerful built-in verification. ### Why It Works — The "Why" Layer The two free surfaces of the test piece are stress-free (in a fluid or air-coupled UT) or fixed (in a rigidly clamped sample). These boundary conditions **force** the displacement amplitude to be maximum at the surfaces, which mathematically demands that an integer number of half-wavelengths fit exactly into the thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often forget that the **fundamental resonance** ($n = 1$) gives the *thinnest measurable piece*. Thicker pieces require lower fundamental frequencies, and if the piece is too thick, the required frequency falls below the practical UT range (< 0.5 MHz) and the technique fails. --- ## 2.2 Through Transmission — Theoretical Operation ### Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — Probe Alignment:** The transmitter and receiver are coaxially aligned on opposite faces of the test piece. - **Step 2 — Continuous / Pulsed Wave:** A known amplitude signal is sent through. - **Step 3 — Reference Reading:** With no defect, a "**reference signal**" amplitude $A_0$ is recorded. - **Step 4 — Scanning:** The probe pair is scanned along the surface. - **Step 5 — Attenuation Analysis:** Any region where the received amplitude drops below a threshold (e.g., $A < 0.5\,A_0$) indicates a defect. - **Step 6 — Optional Sizing:** The drop magnitude correlates with defect size; comparison with calibrated reference blocks yields quantitative size. ### Strengths and Limitations | Strength | Limitation | |----------|------------| | No "dead zone" issues | Needs access to both sides | | Less affected by surface roughness | Cannot give exact depth of defect | | Highly sensitive to laminar defects (e.g., lack of fusion) | Misalignment causes false indications | | Very high S/N ratio possible | Coupling needed on both sides | --- ## 2.3 Pulse Echo Testing — Theoretical Operation ### Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — Pulse Generation:** The pulser/receiver unit applies a high-voltage spike (e.g., 100–400 V) to the piezoelectric crystal for a few **nanoseconds**, generating a broadband pulse. - **Step 2 — Transmission:** The pulse enters the test material via couplant. - **Step 3 — Time Base Initiation:** A horizontal sweep (typically calibrated in mm or inches) begins on the CRT. - **Step 4 — Echo Returns:** Reflections from: - the **front surface** (initial pulse — used as reference), - any **internal defect**, - the **back surface** (back-wall echo). - **Step 5 — Display:** Peaks are displayed at times $t_f$, $t_d$, $t_b$ on an **A-scan** display. - **Step 6 — Defect Location:** Distance to defect = $d = v \cdot t_d / 2$ (the factor of 2 is because the pulse travels *to* the defect and *back*). - **Step 7 — Thickness:** $T = v \cdot t_b / 2$. ### Critical Concept — "Dead Zone" > [!WARNING] > The transducer continues to "ring" (reverberate) after the initial pulse for a duration called the **ring-down time**. During this time, it cannot detect echoes. This blind region immediately below the probe surface is called the **Dead Zone** ($DZ$), given by $DZ = v \cdot \tau / 2$ where $\tau$ is the pulse width. Surface defects inside this zone are **missed**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table verbatim.** These formulas appear in nearly every Part B (14-mark) question on Ultrasonic Testing. | # | Technique | Formula | Description | Units | |---|-----------|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | Resonance | $t = \dfrac{n v}{2 f_n}$ | Thickness from $n$-th resonance | m, with $f_n$ in Hz | | 2 | Resonance | $f_n = \dfrac{n v}{2 t}$ | $n$-th resonance frequency | Hz | | 3 | Resonance (Series) | $f_1 : f_2 : f_3 = 1 : 2 : 3$ | Harmonic spacing | dimensionless | | 4 | Pulse Echo (Defect) | $d = \dfrac{v \, t_d}{2}$ | Distance to defect | m | | 5 | Pulse Echo (Thickness) | $T = \dfrac{v \, t_b}{2}$ | Thickness from back-wall echo | m | | 6 | Pulse Echo (Dead Zone) | $DZ = \dfrac{v \, \tau}{2}$ | Minimum detectable depth | m | | 7 | All techniques | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Longitudinal wave speed in a solid (thin rod) | m/s | | 8 | All techniques | $v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\sigma)}{\rho(1+\sigma)(1-2\sigma)}}$ | Longitudinal wave speed (bulk) | m/s | | 9 | All techniques | $v_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}}$ | Transverse (shear) wave speed | m/s | | 10 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Ultrasonic wavelength | m | | 11 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot v$ | Impedance of medium | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | | 12 | Transmission Coefficient | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Energy transmitted across interface | dimensionless | > **Note on standard material constants (for steel, used in numericals):** > - $v_L \approx \mathbf{5920 \text{ m/s}}$ > - $v_T \approx \mathbf{3250 \text{ m/s}}$ > - $\rho \approx \mathbf{7850 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ --- ## 2.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix — Engineering Utility | Parameter | Resonance | Through Transmission | Pulse Echo | |-----------|-----------|---------------------|------------| | Typical frequency | 0.5 – 10 MHz | 1 – 10 MHz | 1 – 25 MHz | | Best for | Thickness of thin sheets | Laminar defects, porosity | Flaw location & sizing | | Depth info | No | No | Yes (primary advantage) | | One-sided access | Yes (1-probe mode) | No (both sides needed) | Yes (single side) | | Sensitivity to small defects | Low | High | High | | Equipment complexity | Moderate | High (2 probes) | Moderate (1 probe) | | Dead zone problem | No | No | **Yes** | | Industrial use | Wall thickness, tube wall | Billets, plates, adhesive bonds | Pressure vessels, welds, shafts | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Resonance Condition ### Starting Point: Standing Wave in a Plate Consider a plate of thickness $t$ with two parallel free surfaces. A continuous longitudinal ultrasonic wave of frequency $f$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on the front surface. The wave reflects off the back surface, travels back, reflects again, and so on. The incident and reflected waves superpose. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Incident wave:} \quad & y_1(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t) \\[4pt] \text{Reflected wave:} \quad & y_2(x,t) = A \sin(kx + \omega t) \\[4pt] \text{Superposition (resultant):} \quad & y(x,t) = y_1 + y_2 \\[4pt] & y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t) + A \sin(kx + \omega t) \end{aligned} $$ Apply the standard trigonometric identity: $\sin P + \sin Q = 2 \sin\left(\dfrac{P+Q}{2}\right) \cos\left(\dfrac{P-Q}{2}\right)$ $$ \begin{aligned} y(x,t) & = 2A \sin(kx) \cos(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **standing wave equation**. The spatial part is $2A \sin(kx)$, which must satisfy the free-surface boundary conditions. ### Applying the Free-Surface Boundary Condition For free surfaces, the displacement amplitude is **maximum** at the surfaces (antinode condition). Taking the front surface at $x = 0$ and the back surface at $x = t$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Antinode at } x = 0 \quad & \Rightarrow \quad \sin(0) = 0 \text{ (trivially satisfied)} \\[4pt] \text{Antinode at } x = t \quad & \Rightarrow \quad \sin(k t) = \pm 1 \\[4pt] \Rightarrow \quad & k t = \dfrac{n \pi}{2}, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots \text{ (odd fundamental modes)} \end{aligned} $$ For the most general resonance (allowing any integer mode number $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$), the full standing-wave condition in a plate with antinodes at both ends is: $$ \begin{aligned} k t & = n \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \\[6pt] \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot t & = n \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\[6pt] t & = n \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Resonance Formula Substituting $\lambda = v/f$: $$ \begin{aligned} t & = n \cdot \dfrac{v}{2 f} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,t = \dfrac{n v}{2 f_n}\,} \end{aligned} $$ where: - $t$ = thickness of the test piece (m) - $n$ = harmonic number (1, 2, 3, ...) - $v$ = ultrasonic wave speed in the material (m/s) - $f_n$ = frequency of the $n$-th resonance (Hz) ### Verification of the Harmonic Series For a fixed thickness $t$, the resonance frequencies form a series: $$ \begin{aligned} n = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad & f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2t} \\[4pt] n = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad & f_2 = \dfrac{2v}{2t} = 2 f_1 \\[4pt] n = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad & f_3 = 3 f_1 \\[4pt] \Rightarrow \quad & f_1 : f_2 : f_3 : \ldots = 1 : 2 : 3 : \ldots \end{aligned} $$ This harmonic spacing is a **self-checking feature** in the laboratory: if the measured frequencies are not in integer ratios, the test conditions (couplant, parallelism, plate flatness) are flawed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Pulse Echo Range Equation A pulse travels from the probe, reflects off a defect at distance $d$, and returns. Total path length = $2d$. Total time = $t_d$. With constant wave speed $v$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Distance travelled in time } t_d & = v \cdot t_d \\[4pt] \text{But this distance} & = 2d \quad \text{(down and back)} \\[4pt] 2 d & = v \cdot t_d \\[4pt] \boxed{\,d = \dfrac{v \cdot t_d}{2}\,} \end{aligned} $$ The factor of 2 is the **single most important** conceptual point in pulse echo UT — examiners love asking it. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A steel plate is inspected by the **Resonance Testing** method. The longitudinal wave speed in steel is **5920 m/s**. Successive resonance peaks are observed at **1.0 MHz, 2.0 MHz, and 3.0 MHz**. Determine: > (a) The thickness of the plate. > (b) The third harmonic frequency, and verify. > (c) The minimum thickness that can be measured if the upper frequency limit of the instrument is 10 MHz. ### Part (a) — Thickness Use the fundamental frequency $f_1 = 1.0$ MHz = $1.0 \times 10^6$ Hz, with $n = 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} t & = \dfrac{n \, v}{2 \, f_1} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{1 \times 5920}{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^6} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{5920}{2 \times 10^6} \\[6pt] & = 2.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,t & = 2.96 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) — Third Harmonic Verification $$ \begin{aligned} f_3 & = 3 f_1 = 3 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ The series $f_1 : f_2 : f_3 = 1 : 2 : 3$ is confirmed by the data. ### Part (c) — Minimum Measurable Thickness at 10 MHz For the thinnest piece, we use the highest usable frequency at the **first** resonance ($n = 1$, $f = 10$ MHz): $$ \begin{aligned} t_{min} & = \dfrac{1 \times 5920}{2 \times 10 \times 10^6} \\[6pt] & = 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,t_{min} & = 0.296 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pulse Echo > **Problem:** A **Pulse Echo** test is performed on a steel block. The back-wall echo appears at **10 μs** and a defect echo appears at **3.5 μs**. Take $v = 5920$ m/s. Find (a) the thickness of the block, and (b) the depth of the defect below the surface. ### Part (a) — Thickness $$ \begin{aligned} T & = \dfrac{v \cdot t_b}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{5920 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,T & = 0.0296 \text{ m} = 29.6 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) — Defect Depth $$ \begin{aligned} d & = \dfrac{v \cdot t_d}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{0.02072}{2} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,d & = 0.01036 \text{ m} = 10.36 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — When to Use What | Defect Type | Best Technique | Reason | |-------------|----------------|--------| | Sub-surface crack in a thick forging | **Pulse Echo** | Depth localization needed | | Lack of fusion in a brazed joint | **Through Transmission** | Planar defect perpendicular to beam gives strong attenuation | | Wall thinning in a boiler tube | **Resonance** | Fast, accurate thickness without complex scanning | | Porosity cluster in a casting | **Pulse Echo** (B-scan) | Cross-sectional view shows distribution | | Delamination in a composite | **Through Transmission** | Laminar defects parallel to beam fully block transmission | | Surface-breaking crack | **Pulse Echo with surface wave probe** | Surface waves travel along the surface | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Resonance Testing — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oscillator
Variable Frequency] --> B[High Frequency
Amplifier] B --> C[Transmitting
Transducer] C --> D[Test Specimen
with Couplant] D --> E[Receiving
Transducer] D -.Optional
Same Transducer.-> C E --> F[Tuned
Amplifier] F --> G[Detector and
Display Unit] G --> H[Resonance Peaks
on Frequency Axis] H --> I[Thickness
Computation t = nv/2f] ``` **Operating Cycle:** 1. The **Oscillator** sweeps frequencies (typically CW signal). 2. The **Transmitting Transducer** launches ultrasound into the part. 3. When the part's natural mode matches, a **standing wave** builds up. 4. The **Receiving Transducer** (or the same transmitter in echo mode) detects amplitude peaks. 5. Peaks appear on the **Display** at resonance frequencies. 6. The **Thickness** is computed by $t = nv / 2f$. --- ## 4.2 Through Transmission Technique — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulser or
CW Generator] --> B[Transmitting
Probe] B --> C[Test Piece
Front Face] C --> D[Test Piece
Body] D --> E[Test Piece
Back Face] E --> F[Receiving
Probe] F --> G[Amplifier and
Rectifier] G --> H[Display
Amplitude vs Time] H --> I{Amplitude
Below
Threshold?} I -- Yes --> J[DEFECT
INDICATED] I -- No --> K[Material
Sound] ``` **Key Visual Cues:** - Two separate probes on **opposite** faces. - Defect causes a **drop in received amplitude** (no echo is sought). - Display does **not** show defect depth — only its presence and projected size. --- ## 4.3 Pulse Echo Testing — Comprehensive Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Synchronizer
Clock] --> B[Pulser
100 to 400 V Spike] A --> C[Time Base
Generator] B --> D[Transducer
Piezoelectric] D --> E[Test Material
via Couplant] E --> F[Reflection from
Defect] E --> G[Reflection from
Back Wall] E --> H[Reflection from
Front Surface] F --> D G --> D H --> D D --> I[Receiver
Amplifier] I --> J[Detector and
Video Amp] J --> K[A Scan
Display CRT] C --> K K --> L[Time Markers
on X Axis] L --> M[Defect Location
d = vt divided by 2] ``` **Key Visual Cues:** - The **Synchronizer** is the heart — it triggers the pulser and the time base simultaneously. - A single **transducer** is used in a "transmit-receive" (T-R) switch mode. - The **A-scan** shows vertical amplitude peaks at horizontal time positions. - The **defect echo** appears between the **initial pulse** and the **back-wall echo**. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Schematic of the Three Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Ultrasonic Testing
Module 3] Root --> R[Resonance Testing] Root --> TT[Through Transmission] Root --> PE[Pulse Echo Testing] R --> R1[Single or Dual Probe] R --> R2[CW Variable Frequency] R --> R3[Output: f versus amplitude peaks] R --> R4[Application: Thickness gaging] TT --> TT1[Two Probes: Tx and Rx] TT --> TT2[Opposite Sides of Part] TT --> TT3[Output: Amplitude drop] TT --> TT4[Application: Laminar defects and porosity] PE --> PE1[Single Probe T and R] PE --> PE2[Short Pulse Generation] PE --> PE3[Output: A scan with echoes] PE --> PE4[Application: Flaw location and sizing] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** **Q: Differentiate between Through Transmission and Pulse Echo techniques of ultrasonic testing. List two advantages of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Through Transmission | Pulse Echo | |---------|---------------------|------------| | Number of probes | Two (separate Tx and Rx) | One (single probe, T-R switch) | | Access required | Both sides of the test piece | Only one side | | Defect detection principle | Attenuation of transmitted signal | Time of flight of reflected echo | | Defect depth information | Not available | Available via $d = v t_d / 2$ | **Advantages of Through Transmission:** 1. No dead zone — the entire thickness is inspectable. 2. Highly sensitive to laminar-type defects (lack of fusion, delamination). **Advantages of Pulse Echo:** 1. Only one-sided access required — suitable for in-service inspection. 2. Provides quantitative information on defect depth, size, and orientation. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Define 'Resonance' in the context of ultrasonic testing. Derive the relation between thickness and resonance frequency.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Resonance in ultrasonic testing is the condition in which the test specimen vibrates with maximum amplitude when excited by an ultrasonic wave whose frequency matches one of the natural frequencies of the specimen. **Derivation (sketch):** When a continuous ultrasonic wave of frequency $f$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a plate of thickness $t$, standing waves are formed when the thickness equals an integer number of half-wavelengths: $$ \begin{aligned} t & = n \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = n \cdot \dfrac{v}{2 f} \\[6pt] \Rightarrow \quad t & = \dfrac{n v}{2 f} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f_n = \dfrac{n v}{2 t} \end{aligned} $$ The resonance frequencies form a harmonic series: $f_1 : f_2 : f_3 = 1 : 2 : 3$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (1 × 14 Marks = 14 Marks) *Internal choice between Question A and Question B* --- ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Q: (a) Explain the principle of Pulse Echo Testing Technique with a neat block diagram. Discuss the A-scan display and explain how defect location and thickness are determined from it. (7 Marks)** **(b) A steel block is inspected by Pulse Echo method. A defect echo is observed at 6 μs and the back-wall echo is at 18 μs. The longitudinal wave velocity in steel is 5920 m/s. Calculate: (i) depth of the defect from the scanning surface, and (ii) total thickness of the block. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Principle:** A short-duration high-voltage electrical pulse is applied to a piezoelectric transducer, which converts it into a mechanical ultrasonic pulse. This pulse propagates into the test material. When it encounters an acoustic impedance mismatch (at a defect or at the back wall), a portion of the energy is reflected back. The same transducer receives these echoes, and the time of flight is displayed on an A-scan. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sync] --> B[Pulser] A --> C[Time Base] B --> D[Transducer] D --> E[Material] E --> D D --> F[Receiver] F --> G[Display A-Scan] C --> G ``` **A-Scan Display:** A 2D plot of **Amplitude (Y-axis) vs Time (X-axis)**. - The **initial pulse** (transmission pulse) appears at $t = 0$. - A **defect echo** appears at time $t_d$ (if a defect is present). - The **back-wall echo** appears at time $t_b$. **Defect Location:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t_d}{2}$ **Thickness:** $T = \dfrac{v \cdot t_b}{2}$ **Couplant:** Required between transducer and surface to exclude air (which has very low acoustic impedance) and allow efficient transmission. --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $t_d = 6\ \mu\text{s} = 6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$, $t_b = 18\ \mu\text{s} = 18 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$, $v = 5920\ \text{m/s}$. **(i) Depth of defect:** $$ \begin{aligned} d & = \dfrac{v \cdot t_d}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{0.03552}{2} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,d & = 0.01776 \text{ m} = 17.76 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Thickness of block:** $$ \begin{aligned} T & = \dfrac{v \cdot t_b}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 18 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\[6pt] & = \dfrac{0.10656}{2} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,T & = 0.05328 \text{ m} = 53.28 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ **Distance of defect from back wall** = $T - d = 53.28 - 17.76 = 35.52$ mm. --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]** **Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Resonance Testing method for thickness measurement. (7 Marks)** **(b) An aluminum plate is tested by the resonance method. Resonances are observed at 1.5 MHz, 3.0 MHz, and 4.5 MHz. Given the longitudinal wave speed in aluminum as 6320 m/s, find: (i) the thickness of the plate, (ii) the expected 4th harmonic frequency, and (iii) the minimum thickness that can be measured if the maximum usable frequency of the instrument is 12 MHz. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Principle:** A continuous wave ultrasonic signal of variable frequency is transmitted into the plate. The plate resonates (vibrates with maximum amplitude) whenever its thickness accommodates a whole number of half-wavelengths. The frequencies at which resonance occurs are recorded, and the thickness is computed. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Variable Frequency
Oscillator] --> B[Amplifier] B --> C[Transmitting
Transducer] C --> D[Test Plate] D --> E[Receiving
Transducer] E --> F[Amplifier and
Detector] F --> G[Display
Resonance Peaks] ``` **Procedure:** 1. Sweep the frequency from a low value to a high value. 2. Observe amplitude peaks at certain frequencies $f_1, f_2, f_3, \ldots$ 3. Verify that $f_1 : f_2 : f_3 = 1 : 2 : 3$ (harmonic series). 4. Compute thickness: $t = n v / 2 f_n$. **Key Formula:** $\boxed{t = \dfrac{n v}{2 f_n}}$ **Applications:** Thickness measurement of sheets, tubes, vessel walls. --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $f_1 = 1.5$ MHz, $f_2 = 3.0$ MHz, $f_3 = 4.5$ MHz, $v = 6320$ m/s. **(i) Thickness of plate (using $f_1$):** $$ \begin{aligned} t & = \dfrac{1 \times 6320}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{6320}{3 \times 10^6} \\[6pt] \boxed{\,t & = 2.107 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.107 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Expected 4th harmonic frequency:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_4 & = 4 \times f_1 = 4 \times 1.5 \\[6pt] \boxed{\,f_4 & = 6.0 \text{ MHz}\,} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Minimum thickness at 12 MHz:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{min} & = \dfrac{1 \times 6320}{2 \times 12 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{6320}{24 \times 10^6} \\[6pt] & = 2.633 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,t_{min} & = 0.2633 \text{ mm}\,} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students lose marks on UT questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Pulse Echo** — the pulse travels *to and back*, so the formula is $d = vt/2$, not $d = vt$. **Loss: 2–3 marks per occurrence.** > 2. **Mixing up $f_1$ and $f_n$** — students write $t = nv/(2f_1)$ instead of $t = nv/(2f_n)$. Always match the harmonic number $n$ with the corresponding frequency $f_n$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > 3. **Not mentioning the role of couplant** — examiners specifically look for this concept. The couplant excludes air and matches impedance. **Loss: 1 mark per omission.** > 4. **Forgetting to write the unit conversion** — e.g., writing MHz as Hz in formula. Always convert $1.5\ \text{MHz} = 1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.** > 5. **Confusing the three techniques in comparative questions** — make a clear table with at least 4 distinguishing parameters. > 6. **In resonance problems, failing to verify the harmonic series** — examiners award bonus marks for this self-check. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the exam.** - ✅ **Resonance Testing** uses **continuous waves** of **varying frequency**; thickness found from $t = nv/(2f_n)$. - ✅ **Through Transmission** uses **two probes on opposite sides**; defect detected by **amplitude drop**, not by echoes. - ✅ **Pulse Echo** uses **one probe** (T-R switch) on a **single side**; defect located by **time of flight** of echoes. - ✅ **Always** include the factor of 2 in pulse echo range equations: $d = vt/2$ and $T = vt/2$. - ✅ **Resonance frequencies form a harmonic series**: $f_1 : f_2 : f_3 = 1 : 2 : 3$ — this is a built-in verification. - ✅ **Standard reference values for steel**: $v_L \approx 5920$ m/s, $v_T \approx 3250$ m/s, $\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m³. - ✅ **Couplant is mandatory** for all UT techniques — it excludes air (which has acoustic impedance ~115,000× lower than steel). - ✅ **Dead zone** in pulse echo: $DZ = v\tau/2$ where $\tau$ = pulse width; surface defects inside the DZ are **missed**. - ✅ **Wavelength rule**: minimum measurable thickness in resonance = $\lambda/2 = v/(2f_{max})$. - ✅ **A-scan** = Amplitude vs Time (most common display); **B-scan** = cross-sectional image; **C-scan** = plan view. - ✅ **Through Transmission** is best for **laminar defects** (delamination, lack of fusion). - ✅ **Pulse Echo** is the **most widely used** UT method in industry because it requires only one-sided access. - ✅ **Resonance** is best for **rapid thickness gaging** of thin sheets and tubes. - ✅ **Acoustic Impedance** $Z = \rho v$ governs reflection/transmission at interfaces; mismatched $Z$ causes large reflections (good for defect detection). - ✅ The relationship $v = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ (for thin rods) or $v_L = \sqrt{E(1-\sigma)/[\rho(1+\sigma)(1-2\sigma)]}$ (for bulk) links wave speed to elastic moduli.

topics.title: Instruments and Accessories for UT: Transducers, Types, Frequencies, and Sizes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruments and Accessories for UT: Transducers, Types, Frequencies, and Sizes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **transducer** in Ultrasonic Testing (UT) is a device that converts one form of energy into another. Specifically, an ultrasonic transducer converts **electrical energy into mechanical (acoustic) vibrations** (in the ultrasonic frequency range, typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**) and vice versa. It functions as the critical interface between the electronic pulser/receiver unit and the test specimen.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An ultrasonic transducer is the heart of any UT system. It acts both as a transmitter (converting electrical pulses from the pulser into ultrasonic waves) and as a receiver (converting returning ultrasonic echoes back into electrical signals for display and analysis).

The most widely used transducer principle in industrial UT is the **piezoelectric effect**, discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880. Modern transducers typically use a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate - **PZT**, or quartz) cut to a precise thickness that determines its natural resonant frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine shouting into a long canyon and listening for the echo. Your voice is the input, the canyon walls reflect the sound, and your ears receive the echo. In UT, the **transducer is the "voice and the ear"** combined into one device. 

- **As the "voice"** (transmitter), it sends out high-frequency sound pulses (too high for humans to hear) into a metal component.
- **As the "ear"** (receiver), it listens for echoes bouncing back from internal flaws like cracks, voids, or inclusions.

Think of the piezoelectric crystal as a **drum head with a memory**: when you tap it with electricity, it vibrates and produces sound; when sound waves hit it, it "remembers" by producing a small electrical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Highlight:** The thickness of the piezoelectric crystal determines the transducer's frequency. **Thinner crystal = Higher frequency = Better resolution but less penetration.** This is a fundamental trade-off in UT.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and parameters are essential in UT transducer design and selection:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal wave velocity in steel | $c_L$ | **5920** | m/s |
| Shear wave velocity in steel | $c_S$ | **3255** | m/s |
| Density of PZT | $\rho_{PZT}$ | **7500 – 7800** | kg/m³ |
| Acoustic impedance of steel | $Z_{steel}$ | **46 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Acoustic impedance of PZT | $Z_{PZT}$ | **30 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Piezoelectric strain constant ($d_{33}$) | $d_{33}$ | **~400 × 10⁻¹²** | m/V |
| Standard UT frequency range | $f$ | **0.5 – 25** | MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is defined as $Z = \rho \cdot c$, where $\rho$ is density and $c$ is the wave velocity. Mismatch in $Z$ between the couplant and the test piece is the reason couplant (oil, gel) is used between the transducer and specimen.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle: The Piezoelectric Effect

The piezoelectric effect operates in two complementary modes:

1. **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** (Receiver Mode): When mechanical stress is applied to certain non-centrosymmetric crystals, an electrical charge appears on their surfaces. This is how returning ultrasonic echoes are converted back into measurable electrical signals.

2. **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect** (Transmitter Mode): When an alternating voltage is applied across the faces of a piezoelectric crystal, the crystal mechanically deforms (expands and contracts) at the same frequency. This is how the high-frequency ultrasonic waves are generated.

## 2.2 Construction of a Typical UT Transducer

A standard piezoelectric UT transducer contains the following components, arranged in a layered sandwich:

- **Piezoelectric Element (Crystal):** The active element, typically PZT, cut into a disc of specific diameter and thickness.
- **Backing/Damping Block:** A high-attenuation material (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite) bonded to the back of the crystal. It absorbs backward-radiated energy, shortens the pulse length, and improves axial resolution.
- **Wear Plate / Faceplate:** A protective layer (often alumina or polymer) at the front face that protects the crystal from wear and corrosion while transmitting ultrasonic energy into the test piece.
- **Housing (Case):** A metallic enclosure that provides mechanical support and electrical shielding.
- **Electrical Connector:** Coaxial connector (typically BNC or Lemo) for connection to the UT instrument.
- **Matching Layer:** An intermediate layer between the crystal and the wear plate designed to improve energy transmission into the test piece (Z-matching).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Backing Block** determines pulse characteristics. A *highly damped* (heavy backing) transducer gives **short pulses with broad bandwidth** (good resolution, poor penetration). A *lightly damped* transducer gives **long pulses with narrow bandwidth** (good sensitivity, poor resolution).

## 2.3 Classification of UT Transducers (Types)

### A. Based on the Physical Principle

| Type | Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric** | Piezoelectric effect (most common) | General industrial UT |
| **Electromagnetic Acoustic (EMAT)** | Lorentz force / Magnetostriction | Hot/cold surfaces, no couplant needed |
| **Capacitive (CVUT)** | Electrostatic force variation | High-temperature, rough surfaces |
| **Laser-Based (LUT)** | Thermoelastic expansion | Remote inspection, very high temps |

### B. Based on Application/Geometry

1. **Normal/Straight Beam Probe (Longitudinal Wave):** Sends and receives longitudinal waves perpendicular to the test surface. Most common.
2. **Angle Beam Probe:** Uses a plastic wedge to introduce waves at an angle, generating shear (transverse) waves in the test piece (e.g., 45°, 60°, 70°).
3. **Dual Element Probe:** Contains two separate crystals — one transmitter and one receiver. Used for near-surface flaw detection and thickness gauging.
4. **Focused Probe:** Uses a curved piezoelectric element or an acoustic lens to focus the beam at a specific depth, providing high resolution at that depth.
5. **Phased Array Probe (PAUT):** Contains multiple small elements (typically 16–128) that can be individually pulsed with time delays, allowing electronic beam steering and focusing.

## 2.4 Frequency and Size Selection — The Engineering Trade-offs

### Frequencies

The choice of frequency is governed by the trade-off between **penetration** and **resolution**:

- **Low Frequency (0.5 – 1 MHz):** Long wavelength, deep penetration in highly attenuating or coarse-grain materials (e.g., cast iron, concrete, composites). Poor resolution of small flaws.
- **Medium Frequency (1 – 5 MHz):** General purpose for most ferrous and non-ferrous metals. Best balance for most applications.
- **High Frequency (5 – 25 MHz):** Short wavelength, excellent resolution. Limited penetration, used for thin sections, precision components, and laboratory work.
- **Very High Frequency (>25 MHz):** Research and specialized applications (e.g., microelectronics, thin films).

### Sizes (Crystal Diameter)

Transducer sizes are typically standardized:

- **Small diameter (6 mm – 10 mm):** Better near-field resolution, used on curved surfaces, small areas, and for focused probes.
- **Medium diameter (10 mm – 25 mm):** General purpose (most common is **20 mm** or **0.5 inch / 12.7 mm**).
- **Large diameter (25 mm – 50 mm):** Higher energy output, better penetration in highly attenuating materials, but larger near-field dead zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU standard):** The most commonly used general-purpose transducer is a **2 MHz to 5 MHz, 20 mm diameter** piezoelectric normal beam probe.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength depends on velocity in medium and frequency | m |
| 2 | Near Field Length (Fresnel Zone) | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4c}$ | Distance from transducer to last axial maximum | m |
| 3 | Beam Spread (Half-angle) | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$ | Divergence in far field | radians |
| 4 | Beam Diameter at distance $x$ | $d = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda \, x}{D}$ | For $x \gg N$ | m |
| 5 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Resistivity to acoustic wave propagation | kg/(m²·s) Rayl |
