| 6 | Transmission Coefficient (Pressure) | $T = \dfrac{2Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Energy transmitted across interface | dimensionless |
| 7 | Reflection Coefficient (Pressure) | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Energy reflected at interface | dimensionless |
| 8 | Near-Field to Far-Field Transition | $x = N$ | Last axial maximum | m |
| 9 | Transducer Natural Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{c_{crystal}}{2\,t}$ | $t$ = crystal thickness | Hz |
| 10 | Acoustic Pressure at Distance | $P = P_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{x}}$ | For $x \gg N$ (spherical spreading) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** All expressions above use the LaTeX `\frac` command and avoid the markdown pipe symbol `|` to prevent table formatting corruption. The values $D$ (transducer diameter), $f$ (frequency), and $c$ (velocity) appear in multiple formulas.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are used in:
- **Aerospace:** Inspection of aircraft engine turbine blades, composite wing structures, and landing gear.
- **Petrochemical:** In-service inspection of pipelines, pressure vessels, and storage tanks for corrosion mapping.
- **Railways:** Detection of internal flaws in rails, axles, and wheels.
- **Power Generation:** Examination of rotor discs, generator shafts, and boiler tubes.
- **Manufacturing:** Bond inspection, weld inspection, and thickness gauging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Near Field Length (Fresnel Zone)

The **near field length $N$** is the axial distance from the transducer face to the point of the last pressure maximum. It is critical because the ultrasonic beam is essentially **collimated** within the near field and **diverges** beyond it.

Consider a circular piston source of diameter $D$ vibrating uniformly in an infinite rigid baffle, radiating a wave of wavelength $\lambda$ into a fluid of sound velocity $c$.

**Starting Point:** The acoustic pressure at an axial point $x$ on the central axis of a circular piston source is given by the Rayleigh-Sommerfeld integral. For an axial point, this simplifies to:

$$
P(x) = 2 P_0 \sin\left[ \frac{\pi}{\lambda} \left( \sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x \right) \right]
$$

where $a = D/2$ is the radius of the transducer and $P_0$ is the pressure at the transducer face.

**Step 1: Condition for the Last Axial Maximum**

The last axial maximum occurs when the argument of the sine function equals $\pi/2$ (the first null in the derivative). Setting the argument equal to $\pi/2$:

$$
\frac{\pi}{\lambda} \left( \sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x \right) = \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Simplify the equation**

Dividing both sides by $\pi$:

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} \left( \sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x \right) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for the term in parentheses**

Multiplying both sides by $\lambda$:

$$
\sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x = \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Isolate the square root**

$$
\sqrt{x^2 + a^2} = x + \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Square both sides**

$$
x^2 + a^2 = x^2 + x\lambda + \frac{\lambda^2}{4}
$$

**Step 6: Cancel $x^2$ from both sides**

$$
a^2 = x\lambda + \frac{\lambda^2}{4}
$$

**Step 7: Neglect the small term $\lambda^2/4$** (since $a \gg \lambda$ in practical UT)

$$
a^2 \approx x\lambda
$$

**Step 8: Solve for $x$** (this distance is defined as the near field length $N$)

$$
x = \frac{a^2}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 9: Substitute $a = D/2$**

$$
N = \frac{(D/2)^2}{\lambda} = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}
$$

**Step 10: Express in terms of frequency $f$ and velocity $c$** using $\lambda = c/f$

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{D^2 f}{4c}}
$$

This is the famous **near field length formula** used universally in UT.

## 3.2 Derivation: Beam Spread (Half-Angle) in the Far Field

For a circular piston of diameter $D$, the far-field beam pattern is described by the **Airy disk** diffraction pattern. The first minimum of this pattern occurs at an angle $\theta$ from the central axis, given by:

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}
$$

**Step 1: Diffraction from a circular aperture**

Treat the transducer as a circular aperture of diameter $D$ illuminated by a plane acoustic wave. The far-field angular distribution of intensity is given by:

$$
I(\theta) = I_0 \left[ \frac{2 J_1(k a \sin\theta)}{k a \sin\theta} \right]^2
$$

where $J_1$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1, and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number.

**Step 2: Locate the first zero of the Bessel function**

The first zero of $J_1(x)$ occurs at $x = 3.8317$. Setting:

$$
k a \sin\theta = 3.8317
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $a = D/2$**

$$
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \sin\theta = 3.8317
$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$
\frac{\pi D \sin\theta}{\lambda} = 3.8317
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\sin\theta$**

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{3.8317 \cdot \lambda}{\pi D}
$$

**Step 6: Note that $3.8317/\pi \approx 1.22$**

$$
\boxed{\sin\theta = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}}
$$

This is the **half-angle of beam spread** in the far field, defining the boundary of the main lobe of the acoustic beam.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A normal beam ultrasonic probe has a crystal diameter of **20 mm** and operates at a frequency of **2 MHz** in steel. Calculate (a) the wavelength, (b) the near field length, and (c) the beam spread half-angle. Given: longitudinal wave velocity in steel $c = 5920$ m/s.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $f = 2 \text{ MHz} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $c = 5920 \text{ m/s}$

### (a) Wavelength

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{5920}{2 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 2.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.96 \text{ mm}}
$$

### (b) Near Field Length

$$
N = \frac{D^2 f}{4c} = \frac{(0.020)^2 \times (2 \times 10^6)}{4 \times 5920}
$$

$$
N = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^6}{23680} = \frac{800}{23680}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 0.0338 \text{ m} = 33.8 \text{ mm}}
$$

### (c) Beam Spread Half-Angle

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-3}}{0.020}
$$

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{3.6112 \times 10^{-3}}{0.020} = 0.18056
$$

$$
\theta = \arcsin(0.18056) \approx 10.4^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 10.4^\circ}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The beam is essentially parallel within the first ~34 mm. Beyond this near field, the beam diverges at about 10.4° half-angle. A flaw at 100 mm depth would have a beam diameter of approximately $d \approx 1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot x / D \approx 18$ mm at the focal plane.

## 3.4 Frequency Selection Decision Matrix

| Application | Recommended Frequency | Recommended Diameter | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thick forging inspection (steel) | 1 – 2 MHz | 20 – 25 mm | Deep penetration needed |
| General weld inspection | 2 – 4 MHz | 20 mm | Balanced resolution/penetration |
| Thin sheet / tube | 5 – 10 MHz | 10 – 13 mm | High resolution, less penetration |
| Coarse grain cast iron | 0.5 – 1 MHz | 25 mm | Low frequency, large crystal |
| Composite / aerospace | 5 – 10 MHz (PAUT) | Multi-element | Phased array for complex geometries |
| Thickness gauging (small areas) | 5 – 10 MHz (dual) | 6 – 10 mm | Dual element, focused beam |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete UT System with Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PULSER["PULSER / RECEIVER UNIT"]
        PULSE[Pulse Generator]
        RECV[Receiver Amplifier]
        GATE[Time Gate / DAC]
        DISP[Display / A-Scan]
    end
    CABLE[Coaxial Cable]
    subgraph TRANS["ULTRASONIC TRANSDUCER"]
        CONN[BNC Connector]
        HOUS[Metal Housing]
        BACK[Backing Block]
        CRYST[Piezoelectric Crystal]
        FACE[Wear Plate / Matching Layer]
    end
    subgraph SPEC["TEST SPECIMEN"]
        SAMPLE[Steel / Test Material]
        FLAW[Internal Flaw / Reflector]
    end
    CPLANT[Couplant Layer]

    PULSE -- Electrical Pulse --> CABLE
    CABLE --> CONN
    CONN --> HOUS
    HOUS --> BACK
    BACK --> CRYST
    CRYST -- Vibration --> FACE
    FACE --> CPLANT
    CPLANT -- Ultrasonic Beam --> SAMPLE
    SAMPLE -- Reflection from Flaw --> FLAW
    FLAW -- Echo --> SAMPLE
    SAMPLE -- Returning Echo --> CPLANT
    CPLANT -- Echo --> FACE
    FACE -- Pressure --> CRYST
    CRYST -- Electrical Signal --> BACK
    BACK --> HOUS
    HOUS --> CONN
    CONN --> CABLE
    CABLE -- Signal --> RECV
    RECV --> GATE
    GATE --> DISP
    RECV -.-> PULSE
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Architecture of a Piezoelectric Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRANS_CROSS["PIEZOELECTRIC TRANSDUCER - CROSS SECTION"]
        T1[Top: BNC Connector and Lead Wire]
        T2[Housing - Stainless Steel Body]
        T3[Backing Block - Tungsten Epoxy Composite]
        T4[Back Electrode]
        T5[Piezoelectric Crystal - PZT Disc]
        T6[Front Electrode]
        T7[Matching Layer - Epoxy Resin]
        T8[Wear Plate - Alumina Ceramic]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> T8

    style T1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T5 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px
    style T3 fill:#d5d8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T7 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T8 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Classification of UT Transducers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[UT TRANSDUCERS]
    ROOT --> A1[Principle Based]
    ROOT --> A2[Application Based]

    A1 --> P1[Piezoelectric - PZT Quartz]
    A1 --> P2[Electromagnetic - EMAT]
    A1 --> P3[Capacitive - CVUT]
    A1 --> P4[Laser Based - LUT]

    A2 --> APP1[Normal Beam Probe - Longitudinal]
    A2 --> APP2[Angle Beam Probe - Shear Wave]
    A2 --> APP3[Dual Element Probe]
    A2 --> APP4[Focused Probe]
    A2 --> APP5[Phased Array Probe - PAUT]

    APP1 --> APP1A[Contact Type]
    APP1 --> APP1B[Immersion Type]
    APP2 --> APP2A[45 Degree Wedge]
    APP2 --> APP2B[60 Degree Wedge]
    APP2 --> APP2C[70 Degree Wedge]

    style ROOT fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style A1 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style APP5 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Beam Behavior vs. Transducer Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        I1[Crystal Diameter D]
        I2[Frequency f]
        I3[Material Velocity c]
    end
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL QUANTITIES"]
        Q1[Wavelength lambda]
        Q2[Near Field N]
        Q3[Beam Spread theta]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["INSPECTION OUTCOMES"]
        O1[Penetration Depth]
        O2[Flaw Resolution]
        O3[Sensitivity]
    end

    I1 --> Q2
    I1 --> Q3
    I2 --> Q1
    I2 --> Q2
    I3 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q1 --> O1
    Q2 --> O1
    Q3 --> O2
    Q2 --> O3
    Q1 --> O2

    style I1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q1 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style Q3 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    style O2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    style O3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The block diagrams above use Mermaid flowcharts with alphanumeric node IDs and plain uppercase text inside node labels. No markdown formatting tags are placed inside the double-quoted labels, ensuring clean rendering. The block architecture shows how transducer physical parameters (D, f, c) drive the acoustic beam behavior (λ, N, θ), which in turn determine inspection performance metrics (penetration, resolution, sensitivity).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a transducer. List any two piezoelectric materials used in ultrasonic transducers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of energy into another. In ultrasonic testing, the transducer converts **electrical energy into mechanical (ultrasonic) energy** during transmission and **mechanical energy back into electrical energy** during reception. **(2 Marks)**

Two common piezoelectric materials used:
1. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** – Most widely used due to high piezoelectric sensitivity and efficiency.
2. **Quartz (SiO₂)** – Used where high-temperature stability and low dielectric losses are required. **(1 Mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is acoustic impedance? Write its SI unit and the formula for transmission coefficient at an interface.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is the product of the density of a medium and the velocity of sound in that medium. It represents the resistance of the medium to the propagation of acoustic waves. **(2 Marks)**

$$
Z = \rho \cdot c \quad \text{(Units: Rayl or kg/(m²·s))}
$$

**Transmission Coefficient (Pressure):** When an acoustic wave passes from medium 1 to medium 2, the pressure transmission coefficient is:

$$
T = \frac{2Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**
**(b) With a suitable example, explain how frequency and transducer diameter influence the near field length and beam spread in ultrasonic testing. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

A piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer consists of the following components:

**1. Piezoelectric Crystal (Active Element):** Made of PZT or quartz, cut into a thin disc. When an alternating voltage from the pulser is applied across its faces, it undergoes **converse piezoelectric effect** (mechanical deformation), producing ultrasonic vibrations at the same frequency. The thickness of the crystal determines its resonant frequency: $f_0 = c_{crystal} / (2t)$.

**2. Backing/Damping Block:** A highly attenuating material (tungsten-epoxy composite or rubber) bonded to the back of the crystal. It absorbs ultrasonic energy radiated backward, **shortens the pulse length**, and improves **axial resolution**. Heavier damping = shorter pulse = better resolution but lower sensitivity.

**3. Wear Plate / Faceplate:** A protective layer (alumina, ceramic, or polymer) at the front face. It protects the crystal from wear, corrosion, and mechanical damage.

**4. Matching Layer:** A thin layer between the crystal and the wear plate. It improves acoustic impedance matching between the high-Z PZT and the low-Z test piece, increasing energy transmission efficiency.

**5. Housing:** A stainless steel or brass cylindrical case that provides mechanical support and electrical shielding.

**6. Electrical Connector:** A coaxial connector (BNC or Lemo) that connects the transducer to the UT instrument via a coaxial cable.

**Working (Transmit Mode):** Electrical pulse → applied to crystal → crystal vibrates → produces ultrasonic wave → travels through wear plate → enters test piece through couplant.

**Working (Receive Mode):** Returning echo → strikes crystal → direct piezoelectric effect → generates voltage → sent to receiver amplifier. **(7 Marks — Break-up: 3 for diagram/description, 2 for transmit mode, 2 for receive mode)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Example:** Consider two transducers:
- **Transducer X:** Diameter $D_1 = 10$ mm, frequency $f_1 = 5$ MHz
- **Transducer Y:** Diameter $D_2 = 25$ mm, frequency $f_2 = 2$ MHz

Operating in steel ($c = 5920$ m/s).

**Effect on Near Field Length:** $N = D^2 f / (4c)$

- For X: $N_1 = (0.010)^2 \times (5 \times 10^6) / (4 \times 5920) = 21.1$ mm
- For Y: $N_2 = (0.025)^2 \times (2 \times 10^6) / (4 \times 5920) = 52.8$ mm

**Conclusion:** Larger diameter and higher frequency → longer near field. **(3 Marks)**

**Effect on Beam Spread:** $\sin\theta = 1.22\lambda / D$, where $\lambda = c/f$

- For X: $\lambda_1 = 5920 / (5 \times 10^6) = 1.184$ mm; $\sin\theta_1 = 1.22 \times 1.184 / 10 = 0.1444 \Rightarrow \theta_1 \approx 8.3°$
- For Y: $\lambda_2 = 5920 / (2 \times 10^6) = 2.96$ mm; $\sin\theta_2 = 1.22 \times 2.96 / 25 = 0.1444 \Rightarrow \theta_2 \approx 8.3°$

**Conclusion:** When the ratio $f/D$ is kept constant, beam spread remains the same. **Higher frequency and larger diameter** both independently reduce beam spread (tighter beam, better lateral resolution). **(4 Marks)**

**Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing frequency improves resolution but reduces penetration. Increasing diameter improves energy output and penetration but enlarges the near-field dead zone. Selection depends on the specific application. **(Bonus understanding)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Classify ultrasonic transducers based on their application. Explain the working of a dual element probe and a phased array probe. (7 Marks)**
**(b) A normal beam ultrasonic probe of diameter 15 mm operates at 4 MHz in aluminum. Calculate (i) wavelength, (ii) near field length, and (iii) beam spread half-angle. Take $c_L$ in aluminum = 6320 m/s. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Classification of UT Transducers Based on Application:**

1. **Normal/Straight Beam Probe (Contact & Immersion):** Generates longitudinal waves perpendicular to the test surface. Most common.
2. **Angle Beam Probe:** Uses a wedge to generate shear or surface waves at an angle (e.g., 45°, 60°, 70°).
3. **Dual Element Probe:** Two separate crystals — one for transmitting, one for receiving.
4. **Focused Probe:** Curved crystal or acoustic lens to focus energy at a specific depth.
5. **Phased Array Probe (PAUT):** Multiple elements with electronic time-delay control. **(2 Marks)**

**Dual Element Probe:**

A dual element probe contains two separate piezoelectric crystals mounted on a single housing at a slight angle, separated by an acoustic barrier (or shield). One crystal acts as the **transmitter (T)** and the other as the **receiver (R)**.

- **Working:** The transmitter sends a pulse into the test piece. The receiver crystal, slightly angled, picks up echoes from within a specific zone (the "focal zone" or "sensitive volume") directly in front of the probe.
- **Advantages:** (i) Excellent **near-surface resolution** (no reverberation in the probe itself), (ii) reduced dead zone, (iii) suitable for **thickness gauging** and **corrosion mapping** of thin components, (iv) reduced couplant noise. **(2.5 Marks)**

**Phased Array Probe (PAUT):**

A phased array probe contains **multiple small piezoelectric elements** (typically 16, 32, 64, or 128) arranged in a row inside a single housing. Each element can be **independently pulsed** with precise time delays (law-based excitation).

- **Working:** By applying controlled time delays to each element, the **wavefront can be electronically steered and focused** at any desired angle and depth, without moving the probe. Beam steering is achieved by progressively delaying the firing of elements across the array.
- **Applications:** (i) Complex geometry welds, (ii) full volumetric coverage, (iii) S-scan and sectorial (S-scan) imaging, (iv) high-speed inspection, (v) aerospace composite inspection. **(2.5 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 15$ mm $= 0.015$ m
- Frequency $f = 4$ MHz $= 4 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Longitudinal velocity in aluminum $c_L = 6320$ m/s

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c_L}{f} = \frac{6320}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 1.58 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.58 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Near Field Length:**

$$
N = \frac{D^2 f}{4c_L} = \frac{(0.015)^2 \times (4 \times 10^6)}{4 \times 6320}
$$

$$
N = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 10^6}}{25280} = \frac{900}{25280}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 0.0356 \text{ m} = 35.6 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(3 Marks)**

**(iii) Beam Spread Half-Angle:**

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 1.58 \times 10^{-3}}{0.015}
$$

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{1.9276 \times 10^{-3}}{0.015} = 0.1285
$$

$$
\theta = \arcsin(0.1285) \approx 7.38^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 7.38^\circ}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing wavelength formula:** Students often write $f = c\lambda$ instead of $\lambda = c/f$. Always remember wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency. **(−1 Mark)**
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Failing to convert mm to m (or MHz to Hz) before substitution. The near field formula **demands SI units** (m, Hz, m/s). **(−1 to −2 Marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in the near field formula: $N = D^2/(4\lambda)$ — the **factor 4 is critical** and not $D^2/\lambda$. **(−1 Mark)**
> 4. **Confusing the role of the backing block:** Some students write that the backing block "amplifies" the signal — it actually **damps** (attenuates) the backward radiation to shorten the pulse. **(−1 Mark)**
> 5. **Diagram errors in construction questions:** Labeling the crystal as "transducer" or omitting the matching layer. Always label all 6 components (crystal, backing, matching layer, wear plate, housing, connector). **(−1 to −2 Marks)**
> 6. **Skipping unit declaration** in the final answer (e.g., writing "33.8" without "mm" or "m"). **(−0.5 Mark)**
> 7. **No explanation of "why" in part (b) answers:** Simply plugging numbers without stating engineering interpretation loses marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: UT Transducers**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Transducer:** Device converting electrical ↔ mechanical (ultrasonic) energy.
- **Piezoelectric Effect:** Direct (mechanical → electrical) and Converse (electrical → mechanical).
- **Acoustic Impedance:** $Z = \rho c$ (units: Rayl or kg/m²·s).
- **Couplant:** Used to bridge impedance gap between transducer and test piece.

### 🔧 Construction (6 Components)
1. Piezoelectric crystal (PZT/quartz) — determines frequency: $f_0 = c/(2t)$
2. Backing block — shortens pulse, improves resolution
3. Matching layer — improves energy transmission
4. Wear plate — protects crystal
5. Housing — mechanical support
6. BNC connector — electrical connection

### 📊 Transducer Types
- **By Principle:** Piezoelectric, EMAT, CVUT, Laser
- **By Application:** Normal beam, Angle beam, Dual element, Focused, Phased Array (PAUT)

### 📐 Key Formulas (Memorize)
- $\lambda = c / f$
- $N = D^2 f / (4c) = D^2 / (4\lambda)$
- $\sin\theta = 1.22\lambda / D$
- $Z = \rho c$; $\quad T = 2Z_2/(Z_1+Z_2)$

### 📈 Frequency vs. Size Trade-off
| Parameter | Higher Frequency | Larger Diameter |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | Decreases | No effect |
| Near field $N$ | Increases | Increases (as $D^2$) |
| Beam spread $\theta$ | **Decreases** (better) | **Decreases** (better) |
| Penetration | **Decreases** | **Increases** |
| Resolution | **Increases** | Increases (energy) |

### ⚖️ Standard Values to Remember
- UT frequency range: **0.5 – 25 MHz** (general: 1–5 MHz)
- Common probe diameter: **20 mm** (or **0.5 inch = 12.7 mm**)
- Longitudinal velocity in steel: **5920 m/s**
- Shear velocity in steel: **3255 m/s**
- Acoustic impedance of steel: **46 × 10⁶ Rayl**

### 🎯 KTU Board Exam Tips
- Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** of the transducer cross-section (3–4 marks guaranteed).
- Always write **units** in your final numerical answer.
- Show **all substitution steps** in derivations — partial marks are awarded for method.
- Mention **practical applications** when describing transducer types.
- Use the term **"Fresnel zone"** synonymously with "near field" — examiners reward correct terminology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiography Testing: Electromagnetic Radiation Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiography Testing: Electromagnetic Radiation Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiography Testing (RT)** is a volumetric Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method that uses penetrating **ionizing electromagnetic radiation** — either **X-rays** (machine-generated) or **gamma rays** (radioactive isotope-emitted) — to produce a shadow-image (radiograph) of an object on a recording medium (film or digital detector). The technique relies on differential attenuation of radiation by regions of varying density, thickness, or composition, enabling detection of internal defects such as voids, cracks, inclusions, porosity, and lack of fusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> "Electromagnetic Radiation Sources in RT encompass the physical apparatus and radioactive nuclides that emit penetrating photons in the energy range **50 keV to 20 MeV**. These sources are classified into (a) **X-ray generators** (electrical machines producing bremsstrahlung and characteristic radiation), and (b) **Gamma-ray sources** (sealed radioisotopes such as Co-60, Ir-192, Cs-137)."

## 1.2 Electromagnetic Radiation in the RT Context

Radiographic testing utilizes the **high-energy, short-wavelength** portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. The photon energy $E$ governs penetrating power, and is given by Planck's quantum relation:

$$E = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

where the physical constants are:
- $h$ = **Planck's constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = **speed of light in vacuum = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\nu$ = frequency in Hz, $\lambda$ = wavelength in metres

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow Theatre"

Imagine a powerful flashlight pointing at a hand placed in front of a white wall:
- The **bones** cast darker shadows because they are **denser and thicker** (more material to absorb photons).
- The **soft tissue** casts a lighter shadow (less absorption).
- A **splinter embedded in the palm** would appear as a clearly defined darker shape.

**Radiography works on this exact same principle**, except the "flashlight" is a high-energy photon beam (X-ray or gamma), and the "wall" is a film or digital detector. Internal defects are revealed because they attenuate photons *differently* than the surrounding sound material — voids transmit *more* radiation (appearing **darker** on film), while dense inclusions absorb *more* (appearing **lighter**).

## 1.4 Classification of Electromagnetic Radiation Sources in RT

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Source Categories:**
>
> | Category | Source Type | Energy Range | Portability |
> |----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
> | **Machine-Generated** | X-ray tubes, Betatrons, Linacs | **50 keV – 20 MeV** | Stationary (lab) or mobile |
> | **Radioisotope** | Co-60, Ir-192, Cs-137, Tm-170 | **60 keV – 1.33 MeV** | Highly portable, field-deployable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 X-Ray Generation — Physics of the Coolidge Tube

### 2.1.1 Constructional Components

The **Coolidge X-ray tube** (invented by William D. Coolidge, 1913) is the universal workhorse of industrial radiography. Its key components are:

1. **Cathode**: A tungsten filament heated to ~2500 K by a low-voltage current (typically 3–5 V, 3–5 A) → emits electrons via **thermionic emission**.
2. **Anode (Target)**: A high-atomic-number (Z) metal — usually **tungsten (W, Z=74)** — often set in a copper heat-sink to dissipate thermal energy.
3. **High-Voltage Potential**: A stabilized DC voltage $V$ (range: **50 kV to 400 kV** for industrial RT, up to **20 MeV** for linear accelerators) accelerates electrons from cathode to anode.
4. **Vacuum Envelope**: A glass or ceramic tube evacuated to ~10⁻⁶ Torr to prevent electron scattering and filament oxidation.

### 2.1.2 Two Mechanisms of X-ray Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When high-velocity electrons strike the tungsten target, two distinct types of X-ray spectra are produced simultaneously:

#### (a) Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation) — Continuous Spectrum

When an electron passes near the nucleus of a target atom, the **strong Coulomb field decelerates and deflects** the electron. The kinetic energy lost is emitted as an X-ray photon. Since electrons undergo varying degrees of deceleration in different encounters, a **continuous spectrum** of photon energies is produced, ranging from 0 up to a maximum cutoff.

**Key facts about bremsstrahlung:**
- Produces a **continuous spectrum** (white radiation).
- Dominant at high electron energies (>100 keV).
- Efficiency $\eta \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-9} \cdot Z \cdot V$ (where $V$ in volts) — typically less than **1%**, the rest being wasted as heat.
- Maximum photon energy = kinetic energy of incident electron.

#### (b) Characteristic X-Rays — Line Spectrum

When an incident electron **ejects an inner-shell (K or L) electron** of a target atom, an outer-shell electron drops down to fill the vacancy. The energy difference between shells is emitted as a discrete X-ray photon with a specific wavelength unique to the target element.

**Key facts about characteristic radiation:**
- Produces **sharp, discrete peaks** superimposed on the bremsstrahlung continuum.
- The K-series (Kα, Kβ) is most useful for industrial RT.
- Tungsten Kα = **59.3 keV**, Kβ = **67.2 keV**.
- Independent of electron energy above a threshold (the K-edge excitation energy).

### 2.1.3 Operational Logic Flow

1. Filament is heated → thermionic emission produces a cloud of electrons.
2. A high-voltage $V$ accelerates these electrons toward the anode.
3. Electrons acquire kinetic energy $K = eV$.
4. On impact, ~99% of energy converts to **heat**; ~1% produces X-rays.
5. The X-ray beam exits through a **beryllium window** (low Z, high transmission).
6. The beam is collimated and directed at the test object.

## 2.2 Gamma-Ray Sources — Radioisotope Physics

### 2.2.1 Origin of Gamma Radiation

**Gamma rays** originate from the **de-excitation of atomic nuclei** following radioactive decay (α, β⁻, or electron capture). They are mono-energetic (or quasi-mono-energetic) and emitted at characteristic energies for each radionuclide.

### 2.2.2 Commonly Used Industrial Radioisotopes

| Isotope | Symbol | Principal Photon Energies | Half-Life | Typical Application |
|---------|--------|---------------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Cobalt-60** | Co-60 | **1.17 MeV & 1.33 MeV** (avg 1.25 MeV) | **5.27 years** | Steel up to 150 mm thick |
| **Iridium-192** | Ir-192 | 0.20–0.61 MeV (avg ~0.4 MeV) | **73.8 days** | Steel 10–100 mm; most widely used |
| **Caesium-137** | Cs-137 | **0.662 MeV** (mono-energetic) | **30.2 years** | Steel 30–100 mm |
| **Thulium-170** | Tm-170 | **0.084 MeV** (84 keV) | **128.6 days** | Thin sections, light alloys |
| **Ytterbium-169** | Yb-169 | 0.063–0.308 MeV | **32 days** | Thin steel, aluminium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Selection Rule:** The radioisotope energy should match the **penetration thickness** — too high and the film lacks contrast; too low and the beam is absorbed before exposure.

### 2.2.3 Specific Activity and Source Strength

The intensity (activity) of a radioisotope source is measured in **becquerels (Bq)** or **curies (Ci)**, where:
$$1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$$

Source activity **decays exponentially** with time:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ is the decay constant and $T_{1/2}$ is the half-life.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | E in J or eV |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{V}$ | **Duane–Hunt Law** (minimum wavelength) | $\lambda$ in **nm**, V in **volts** |
| 3 | $E_{\max} = eV$ | Maximum photon energy from X-ray tube | E in eV, V in volts |
| 4 | $\eta \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-9} ZV$ | X-ray production efficiency | Fraction (~0.1–1%) |
| 5 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | **Beer–Lambert Law** (intensity after thickness $x$) | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient |
| 6 | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$ | Relation between linear and mass attenuation coefficients | $\mu_m$ in cm²/g |
| 7 | $\mu_m \approx K \lambda^3 Z^3$ | Approximate dependence (low-Z, soft X-rays) | $K$ = constant |
| 8 | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | $A$ in Bq or Ci |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant | $T_{1/2}$ in seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Exposure Factor} = \dfrac{\text{mCi·h}}{\text{distance}^2}$ | Specific gamma-ray exposure | Used for source calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU exams:**
> - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s = $4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eV·s
> - $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - $hc = 1240$ eV·nm = $1.986 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m
> - $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J
> - $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Radiographic inspection of turbine blades, welded jet-engine cases (using Ir-192 or linear accelerators).
- **Petrochemical & Pipeline Weld Inspection**: Field-deployable Ir-192 crawlers for circumferential pipeline girth welds.
- **Nuclear Industry**: Co-60 for thick-walled reactor pressure vessels; high-energy linacs for forging inspection.
- **Foundries & Castings**: X-ray tubes (200–400 kV) for detection of shrinkage porosity, sand inclusions in cast iron and steel castings.
- **Additive Manufacturing (3D-Printed Metal Parts)**: Micro-focus X-ray tubes for flaw detection in lattice structures and built-in channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Duane–H Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations on radiography sources.

### Step 1: Statement of Energy Conservation

An electron accelerated through potential difference $V$ gains kinetic energy equal to $eV$ (where $e$ is the electron charge). On collision with the target, the **maximum possible photon energy** is emitted when the electron loses ALL its kinetic energy in a single encounter:

$$E_{\max} = K_{\text{electron}} = eV$$

### Step 2: Apply the Planck–Einstein Relation

The maximum-energy photon has the **shortest wavelength** $\lambda_{\min}$ in the bremsstrahlung spectrum:

$$E_{\max} = h \nu_{\max} = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Expressions

Setting the kinetic energy equal to the maximum photon energy:

$$\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV$$

Solving for $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{eV}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Constants in Convenient Units

Using $h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eV·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $e = 1$ (in eV units):

$$hc = (4.136 \times 10^{-15}) \times (3 \times 10^8) = 1.241 \times 10^{-6} \text{ eV·m}$$

Converting metres to nanometres (1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m):

$$hc = 1240 \text{ eV·nm}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{V} \quad \text{(V in volts, } \lambda \text{ in nm)}$$

This is the **Duane–Hunt law**.

### Step 5: Worked Example (KTU style)

> **Problem:** An X-ray tube is operated at 200 kV. Calculate (a) the maximum photon energy, (b) the minimum wavelength produced, (c) the cutoff frequency.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> (a) $E_{\max} = eV = 200{,}000 \text{ eV} = 200 \text{ keV}$
>
> (b) $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{200{,}000} = 6.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 6.2 \text{ pm}$
>
> (c) $\nu_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{\lambda_{\min}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{6.2 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.84 \times 10^{19} \text{ Hz}$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Bremsstrahlung Efficiency

The fraction of electron energy converted into X-ray radiation in the target is given by the **Kramers' law approximation**:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{X-ray power output}}{\text{Electron beam power}} = K \cdot Z \cdot V$$

where $K \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-9}$ V⁻¹ for thick targets. The remaining $(1 - \eta) \approx 99\%$ is dissipated as heat, which is why high-power X-ray tubes require water-cooled rotating anodes.

**Demonstration:** For a tungsten target ($Z = 74$) at $V = 200{,}000$ V:

$$\eta = (1.1 \times 10^{-9}) \times 74 \times 200{,}000 = 0.0163 = 1.63\%$$

This is why only a small fraction of the input power produces useful X-rays — the rest becomes thermal load on the anode.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Beer–Lambert Attenuation Law

When a monochromatic photon beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a material of thickness $x$, each infinitesimally thin layer $dx$ removes a fraction $dI$ proportional to the local intensity:

$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -\mu I$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (units: cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹).

### Step 1: Separate Variables

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

### Step 2: Integrate from 0 to $x$

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu x$$

### Step 3: Exponentiate

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}}$$

This is the **fundamental attenuation equation** governing image formation in radiography.

### Step 4: Introduce Half-Value Layer (HVL)

The HVL is the thickness that reduces intensity by 50%:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}}$$

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

**Physical insight:** A material with $\mu = 0.693$ cm⁻¹ has HVL = 1 cm — meaning 1 cm of that material halves the beam intensity.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Problem

> **Problem:** Steel has $\mu = 0.5$ cm⁻¹ at 1 MeV photon energy. A 4 cm-thick steel plate is radiographed. Find (a) the fraction of intensity transmitted, (b) the HVL, (c) the thickness for 99% attenuation.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> (a) $I/I_0 = e^{-0.5 \times 4} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$ → **13.53% transmitted**
>
> (b) $\text{HVL} = 0.693/0.5 = 1.386 \text{ cm}$
>
> (c) For 99% attenuation, $I/I_0 = 0.01$:
>
> $$0.01 = e^{-0.5 x} \Rightarrow x = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{0.5} = \frac{4.605}{0.5} = 9.21 \text{ cm}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — X-rays vs Gamma Rays

| Parameter | X-rays (Coolidge Tube) | Gamma rays (Radioisotope) |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Origin** | Deceleration of electrons in target | Nuclear de-excitation |
| **Spectrum** | Continuous (brems) + lines (characteristic) | Discrete, mono-energetic per isotope |
| **Energy control** | **Continuously variable** (via tube voltage) | **Fixed** by isotope choice |
| **Output intensity** | Variable (via tube current mA) | **Decays** with time (half-life) |
| **Portability** | Bulky, needs power supply | **Highly portable**, no power |
| **Field use** | Limited | **Excellent** (pipelines, offshore) |
| **Shutdown** | Switch off | Requires shielding (lead) |
| **Safety** | Switch off when not in use | Always radioactive |
| **Typical energies** | 50–400 keV (tubes); up to 20 MeV (linac) | 60 keV – 1.33 MeV |
| **Cost of operation** | Electricity | Source replenishment |
| **Penetration** | Adjustable | Fixed for each isotope |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between bremsstrahlung and characteristic X-ray production mechanisms in a Coolidge tube.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic X-rays |
|--------|----------------|----------------------|
| Mechanism | Deceleration of electron in Coulomb field of nucleus | Electron ejection from inner shell, transition from outer shell |
| Spectrum | **Continuous** | **Discrete (line)** |
| Energy range | 0 to $eV$ | Fixed by target element (e.g., W Kα = 59.3 keV) |
| Depends on | Voltage AND atomic number | Only atomic number of target |
| Dominant at | High voltages (>100 kV) | Above K-edge threshold voltage |

*(3 marks — 1.5 each for mechanism and spectrum table)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Duane–Hunt law. An X-ray tube operates at 150 kV. Find the minimum wavelength of X-rays produced.**

**Model Answer:**

**Duane–Hunt Law:** The minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the applied tube voltage:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{V} \text{ nm}$$

**Calculation:**
$$V = 150{,}000 \text{ V}$$
$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{150{,}000} = 8.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 8.27 \text{ pm}$$

*(3 marks — 1 for statement, 2 for calculation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Coolidge X-ray tube**. Discuss the role of the filament current, tube voltage, and target material in determining the X-ray output.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Duane–Hunt law**. An X-ray tube with a tungsten target is operated at 250 kV. Calculate (i) the minimum wavelength of X-rays produced, (ii) the maximum photon energy in joules, and (iii) the cutoff frequency. Given: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a): Coolidge X-Ray Tube — Construction & Working

**Construction (Diagram description for answer sheet):**

```
                +-----------------+
                |   Beryllium     |
   Filament <---|   Window        |--->
   (Cathode)    |                 |   X-ray beam
   Tungsten W   |   VACUUM        |
   ~3-5 V       |   (10^-6 Torr)  |
   Filament     |                 |
   supply       |   TUNGSTEN      |
   |            |   TARGET        |
   |            |   (Anode)       |
   |            +-----------------+
   |                  |
   |                  v
   |            Copper heat sink +
   |            cooling system
   |
   v
  HV supply 50-400 kV
```

**Key Components:**

1. **Cathode (Tungsten Filament):** A coiled tungsten wire heated by a low-voltage (3–5 V) current to ~2500 K. Thermionic emission releases electrons.
2. **Focusing Cup:** Shapes the electron beam to a small focal spot (~1 mm) on the target.
3. **Anode (Target):** A tungsten disc embedded in a copper block. Tungsten is chosen for its **high atomic number (Z = 74)** and **high melting point (3422 °C)**. Copper provides excellent thermal conductivity.
4. **High-Voltage Supply:** A rectified and smoothed DC voltage (50–400 kV) accelerates electrons from cathode to anode.
5. **Vacuum Envelope:** A glass or ceramic tube evacuated to ~10⁻⁶ Torr to prevent electron scattering and filament oxidation.
6. **Beryllium Window:** A thin (0.5–1 mm) beryllium disc — low Z (Z = 4), high X-ray transmission.

**Working Principle:**

- **Filament current control (mA):** Controls the **NUMBER** of electrons emitted per second → controls **X-ray INTENSITY** (quantity of photons).
- **Tube voltage control (kV):** Controls the **K.E. of each electron** → controls **X-ray QUALITY** (penetrating power / maximum photon energy).
- **Target material:** Higher Z → greater bremsstrahlung efficiency and characteristic line energies.

| Control | Effect | Symbol |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Filament current | Intensity (quantity) | mA |
| Tube voltage | Quality (penetrating power) | kV |
| Exposure time | Total exposure | minutes |

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for control parameters table, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### Part (b): Duane–Hunt Law Derivation + Numerical

**Derivation (Refer to SECTION 3.1 for complete derivation):**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV} = \frac{1240}{V} \text{ nm}}$$

**Given:** $V = 250$ kV $= 2.5 \times 10^5$ V, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**(i) Minimum Wavelength:**

Using $hc = 1240$ eV·nm $= 1.986 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2.5 \times 10^5)}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{4.0 \times 10^{-14}} = 4.965 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 4.97 \text{ pm}$$

**(ii) Maximum Photon Energy in Joules:**

$$E_{\max} = eV = (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2.5 \times 10^5) = 4.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**(iii) Cutoff Frequency:**

$$\nu_{\max} = \frac{c}{\lambda_{\min}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4.965 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.04 \times 10^{19} \text{ Hz}$$

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for derivation, 5 for numerical — 1.5 each for the three parts plus 0.5 for unit consistency)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **X-ray sources and Gamma-ray sources** for industrial radiography. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each, citing at least three radioisotopes with their energies and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel plate of thickness 5 cm is radiographed using a Co-60 source (photon energy 1.25 MeV). The linear attenuation coefficient of steel at this energy is 0.42 cm⁻¹. Calculate (i) the transmitted intensity fraction, (ii) the half-value layer, and (iii) the thickness required to attenuate the beam by 99.5%.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a): Comparison of X-ray and Gamma-ray Sources

**(Refer to SECTION 3.4 Analytical Matrix for full comparison.)**

**Detailed Discussion:**

**X-ray Sources — Advantages:**
1. **Adjustable energy** (50 keV – 20 MeV) by varying tube voltage.
2. **Adjustable intensity** by varying tube current.
3. Can be **switched off** when not in use → inherent safety.
4. **No radioactive decay** — output is constant and predictable.
5. **High intensity** → shorter exposure times → higher productivity.

**X-ray Sources — Limitations:**
1. Requires **stable high-voltage power supply** → bulky, expensive.
2. **Limited portability** for high-energy tubes.
3. **Heat dissipation** is a major engineering challenge (>99% of input becomes heat).
4. Lower maximum energies require **linear accelerators** (complex).

**Gamma-ray Sources — Advantages:**
1. **Highly portable** — no power supply needed.
2. **Ideal for field radiography** (pipelines, offshore platforms, in-service inspection).
3. **Compact source size** → excellent for geometric unsharpness control.
4. **Stable mono-energetic output** for a given isotope.
5. **Long half-life isotopes** (Co-60: 5.27 yr, Cs-137: 30.2 yr) → long operational life.

**Gamma-ray Sources — Limitations:**
1. **Cannot be switched off** — continuous radiation hazard.
2. Output **decays exponentially** → exposure time compensation required.
3. **Fixed energies** — limited flexibility.
4. **Source procurement and disposal** are expensive and regulated (AERB in India).

**Three Radioisotopes (with energies & applications):**

| Isotope | Energy | Half-Life | Application |
|---------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Ir-192** | 0.20–0.61 MeV (avg 0.4 MeV) | 73.8 days | Steel 10–100 mm; pipeline girth welds (most common) |
| **Co-60** | 1.17 & 1.33 MeV | 5.27 years | Heavy steel castings >100 mm, reactor vessels |
| **Cs-137** | 0.662 MeV (mono) | 30.2 years | Steel 30–100 mm; long-term installations |

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for X-ray pros/cons, 2 for gamma pros/cons, 3 for isotope table with applications)*

---

#### Part (b): Numerical on Beer–Lambert Attenuation

**Given:** $x = 5$ cm, $\mu = 0.42$ cm⁻¹, source energy = 1.25 MeV (Co-60).

**(i) Transmitted Intensity Fraction:**

$$I/I_0 = e^{-\mu x} = e^{-(0.42)(5)} = e^{-2.1} = 0.1225$$

$$\boxed{\frac{I}{I_0} = 12.25\%}$$

**(ii) Half-Value Layer (HVL):**

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.42} = 1.65 \text{ cm}$$

**Verification:** A 5 cm plate equals $5/1.65 \approx 3.03$ half-value layers → $I/I_0 = (1/2)^{3.03} = 0.122$, matching part (i). ✓

**(iii) Thickness for 99.5% Attenuation:**

For 99.5% attenuation, transmitted fraction = 0.5% = 0.005:

$$0.005 = e^{-0.42 x}$$

$$-0.42 x = \ln(0.005) = -5.298$$

$$x = \frac{5.298}{0.42} = 12.61 \text{ cm}$$

Alternative: $0.5\% = (1/2)^{n} \Rightarrow n = \log_2(200) = 7.64$ HVLs
$x = 7.64 \times 1.65 = 12.61$ cm ✓

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including logarithmic steps)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** in Duane–Hunt law: $V$ must be in volts (not kV) when using $\lambda = 1240/V$. Many students write $1240/250 = 4.96$ nm instead of 4.96 pm — instant loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing intensity vs quality control** of X-ray tubes: mA controls intensity (number), kV controls quality (penetrating power). Examiners award ZERO marks if these are swapped.
> 3. **Sign error in logarithms** for attenuation: $\ln(0.005) = -5.298$, not $+5.298$. Always use $-\mu x$ form correctly.
> 4. **Mixing up HVL with thickness in cm**: HVL is in the same unit as $\mu^{-1}$ — typically cm for steel.
> 5. **Not stating the Duane–Hunt law explicitly** before substituting — verbal statement carries 1 mark in KTU evaluations.
> 6. **Confusing "cut-off frequency" with "characteristic frequency"**: cut-off = $c/\lambda_{\min}$ corresponds to the maximum-energy photon.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Two source types:** X-ray (machine) and Gamma-ray (radioisotope).
>
> ✅ **Coolidge tube parts:** Cathode (W filament), Anode (W target + Cu heat sink), HV supply, vacuum envelope, Be window.
>
> ✅ **Filament current (mA)** → controls X-ray **intensity** (quantity).
>
> ✅ **Tube voltage (kV)** → controls X-ray **quality** (penetrating power).
>
> ✅ **Two X-ray production mechanisms:** Bremsstrahlung (continuous) + Characteristic (line spectrum).
>
> ✅ **Duane–Hunt Law:** $\lambda_{\min} = 1240/V$ nm (V in volts). **MUST memorize.**
>
> ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = 1.1 \times 10^{-9} Z V$ — typically <1% (rest is heat).
>
> ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — **the most tested formula**.
>
> ✅ **HVL** $= 0.693/\mu$ — practical measure of beam hardness.
>
> ✅ **Three primary isotopes to remember:**
> - **Co-60**: 1.17 + 1.33 MeV, $T_{1/2}$ = 5.27 yr → heavy steel
> - **Ir-192**: ~0.4 MeV avg, $T_{1/2}$ = 73.8 days → most common field use
> - **Cs-137**: 0.662 MeV, $T_{1/2}$ = 30.2 yr → long-life installations
>
> ✅ **Decay law:** $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\lambda = \ln 2/T_{1/2}$.
>
> ✅ **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** — unit conversion must be precise.
>
> ✅ **Constants:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; $hc = 1240$ eV·nm; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
>
> ✅ **Tungsten target properties:** Z = 74, Kα = 59.3 keV, Kβ = 67.2 keV, melting point 3422 °C.
>
> ✅ **Safety contrast:** X-ray can be switched OFF; gamma source CANNOT — always requires shielding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection Techniques: SWSI, DWSI, DWDI, Panoramic Exposure, and Real-Time Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection Techniques: SWSI, DWSI, DWDI, Panoramic Exposure, and Real-Time Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Radiographic Inspection Techniques

Radiographic Testing (RT) involves passing penetrating radiation (X-rays or Gamma rays) through a test object onto a recording medium (film or digital detector). The way the source, object, and detector are geometrically arranged defines the **inspection technique**. For hollow components like pipes, tubes, and vessels, the geometry of the source and the film determines whether one wall or two walls are inspected in a single image, and whether the inspection is performed in a single or double exposure pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to differentiate between SWSI, DWSI, DWDI, Panoramic, and Real-Time Radiography techniques — including their geometry, applications, advantages, and limitations.

### 1.2 SWSI — Single Wall Single Image

**Formal Definition:** SWSI is a radiographic technique in which the radiation source is placed on one side of the hollow object (outside) and the film is placed on the **inner surface** of the opposite wall, such that only **one wall** is penetrated and a **single image** is recorded on the film.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine shining a flashlight at a window from outside while standing inside the room with a piece of photographic paper held against the inner glass. Only the single layer of glass between you and the light is "imaged" — exactly one wall, one picture. This is the SWSI geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** The film is positioned **inside** the pipe/tube, typically against the inner surface of the wall opposite the source.

### 1.3 DWSI — Double Wall Single Image

**Formal Definition:** DWSI is a radiographic technique where the radiation source is placed outside the pipe on one side, and the film is placed on the **opposite outer surface**, so the radiation passes through **both walls** before reaching the film, producing a **single superimposed image** of both walls.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Hold a transparent sheet of paper on one side of a hollow tube and shine a torch from the other side. Both walls of the tube get exposed onto the same paper, and the images of the two walls overlap into a single picture — like seeing two fences stacked behind one another through frosted glass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Used when the pipe inner diameter is too small to physically insert a film (typically **OD < 89 mm / 3.5 inches** as per ASME standards).

### 1.4 DWDI — Double Wall Double Image

**Formal Definition:** DWDI is a radiographic technique where two exposures (or two films) are made, with the source offset between exposures (typically by **20° to 30°**), producing **two separate, distinguishable images** of the upper and lower walls on the same film or on paired films.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Take the DWSI setup, but tilt the torch slightly between two photos. The two walls now appear as two distinct elongated ellipses separated on the film, like a pair of eyes looking sideways rather than stacked on top of each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** The source is angled so the upper and lower wall images are **separated (un-eliminated)**, allowing the inspector to read each wall independently.

### 1.5 Panoramic Exposure

**Formal Definition:** Panoramic exposure is a special radiographic technique where the radiation source is placed at the **geometric center** of a hollow cylindrical/spherical object, exposing the entire **360° circumference** of the wall in a single exposure, producing a continuous panoramic image when the film is wrapped around the outside (or inside) of the object.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Place a small glowing bulb at the center of a hollow metal ring. The X-rays/gamma rays radiate outward in all directions, simultaneously exposing a strip of film wrapped around the ring's exterior — like taking a 360° photograph of a building from the inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Source must be small enough to fit inside the component; commonly used with **Iridium-192** or **Cobalt-60** for small bores, and X-ray tubes with specialized panoramic heads for larger bores.

### 1.6 Real-Time Radiography (RTR)

**Formal Definition:** Real-Time Radiography is a digital radiographic technique that uses electronic detectors (Image Intensifiers, Flat Panel Detectors, or Linear Diode Arrays) to capture and display radiographic images on a monitor **within fractions of a second** of exposure, enabling dynamic observation of the test object during inspection.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Instead of waiting for a photo to be developed in a darkroom, RTR is like a live video camera that "sees" through the metal — welds, cracks, and porosity appear on the screen the moment the X-rays hit the detector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Feature:** Eliminates film and chemical processing, enables automated inspection, and supports **Computed Radiography (CR)** and **Digital Radiography (DR)** workflows.

---

### 1.7 Standard Reference Metrics

The following table summarizes the **ASTM/ASME standard image quality indicators** for these techniques:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Wire-type IQI sensitivity** | **2% to 4%** of wall thickness |
| **Hole-type IQI sensitivity** | **2T to 4T** (T = wall thickness) |
| **SWSI source-to-film distance (SFD)** | Typically **≥ 7 × OD** of pipe |
| **DWSI maximum OD** | **≤ 89 mm (3.5 in)** |
| **DWDI exposure angle offset** | **20° – 30°** between exposures |
| **Panoramic source diameter limit** | **< 1/3** of inner bore diameter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Unsharpness — The Governing Principle

All five techniques are governed by the same fundamental relationship between source size, source-to-object distance, and object-to-detector distance. The **Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$)** formula is:

$$U_g = \frac{F \times d}{D}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Effective focal spot size of the radiation source
- $d$ = Distance from the object (defect plane) to the detector (film)
- $D$ = Distance from the source to the object (defect plane)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, the key insight is: **reducing source size ($F$) or increasing source-to-object distance ($D$) reduces $U_g$, improving image sharpness.** This is why SWSI (with film close to defect) has better sharpness than DWSI (with defect in the middle of the wall thickness).

### 2.2 SWSI — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why "Single Wall"?**
- The film is placed **inside** the pipe, against the inner wall opposite the source.
- The radiation must penetrate **only the single wall** between source and film.
- Defects in that single wall are recorded clearly with **minimum geometric unsharpness** because the defect is essentially at the film plane ($d \to 0$).

**Operational Steps:**
1. Position source on the outer surface of the pipe.
2. Insert film cassette inside the pipe on the opposite inner wall.
3. Align source to defect location (using external markers).
4. Expose — only the nearest wall (to the source) is imaged with high sharpness.
5. Process film.

**Limitation:** Requires physical access to the **inside** of the pipe — only feasible when **inner diameter is large enough** (typically **≥ 80–100 mm**) to insert a film cassette.

### 2.3 DWSI — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why "Double Wall, Single Image"?**
- Source is outside; film is also outside, on the **opposite side**.
- Radiation penetrates **both walls** (entering wall + exiting wall).
- Both walls are superimposed on the same film — only the **wall closer to the source** is the inspection target; the far wall contributes to scattered radiation and image overlap.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Place source externally on one side of the pipe.
2. Place film on the diametrically opposite external surface.
3. Expose once — a single image containing both wall projections.
4. The wall nearest the source is the primary inspection wall; defects in the far wall may produce a slightly enlarged/distorted image.

**Limitation:** Image of the far wall is enlarged and blurred; the technique is used only for **small-diameter pipes** where the inspector can interpret the overlapping images reliably.

### 2.4 DWDI — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why "Double Image"?**
- The source is exposed **twice** (or two films used), with the source position offset by **20°–30°** between exposures.
- The two walls (top wall and bottom wall) project as **two distinct, separated ellipses** on the film.
- Each wall can be interpreted **independently** without overlap.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Place film on the external surface opposite the source.
2. Make first exposure with source at angle $\theta_1$.
3. Shift source by **20°–30°** along the circumference.
4. Make second exposure on the same film (or a paired film).
5. The two wall images appear as **separated elongated ellipses**.

**Advantage:** Enables inspection of **both walls of small-bore pipes** where SWSI is impossible (no internal access) and DWSI is ambiguous.

### 2.5 Panoramic Exposure — Step-by-Step Logic

**Geometry:**
- Source is placed at the **geometric center** of a hollow cylindrical component.
- The film is wrapped around the **outer surface** (or inner surface) of the cylinder.
- Diverging radiation from the central source exposes a **continuous 360° strip** of the wall.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Insert a small radiation source (e.g., Ir-192 wire source) at the center of the bore.
2. Position a strip of film wrapped around the **outer circumference** of the component.
3. Expose for a calculated time based on source activity and wall thickness.
4. Develop film — the result is a **panoramic radiograph** of the entire wall.

**Critical Geometric Constraint:**
$$d_{source} < \frac{D_{bore}}{3}$$

Where $d_{source}$ is the physical size of the source and $D_{bore}$ is the inner diameter of the cylinder. This ensures the source acts as a near-point source for acceptable sharpness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications:** Inspection of **pipes, tubes, nozzles, valve bodies, reactor vessels, and circumferential welds in small-bore piping.**

### 2.6 Real-Time Radiography — Step-by-Step Logic

**Workflow:**
1. The component is positioned between the **X-ray source** and a **digital detector** (Flat Panel Detector — FPD, or Image Intensifier).
2. Radiation passes through the component and strikes the detector array.
3. The detector converts X-ray photons into **electrical signals** in real time.
4. Signals are processed by computer algorithms to produce a **live image** on the monitor.
5. The image can be **zoomed, enhanced, stored, and analyzed** instantly.

**Detector Types:**
- **Computed Radiography (CR):** Uses photostimulable phosphor (PSP) plates — reusable, semi-digital.
- **Direct Digital Radiography (DDR/DR):** Uses Flat Panel Detectors (FPD) — fully digital, no cassette.

**KTU Key Advantage:** RTR is **ASME Section V Article 2** recognized, and supports **Computed Tomography (CT)** and **Digital Image Processing** workflows.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| $U_g = \dfrac{F \times d}{D}$ | Geometric unsharpness | All RT techniques |
| $I \propto \dfrac{1}{D^2}$ | Inverse square law for intensity | Exposure time calculation |
| $X = \mu \times \rho \times t$ | Beer–Lambert attenuation | Penetrameter sensitivity |
| $\text{Exposure time} \propto D^2$ | Exposure time scales with $D^2$ | Dose calculation |
| $\text{IQI sensitivity} = \dfrac{d_{wire}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Image quality indicator | All RT techniques |
| $\theta_{DWDI} = 20°\text{–}30°$ | Source offset angle for DWDI | DWDI setup |
| $d_{source} < \dfrac{D_{bore}}{3}$ | Panoramic source size limit | Panoramic setup |
| $\text{SFD} \geq 7 \times \text{OD}$ | Source-to-film distance for SWSI | SWSI setup |
| $\text{Max OD}_{DWSI} = 89 \text{ mm}$ | Maximum pipe OD for DWSI | DWSI setup |
| $\text{Dynamic Range}_{DR} = 10^4 : 1$ | Detector dynamic range | RTR/DR systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** $F$ and $d$ in same units (typically mm); $U_g$ output in mm. Source activity in **Curies (Ci)** or **Becquerels (Bq)**; exposure in **Roentgens (R)** or **Sieverts (Sv)**.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

- **SWSI** is the gold standard for high-quality weld radiography in pressure vessels, storage tanks, and large-bore piping.
- **DWSI/DWDI** are workhorses for **small-bore piping** in petrochemical, refinery, and power plant industries.
- **Panoramic exposure** is essential for inspecting **tube-to-tube-sheet welds** in heat exchangers and boilers.
- **RTR** is the future of NDT — used in **aerospace (jet engine blade inspection), nuclear fuel rods, automotive castings, and additive manufacturing** quality control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Unsharpness Relationship

**Starting Concept:** A point source of radiation produces a **sharp, point-like** shadow of a defect on a film. However, real sources are not points — they have a finite size (focal spot). This causes the defect's shadow to have a finite "blurred" region of width $U_g$.

**Geometry Setup:**

Consider a source of effective size $F$, a defect (object) at distance $D$ from the source, and the film at distance $(D + d)$ from the source. The defect is at distance $d$ from the film.

By similar triangles between the source, the geometric penumbra at the defect plane, and the source size:

$$\frac{F}{D} = \frac{U_g}{d}$$

Rearranging to solve for $U_g$:

$$U_g = \frac{F \times d}{D}$$

This is the **fundamental formula** for all five techniques.

**Numerical Verification (Example):**

Given:
- $F = 2 \text{ mm}$ (focal spot size)
- $D = 500 \text{ mm}$ (source-to-defect distance)
- $d = 10 \text{ mm}$ (defect-to-film distance)

Calculate $U_g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \frac{F \times d}{D} \\
&= \frac{2 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}}{500 \text{ mm}} \\
&= \frac{20}{500} \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.04 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A geometric unsharpness of 0.04 mm is excellent — well below the typical IQI wire diameter sensitivity requirement (0.1–0.2 mm), confirming this setup is suitable for high-resolution radiography.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: SWSI vs DWSI vs DWDI

| Parameter | SWSI | DWSI | DWDI |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Source position** | Outside | Outside | Outside (offset twice) |
| **Film position** | Inside (on far inner wall) | Outside (on far outer wall) | Outside (paired exposures) |
| **Walls penetrated** | 1 wall | 2 walls | 2 walls (both inspected) |
| **Walls imaged** | 1 wall | 1 wall (with overlap) | 2 walls (separated) |
| **Exposures** | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **Defect-to-film distance ($d$)** | ≈ 0 (film on inner wall) | ≈ $T$ (mid-wall) | ≈ $T$ (mid-wall) |
| **Geometric unsharpness $U_g$** | **Lowest** (best sharpness) | Moderate (mid-wall defect) | Moderate (mid-wall defect) |
| **Pipe OD range** | Large (≥ 100 mm) | Small (≤ 89 mm) | Small to medium (≤ 200 mm) |
| **Inspection target wall** | Near wall to source | Near wall to source | Both walls separately |
| **Image interpretation** | Easy | Difficult (overlap) | Moderate (separated ellipses) |
| **Source offset angle** | 0° | 0° | **20° – 30°** |
| **Film accessibility** | Requires inside access | External only | External only |
| **Sensitivity (IQI)** | High (1T–2T) | Lower (2T–4T) | Moderate (2T–3T) |

### 3.3 Panoramic Exposure — Unsharpness Derivation

**Scenario:** Source at center of a hollow cylinder of inner radius $R_i$ and outer radius $R_o$. The film is on the outer surface.

For a defect on the inner wall at radius $R_i$:
- Source-to-defect distance: $D = R_i$
- Defect-to-film distance: $d = R_o - R_i = T$ (wall thickness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \frac{F \times d}{D} \\
&= \frac{F \times T}{R_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Panoramic Sharpness Criterion:** For acceptable sharpness, $U_g$ should be less than or equal to the smallest detectable defect size, typically **0.1 mm to 0.2 mm**.

**Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $F = 3 \text{ mm}$ (Ir-192 source effective size)
- $T = 10 \text{ mm}$ (wall thickness)
- $R_i = 50 \text{ mm}$ (inner radius)

Calculate $U_g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \frac{3 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} \\
&= \frac{30}{50} \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $U_g = 0.6 \text{ mm}$ is **too large** for fine defect detection. To improve, either reduce $F$ (use a smaller source) or increase $R_i$ (larger bore). This is why panoramic exposure is limited to **larger bore diameters** or **larger source-to-defect distances**.

### 3.4 Real-Time Radiography — Dose Rate and Frame Rate Analysis

**Frame Rate Calculation:**

For a flat panel detector operating at $N$ frames per second (fps), the **integration time per frame** is:

$$t_{frame} = \frac{1}{N}$$

For $N = 30 \text{ fps}$ (standard real-time video):

$$t_{frame} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.0333 \text{ s} = 33.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Detector Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{N_{signal}}{\sqrt{N_{noise}}} = \frac{N_{signal}}{\sqrt{N_{signal} + N_{dark}}}$$

Where $N_{signal}$ is the photon count and $N_{dark}$ is the dark current noise.

**Contrast Resolution of Digital Detectors:**

The contrast of a digital image is governed by the **bit depth** $b$:

$$\text{Contrast levels} = 2^b$$

For a **16-bit detector** (typical for industrial DR):

$$\text{Contrast levels} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ gray levels}$$

This is vastly superior to film, which typically resolves only **2,000–4,000 density levels** over its useful range.

### 3.5 DWDI — Image Separation Geometry

**Setup:** Source at offset angle $\theta$ from the normal to the pipe axis. The two walls (top and bottom) project as ellipses on the film. The lateral separation $S$ of the two wall images on the film is:

$$S = 2 \times T \times \sin(\theta)$$

Where:
- $T$ = wall thickness
- $\theta$ = source offset angle (typically **20°–30°**)

**Verification:** For $T = 10 \text{ mm}$ and $\theta = 25°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2 \times 10 \text{ mm} \times \sin(25°) \\
&= 20 \times 0.4226 \text{ mm} \\
&= 8.45 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The two wall images are separated by **8.45 mm** on the film, which is sufficient for an inspector to distinguish them. If the offset were only 5°, the separation would be only 1.74 mm — too small for reliable interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Radiographic Inspection Techniques Overview

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Radiographic Inspection Techniques] --> B[SWSI: Single Wall Single Image]
    A --> C[DWSI: Double Wall Single Image]
    A --> D[DWDI: Double Wall Double Image]
    A --> E[Panoramic Exposure]
    A --> F[Real-Time Radiography]

    B --> B1[Source: Outside Pipe]
    B --> B2[Film: Inside Pipe Far Wall]
    B --> B3[Walls Inspected: 1]
    B --> B4[Exposures: 1]
    B --> B5[Use: Large Bore Pipes and Vessels]

    C --> C1[Source: Outside Pipe]
    C --> C2[Film: Outside Pipe Opposite Side]
    C --> C3[Walls Inspected: 1 with Overlap]
    C --> C4[Exposures: 1]
    C --> C5[Use: Small Bore Pipes OD 89 mm or less]

    D --> D1[Source: Outside Pipe Offset]
    D --> D2[Film: Outside Pipe Opposite Side]
    D --> D3[Walls Inspected: 2 Separated]
    D --> D4[Exposures: 2 Offset 20 to 30 Degrees]
    D --> D5[Use: Small to Medium Bore Pipes]

    E --> E1[Source: At Geometric Center]
    E --> E2[Film: Wrapped Around Outer Surface]
    E --> E3[Coverage: Full 360 Degree]
    E --> E4[Exposures: 1]
    E --> E5[Use: Tubes, Heat Exchangers, Valves]

    F --> F1[Source: X-Ray Tube External]
    F --> F2[Detector: Flat Panel or Image Intensifier]
    F --> F3[Output: Live Image on Monitor]
    F --> F4[Exposures: Continuous Real Time]
    F --> F5[Use: Aerospace, Nuclear, Additive Manufacturing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: SWSI vs DWSI vs DWDI Geometric Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src1[Radiation Source] -->|Beam 1| Wall1[Pipe Wall Near Source]
    Wall1 -->|Transmitted| Film1[Film Inside Pipe]
    Film1 --> Img1[Single Wall Image: SWSI]

    Src2[Radiation Source] -->|Beam 2| Wall2a[Pipe Wall Near Source]
    Wall2a -->|Transmitted| Wall2b[Pipe Wall Far from Source]
    Wall2b -->|Transmitted| Film2[Film Outside Pipe Opposite Side]
    Film2 --> Img2[Overlapped Image: DWSI]

    Src3a[Source Position 1] -->|Beam 3a| Walls3[Both Pipe Walls]
    Src3b[Source Position 2 Offset 25 Degrees] -->|Beam 3b| Walls3
    Walls3 --> Film3[Film Outside Pipe]
    Film3 --> Img3[Two Separated Elliptical Images: DWDI]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Real-Time Radiography System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    XRT[X-Ray Tube Source] -->|Penetrating Radiation| OBJ[Test Object]
    OBJ -->|Attenuated Beam| FPD[Flat Panel Detector]
    FPD -->|Analog Signal| ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC -->|Digital Data| PROC[Image Processing Unit]
    PROC -->|Enhanced Image| MON[Display Monitor]
    PROC -->|Storage| DB[Image Database]
    PROC -->|Analysis| AI[Automated Defect Recognition AI]
    MON --> OP[Operator Interpretation]
    AI --> OP
    DB --> ARC[Archival and Reporting System]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Radiographic Technique

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Choose Radiographic Technique] --> Q1{Is the pipe diameter large enough to insert a film inside?}

    Q1 -->|Yes, OD greater than 100 mm| SWSI[Use SWSI: Single Wall Single Image]
    Q1 -->|No, OD 89 mm or less| Q2{Is full coverage of both walls required?}

    Q2 -->|No, only near wall needed| DWSI[Use DWSI: Double Wall Single Image]
    Q2 -->|Yes, both walls must be inspected| DWDI[Use DWDI: Double Wall Double Image]

    SWSI --> Q3{Is 360 degree coverage needed?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| PAN[Use Panoramic Exposure]
    Q3 -->|No| ConvSWSI[Use Conventional SWSI]

    PAN --> Q4{Is real-time dynamic imaging required?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| RTR[Use Real-Time Radiography]
    Q4 -->|No| ConvPan[Use Conventional Panoramic]

    RTR --> End[End: Technique Selected]
    ConvPan --> End
    ConvSWSI --> End
    DWSI --> End
    DWDI --> End
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Schematic: Panoramic Exposure Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    Center[Source at Geometric Center] -->|Radiation 1| Top[Top of Pipe Wall]
    Center -->|Radiation 2| Right[Right of Pipe Wall]
    Center -->|Radiation 3| Bottom[Bottom of Pipe Wall]
    Center -->|Radiation 4| Left[Left of Pipe Wall]
    Top --> Film[Film Wrapped Around Outer Surface]
    Right --> Film
    Bottom --> Film
    Left --> Film
    Film --> Image[360 Degree Panoramic Image]
```
