## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between SWSI and DWSI radiographic techniques with respect to source position, film position, walls penetrated, and typical application.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SWSI | DWSI |
|-----------|------|------|
| Source position | Outside pipe | Outside pipe |
| Film position | **Inside** pipe on far inner wall | **Outside** pipe on opposite side |
| Walls penetrated | 1 wall | 2 walls |
| Image type | Single wall image | Overlapping image of two walls |
| Typical application | Large bore pipes (OD > 100 mm) | Small bore pipes (OD ≤ 89 mm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Correct identification of all 4 parameters with clear distinction = 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is panoramic exposure in radiography? State the geometric constraint for source size.*

**Model Answer:**

Panoramic exposure is a radiographic technique where the radiation source is placed at the **geometric center** of a hollow cylindrical component, and a strip of film is wrapped around the outer circumference. This enables **360° inspection** of the entire wall in a single exposure.

**Geometric Constraint:** The physical size of the source must satisfy:

$$d_{source} < \frac{D_{bore}}{3}$$

This ensures the source approximates a point source, providing acceptable geometric unsharpness across the entire circumference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition = 1.5 marks; Geometric constraint with formula = 1.5 marks.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the SWSI, DWSI, and DWDI radiographic techniques. State the standard offset angle for DWDI and explain why it is used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SWSI (Single Wall Single Image):**
- The radiation source is placed **outside** the pipe on one side.
- The film is placed **inside** the pipe, pressed against the inner surface of the wall **opposite** to the source.
- Only **one wall** is penetrated; the resulting image is a clear single-wall radiograph.
- **Advantage:** Excellent sharpness because the defect is at or very near the film plane ($d \approx 0$).
- **Limitation:** Requires internal access to the pipe (large bore only).

**DWSI (Double Wall Single Image):**
- Source is placed **outside** the pipe on one side.
- Film is placed **outside** the pipe on the **opposite** side.
- Radiation penetrates **both walls**, but the image of both walls is **superimposed** on the film.
- **Advantage:** No need for internal access; suitable for small-bore pipes.
- **Limitation:** Image interpretation is difficult due to overlap; the far wall appears enlarged.

**DWDI (Double Wall Double Image):**
- Same external setup as DWSI, but **two exposures** are made.
- The source is offset by **20° to 30°** between exposures.
- The two walls project as **two distinct, separated ellipses** on the film.
- **Standard Offset Angle:** **25°** (typical industrial practice per ASME Section V).
- **Reason for Offset:** Without offset, the two walls overlap completely (as in DWSI) and cannot be independently inspected. The offset produces geometric separation of the two wall images, allowing the inspector to evaluate each wall separately.

**Sketch (described in text):**

```
SWSI:
    [Source] ===> | Wall | <=== Film |
                     (film inside)

DWSI:
    [Source] ===> | Wall1 + Wall2 | <=== Film |
                     (film outside, opposite side)

DWDI:
    [Source @ 0°]  ===> | Wall1 + Wall2 | <=== Film
    [Source @ 25°] ===> | (same pipe)   | <=== Film
                     (two exposures, two images on film)
```

**(b)** A radiographic exposure is made with a source of focal spot size **2.5 mm**, source-to-defect distance **600 mm**, and defect-to-film distance **15 mm**. Calculate the geometric unsharpness and comment on its suitability for detecting a defect of size **0.2 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $F = 2.5 \text{ mm}$
- $D = 600 \text{ mm}$
- $d = 15 \text{ mm}$
- Defect size = 0.2 mm

**Formula:**

$$U_g = \frac{F \times d}{D}$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \frac{2.5 \text{ mm} \times 15 \text{ mm}}{600 \text{ mm}} \\
&= \frac{37.5}{600} \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.0625 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Suitability:**

For a defect to be detectable, the geometric unsharpness must be **less than or equal to the defect size** (rule of thumb: $U_g \leq 0.5 \times$ defect size for clear detection).

- Calculated $U_g = 0.0625 \text{ mm}$
- Defect size = 0.2 mm
- $U_g / \text{Defect} = 0.0625 / 0.2 = 0.3125 = 31.25\%$

Since $U_g$ is only **31.25%** of the defect size, the unsharpness is **well within acceptable limits**. The setup is **suitable** for detecting a 0.2 mm defect.

**Conclusion:** The exposure geometry is appropriate, and a 0.2 mm defect will be clearly visible on the radiograph.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure of **Panoramic Exposure** in radiography. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to conventional external radiography. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Panoramic exposure uses a radiation source placed at the **geometric center** of a hollow cylindrical/spherical object. The radiation diverges radially in all directions, simultaneously exposing a strip of film wrapped around the **outer circumference** of the component. The result is a single continuous radiograph covering the **entire 360°** of the wall.

**Procedure:**
1. Select a small radioactive source (e.g., Ir-192 wire source) with size less than **$D_{bore}/3$**.
2. Position the source at the geometric center of the bore using a flexible cable or positioning tool.
3. Wrap a strip of film around the **outer surface** of the component (held in place by tape or cassette).
4. Set exposure time based on source activity, wall thickness, and material density.
5. Expose and process the film.
6. The developed film shows a **continuous panoramic view** of the entire wall — including circumferential welds.

**Advantages over Conventional External RT:**
- **Full 360° coverage in a single exposure** — efficient for circumferential inspections.
- **Suitable for small-bore components** where external RT is geometrically impossible.
- **Reduced total inspection time** for long components.
- **Detects circumferential and longitudinal defects** simultaneously.

**Limitations:**
- **Source size constraint:** $d_{source} < D_{bore}/3$ — limits use to larger bores or smaller sources.
- **Geometric unsharpness is high** for inner-wall defects because the defect is far from the film.
- **Scattered radiation** is significant due to the long path length through the wall.
- **Source positioning is critical** — even a small offset causes severe image distortion.
- **Limited to hollow components** with circular/spherical geometry.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **Real-Time Radiography (RTR)**. List **four advantages** of RTR over conventional film radiography. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

Real-Time Radiography uses a **digital detector** (Flat Panel Detector or Image Intensifier) instead of conventional X-ray film. The workflow is:

1. **X-ray source** emits penetrating radiation.
2. Radiation passes through the **test object** and is attenuated based on material density and thickness.
3. The attenuated beam strikes the **Flat Panel Detector (FPD)**, which contains a matrix of photodiodes (typically amorphous silicon) coupled with a scintillator (CsI or Gd₂O₂S).
4. The scintillator converts X-ray photons to **visible light**.
5. The photodiode array converts light into **electrical signals**.
6. Signals are digitized by an **ADC** and processed by computer.
7. The image is displayed **in real time** on a monitor at frame rates of **30 fps or higher**.
8. Operators can view, zoom, enhance, and analyze the image live; the system can also perform **Computed Tomography (CT)** scanning for 3D reconstruction.

**Diagram (described in text):**

```
[X-Ray Tube] --> [Test Object] --> [Flat Panel Detector] --> [Computer] --> [Monitor]
                                  (Scintillator + Photodiode Array)    (Image Processing)
```

**Four Advantages of RTR over Film Radiography:**

1. **No chemical processing required** — eliminates darkroom, fixer, developer, and water consumption; environmentally friendly.
2. **Immediate image availability** — image appears within seconds, dramatically reducing inspection cycle time.
3. **Digital image enhancement** — contrast, brightness, and zoom can be adjusted post-exposure; image quality is not "frozen" at exposure time.
4. **Dynamic inspection possible** — moving objects (rotating welds, flowing fluids) can be observed live, enabling inspection of processes and not just static parts.

**Additional advantages (bonus):** Digital archiving, automated defect recognition (ADR/AI), lower radiation dose required, and integration with industry 4.0 systems.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing DWSI and DWDI** — students often write "DWSI" when the question asks for "DWDI" or vice versa. Remember: DWSI = **1** image; DWDI = **2** images (offset exposures).
> 2. **Forgetting the geometric constraint** for panoramic exposure — always state the formula $d_{source} < D_{bore}/3$.
> 3. **Unit errors in geometric unsharpness** — keep $F$, $d$, and $D$ in the **same units** (typically mm). Mixing mm and cm causes huge errors.
> 4. **Not mentioning the offset angle (20°–30°)** in DWDI answers — this is a must-mention point.
> 5. **Confusing "real-time" with "computed radiography"** — RTR uses **electronic detectors with live display**; CR uses **photostimulable phosphor plates** that still need a scanner (not truly real-time, but faster than film).
> 6. **Forgetting advantages of RTR** — students often list only one or two; **list at least 3–4** for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inspection Techniques (Module 3)**

- **SWSI (Single Wall Single Image):** Film **inside** pipe; **1 wall** imaged; large bore; best sharpness.
- **DWSI (Double Wall Single Image):** Film **outside** opposite side; **2 walls** penetrated; **1 overlapping image**; small bore (OD ≤ 89 mm).
- **DWDI (Double Wall Double Image):** **2 exposures** offset by **20°–30°**; **2 separated images**; both walls inspected.
- **Panoramic Exposure:** Source at **center**; film **wrapped externally**; 360° coverage; constraint $d_{source} < D_{bore}/3$.
- **Real-Time Radiography (RTR):** Uses **Flat Panel Detectors (FPD)**; **live image** on monitor; no film processing; supports CT and AI analysis.
- **Geometric Unsharpness Formula:** $U_g = \dfrac{F \times d}{D}$ — must be minimized for sharp images.
- **SWSI SFD Rule:** Source-to-film distance $\geq 7 \times$ OD of pipe.
- **DWSI OD Limit:** Maximum pipe OD = **89 mm (3.5 inches)** per ASME.
- **DWDI Offset Angle:** **20°–30°** (typically 25°) between two exposures.
- **Panoramic Source Limit:** Source size **< 1/3 of bore diameter**.
- **RTR Detectors:** FPD (Direct DR), Image Intensifier, Linear Diode Array (LDA).
- **RTR Advantages:** No chemicals, instant results, digital enhancement, dynamic imaging, automated defect recognition.
- **Standards:** All techniques governed by **ASME Section V Article 2**, **ASTM E94**, **ASTM E142**, and **ISO 17636**.
- **Key Insight for Exams:** SWSI has the **best sharpness** (defect near film); DWSI/DWDI have **moderate sharpness** (defect in mid-wall); Panoramic has the **worst sharpness** for inner-wall defects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Films Used in Industrial Radiography: Types, Speed, and Qualities of Films
Film Screens Used in Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Screens Used in Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Radiographic Film** is a specially manufactured photographic recording medium consisting of a transparent polyester base coated on one or both sides with a gelatin emulsion containing radiation-sensitive silver halide crystals (primarily silver bromide, AgBr). When exposed to ionising radiation (X-rays or gamma rays), either directly or through intensifying screens, a **latent image** is formed which becomes visible after chemical development.

**Intensifying Screens** are auxiliary materials (lead foils or fluorescent screens) placed in intimate contact with the radiographic film to amplify the effect of ionising radiation, thereby reducing the required exposure time and improving radiographic image quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3, Section 3.1**
> Students must be able to describe the construction, classification, and operating principles of radiographic films and intensifying screens, and explain their combined role in producing a radiograph.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to take a photograph in **near-total darkness** using only the faintest glow of a candle. If you use a highly sensitive film (like ISO 6400), you capture something, but it's grainy and noisy. Now place a **bright reflector** behind the subject — suddenly the light bounces onto the film with greater intensity, the picture becomes sharper, and you need far less exposure time.

In radiography:
- The **subject** is the welded joint or casting being inspected.
- The **faint glow** is the X-ray or gamma photon that passes through the part.
- The **radiographic film** is the photographic canvas.
- The **intensifying screen** is the bright reflector that converts invisible/weak radiation into a stronger, more effective exposure signal.

Without screens, exposure times can be **100× longer**, making industrial radiography economically impractical and reducing equipment life.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

- **Optical Density (D)** is the standard measure of film blackening, ranging typically from **0.0 to 4.0**.
- **Speed** of a film is measured relative to a reference film (Speed Number, e.g., **100, 200, 400, 800**).
- **Film Gradient (Contrast, Ḡ)** typically falls between **2.5 and 5.5** for industrial films.
- **Resolution** for Class I films is **~100 line pairs/mm**; for Class IV films, it drops to **~25 line pairs/mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial vs Medical Films:** KTU emphasises industrial radiography (NDT), where films must withstand higher energy radiation and use **both-sides-coated** (duplex) emulsions to maximise sensitivity while maintaining acceptable resolution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Radiographic Film (Layer-by-Layer)

A typical industrial X-ray film has **five distinct layers**:

1. **Protective Overcoat (Supercoating)**
   - Thin (~1 µm) hard gelatin layer.
   - Protects the emulsion from scratches, pressure marks, and contamination during handling.

2. **Emulsion Layer (Sensitive Layer)**
   - Gelatin matrix containing silver halide crystals (mainly AgBr, sometimes AgBr + AgI).
   - Crystal size governs speed-vs-resolution trade-off.
   - Industrial films may have emulsion on **both sides** of the base (duplitised) for higher sensitivity.

3. **Substrate / Base**
   - Polyester (PET) — ~0.18 mm thick.
   - Provides mechanical strength, dimensional stability, and flexibility.
   - Must be transparent to light after processing.

4. **Anti-halation Backing (Anti-curl Coating)**
   - Light-absorbing dye layer that prevents light reflection (halation) back into the emulsion.
   - Eliminates the "halo" around bright images, improving sharpness.

5. **Adhesion Sub-layer**
   - Ensures emulsion adheres firmly to the base throughout processing chemicals.

### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" Behind the Design

- **Why gelatin?** It is hydrophilic (allows chemical penetration during developing), transparent (doesn't block visible light), and permeable to processing solutions.
- **Why AgBr?** Its crystal lattice is highly susceptible to forming stable **sensitivity specks** when struck by a photon or electron.
- **Why polyester base?** Glass bases (used historically) were fragile; triacetate bases warped; polyester gives the best combination of strength, stability, and low cost.

### 2.3 Film Classification (ASTM E1815 / KTU Equivalent)

| Class | Speed (Relative) | Grain | Use Case | Resolution (lp/mm) |
|-------|------------------|-------|----------|--------------------|
| I     | Very Slow        | Very Fine | High-precision critical parts | ~100 |
| II    | Slow             | Fine | Aerospace, weld inspection | ~70 |
| III   | Medium           | Medium | General industrial NDT | ~40 |
| IV    | Fast             | Coarse | Thick sections, high-energy | ~25 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Students frequently confuse **speed** with **sensitivity**. Speed refers to the film's response under a *defined reference exposure*, while sensitivity (in NDT sense) refers to the **minimum detectable flaw size**. These are inverse-correlated but distinct concepts.

### 2.4 Intensifying Screens — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Lead Foil Screens (Metallic Screens)
- Thin lead sheets (typically **0.02–0.15 mm**) placed in front of and behind the film.
- Operate by emitting **secondary electrons** when struck by high-energy X-rays/gamma rays.
- These electrons expose the film emulsion more efficiently than the primary photons.
- **Critical Use:** Energies above **120 kV** (X-rays) or with **Ir-192, Co-60** sources.
- Reduce exposure time by a factor of **3 to 10×** depending on energy.

#### B. Fluorescent (Calcium Tungstate / Rare Earth) Screens
- Coated with phosphor crystals (CaWO₄ historically; rare-earth like **Gd₂O₂S:Tb** in modern screens).
- Convert X-ray photons to **visible light**, which then exposes the film.
- Provide exposure reduction up to **100×** at low energies.
- **Limitation:** Increased image unsharpness due to light diffusion; used primarily in medical radiology, less common in industrial NDT above 200 kV.

#### C. Salt Screens (Lead-foil + Fluorescent combined)
- Lead foil screen with a thin fluorescent layer facing the film.
- Combines electron-emission action with light-emission action.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Optical Density | $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | dimensionless | $I_0$ = incident light, $I$ = transmitted light |
| 2 | Average Gradient (Contrast) | $\bar{G} = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}$ | dimensionless | Slope of straight-line portion of H&D curve |
| 3 | Film Speed | $S = \dfrac{1}{E}$ where $E$ = exposure to reach $D = 1.0$ above base+fog | (Röntgen)$^{-1}$ or relative | Inverse of required exposure |
| 4 | Exposure with Intensifying Screens | $E_{screen} = \dfrac{E_{direct}}{F}$ | Röntgen | $F$ = intensification factor (typical 2–100) |
| 5 | Screen Speed Number | $S_{screen} = S_{direct} \times F$ | relative | e.g., 400-speed film + 4× screen = 1600 effective |
| 6 | Unsharpness due to Screen | $U_s = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{L}$ | mm | $d$ = grain size, $t$ = screen thickness, $L$ = source-to-screen distance |
| 7 | Latitude | $L = \log E_2 - \log E_1$ | log E | Exposure range producing usable densities |
| 8 | Base + Fog Density | $D_0 = \log\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I_{unexposed}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Should be ≤ 0.20 for fresh industrial film |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars and divisions inside formulas are written using `\dfrac` or `\vert` syntax, NOT the vertical pipe `|` symbol, to preserve the integrity of the markdown table renderer.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In aerospace weld inspection (e.g., turbine disc welds), Class II films with **fine-grain lead foil screens** are standard. In thick pressure vessel shells (100+ mm steel) examined with **Co-60**, Class IV films with **0.15 mm lead foil** screens reduce 30-minute exposures to under 5 minutes, dramatically improving throughput and reducing radiation dose to operators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optical Density from Radiographic Exposure

The optical density $D$ of a processed film is the foundation of all quantitative film analysis. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define light transmission through the film.**

When a beam of light of intensity $I_0$ strikes the developed film, a portion is absorbed by the silver grains, and the remaining intensity $I$ emerges. The fraction of light transmitted is:

$$
T = \frac{I}{I_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Define opacity.**

Opacity is the reciprocal of transmission:

$$
O = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{I_0}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Optical Density as the base-10 logarithm of opacity.**

This is a deliberate choice so that each unit of density corresponds to a **10-fold reduction** in transmitted light — convenient logarithmic scaling:

$$
D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Verify limiting cases.**

- If all light passes through (clear film): $I = I_0$, so $D = \log_{10}(1) = 0$.
- If 10% passes: $I = 0.1 I_0$, so $D = \log_{10}(10) = 1.0$.
- If 1% passes: $I = 0.01 I_0$, so $D = \log_{10}(100) = 2.0$.

**Step 5 — Final useful form.**

The density that an inspector actually *measures* using a densitometer is **Net Density**, which subtracts the base+fog contribution:

$$
D_{net} = D_{measured} - D_{base+fog}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Gradient (Ḡ) of the Characteristic (H&D) Curve

The Hurter–Driffield (H&D) curve plots $\log E$ on the x-axis and $D$ on the y-axis. The "useful" portion is approximately a straight line between density values **2.0 and 4.0** (industrial standard).

**Step 1 — Identify two points on the linear region.**

Let $(D_1, \log E_1)$ and $(D_2, \log E_2)$ be the endpoints of the straight-line region, with $D_1 = 2.0$ and $D_2 = 4.0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the slope of the secant.**

The slope of the straight-line segment is the average gradient:

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute standard density values.**

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log E_2 - \log E_1} = \frac{2.0}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical evaluation.**

Suppose $E_1 = 1.0$ mR and $E_2 = 4.0$ mR.

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10}(4.0) - \log_{10}(1.0)} = \frac{2.0}{0.6021 - 0} = 3.32
$$

This value of $\bar{G} \approx 3.3$ is typical of a **Class II** industrial film.

### 3.3 Derivation: Exposure Reduction with Intensifying Screens

**Step 1 — Define exposure without screens.**

Let $E_{direct}$ be the exposure (in Röntgens) required to achieve a target density $D_{target} = 2.0$ using the film alone (with no screens).

**Step 2 — Define Intensification Factor (IF or F).**

The intensification factor is the ratio of exposures:

$$
F = \frac{E_{direct}}{E_{screen}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for required exposure with screens.**

$$
E_{screen} = \frac{E_{direct}}{F}
$$

**Step 4 — Example calculation.**

Suppose $E_{direct} = 100$ mR using a 200-speed film alone. Using a fluorescent screen with $F = 10$:

$$
E_{screen} = \frac{100}{10} = 10\ \text{mR}
$$

The exposure time is correspondingly **10 times shorter**. If a 200-speed film produces good results, then with this screen the *effective* system speed is:

$$
S_{effective} = 200 \times 10 = 2000
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Film/Screen Selection for Common NDT Tasks

| Material | Thickness (mm) | Source | Film Class | Screen Type | Expected IF |
|----------|----------------|--------|------------|-------------|------------|
| Aluminium | 5–25 | X-ray 100 kV | II | None (direct) | 1.0 |
| Steel | 10–50 | X-ray 200 kV | II | Pb foil 0.02 mm front + 0.04 mm back | 3–5 |
| Steel | 50–100 | X-ray 400 kV | III | Pb foil 0.10 mm both sides | 5–8 |
| Steel | 100–200 | Co-60 / Ir-192 | IV | Pb foil 0.15 mm both sides | 4–6 |
| Titanium | 25–75 | X-ray 250 kV | II | Pb foil 0.05 mm + fluorescent | 8–12 |
| Composite | 5–20 | X-ray 50 kV | I | Fluorescent (rare-earth) | 50–100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the IF change with energy?** Lead screens work via *electron emission*, which is most efficient at **higher energies** (Compton scattering peak). Fluorescent screens work via *photoelectric absorption*, which dominates at **lower energies**. This is why low-energy radiography (composites, aluminium) favours fluorescent screens, while high-energy (steel, thick sections) favours lead.

### 3.5 Latent Image Formation — The Underlying Chemistry

When a single X-ray photon (or a secondary electron from a screen) is absorbed by an AgBr crystal, it liberates an electron:

$$
\text{AgBr crystal} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{AgBr}^* + e^-
$$

The electron migrates to a **sensitivity speck** (a defect site containing Ag₂S). Trapped here, it attracts a mobile silver ion:

$$
\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}^{0}
$$

This forms a stable atom of metallic silver — the **latent image** speck. During development, the reducer (hydroquinone + phenidone) catalyses further reduction of the *entire* crystal containing the speck to metallic silver:

$$
\text{AgBr + Developer} \rightarrow \text{Ag}^{0}\ (\text{black}) + \text{Br}^- + \text{oxidised developer}
$$

Undeveloped crystals are then dissolved by the **fixer** (sodium thiosulphate, Na₂S₂O₃):

$$
\text{AgBr} + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_3[\text{Ag}(\text{S}_2\text{O}_3)_2] + \text{NaBr}
$$

The image is now permanent and composed of metallic silver grains.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Layered Structure of a Duplitised Industrial X-ray Film

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PROTECTIVE OVERCOAT - Hard Gelatin Layer - Prevents Scratches"]
    nodeB["EMULSION FRONT SIDE - AgBr Crystals in Gelatin - Primary Sensitivity Layer"]
    nodeC["POLYESTER BASE - 0.18 mm PET - Mechanical Support and Dimensional Stability"]
    nodeD["EMULSION BACK SIDE - AgBr Crystals in Gelatin - Secondary Sensitivity Layer"]
    nodeE["ANTI HALATION BACKING - Light Absorbing Dye Layer - Prevents Halo Formation"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE

    nodeF["X RAY PHOTON OR SECONDARY ELECTRON ENTERS HERE"]
    nodeF -.-> nodeA

    nodeG["LATENT IMAGE FORMS IN BOTH EMULSION LAYERS"]
    nodeG -.-> nodeB
    nodeG -.-> nodeD
```

```mermaid
    subgraph Source["RADIATION SOURCE"]

        nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    end

    subgraph Process["FILM PROCESSING"]
        nodeP1["DEVELOPMENT 5 min at 20 C"]

        nodeP4["WASHING AND DRYING"]
        nodeP1 --> nodeP2
        nodeP2 --> nodeP3
        nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    end

    subgraph Output["RADIOGRAPHIC IMAGE"]

    end

    nodeS1 --> nodeO1
    nodeO1 --> nodeC1

```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Decision Logic for Film/Screen Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START Identify Test Material and Thickness"]
    nodeT1{"Material Thickness"}
    nodePath1["Less than 25 mm Aluminium or Magnesium"]
    nodePath2["25 to 75 mm Steel"]
    nodePath3["More than 75 mm Steel"]
    nodePath4["More than 100 mm Heavy Steel"]

    nodeF1["Use Class I or II Fine Grain No Screens or 0.02 mm Pb Front Only"]
    nodeF2["Use Class II Fine Grain 0.05 mm Pb Foil Both Sides"]
    nodeF3["Use Class III Medium Grain 0.10 mm Pb Foil Both Sides"]
    nodeF4["Use Class IV Coarse Grain 0.15 mm Pb Foil Both Sides Co60 Source"]

    nodeStart --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodePath1
    nodeT1 --> nodePath2

    nodeT1 --> nodePath4
    nodePath1 --> nodeF1
    nodePath2 --> nodeF2
    nodePath3 --> nodeF3
    nodePath4 --> nodeF4
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Hurter–Driffield Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph axes["H and D CURVE COORDINATE SYSTEM"]
        axisX["X AXIS log Exposure"]
        axisY["Y AXIS Optical Density D"]
    end

    subgraph regions["KEY REGIONS OF H and D CURVE"]
        nodeR1["REGION A TOE - Low Exposure - Base plus Fog Density D0"]
        nodeR2["REGION B STRAIGHT LINE - Useful Working Range - D between 2.0 and 4.0"]
        nodeR3["REGION C SHOULDER - Saturation - Maximum Density Dmax"]
        nodeR4["REGION D REVERSAL - Solarisation - High Exposure Reduces Density"]
    end

    axisX --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4

    nodeSlope["AVERAGE GRADIENT G Bar = Slope of Straight Line Region = 2.0 divided by log E2 minus log E1"]
    nodeSlope --> nodeR2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams instead of physical sketches?** Radiographic film cross-sections and H&D curves require precise geometric detail that Mermaid cannot faithfully reproduce. The block-level functional diagrams above instead map the **engineering interactions, signal flow, and decision logic** — which is what KTU examiners typically expect in answer sheets when visual aids are permitted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Explain the role of intensifying screens in industrial radiography. Why are lead foil screens preferred over fluorescent screens for steel sections thicker than 50 mm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Intensifying screens reduce the exposure time required to produce a usable radiographic image and improve overall radiographic efficiency. They work by converting the incident X-ray or gamma radiation into either secondary electrons (lead foil screens) or visible light (fluorescent screens) that more effectively expose the film emulsion.

Lead foil screens are preferred for thick steel sections because:
1. They operate via **secondary electron emission**, which is most efficient at **high photon energies** (above 200 keV) where Compton scattering dominates in steel.
2. They introduce **less image unsharpness** than fluorescent screens, since no visible-light diffusion occurs.
3. They are mechanically robust and chemically inert, suitable for harsh industrial environments.
4. Typical intensification factor of **3 to 8** is sufficient without sacrificing image quality.

Fluorescent screens are better suited for **low-energy** radiography (e.g., aluminium, composites) where the photoelectric effect dominates.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for stating role, 1 mark for lead foil mechanism, 1 mark for energy rationale)**

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define optical density and base-plus-fog density. Why must an interpreter subtract base+fog density before evaluating a radiograph?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Optical Density (D)** is the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of incident light intensity to transmitted light intensity through a processed film:

$$
D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

**Base-plus-Fog Density ($D_0$)** is the small amount of density always present in an unexposed but fully processed film, arising from the inherent density of the polyester base plus slight fogging of the emulsion during manufacturing, storage, or processing.

The interpreter must subtract $D_0$ to obtain the **net density** that truly represents the radiographic exposure:

$$
D_{net} = D_{measured} - D_0
$$

This correction is essential because base+fog density would otherwise **artificially reduce contrast**, leading to misinterpretation of flaw indications.

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for D definition, $D_0$ definition, and rationale for subtraction)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper OEMET724]

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the construction of a duplitised industrial X-ray film. Explain the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify radiographic films as per ASTM standards. Compare Class II and Class IV films with respect to grain size, relative speed, resolution, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Film Construction – 7 marks:**

A duplitised industrial X-ray film has **five layers** (label and describe each):

1. **Protective Overcoat** (~1 µm hard gelatin): Guards emulsion against scratches and pressure marks. (1 mark)
2. **Emulsion Layers** (both sides, ~10–15 µm each): Gelatin matrix with AgBr crystals; the photosensitive layer where the latent image forms. Duplitised design doubles sensitivity. (2 marks)
3. **Polyester Base** (~0.18 mm PET): Provides mechanical strength, flexibility, and dimensional stability; transparent after processing. (1 mark)
4. **Anti-halation Backing**: Light-absorbing dye prevents back-reflection (halation) that would blur the image. (1 mark)
5. **Subbing Layer / Adhesion Promoter**: Ensures emulsion firmly adheres to the base throughout wet processing. (1 mark)

Working principle: Incident X-ray photon → absorbed by AgBr → forms latent image speck → developed to metallic silver → produces optical density. (1 mark)

**(b) Film Classification – 7 marks:**

ASTM E1815 classification table:

| Parameter | Class II | Class IV |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Relative Speed | Slow (e.g., 100) | Fast (e.g., 800) |
| Grain Size | Fine | Coarse |
| Resolution | ~70 lp/mm | ~25 lp/mm |
| Exposure Time | Long | Short |
| Typical Application | Aerospace welds, critical castings | Thick steel, Co-60 inspection |
| Image Quality | Very high contrast | Lower contrast, wider latitude |

(5 marks for comparison; 2 marks for application scenarios)

---

#### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper OEMET724 Alternate]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of lead foil intensifying screens. Derive the relationship between exposure with and without screens using the intensification factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a characteristic (H&D) curve? Sketch and explain its salient regions. Define and calculate the average gradient Ḡ for a film whose useful density range from 2.0 to 4.0 corresponds to exposures from 1.0 mR to 4.0 mR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lead Foil Screens – 7 marks:**

**Principle (4 marks):** Lead foil screens are thin lead sheets (0.02–0.15 mm) placed in direct contact with the film. When high-energy X-rays or gamma photons strike the lead, they undergo **Compton scattering and photoelectric absorption**, ejecting **secondary electrons**. These electrons have much shorter ranges in the film emulsion than the original photons and deposit their energy very efficiently in the AgBr crystals, producing many more developable latent images per unit of incident exposure.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $E_{direct}$ = exposure required without screens to achieve $D = 2.0$. The **intensification factor** $F$ is defined as:

$$
F = \frac{E_{direct}}{E_{screen}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
E_{screen} = \frac{E_{direct}}{F}
$$

**Example:** If $E_{direct} = 60$ mR and $F = 5$, then $E_{screen} = 12$ mR — exposure time reduced by 5×.

**(b) H&D Curve – 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Hurter–Driffield curve is a plot of **optical density $D$** (y-axis) versus **logarithm of exposure $\log E$** (x-axis) for a given film processed under standard conditions. It characterises the film's response.

**Sketch and Regions (3 marks):**
- **Toe (low exposure):** Slow rise from base+fog density $D_0$.
- **Straight-line region:** Linear portion — used for quantitative radiography; corresponds to densities 2.0 to 4.0.
- **Shoulder:** Curve flattens as AgBr is exhausted; $D_{max}$ reached.
- **Reversal (Solarisation):** At very high exposure, density actually decreases due to bromine re-halogenation of silver.

**Average Gradient Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}
$$

Substituting $D_1 = 2.0$, $D_2 = 4.0$, $E_1 = 1.0$ mR, $E_2 = 4.0$ mR:

$$
\bar{G} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10}(4.0) - \log_{10}(1.0)} = \frac{2.0}{0.6021 - 0} = 3.32
$$

This indicates a **medium-high contrast** film.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in OEMET724 Film/Screen Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing speed with sensitivity (–1 to –2 marks):** Speed refers to *response to exposure*, sensitivity in NDT context refers to *minimum detectable flaw size*. They are inversely related but conceptually distinct.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract base+fog density (–1 mark):** Any density calculation must explicitly show the subtraction step.
> 3. **Using fluorescent screens at high energies (–2 marks):** Showing the wrong screen-film combination for thick steel sections is a common error. Always link energy to screen mechanism (photoelectric vs Compton).
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Density is dimensionless but exposure must always be in mR or R; thickness in mm; resolution in lp/mm.
> 5. **Omitting the *Why* layer in film construction (–1 mark):** Naming layers without explaining the *function* of each layer costs marks in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Film Layers (in order, top to bottom):**
- Protective Overcoat → Front Emulsion → Polyester Base → Back Emulsion → Anti-halation Backing

**Film Class Mnemonic — "I.V.M.F":** "**I**mpeccable **V**ery **M**edium **F**ast" → I (slowest, finest), II, III, IV (fastest, coarsest).

**Key Formulas (memorise these):**
- $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$
- $D_{net} = D_{measured} - D_0$
- $\bar{G} = (D_2 - D_1) / (\log E_2 - \log E_1)$
- $E_{screen} = E_{direct} / F$
- $S_{effective} = S_{film} \times F$

**Screen Selection Rules:**
- **Low energy (≤120 kV):** Fluorescent screens, IF up to 100.
- **High energy (>200 kV, Co-60):** Lead foil screens, IF = 3–8.
- **Direct exposure (no screen):** Used only for finest resolution at very low energies.

**ASTM Density Standards (industrial NDT):**
- $D_{target} \geq 2.0$ for acceptable radiograph
- $D_{max} \leq 4.0$ to avoid saturation
- Base+Fog $D_0 \leq 0.20$ for fresh film
- Useful latitude: density 2.0 to 4.0

**Process Sequence (memory hook — "DSFW"):** **D**evelop → **S**top → **F**ix → **W**ash/Dry.

**Typical Intensification Factors (range):**
- 0.02 mm Pb foil: ~3×
- 0.10 mm Pb foil: ~5–6×
- 0.15 mm Pb foil: ~6–8×
- Rare-earth fluorescent: up to 100×

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked about "film-screen combination," always specify *all four* parameters — film class, screen type, screen thickness, and intended energy range. Examiners award full marks only when these four are clearly stated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiographic Quality** is the combined measure of **sensitivity, contrast, definition, and density** that determines the ability of a radiograph to reveal fine discontinuities in the test object. It quantifies *how clearly* the recorded image represents the internal structure of the inspected material.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the quality of a radiograph is evaluated through the following five primary characteristics:

1. **Radiographic Sensitivity** – the smallest percentage change in specimen thickness that is detectable on the radiograph (expressed as a percentage).
2. **Radiographic Contrast** – the difference in optical density between two adjacent areas of the radiograph.
3. **Definition (Sharpness)** – the clarity and crispness of the image of an object edge or boundary.
4. **Density (Optical Density)** – the degree of blackening of the developed film, measured by a densitometer.
5. **Latitude** – the range of specimen thickness that can be recorded with acceptable density and contrast on a single film.

Mathematically, **Radiographic Sensitivity** is given by:

$$\text{Sensitivity (\%)} = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 100$$

where $\Delta x$ is the smallest detectable thickness change and $x$ is the total thickness traversed by the beam.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a radiograph like a photograph of a birthday cake sliced down the middle.**
> A *good photograph* (analogous to a *good radiograph*) must clearly show: 🎂
> - **Sharpness** of the icing patterns (Definition).
> - **Difference in colour/shade** between the sponge and the cream filling (Contrast).
> - **Overall darkness** must be just right — not too washed out and not pitch-black (Density).
> - **Tiny hidden objects** like a coin inside the cake must be visible (Sensitivity).
> - **Range of layers** from cherry on top to base plate must all be visible (Latitude).

If any of these are missing, the photograph fails its purpose — similarly, a radiograph must satisfy *all* quality criteria to reliably detect defects like cracks, porosity, or inclusions.

## 1.3 Key Quality Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical KTU-Recommended Range |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Density (D) | $D$ | **2.0 – 4.0** |
| Geometric Unsharpness | $U_g$ | **≤ 0.5 mm** (for general RT) |
| Sensitivity (ASTM) | $S$ | **1 % – 2 %** |
| Source-to-Film Distance | $D$ | **≥ 7 × t × d^0.6** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A radiograph of acceptable quality must show the required **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)** sensitivity, exhibit a **density between 2.0 and 4.0**, and have **geometric unsharpness below 0.5 mm** for general industrial radiography.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Radiographic Quality

A radiograph is judged by **five interacting pillars**. None can be neglected; improving one often degrades another. A skilled radiographer must balance them.

### Pillar 1 — Radiographic Sensitivity
- **Definition:** Ability of the radiograph to reveal small discontinuities or small thickness changes.
- **Expression:** Expressed as a percentage, derived from the IQI (penetrameter).
- **Two types:**
  * **Absolute Sensitivity** → measured directly from an IQI image.
  * **Relative Sensitivity** → ratio of smallest detectable feature to specimen thickness.
- **Governing factors:** Source size, SFD, OFD, film grain, exposure energy, scatter control.

### Pillar 2 — Radiographic Contrast
- **Definition:** The difference in optical density between two adjacent regions of a radiograph.
- **Two components:**
  * **Subject Contrast ($C_s$)** — due to differences in thickness, density, or composition.
  * **Film Contrast ($C_f$)** — inherent property of the film (slope of the characteristic curve).
- **Total Contrast:** $\Delta D = C_s \times C_f$
- **Higher kV reduces subject contrast** (harder beam, less differential absorption).

### Pillar 3 — Definition (Sharpness)
- **Definition:** Crispness of the boundary of an image feature.
- **Loss of definition** is called **unsharpness ($U$)**.
- **Three types of unsharpness:**
  * **Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$)** — due to finite focal-spot size.
  * **Film Unsharpness ($U_f$)** — due to film grain and emulsion thickness.
  * **Movement Unsharpness ($U_m$)** — due to relative motion between source, object, and film.
- **Total Unsharpness (approximate):** $U_t \approx (U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_m^2)^{1/2}$

### Pillar 4 — Optical Density
- **Definition:** The logarithmic measure of film blackening.
- **Mathematical form:**
$$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)$$
where $I_0$ = incident light intensity, $I_t$ = transmitted light intensity.
- **Optimal viewing range:** **2.0 to 4.0** for manual viewers; up to **4.5** for high-intensity viewers.

### Pillar 5 — Latitude
- **Definition:** The range of specimen thickness over which the radiograph yields densities within the useful range (2.0–4.0).
- **High-kV exposures** → wider latitude, but reduced contrast.
- **Low-kV exposures** → narrow latitude, but high contrast.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical Density | $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I_t)$ | $I_0$ = incident light, $I_t$ = transmitted light |
| 2 | Geometric Unsharpness | $U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{D_{sf}}$ | $d$ = focal spot (mm), $t$ = OFD (mm), $D_{sf}$ = SFD (mm) |
| 3 | Total Unsharpness | $U_t \approx \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_m^2}$ | mm |
| 4 | Radiographic Sensitivity | $S(\%) = \dfrac{\Delta x}{x} \times 100$ | $\Delta x$ = min. detectable thickness, $x$ = total thickness |
| 5 | ASTM Hole Sensitivity | $S_{ASTM} = \dfrac{d_{hole}}{t_{specimen}} \times 100$ | $d_{hole}$ = smallest visible hole diameter |
| 6 | Wire Sensitivity | $S_w = \dfrac{d_{wire}}{t_{specimen}} \times 100$ | $d_{wire}$ = smallest visible wire diameter |
| 7 | Inverse Square Law | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{D_{sf}^2}$ | $I$ = intensity, $D_{sf}$ = source-to-film distance |
| 8 | Min. SFD Rule | $D_{sf,\min} \approx 7 \, t \, d^{0.6}$ | $t$ = specimen thickness, $d$ = source size (mm) |
| 9 | Subject Contrast | $C_s = \dfrac{I_1 - I_2}{I_1}$ | $I_1, I_2$ = intensities through two regions |
| 10 | Film Gamma | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta D}{\Delta \log E}$ | $E$ = exposure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All lengths in **millimetres (mm)**; densities are **dimensionless**; sensitivity is **percentage (%))**; exposures in **Roentgens (R)** or **Coulombs/kg (C/kg)**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Nuclear:** High-sensitivity radiographs (1 %–2 %) detect micro-cracks in turbine blades, fuel rods, and welded reactor vessels.
- **Pipeline & Pressure Vessel Inspection:** IQI sensitivity of 2 % is mandatory per ASME Sec V & Sec VIII.
- **Casting & Forging Quality Control:** Density control (2.0–4.0) ensures volumetric defects like porosity, shrinkage, and inclusions are not missed.
- **Additive Manufacturing (3D-Printed Parts):** Sharp definition is critical to detect lack-of-fusion defects between layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$)

### Step 1 — Problem Setup
Consider a source of finite focal-spot **diameter $d$** placed at distance **$D_{sf}$** from the film. A small object (a flaw or detail) is at distance **$t$** from the film (Object-to-Film Distance, OFD). The source-to-object distance is therefore **$(D_{sf} - t)$**.

### Step 2 — Similar-Triangle Construction
The flaw edge casts a shadow that has a sharp central **umbra** and a blurred peripheral **penumbra** of width $U_g$. By projecting rays from the two extreme edges of the focal spot through the flaw edge to the film, two similar triangles are formed.

The smaller triangle has height $(D_{sf} - t)$ and base $d$ (the focal-spot diameter).  
The larger triangle has height $D_{sf}$ and base $(d + U_g)$ (the full shadow including penumbra).

### Step 3 — Ratio Equation
By similarity of the two triangles:

$$\frac{d}{D_{sf} - t} \;=\; \frac{d + U_g}{D_{sf}}$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Multiplication
Multiplying both sides:

$$d \cdot D_{sf} \;=\; (d + U_g)(D_{sf} - t)$$

$$d \cdot D_{sf} \;=\; d(D_{sf} - t) + U_g(D_{sf} - t)$$

$$d \cdot D_{sf} \;=\; d \cdot D_{sf} - d \cdot t + U_g(D_{sf} - t)$$

### Step 5 — Simplification
The $d \cdot D_{sf}$ terms on both sides cancel:

$$0 \;=\; -d \cdot t + U_g(D_{sf} - t)$$

$$d \cdot t \;=\; U_g(D_{sf} - t)$$

Since the specimen is much closer to the film than to the source, $(D_{sf} - t) \approx D_{sf}$, so:

$$\boxed{U_g \;=\; \frac{d \cdot t}{D_{sf}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - $U_g$ **increases** with larger focal spot $d$ — use a **small focal spot**.
> - $U_g$ **increases** with larger OFD $t$ — keep the object **close to the film**.
> - $U_g$ **decreases** with larger SFD $D_{sf}$ — increase the **source-to-film distance**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum SFD Rule

Starting from $U_g \le U_{g,\text{allow}}$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{d \cdot t}{D_{sf}} \;\le\; U_{g,\text{allow}}$$

$$D_{sf} \;\ge\; \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\text{allow}}}$$

Setting the allowable geometric unsharpness to a function of thickness $U_{g,\text{allow}} = 0.2 \, t$ (a common KTU/empirical rule of thumb), we obtain:

$$D_{sf,\min} \;=\; \frac{d \cdot t}{0.2 \, t} \;=\; 5d$$

Refining with the empirical exponent 0.6 used in ASME Sec V:

$$\boxed{D_{sf,\min} \;\approx\; 7 \, t \, d^{0.6}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Geometric Unsharpness

**Given:**
- Focal spot size $d = 2.0$ mm
- Object-to-film distance $t = 25$ mm (specimen thickness + mounting)
- Source-to-film distance $D_{sf} = 700$ mm

**Step 1 — Write the formula.**

$$U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D_{sf}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$U_g = \frac{2.0 \times 25}{700}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator.**

$$U_g = \frac{50}{700}$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$U_g = 0.0714 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with limit.**
$U_g = 0.0714$ mm $\le 0.5$ mm ✓ (acceptable for general RT).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Radiographic Sensitivity

**Given:**
- Specimen thickness $x = 25$ mm of mild steel
- Smallest visible wire on IQI after exposure: $d_{wire} = 0.20$ mm

**Step 1 — Formula for wire sensitivity.**

$$S_w = \frac{d_{wire}}{t_{specimen}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$S_w = \frac{0.20}{25} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute ratio.**

$$S_w = 0.008 \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{S_w = 0.8 \, \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A sensitivity of 0.8 % is **excellent** (better than the typical 2 % required by ASME) — the radiograph is capable of detecting sub-millimetre thickness variations.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Optical Density

**Given:**
- Incident light intensity on the film region: $I_0 = 10{,}000$ lux
- Transmitted light intensity through the film: $I_t = 100$ lux

**Step 1 — Formula.**

$$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{100}\right) = \log_{10}(100)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$D = 2.0$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
Density $D = 2.0$ is **exactly at the lower KTU-acceptable limit** — the image is just readable on a standard viewer.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Quality Parameter | Improved by | Degraded by | KTU-Optimal Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity ($S$) | Smaller $d$, larger $D_{sf}$, smaller $t$ | High $kV$ (reduces contrast), large grain film | **1 %–2 %** |
| Contrast ($\Delta D$) | Lower $kV$, high-$\gamma$ film | Scatter, high $kV$, over-development | High (qualitative) |
| Definition ($U_g$) | Smaller $d$, smaller $t$, larger $D_{sf}$ | Larger $d$, larger $t$, motion | **$U_g \le 0.5$ mm** |
| Density ($D$) | Longer exposure, proper development | Under/over-exposure, bad chemistry | **2.0 – 4.0** |
| Latitude | Higher $kV$, wide-latitude film | Low $kV$ | **Wide, project-dependent** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Factors Affecting Radiographic Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Radiographic Quality] --> B[Geometric Factors]
    A --> C[Film Factors]
    A --> D[Exposure Factors]
    A --> E[Processing Factors]
    A --> F[Scatter Factors]

    B --> B1[Focal Spot Size d]
    B --> B2[Source to Film Distance]
    B --> B3[Object to Film Distance]
    B1 --> G1[Geometric Unsharpness Ug]
    B2 --> G1
    B3 --> G1

    C --> C1[Film Grain]
    C --> C2[Film Speed]
    C --> C3[Film Contrast Gamma]
    C1 --> G2[Film Unsharpness Uf]
    C3 --> G3[Image Contrast]

    D --> D1[kV Selection]
    D --> D2[mA Selection]
    D --> D3[Exposure Time]
    D1 --> G4[Penetration and Latitude]
    D3 --> G5[Film Density]

    E --> E1[Developer Temperature]
    E --> E2[Development Time]
    E --> E3[Chemistry Strength]
    E1 --> G5
    E2 --> G5
    E3 --> G5

    F --> F1[Scattered Radiation]
    F1 --> G6[Reduced Contrast and Fog]
    F1 --> G7[Lead Screens and Collimation Reduce]

    G1 --> H[Final Radiographic Image]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
```

## 4.2 IQI Sensitivity Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start] --> P2[Place IQI on Source Side of Specimen]
    P2 --> P3[Expose Radiograph]
    P3 --> P4[Develop Film]
    P4 --> P5[Identify Smallest Visible IQI Feature]
    P5 --> P6[Calculate Sensitivity Percentage]
    P6 --> P7[Compare Against Acceptance Standard ASME or IS]
    P7 --> P8{Sensitivity Acceptable?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Accept Radiograph]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Reshoot with Improved Parameters]
    P10 --> P2
    P9 --> P11[End]
    P11 --> P12[Final Report Signed by Level II Examiner]
```

## 4.3 Optical Density Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Light Source] --> M2[Collimator]
    M2 --> M3[Radiograph on Stage]
    M3 --> M4[Transmitted Light]
    M4 --> M5[Photocell Detector]
    M5 --> M6[Microprocessor]
    M6 --> M7[Digital Density Display]
    M7 --> M8{Density in 2.0 to 4.0 Range?}
    M8 -- Yes --> M9[Acceptable Reading]
    M8 -- No --> M10[Adjust Exposure or Development]
    M10 --> M11[Reshoot or Reprocess]
    M11 --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first flowchart identifies the **five input groups** governing quality and traces each down to the **final image**. The second diagram shows the **procedural logic** an examiner follows using an IQI (penetrameter). The third illustrates how **density** is measured instrumentally using a densitometer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define radiographic sensitivity. List two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**
> Radiographic sensitivity is the smallest percentage change in specimen thickness that can be detected on a radiograph, expressed as $S(\%) = (\Delta x / x) \times 100$.  
> **Factors affecting sensitivity:**  
> (i) Focal-spot size of the source.  
> (ii) Source-to-film distance (SFD).  
> (iii) Film grain size (any two).

*(Marks: 1 + 2 = 3)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the permissible range of optical density for an acceptable radiograph?**

**Model Answer:**
The permissible range of optical density for an acceptable industrial radiograph is **2.0 to 4.0**. Below 2.0 the image is too thin (insufficient blackening for proper viewing); above 4.0 the film becomes too dark to read on a standard illuminator (requires a high-intensity viewer with optical density up to 4.5).

*(Marks: 3)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various **factors affecting the quality of a radiograph**. Discuss how each factor influences image quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radiographic exposure uses a source of focal spot **2.5 mm**, an SFD of **800 mm**, and an OFD of **30 mm**. Calculate the geometric unsharpness and comment on its acceptability. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution A(a)

**Factors Affecting Radiographic Quality (each ~1 mark):**

1. **Geometric Factors** — focal spot size, SFD, OFD. Large $d$ and large $t$ increase $U_g$.
2. **Film Factors** — grain, contrast, speed. Coarser grain reduces definition.
3. **Exposure Factors** — kV, mA, time. kV affects contrast and latitude.
4. **Processing Factors** — developer temperature, time, agitation, chemistry.
5. **Scattered Radiation** — degrades contrast; controlled by filters, collimators, lead screens.

*Diagram (2 marks):* Refer to the flowchart in Section 4.1.

### Solution A(b)

**Step 1 — Write formula.**

$$U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D_{sf}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$U_g = \frac{2.5 \times 30}{800}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$U_g = \frac{75}{800} = 0.09375 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**
$U_g = 0.094$ mm $\le 0.5$ mm → **Acceptable** for general industrial radiography.

*(Marks: 2 for formula + 3 for substitution + 2 for comment = 7)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**. Explain with sketches the **hole-type and wire-type IQIs**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 20 mm thick aluminium plate is radiographed and the smallest wire visible on a wire-type IQI has a diameter of **0.16 mm**. Calculate the radiographic sensitivity and state whether it meets the ASME requirement of 2 %. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution B(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** An IQI, also called a **penetrameter**, is a device placed on the source side of the specimen during exposure to verify the sensitivity of the radiographic technique. It is *not* used to measure defect size.

**Hole-type IQI (2 marks):** A rectangular plate of the same material as the specimen with three rows of holes of diameters equal to **1T, 2T, and 4T**, where T is the specimen thickness (per ASTM E747).

**Wire-type IQI (2 marks):** A set of **seven parallel wires** of progressively increasing diameter, typically ranging from 0.05 mm to 1.6 mm (per ASTM and EN standards). The diameter of the thinnest visible wire determines sensitivity.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Show both IQIs labelled with diameters.

### Solution B(b)

**Step 1 — Wire sensitivity formula.**

$$S_w = \frac{d_{wire}}{t_{specimen}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$S_w = \frac{0.16}{20} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$S_w = 0.008 \times 100 = 0.8 \, \%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ASME 2 % requirement.**
$0.8\,\% < 2\,\%$ → Sensitivity is **better (finer) than required**, hence the radiograph **MEETS** the ASME quality standard.

*(Marks: 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of $U_g$** — always write **mm** in the final answer (KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit).
> 2. **Inverting the sensitivity formula** — students often write $x/\Delta x$ instead of $\Delta x/x$. Remember: smaller the visible IQI feature, *higher* the sensitivity, *lower* the percentage.
> 3. **Confusing optical density with radiographic density** — they are related but not identical; density is *logarithmic*.
> 4. **Skipping the comment step** — in numerical questions, the final **"comment"** or **"interpretation"** carries 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without labels** — KTU examiners award marks for *labelled diagrams* only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quality of a Good Radiograph:**

- ✅ Quality is judged by **5 pillars**: sensitivity, contrast, definition, density, latitude.
- ✅ Geometric unsharpness formula: $U_g = d \cdot t / D_{sf}$ — **always in mm**.
- ✅ Optical density: $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I_t)$; **acceptable range = 2.0 – 4.0**.
- ✅ Sensitivity: $S(\%) = (\Delta x / x) \times 100$; **lower % = better quality**.
- ✅ IQI (penetrameter) is placed on **source side** of the specimen.
- ✅ Hole-type IQI holes: **1T, 2T, 4T**; wire-type IQI uses 7 wires.
- ✅ Minimum SFD rule: $D_{sf,\min} \approx 7 \, t \, d^{0.6}$ (ASME).
- ✅ **Small focal spot + large SFD + small OFD** = sharper image.
- ✅ **Low kV** = high contrast, narrow latitude; **High kV** = low contrast, wide latitude.
- ✅ **Scattered radiation** reduces contrast — controlled using **filters, collimators, lead screens**.
- ✅ Total unsharpness: $U_t \approx \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_m^2}$.
- ✅ Three types of unsharpness: **Geometric ($U_g$), Film ($U_f$), Movement ($U_m$)**.
- ✅ ASME Sec V accepts sensitivity up to **2 %** (general); aerospace demands **1 %–1.5 %**.
- ✅ Densitometer is the instrument used to measure optical density.
- ✅ Radiographic film characteristic curve slope = **film gamma ($\gamma$)** = inherent contrast.
- ✅ Always specify **units and comments** in numerical answers to fetch full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film Processing, Interpretation, and Evaluation of Test Results
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Processing, Interpretation, and Evaluation of Test Results

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Film Processing — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Film Processing** in Industrial Radiography refers to the sequence of strictly controlled chemical and physical operations — *developing, stopping, fixing, washing, and drying* — applied to an exposed radiographic film under safelight conditions in a darkroom, in order to convert the invisible latent image produced by X-ray or gamma-ray exposure into a permanent, viewable, and quantitatively interpretable radiograph (RT image).

The exposed film carries a **latent image** (formed by the partial reduction of silver halide crystals in the film emulsion by ionizing radiation). Processing amplifies this latent image approximately **10⁸ to 10⁹ times** to produce the visible metallic silver image that the radiographer can examine.

### 1.2 Radiographic Interpretation — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiographic Interpretation** is the systematic, disciplined act of examining the processed radiograph under controlled viewing conditions to identify, locate, size, and characterize volumetric or planar discontinuities, using reference Image Quality Indicators (IQIs), densitometers, and codified acceptance standards (e.g., ASME Section V, ASTM E142, ISO 5817).

### 1.3 Evaluation of Test Results

The final cognitive step in the radiographic inspection chain. The interpreter converts visual information on the film into a **binary pass/fail engineering decision** (or a graded acceptance tier), based on:
- The nature of discontinuity (crack, porosity, slag, lack of fusion, etc.)
- The size, distribution, and orientation of the discontinuity
- The applicable **code/standard** (ASME, AWS, EN, IS)
- The **sensitivity** proven by the IQI and the **density** measured by a densitometer.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Darkroom is a Photo Lab, the Radiograph is a Witness Statement"**
>
> Imagine a crime scene where invisible fingerprints have been left on glass. You cannot see them under normal light. A forensic scientist carefully dusts them with powder (the *developer*), fixes them with a sealant spray (the *fixer*), washes off excess dust (the *wash*), and then photographs them. Only then can a detective *interpret* the fingerprint pattern to identify the culprit (the *defect*).
>
> In radiography, the **ionizing radiation** is the dust. The **film emulsion** is the glass. The **darkroom chemistry** is the powder-and-sealant process. The **trained interpreter** is the detective. If the chemistry is wrong, even a perfect exposure produces a *useless* image — the fingerprint is washed away or smeared.

### 1.5 Why This Module Matters (KTU Industry Relevance)

In Kerala's industrial landscape — **Cochin Shipyard, BPCL Kochi Refinery, FACT, NTPC Kayamkulam, BrahMos Aerospace Trivandrum, and the IFFCO fertilizer plants** — radiographic interpretation is a regulated, audit-ready skill. Every weld in a pressure vessel, every pipeline girth weld, and every aerospace component undergoes a chain where the **final sign-off is the interpreter's signature**. A misinterpretation is a **safety hazard** (catastrophic failure of pressurized equipment) and a **legal liability**.

### 1.6 Key Physical Metrics Used in This Topic

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Film base density | D₀ | 0.10 – 0.20 | ASTM E1815 |
| Working density (D-w) | D_w | 2.00 – 4.00 (typical 2.5–3.5) | ASME V Art. 2 |
| Optical density range | ΔD | 1.5 – 3.0 (must exceed code min.) | ASME V T-282 |
| IQI Sensitivity | % | 1% – 4% of specimen thickness | ASTM E142 |
| Developer temperature | T | 20 °C ± 1 °C (manual); 28 °C (auto) | ASTM E94 |
| Development time | t | 5–10 min (manual); 8 min @ 28 °C (auto) | ASTM E94 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five Stages of Film Processing

Film processing is a **sequential, time-and-temperature-critical** process. Skipping or reordering any stage destroys the radiograph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "Develop Stop Fix Wash Dry" (DSFWD)**
> The five sequential stages that any radiographic film must pass through.

#### Stage 1 — Development (Reducing the Latent Image)

- The film is immersed in a **developer solution** (typically Kodak D-19b, Agfa G128, or Ilford ID-19).
- Active chemical: **Hydroquinone + Phenidone (or Elon/Metol)** as reducing agents.
- The **reducing agents donate electrons** to the exposed silver bromide (AgBr) crystals, converting them to **metallic silver (Ag⁰)**, which is black and opaque.
- Exposed crystals develop faster because the latent image specks (Ag₂S, sensitivity centers formed during exposure) act as **catalytic nucleation sites**.
- Unexposed crystals also slowly develop (**fog**) — this is why development is timed strictly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Arrhenius Temperature Dependence**
> Development rate approximately doubles for every **10 °C** rise in developer temperature. This is why the developer bath is held in a thermostatic water jacket at 20 °C ± 1 °C (manual) or 28 °C ± 0.5 °C (automatic processors).

#### Stage 2 — Stop Bath (Acid Neutralization)

- Immersion in a **dilute acetic acid solution (typically 1–3%)** OR a water rinse.
- **Purpose:** Rapidly neutralizes the alkaline developer carried over on the film surface.
- Prevents **continued development** (which would fog the radiograph).
- Prevents **developer contamination** of the acidic fixer bath in the next stage.
- Duration: **30 seconds** (acid stop) or 1–2 minutes (water rinse).

#### Stage 3 — Fixing (Dissolving Unreacted Silver Halide)

- Immersion in **sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃) — "Hypo"** solution.
- Reaction: $\text{AgBr} + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_3[\text{Ag(S}_2\text{O}_3)_2] + \text{NaBr}$
- The unexposed, undeveloped silver halide crystals dissolve into a soluble complex ion and are **washed out of the emulsion**.
- The image is now **light-stable** because no more light-sensitive crystals remain.
- Fixing time: **2× the clearing time** (clearing time = when film becomes visually transparent). Typical 5–15 minutes.

#### Stage 4 — Washing (Removing Hypo Residues)

- **Critical** for archival stability. Residual thiosulfate ions cause **image fade and brown staining** over months/years.
- Minimum wash: **20–30 minutes in running water** (flow rate to change tank volume 4× per hour).
- For archival records (> 5 years), use a **hypo-eliminator** (ammonia or hydrogen peroxide) followed by a **final rinse in distilled water with wetting agent**.

#### Stage 5 — Drying (Removing Water Without Damage)

- Film is hung in a **dust-free, heated drying cabinet** (40–50 °C, low humidity).
- **Automatic processors** use squeegee rollers + heated air knives.
- **DO NOT** dry films over 50 °C — emulsion melts and the image is destroyed.
- Drying time: 15–30 minutes (cabinet), 90 seconds (automatic processor).

### 2.2 The Chemistry in One Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Develop):} \quad & \text{AgBr} + \text{Reducing Agent} \rightarrow \text{Ag}^0 \downarrow + \text{Br}^- \\
\text{Stage 3 (Fix):} \quad & \text{AgBr} + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_3[\text{Ag(S}_2\text{O}_3)_2] + \text{NaBr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where R = Hydroquinone or Phenidone.

### 2.3 Automatic vs Manual Processing

| Parameter | Manual (Tank) | Automatic (Roller-Transport) |
|---|---|---|
| Developer Temp | 20 °C ± 1 °C | 28 °C ± 0.5 °C |
| Development Time | 5–10 min | 8 min (fixed cycle) |
| Total Cycle Time | 60–90 min | 8–12 min |
| Throughput | Low (lab use) | High (production) |
| Consistency | Operator-dependent | Highly consistent |
| Setup Cost | Low | High (processor ~ ₹8–15 lakh) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Optical Density & Image Quality

| Concept | Formula | Symbols / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Density | $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $I_0$ = incident light; $I$ = transmitted light |
| Density behind base + fog | $D_{total} = D_{image} + D_{base+fog}$ | ASTM E1815 |
| Useful Density Range | $D_w$ from 2.0 to 4.0 | ASME V T-282 |
| Radiographic Contrast | $\Delta D = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | Must exceed code minimum |
| IQI Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{d_{IQI}}{T_{specimen}} \times 100\%$ | $d$ = smallest visible wire diameter |
| Geometric Unsharpness | $U_g = \dfrac{(t \cdot F)}{d}$ | FFD; $t$ = source size; $d$ = object-to-film |
| Inverse Square Law | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$ | Intensity vs distance |
| Exposure Equation | $X = \dfrac{K \cdot V^n \cdot Z}{FFD^2}$ | $K$, $V$, $n$ = empirical; $Z$ = material |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tabulation Note (KTU Math Convention):** Inside markdown table cells, vertical bars `|` are NEVER used. For absolute value or norm notation, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ using LaTeX commands to preserve table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessel fabrication** (ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1): every weld must be radiographed to ASME Sec. V Article 2 acceptance criteria.
- **Aerospace welds** (BrahMos Trivandrum): interpretation follows AWS D17.1 or MIL-STD-2154.
- **Pipeline girth welds** (Kochi-Mangaluru pipeline): API 1104 acceptance.
- **Foundry castings** (KELTEC Trivandrum): ASTM E446 reference radiographs for casting defects.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: The Characteristic Curve (H&D Curve) — $D$ vs $\log E$

The **Hurter & Driffield (H&D) curve** is the fundamental working tool of the radiographer. It plots Optical Density ($D$) on the linear Y-axis against the logarithm of Exposure ($\log E$) on the linear X-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> A radiograph is the *visible result* of the H&D curve. Every dark spot on the film corresponds to a point on the curve with a specific $D$ for a specific $E$. Without this curve, "darkness" has no quantitative meaning.

The curve has three regions (from low to high exposure):

1. **Toe (fog + underexposure):** $D < D_{fog} + 0.05$; the image is too light to interpret reliably.
2. **Straight-line (working region):** The slope is **constant** — this is the diagnostic region.
3. **Shoulder + Saturation (overexposure):** $D$ asymptotes; the film is fully black; no information.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Definition of Gradient (Slope):} \quad & G = \dfrac{dD}{d(\log E)} \\
\text{For two points on the straight line:} \quad & G = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1} \\
\text{If } \log E_2 - \log E_1 = 1 \; (\text{i.e., factor of 10 in exposure}): \quad & G = D_2 - D_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Example — Working Out the IQI Sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic 3-mark KTU Part-A style problem** that has appeared in multiple university exams.

**Problem:**
A 20 mm thick mild-steel plate is radiographed. A wire-type IQI (ASTM E747) is placed on the source side. The 6th wire from the thinnest (0.40 mm) is the smallest visible. The wire set has wires of diameter: 0.10, 0.13, 0.16, 0.20, 0.25, **0.32**, 0.40, 0.50, 0.64, 0.80 mm. Calculate the radiographic sensitivity.

**Step 1 — Identify smallest visible wire diameter.**
The visible wire is the 6th, with $d_{IQI} = 0.32$ mm. *(Note: We use the 0.32 mm value — not 0.40 — even though the problem states "6th from thinnest." Always re-verify the wire diameter value from the IQI set table.)*

**Step 2 — Apply the sensitivity formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S (\%) &= \dfrac{d_{IQI}}{T_{specimen}} \times 100 \\
S (\%) &= \dfrac{0.32 \text{ mm}}{20 \text{ mm}} \times 100 \\
S (\%) &= 0.016 \times 100 \\
S (\%) &= 1.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
A sensitivity of 1.6% means the technique can resolve a defect equal to 1.6% of the specimen thickness. Per ASME V T-282 / IS 2595, sensitivity should be **1% to 2%** for general welds. **The technique is acceptable.**

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Example — Densitometer Reading Interpretation

**Problem:**
A radiograph of a 25 mm aluminium casting has the following densitometer readings:
- Background (clean region): $D_{bg} = 1.20$
- Through a 5 mm shim (for density compensation): $D_{comp} = 2.55$
- Through a 5 mm thickener (added material, for density compensation): $D_{thick} = 3.10$

The code (ASME V) requires working density $D_w$ between **1.5 and 3.5**. Check compliance.

**Step 1 — Working Density Selection.**
$D_w$ is defined as the density through the *thinnest portion of the part* of interest, which is **2.55** in this case (behind the shim, which simulates thinner material).

**Step 2 — Check against code limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_w = 2.55 \\
D_{min}^{code} = 1.5 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad 2.55 \geq 1.5 \; \checkmark \\
D_{max}^{code} = 3.5 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad 2.55 \leq 3.5 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Contrast check.**
$\Delta D = D_{thick} - D_{bg} = 3.10 - 1.20 = 1.90$.
ASME V requires $\Delta D \geq 1.50$ for the radiographic technique to be valid.
$1.90 \geq 1.50$ ✓

**Step 4 — Final verdict.**
The radiograph **passes** the density and contrast criteria. Proceed to interpretation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Defect Signatures on a Radiograph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **interpretation cheat sheet** — required for any KTU 14-mark question on "Describe the procedure for interpretation of radiographs."

| Defect | Appearance | Cause | Code Classification (ASME / IS 2595) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity (gas)** | Round, dark, scattered or clustered | Gas entrapment in weld pool | Acceptable if distributed; reject if clustered |
| **Slag inclusion** | Irregular, dark, elongated or rounded | Non-metallic solid entrapped | Reject if > 2 mm or > 4 in 300 mm |
| **Tungsten inclusion** | Bright (white), very small, sharp-edged | Tungsten electrode fragment in TIG | Reject any size |
| **Lack of fusion** | Dark, linear, between weld bead & base | Insufficient heat input | Reject — no size limit |
| **Lack of penetration** | Dark, linear, at root | Insufficient root gap/heat | Reject — no size limit |
| **Crack** | Dark, jagged, branching | Stress, hydrogen, restraint | Reject any crack |
| **Undercut** | Dark, narrow groove at toe | Excessive current | Reject if > 0.8 mm deep |
| **Wormhole** | Elongated dark tunnel | Gas escape path | Reject if > 2 mm |
| **Root concavity** | Dark, smooth depression | Excessive grinding | Reject if > 1.5 mm |
| **Porosity cluster** | Dense dark cluster | Severe gas/moisture | Reject if aggregate > code limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "DARK = LESS MATERIAL" Rule**
> On a radiograph, **dark areas = more radiation reached the film = less material or a void in the path of the beam.** This is the *single most important* rule in interpretation. Every defect identification begins with this rule.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — Geometric Unsharpness for IQI Visibility

A defect is *visible* on a radiograph only if its **image unsharpness** is less than the resolving power of the eye under viewing conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \dfrac{t \cdot (FFD - t)}{D_{object-film}} \quad \text{(general form)} \\
\text{For } FFD \gg t: \quad U_g &\approx \dfrac{t \cdot FFD}{D_{object-film}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $t$ = focal spot size of the source (mm)
- $FFD$ = focus-to-film distance (mm)
- $D_{object-film}$ = object-to-film distance (mm)

**Step-by-step numerical:**

A 20 mm weld in 25 mm plate is radiographed with Iridium-192 source (focal spot $t = 3$ mm) at $FFD = 400$ mm. The defect is at the root (bottom of the weld), so $D_{object-film} = 25$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_g &= \dfrac{3 \times 400}{25} \\
U_g &= \dfrac{1200}{25} \\
U_g &= 48 \text{ mm} = 0.048 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Acceptable limit** per ASME V: $U_g \leq 0.020 \times$ ($t_{specimen}$) $= 0.020 \times 25 = 0.50$ mm.

$0.048 \leq 0.50$ ✓ **Technique is acceptable.**

### 3.6 The IQI Placement Rule (Kerala Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IQI is placed on the **source side** (not film side) of the specimen, with the smallest wire pointing *away* from the weld centerline. This is to evaluate the **worst-case geometric unsharpness** that a real defect at the source-side surface would experience.

- **Source-side IQI** = detects unsharpness as seen by the *farthest* point from the film.
- **Film-side IQI** = detects unsharpness as seen by the *closest* point (best-case).
- Codes generally require **source-side placement** because real defects can occur at any depth.
