## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Figure 1 — The Five-Stage Film Processing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exposed Radiographic Film in Light-Tight Cassette] --> B[Stage 1: DEVELOPMENT in Hydroquinone Phenidone Bath at 20C]
    B --> C{Visual Check under Safelight: Image Appears as Dark Areas on Translucent Base}
    C --> D[Stage 2: STOP BATH in 1-3 Percent Acetic Acid for 30 sec]
    D --> E[Stage 3: FIXING in Sodium Thiosulfate Hypo for 2x Clearing Time]
    E --> F[Stage 4: WASHING in Running Water for 20 to 30 Minutes]
    F --> G[Stage 5: DRYING in Heated Cabinet at 40 to 50C for 15 to 30 Min]
    G --> H[Final Processed Radiograph Ready for Interpretation]
    B --> I[Quality Control: Temp and Time Strictly Monitored]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J{Reject if Fog Staining or Scratches Present}
    J --> H
    J --> K[Re-Process if Minor; Reshoot if Major]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Radiographic Interpretation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: View in Subdued Light Using Illuminated Viewer] --> P2[Step 2: Mask Surroundings with Black Cardboard Opaque Mask]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Scan Film at Low Power 2x to 5x for General Condition]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Identify IQI and Confirm Sensitivity 1 to 2 Percent]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Measure Density with Densitometer 2.0 to 4.0 Acceptable]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Systematic Scan Weld Zone Centerline and HAZ]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Identify Defects using Signature Table]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Compare against Acceptance Code ASME V or IS 2595]
    P8 --> P9{Accept or Reject}
    P9 --> P10[Record on RT Report with Interpreter Signature]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Optical Density vs Log Exposure H and D Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Exposure Region: TOE] -->|Fog Plus Background| B[Linear Working Region: Slope G Constant]
    B -->|Film Sensitive Region| C[Shoulder Plus Saturation Region: Max Density Plateau]
    A -.->|D Less Than 1.0 Unusable| X[Reject Image]
    B -.->|D Between 2.0 and 4.0 Valid| Y[Use for Interpretation]
    C -.->|D Greater Than 4.5 Overexposed| Z[Reject Image]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:** The H&D curve is the **transfer function** of the entire radiographic system. It maps the *physical* (radiation exposure $E$) to the *chemical-optical* (density $D$) to the *perceptual* (brightness seen by the interpreter). The **slope $G$ in the linear region** is the **system gain** — the higher the gain, the higher the contrast, the better the defect visibility.

### 4.4 Figure 4 — IQI Placement and Visibility Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Radiation Source Iridium 192 or X-ray Tube] -->|Beam Diverges| SPEC[Specimen Weld 20 mm Thick]
    SPEC -->|Attenuated Beam| FILM[Radiographic Film Below Specimen]
    IQISRC[Wire IQI Placed on Source Side] -.->|Attenuates Same Path| FILM
    IQIFLM[Optional Film Side IQI] -.-> FILM
    FILM -->|Latent Image| PROC[Film Processor]
    PROC -->|Visible Image| VIEW[Interpreter Identifies Smallest Visible Wire]
    VIEW --> CALC[Calculate Sensitivity Percent]
    CALC --> VERDICT{Acceptable Sensitivity Between 1 and 4 Percent}
    VERDICT --> YES[Technique Valid Proceed to Full Interpretation]
    VERDICT --> NO[Re-shoot with Better Technique]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term "latent image" in radiographic film processing. How is it converted into a visible image?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **latent image** is the invisible, sub-microscopic change produced in the silver halide (AgBr) crystals of the film emulsion when they are exposed to X-ray or gamma radiation. The exposure creates **sensitivity specks** of Ag₂S at crystal defect sites through the trapping of photoelectrons.

Conversion to visible image: In the **developer bath**, the reducing agents (Hydroquinone and Phenidone) donate electrons preferentially to the **sensitivity-specked crystals**, converting AgBr into metallic silver (Ag⁰). The metallic silver grains cluster to form the visible black image. Unexposed crystals remain unconverted and are later dissolved in the **fixer bath**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is an Image Quality Indicator (IQI)? Why is it placed on the source side and not the film side?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**, also called a *penetrameter*, is a small device of known geometry (wire, hole, or duplex type) placed on the specimen during exposure to verify the **sensitivity** of the radiographic technique — i.e., the ability to detect small defects.

It is placed on the **source side** because the **smallest, sharpest** radiographic image is produced for objects in contact with the film. A defect or IQI on the source side experiences the **maximum geometric unsharpness** — the worst-case condition. By verifying visibility on the source side, the radiographer guarantees that *any* defect within the specimen thickness will be at least as detectable. The smallest visible IQI feature is reported as a **percentage of the specimen thickness** (e.g., 2% sensitivity).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 14 marks]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **five stages of manual film processing** in industrial radiography, with the chemistry, temperature, and time requirements at each stage. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A **25 mm thick mild-steel plate** is radiographed using Ir-192. The IQI set has wires 0.10, 0.13, 0.16, 0.20, 0.25, 0.32, 0.40, 0.50, 0.64, 0.80 mm. The smallest visible wire is **0.32 mm**. Calculate the **percentage sensitivity** and state whether the technique is acceptable per ASME V. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*(a) The Five Stages of Manual Film Processing (7 marks):*

The film processing converts the invisible latent image on the exposed film into a permanent, viewable image through five sequential chemical/physical stages. The darkroom must be light-tight except for the **Wratten 6B (red) safelight** filtered for the film type.

**Stage 1 — Development (2 marks):**
- **Solution:** Hydroquinone + Phenidone in alkaline solution (Kodak D-19b or equivalent).
- **Temperature:** 20 °C ± 1 °C (strictly controlled by thermostatic water jacket).
- **Time:** 5 to 10 minutes (varies with film type and developer strength).
- **Action:** Reducing agents convert exposed AgBr crystals to metallic Ag⁰ (black). Unexposed crystals are *not yet* affected.
- **Agitation:** Continuous rocking of hangers to prevent uneven development.

**Stage 2 — Stop Bath (1 mark):**
- **Solution:** 1–3% acetic acid (or running water rinse).
- **Time:** 30 seconds (acid stop) or 1–2 min (water rinse).
- **Action:** Neutralizes the carried-over alkaline developer to halt development. Prevents developer carry-over into the fixer (which would neutralize the acid fixer).

**Stage 3 — Fixing (2 marks):**
- **Solution:** Sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃) — "Hypo" — in acidic buffer.
- **Time:** Twice the *clearing time* (typically 5–15 minutes).
- **Action:** Dissolves unexposed, undeveloped AgBr into a soluble complex (Na₃[Ag(S₂O₃)₂]) which is washed out. The image is now light-stable.
- **Agitation:** First 30 seconds continuously, then at intervals.

**Stage 4 — Washing (1 mark):**
- **Water:** Running water, flow rate to change tank 4× per hour.
- **Time:** 20–30 minutes (minimum).
- **Action:** Removes residual thiosulfate and silver-thiosulfate complexes to prevent **image fade** and **brown staining** over time.

**Stage 5 — Drying (1 mark):**
- **Cabinet:** Heated, dust-free, 40–50 °C, low humidity.
- **Time:** 15–30 minutes.
- **Action:** Removes moisture without damaging emulsion. Drying above 50 °C melts the emulsion.

*(b) Sensitivity Calculation (7 marks):*

**Step 1:** Identify the smallest visible IQI wire.
The smallest visible wire = **0.32 mm** (6th from thinnest).

**Step 2:** Apply the sensitivity formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S (\%) &= \dfrac{d_{IQI}}{t_{specimen}} \times 100 \\
S (\%) &= \dfrac{0.32}{25} \times 100 \\
S (\%) &= 1.28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Check acceptance per ASME V T-282.

For 25 mm plate, ASME V requires a sensitivity of **2% to 4%** for welds, with a target of 2% preferred.
Achieved sensitivity = **1.28%** (which is *better* than the minimum requirement).

**Step 4:** Verdict.
A sensitivity of 1.28% **exceeds** the ASME V requirement (i.e., smaller defects are visible than required). The technique is **acceptable**. The radiograph can proceed to interpretation.

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 marks — Internal Choice Alternative]**
> *(a)* Describe the **procedure for radiographic interpretation** of a weld, including the conditions of viewing, the use of densitometer, and the systematic approach. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A radiograph has the following **densitometer readings**: through clean parent metal = 1.5; through 5 mm shim = 2.4; through 5 mm thickener = 3.2. ASME V requires working density 2.0 to 4.0 and contrast ≥ 1.5. Check compliance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*(a) Procedure for Radiographic Interpretation (7 marks):*

1. **Viewing conditions (1.5 marks):** The film is examined on an **illuminated viewer** of sufficient intensity (≥ 30,000 cd/m² for densities up to 4.0). The room is darkened. Surrounding film area is masked with **opaque black cardboard** to reduce eye fatigue. A **magnifier (2× to 10×)** is used for fine detail.

2. **Densitometer measurement (1.5 marks):** The interpreter measures optical density $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ in three zones: parent metal, weld center, and any thick/thin transitions. Working density $D_w$ must be in the **2.0 to 4.0** range (ASME V). Useful density range $\Delta D$ must be $\geq 1.5$.

3. **IQI verification (1 mark):** The smallest visible IQI wire/hole is identified. Sensitivity is calculated and must meet the code requirement (1% to 2% for high-quality welds, 2% to 4% for general).

4. **Systematic scan (2 marks):** The interpreter scans the film in a **regular pattern** (e.g., serpentine) at low magnification for general condition, then at higher magnification for each region. The **weld zone**, **heat-affected zone (HAZ)**, and **parent metal** are examined separately.

5. **Defect identification & classification (1 mark):** Each observed indication is classified by **shape, location, distribution**, and compared to reference radiographs (ASTM E446, E186, E192 for castings; AWS D1.1 for welds). A pass/reject decision is recorded with **interpreter's signature and Level III certification number**.

*(b) Density & Contrast Check (7 marks):*

**Step 1 — Identify Working Density.**
$D_w$ is the density through the **thinnest section of the part of interest**, i.e., behind the shim.
$D_w = 2.4$

**Step 2 — Check $D_w$ against code limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{min}^{code} = 2.0 &\quad ; \quad D_w = 2.4 \geq 2.0 \; \checkmark \\
D_{max}^{code} = 4.0 &\quad ; \quad D_w = 2.4 \leq 4.0 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Useful Density Range (contrast).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= D_{thick} - D_{parent} \\
\Delta D &= 3.2 - 1.5 \\
\Delta D &= 1.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check contrast against code limit.**
$\Delta D_{min}^{code} = 1.5$.
$1.7 \geq 1.5$ ✓

**Step 5 — Verdict.**
The radiograph **passes** both density and contrast criteria. The technique is **valid** for interpretation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing "developer" when meaning "reducing agent"** — examiners look for the *chemical name* (Hydroquinone + Phenidone).
> 2. **Confusing source-side vs film-side IQI** — this is a 1-mark trick question. Memorize: *source-side is worst-case*; *film-side is best-case*.
> 3. **Forgetting the fog density** — optical density is $D_{total} = D_{image} + D_{fog}$. Some students report only the image density and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Quoting temperatures in Fahrenheit** — KTU expects **°C** (Centigrade) as per IS standards.
> 5. **Skipping the stop bath** — many students describe only 4 stages; the stop bath is mandatory and worth 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the fixer chemistry** — examiners expect either "sodium thiosulfate" or "hypo" or the chemical formula Na₂S₂O₃.
> 7. **Confusing "bright" with "dark" on a radiograph** — the cardinal rule: **dark = void, bright = dense inclusion** (e.g., tungsten). Reverse this and lose the whole defect identification.
> 8. **In sensitivity calculation:** the *IQI value must be the wire diameter, not the wire number*. Many students write "wire no. 6 = 0.32 mm" and get full credit, but some write "wire no. 6 = 0.40 mm" and lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before the Exam**

**A. Film Processing — The 5 Stages (Mnemonic: D-S-F-W-D)**

- ✅ **D**evelop: Hydroquinone + Phenidone, 20 °C, 5–10 min, alkaline.
- ✅ **S**top: Acetic acid 1–3%, 30 sec — **halts development**.
- ✅ **F**ix: Sodium thiosulfate, twice clearing time — **dissolves unexposed AgBr**.
- ✅ **W**ash: Running water, 20–30 min — **prevents image fade**.
- ✅ **D**ry: 40–50 °C cabinet, 15–30 min — **do not exceed 50 °C** (emulsion melts).

**B. Optical Density Facts**

- ✅ Density $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$.
- ✅ Working density $D_w$: 2.0 to 4.0 (ASME V).
- ✅ Contrast $\Delta D \geq 1.5$ (ASME V).
- ✅ $D_{fog} \leq 0.30$ for a fresh film (ASTM E1815).
- ✅ **DARK = VOID, BRIGHT = DENSE INCLUSION.**

**C. IQI Rules**

- ✅ Placed on **source side** (worst-case unsharpness).
- ✅ Smallest visible wire is reported.
- ✅ Sensitivity $S = d_{IQI} / t_{specimen} \times 100$ %.
- ✅ Target: 1% to 2% for high-quality, 2% to 4% for general.

**D. Defect Signatures (Dark = Void)**

- ✅ **Round, scattered** = Porosity.
- ✅ **Irregular, elongated** = Slag inclusion.
- ✅ **Linear at root** = Lack of penetration.
- ✅ **Linear at sidewall** = Lack of fusion.
- ✅ **Branching, jagged** = Crack (reject any size).
- ✅ **Bright, sharp** = Tungsten inclusion (TIG defect).

**E. Geometric Unsharpness**

- ✅ $U_g \approx t \cdot FFD / D_{obj-film}$.
- ✅ Acceptable: $U_g \leq 0.020 \times t_{specimen}$.

**F. The H&D Curve**

- ✅ Toe → Linear (working) → Shoulder.
- ✅ Slope $G = dD / d(\log E)$ — higher $G$ = higher contrast.
- ✅ Industrial films (Class II): $G \approx 4$ to 5.

**G. Codes & Standards to Memorize**

- ✅ **ASME Sec. V Art. 2** — acceptance criteria.
- ✅ **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1** — pressure vessel welds.
- ✅ **ASTM E94** — film processing guidelines.
- ✅ **ASTM E142** — IQI sensitivity verification.
- ✅ **IS 2595** — Indian standard for RT of welds.
- ✅ **ASTM E446 / E186 / E192** — casting reference radiographs.
- ✅ **AWS D1.1** — structural welding code.

**H. Safety Reminder**

- ✅ Darkroom must have **lock from inside** (radiation safety protocol).
- ✅ Safelight filter (Wratten 6B for blue-sensitive film; GBX-2 for green).
- ✅ Process control strips must be run **daily** (sensitivity, contrast, base+fog).
- ✅ **Re-fixing of old radiographs is FORBIDDEN** for archival records.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital Signal Processing (DSP) in NDT

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** is the mathematical manipulation of signals that have been converted from the analog (continuous-time, continuous-amplitude) domain into a discrete (sampled and quantized) numerical form, so that a digital computer or microprocessor can analyse, filter, transform, and extract diagnostic information from the data.

In **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, the raw signal coming out of a transducer (ultrasonic probe, eddy current coil, acoustic emission sensor, etc.) is essentially an **analog voltage versus time waveform**. To extract information such as flaw size, depth, type, and orientation, this waveform is digitised and processed using well-defined mathematical algorithms.

The three foundational pillars of any DSP system in NDT are:

| Pillar | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sampling** | Converting continuous time $t$ into discrete instants $nT$ |
| **Quantization** | Converting continuous amplitude $x(t)$ into discrete levels $x[n]$ |
| **Reconstruction/Analysis** | Recovering defect features from the digitised array |

## 1.2 Digital Image Processing (DIP) in NDT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Digital Image Processing (DIP)** is the use of computer algorithms to manipulate a two-dimensional digital image (a finite matrix of picture elements called *pixels*), in order to enhance visual information, suppress noise, detect edges, segment features, and quantitatively interpret defects seen in radiographs, thermographs, or ultrasonic C-scans.

A digital NDT image is mathematically represented as a 2-D function $f(x, y)$, where $x$ and $y$ are spatial coordinates and $f$ is the intensity (brightness, colour, or grayscale level) at that coordinate. After digitisation:

$$f(x, y) \xrightarrow{\text{sampling + quantization}} I[r, c], \quad r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1; \quad c = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

where $I$ is the $M \times N$ pixel matrix.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Stethoscope"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy (real-world intuitive understanding):**
> Think of an NDT inspector using a stethoscope on a machine. The *raw sound* he hears is the **analog signal** — full of noise, breathing, murmurs. If he wants to *quantify* the heart's condition, he cannot rely on his ear alone. Instead, he plugs the stethoscope into a **digital recorder** that:
> 1. *Samples* the sound at equal time intervals (sampling),
> 2. *Measures* the loudness at each instant as a number (quantization),
> 3. Runs the numbers through a **filter** that removes the breathing noise and isolates the heart murmur (DSP).
> 4. Displays the cleaned waveform on a screen, where weak murmurs are brightened, edges sharpened, and any abnormal "blip" highlighted as a coloured dot (DIP).
>
> That is exactly what happens inside an **Ultrasonic Flaw Detector** or a **Computed Radiography (CR) workstation** in an NDT lab.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

The performance and quality of an NDT digital system is described by the following metrics (all are board-favourite terms):

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** — expressed in **Hz** or **MHz**, the rate at which the analog signal is measured.
- **Sampling Period ($T_s = 1/f_s$)** — expressed in **seconds (s)**.
- **Bit Depth ($b$)** — the number of bits per sample, usually **8-bit (256 levels)** or **16-bit (65 536 levels)**.
- **Dynamic Range** — typically **$\ge 100$ dB** for modern ultrasonic instruments.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — usually **30 – 60 dB** in industrial NDT.
- **Spatial Resolution** — measured in **line pairs per millimetre (lp/mm)** for radiography or **mm/pixel** for digital images.
- **Nyquist Frequency ($f_N = f_s/2$)** — the highest frequency that can be faithfully reconstructed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Principle of Digital Signal Processing in NDT

The complete DSP chain used in any modern NDT instrument follows a five-stage architecture:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — A physical transducer converts a non-electrical quantity (acoustic echo, magnetic flux leakage, infrared emission, X-ray attenuation) into a **voltage signal** $v(t)$.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filtration** — A **low-pass analog filter** removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ *before* sampling, to satisfy the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**.
3. **Sampling & Quantization (A/D Conversion)** — The conditioned signal is converted to a digital sequence $x[n] = x(nT_s)$.
4. **Digital Processing** — The sequence is operated upon by algorithms such as **digital filtering, FFT, correlation, convolution, Hilbert transform, wavelet transform**, etc.
5. **Display / Decision** — The processed result is plotted as an A-scan, B-scan, C-scan, or used to drive an automatic alarm/accept-reject decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:**
> Once aliasing occurs in Step 3, no amount of digital processing in Step 4 can recover the lost information — this is why the anti-aliasing filter in Step 2 is non-negotiable in any NDT instrument.

## 2.2 The Generic Principle of Digital Image Processing in NDT

A digital image in NDT (e.g., a radiograph of a weld) is processed through these stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Using CR plates, flat-panel detectors, CCD cameras, or ultrasonic C-scan mapping.
2. **Image Enhancement** — Contrast stretching, histogram equalisation, brightness/gamma correction, false-colour mapping.
3. **Image Restoration** — Wiener filtering, deblurring, geometric distortion correction.
4. **Image Segmentation** — Thresholding, edge detection (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt), region growing.
5. **Feature Extraction & Classification** — Hough transform, texture analysis, AI-based CNN classifiers for defect type.
6. **Interpretation / Reporting** — Quantitative defect sizing, automated reporting.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **board-defining formulas** for this topic. Memorise them in symbol-meaning form; the derivations are given in SECTION 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | $T_s$ = sampling period | **Hz** |
| 2 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | **Hz** |
| 3 | Quantization Step Size | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^b}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale range, $b$ = bit depth | **V** |
| 4 | Quantization Noise (RMS) | $v_q = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}$ | RMS quantization error | **V** |
| 5 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR = 6.02\,b + 1.76$ | ratio in **dB** | **dB** |
| 6 | Dynamic Range of ADC | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^b)$ | = $6.02\,b$ **dB** | **dB** |
| 7 | Moving-Average Filter Output | $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$ | $N$ = window length | dimensionless |
| 8 | Discrete-Time Convolution | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,h[n-k]$ | $h$ = filter impulse response | dimensionless |
| 9 | Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n/N}$ | $k$ = frequency index | complex |
| 10 | Inverse DFT (IDFT) | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\, e^{+j 2\pi k n/N}$ | reconstructs time signal | complex |
| 11 | Image Mean Intensity | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{r=0}^{M-1}\sum_{c=0}^{N-1} I[r,c]$ | average grey level | **a.u.** |
| 12 | Image Variance (texture) | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum (I[r,c] - \bar{f})^2$ | indicates defect contrast | **a.u.** |
| 13 | Histogram Equalisation Mapping | $s_k = (L-1)\sum_{j=0}^{k} \dfrac{n_j}{N}$ | $L$ = grey levels, $n_j$ = count | grey level |
| 14 | Gaussian Smoothing Kernel | $G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2\sigma^2}$ | $\sigma$ = standard deviation | dimensionless |
| 15 | SNR of an NDT Image | $SNR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)$ | $\mu$, $\sigma$ over ROI | **dB** |
| 16 | Wavelength of Ultrasound | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | $v$ = velocity, $f$ = frequency | **mm** |
| 17 | Time-of-Flight of Flaw Echo | $t = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ | $d$ = flaw depth, $v$ = sound velocity | **s** |
| 18 | Pulse Repetition Frequency Limit | $PRF \le \dfrac{v}{2D_{max}}$ | $D_{max}$ = maximum sound path | **Hz** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** The A-scan is a 1-D time-domain DSP signal. Band-pass filtering, Hilbert transform, and Split-Spectrum Processing (SSP) extract small flaw echoes from grain noise.
- **Eddy Current Testing (ECT):** Impedance plane (lift-off/tilt curves) is generated by DSP-based phase-sensitive detection.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT):** The film/digital plate becomes a 2-D image; DIP reveals sub-millimetre cracks, porosity, and lack of fusion in welds.
- **Infrared Thermography:** Sequential thermal frames are DIP-processed to find delaminations in composites.
- **Acoustic Emission (AE):** DSP detects and locates the source of transient elastic waves from growing cracks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (the foundation of every NDT digitiser)

**Statement (to be proved):** *A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.*

### Step 1 — Define the Ideal Impulse Sampler
The sampled signal is the product of $x(t)$ with an impulse train $\delta_{T_s}(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

This means each sample is "captured" as a weighted impulse.

### Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform
Using the convolution theorem, multiplication in the time domain becomes convolution in the frequency domain. The Fourier transform of the impulse train is another impulse train in frequency:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This equation tells us the spectrum of the sampled signal is the **periodic repetition** of the original spectrum $X(f)$ at intervals of $f_s$.

### Step 3 — Examine the Spectrum
Each replica of $X(f)$ is centred at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \ldots$

If the original bandwidth is $B = f_{max}$, each replica occupies the band $[k f_s - B, \, k f_s + B]$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Non-Overlapping Condition
For the replicas **not to overlap (no aliasing)**, the right edge of the $k = 0$ replica ($+B$) must lie to the left of the left edge of the $k = 1$ replica ($f_s - B$):

$$B < f_s - B$$

Rearranging:

$$2B < f_s \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_s > 2 f_{max}$$

In strict mathematical form (the **Nyquist rate** is the equality case):

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$

### Step 5 — Reconstruction
When the condition is satisfied, an **ideal low-pass filter** with cut-off $f_c = f_s/2$ recovers $X(f)$, and the inverse Fourier transform reconstructs $x(t)$ exactly.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Board Exam:** "**Sample at least twice as fast as the fastest wiggle.**" In ultrasonic NDT, a 5 MHz probe is normally sampled at **$\ge$ 50 MHz**, leaving headroom for the anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Given:** An ideal uniform mid-tread quantiser of $b$ bits over the full-scale range $V_{FS}$.

### Step 1 — Step Size
The voltage between two adjacent quantization levels is:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^b}$$

### Step 2 — Assumed Quantization Error
The error $e = x_{analog} - x_{quantised}$ is uniformly distributed in $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$ with probability density $1/Q$.

### Step 3 — Mean-Square (Power) of the Error

$$E[e^2] = \int_{-Q/2}^{+Q/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{Q}\, de = \frac{1}{Q}\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-Q/2}^{+Q/2} = \frac{1}{Q}\left(\frac{Q^3}{24} + \frac{Q^3}{24}\right) = \frac{Q^2}{12}$$

### Step 4 — RMS Quantization Noise

$$v_{q,rms} = \sqrt{E[e^2]} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}$$

### Step 5 — Signal Power for a Full-Scale Sine Wave
For a sinusoid $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$ with peak $A = V_{FS}/2$, the average power into a 1 Ω load is:

$$P_{sig} = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

### Step 6 — Form the Ratio

$$SQNR = \frac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{Q^2/12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{Q^2} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2b}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^{b}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to dB

$$SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^{b}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 b \log_{10}(4)$$

Using $\log_{10}(4) = 0.6021$ and $10 \times 0.6021 = 6.021$:

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\,b + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}$$

This is the canonical engineering result — **every additional bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range**, a board-favourite statement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) from the Continuous Fourier Transform

### Step 1 — Discretise the Continuous Transform
The Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) of $x(t)$ is:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j 2\pi f t}\,dt$$

We restrict observation to $N$ samples at $t = n T_s$ (with $T_s = 1/f_s$):

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,e^{-j 2\pi f (n/f_s)}\cdot \frac{1}{f_s} \cdot f_s$$

For a sampled, periodic signal of period $N/f_s$, the frequency samples are spaced by $\Delta f = f_s/N$, so $f = k \cdot f_s/N$. Absorbing constants and limits:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

### Step 2 — Express in Sine and Cosine Form (used in boards)

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi k n}{N}\right) \;-\; j\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k n}{N}\right)$$

The magnitude $\vert X[k] \vert$ gives the **amplitude spectrum** and the angle $\angle X[k]$ gives the **phase spectrum** — both central to ultrasonic and acoustic-emission NDT analysis.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Moving-Average Filter (Smoothing of Noisy A-Scan)

A moving-average of length $N$ is a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter with all coefficients $1/N$:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k] = \frac{1}{N}\bigl(x[n] + x[n-1] + \cdots + x[n-N+1]\bigr)$$

### Frequency Response
The DTFT (Discrete-Time Fourier Transform) of the filter is:

$$H(\Omega) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\Omega k} = \frac{1}{N}\cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\Omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\Omega}}$$

Using Euler's identity, this simplifies to:

$$H(\Omega) = \frac{1}{N}\cdot \frac{\sin(\Omega N/2)}{\sin(\Omega/2)}\cdot e^{-j\Omega(N-1)/2}$$

- At $\Omega = 0$: $H(0) = 1$ (DC pass).
- At $\Omega = 2\pi k/N$, $k \ne 0$: $H = 0$ (zeros at multiples of $f_s/N$).

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why moving-average filters are excellent for suppressing **periodic interference** (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup in eddy-current signals) — but they smear sharp flaw echoes. A **band-pass or matched filter** is preferred for defect detection.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Time-of-Flight for Ultrasonic Flaw Detection (Cross-Method)

The pulse travels from the probe to the flaw and back:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = depth of the flaw from the search unit,
- $v$ = longitudinal wave velocity in the test material (for steel $v \approx 5920$ m/s),
- $t$ = measured time between the initial pulse and the flaw echo.

### Worked Numerical Example
A 5 MHz longitudinal probe inspects a steel plate. The flaw echo appears $t = 6.0 \,\mu s$ after the initial pulse. Find the flaw depth.

$$d = \frac{5920 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.03552}{2} = 0.01776 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 17.76 \text{ mm}}$$

This calculation is the bread-and-butter of every UT board problem.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Map for NDT Modalities

| NDT Method | Raw Signal Type | Key DSP Operation | Key DIP Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | 1-D RF time trace (A-scan) | Band-pass filter, Hilbert transform, FFT, Deconvolution | C-scan / B-scan image segmentation |
| Eddy Current Testing (ECT) | 1-D complex impedance vs. time | Phase-sensitive detection, Lissajous (impedance-plane) | Surface-scan image mapping |
| Radiographic Testing (RT) | 2-D X-ray transmission image | Median filter, Wiener filter | Contrast stretching, edge detection, defect sizing |
| Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) | Visual / photographic | Adaptive thresholding | Morphological operations (open/close) |
| Infrared Thermography (IRT) | 2-D time-sequence thermal frames | FFT of thermal decay curve | Thermal contrast, thermal diffusivity mapping |
| Acoustic Emission (AE) | 1-D high-frequency bursts | Threshold detection, hit counting, FFT, wavelets | Source triangulation plot (2-D) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic DSP Chain for an NDT Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specimen Under Test] --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Analog Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Digital Memory Buffer]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    G --> H[Display A-Scan B-Scan C-Scan]
    G --> I[Accept Reject Decision]
    H --> J[Operator / Inspector]
    I --> J
    G --> K[Digital Image Processor DIP Module]
    K --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **specimen** (welded joint, casting, composite) is the physical object.
- The **transducer** converts the NDT phenomenon (ultrasound, eddy current, X-ray) into voltage.
- The **analog conditioner** amplifies and shapes the signal.
- The **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** must be placed **before** the ADC — this is the most-frequently-violated rule.
- The **ADC** performs sampling and quantization.
- The **DSP block** runs all the algorithms (FFT, filter, Hilbert).
- The **display** shows A/B/C-scans to the inspector.
- The **DIP block** is engaged when 2-D images need post-processing.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic DIP Chain for NDT Image Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NDT Image Acquisition CR Plate CCD FPD] --> B[Image Digitization MxN Pixel Matrix]
    B --> C[Pre-Processing Noise Removal]
    C --> D[Image Enhancement Contrast Sharpening]
    D --> E[Image Restoration Geometric Correction]
    E --> F[Image Segmentation Threshold Edge Region]
    F --> G[Feature Extraction Defect Geometry Texture]
    G --> H[Pattern Recognition AI Classifier]
    H --> I[Defect Report Accept Reject Sizing]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The DIP pipeline is **strictly sequential** in classical processing; in modern AI-based systems, the segmentation-and-classification stages are merged inside a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**.
- **Pre-processing** removes detector noise; **enhancement** makes flaws visually obvious; **restoration** corrects systematic distortions; **segmentation** isolates the defect pixels; **feature extraction** measures size, shape, orientation; **classification** decides if it is a crack, pore, slag, or lack of fusion.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — FFT-Based Ultrasonic Flaw Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Specimen With Flaw] --> PR[Ultrasonic Probe 5 MHz]
    PR --> TX[Pulser Receiver]
    TX --> AMP[Variable Gain Amplifier]
    AMP --> LPF[Anti-Aliasing Filter fc 20 MHz]
    LPF --> ADC[ADC 100 MSPS 12 bit]
    ADC --> MEM[Memory N 1024 Samples]
    MEM --> FFT[FFT Processor 1024 point]
    FFT --> MAG[Magnitude Spectrum]
    MAG --> PEAK[Peak Detector 5 MHz Center]
    PEAK --> DSP[Defect Sizing Algorithm]
    DSP --> DSPOUT[Depth Size Type Report]
    MEM --> WIN[Windowed Time Signal]
    WIN --> DSP
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The 5 MHz probe's signal is sampled at **100 MSPS** (well above the **40 MHz Nyquist rate**).
- The 1024-point FFT converts the time signal into a frequency spectrum where the **5 MHz peak** identifies the probe, while **sidebands or harmonic shifts** can indicate non-linear defects (e.g., loose cracks, delaminations).
- The **defect sizing algorithm** then uses time-of-flight (Section 3.5) to compute depth.

## 4.4 Schematic — Image Processing Stages for a Radiograph of a Weld

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Radiograph Weld Joint] --> R2[Histogram Analysis]
    R2 --> R3[Histogram Equalisation]
    R3 --> R4[Median Filter 3x3 Kernel]
    R4 --> R5[Sobel Edge Detection]
    R5 --> R6[Thresholding Otsu Method]
    R6 --> R7[Connected Component Labelling]
    R7 --> R8[Defect Classification Crack Pore Slag]
    R8 --> R9[Quantitative Defect Sizing]
    R9 --> R10[Accept Reject Report per Code]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- A radiograph of a weld is intrinsically low-contrast. **Histogram equalisation** (Formula 13 of SECTION 2) spreads the grey levels to use the full 0–255 range.
- **Median filtering** removes impulse noise from the film grain or detector without blurring edges.
- **Sobel edge detection** highlights the boundary of the weld bead and any internal defects.
- **Otsu thresholding** automatically finds the optimal grey-level cut-off separating defect from background.
- **Connected-component labelling** then identifies each discrete defect as an isolated blob, enabling measurement of area, perimeter, circularity, and aspect ratio.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Define Digital Signal Processing. List the major steps involved in the digital processing of an ultrasonic A-scan signal.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** is the numerical manipulation of signals that have been digitised (sampled in time and quantised in amplitude) using mathematical algorithms implemented on a computer or dedicated processor.

**Major steps in processing an ultrasonic A-scan:**

1. **Signal acquisition** by the ultrasonic transducer (pulser-receiver).
2. **Analog conditioning** — amplification and band-pass filtering.
3. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filtering** to enforce the Nyquist criterion.
4. **A/D conversion** — sampling at $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ and quantising to $b$ bits.
5. **Digital processing** — fast Fourier transform, digital filtering, Hilbert transform, time-gating, peak detection.
6. **Display and interpretation** — plotting the cleaned A-scan, B-scan or C-scan, and computing defect depth from $d = vt/2$.

**(3 marks — full marks for definition + ordered list.)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Digital Image Processing? Explain histogram equalisation with its significance in NDT radiography.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Digital Image Processing (DIP)** is the use of computer algorithms to enhance, restore, segment, and interpret 2-D digital images represented as $M \times N$ matrices of pixels.

**Histogram equalisation** redistributes the grey-level intensities of an image so that the output histogram is approximately uniform. The mapping is given by:

$$s_k = (L-1)\sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1$$

where $L$ is the number of grey levels, $n_j$ is the pixel count at level $j$, and $N$ is the total number of pixels.

**Significance in NDT radiography:** Weld radiographs often have very narrow grey-level distributions clustered in the dark region. Histogram equalisation *spreads* the contrast across the full 0–255 range, making fine defects such as under-bead cracks and porosity visible to the inspector.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for application.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls on this topic:**
> - **Never confuse** the Nyquist rate ($f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$) with the Nyquist *frequency* ($f_N = f_s/2$). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - **Always specify units** (Hz, dB, m/s) — marks are deducted for unitless final answers.
> - When asked for *procedure*, students often jump straight to FFT and **forget the anti-aliasing filter and A/D conversion**, which is the first procedure step and carries weight.
> - In histogram-equalisation derivations, **omit the $(L-1)$ factor** and lose one mark.
> - In UT depth problems, remember the **factor of 2** in $d = vt/2$ — the largest single-cause of mark loss in this module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [Option A]

#### (a) Explain the principle of Digital Signal Processing. With a neat block diagram, describe the various steps involved in digitising and processing an NDT signal. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** DSP operates on discretised signals. An analog NDT signal $x(t)$ is sampled at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$ to produce $x[n] = x(nT_s)$, and the continuous amplitude is quantised into $2^b$ discrete levels where $b$ is the bit depth. Mathematical operations such as addition, multiplication, convolution and the discrete Fourier transform are then applied to extract the desired physical information (defect location, size, type) from the digitised data.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Use Figure 4.1 from SECTION 4 — Specimen → Probe → Signal Conditioner → Anti-Aliasing Filter → ADC → Digital Memory → DSP → Display.)

**Procedure (2 marks):** The complete procedure is:

1. **Acquisition:** Transducer converts the NDT phenomenon to voltage.
2. **Conditioning:** Amplification and gain control.
3. **Anti-aliasing filtering:** Low-pass at $f_c = f_s/2$.
4. **Sampling:** At $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist).
5. **Quantisation:** To $b$ bits, step size $Q = V_{FS}/2^b$.
6. **Digital processing:** Filtering, FFT, correlation, Hilbert.
7. **Display & decision:** A/B/C-scan, accept/reject.

#### (b) An ultrasonic pulse-echo test is conducted on a steel plate with longitudinal wave velocity $v = 5920$ m/s. The transmitted frequency is 4 MHz. If the flaw echo is received $5 \,\mu s$ after the initial pulse, determine: (i) the depth of the flaw, (ii) the wavelength of ultrasound in steel, and (iii) the minimum sampling frequency required by the Nyquist criterion. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flaw depth (3 marks):**
Using the time-of-flight formula for pulse-echo:

$$d = \frac{v t}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.0296}{2} = 0.0148 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d = 14.8 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Wavelength (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{5920}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 1.48 \text{ mm}}$$

**(iii) Minimum sampling frequency (2 marks):**
The highest signal frequency component is 4 MHz. By Nyquist:

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 4 \text{ MHz} = 8 \text{ MHz}$$

In practice, a safety factor of 5–10× is used, so the ADC would be operated at **40–80 MHz**.

$$\boxed{f_{s,min} = 8 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Option B]

#### (a) With a block diagram, explain the principle and procedure of Digital Image Processing as applied to a radiographic image of a weld. List the typical operations in each stage. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** A radiographic image is a 2-D function $f(x, y)$ of X-ray intensity. Digitisation produces an $M \times N$ matrix $I[r, c]$. DIP applies mathematical operations on this matrix to enhance, restore, segment, and interpret the image quantitatively, allowing automated defect detection and sizing.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer Figure 4.2 of SECTION 4 — Acquisition → Digitisation → Pre-processing → Enhancement → Restoration → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Report.)

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. **Acquisition** — by computed radiography (CR) plate or flat-panel detector (FPD).
2. **Digitisation** — $M \times N$ pixel matrix with $L$ grey levels.
3. **Pre-processing** — median filtering, noise removal.
4. **Enhancement** — histogram equalisation, contrast stretching, sharpening.
5. **Restoration** — Wiener filtering, geometric un-distortion.
6. **Segmentation** — thresholding, edge detection (Sobel/Canny), region growing.
7. **Feature extraction & classification** — defect area, perimeter, aspect ratio, CNN-based classification.
8. **Reporting** — accept/reject per code (ASME, IS, EN).

#### (b) Derive the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a uniform mid-tread quantiser of $b$ bits. For a 12-bit ADC used in an ultrasonic flaw detector, calculate the theoretical SQNR and dynamic range. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** (Use the seven-step derivation in Section 3.2.)

Step 1 — Step size: $Q = V_{FS}/2^b$
Step 2 — Error uniform in $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$
Step 3 — Mean-square error: $E[e^2] = Q^2/12$
Step 4 — RMS noise: $v_q = Q/\sqrt{12}$
Step 5 — Signal power (full-scale sine): $P_{sig} = V_{FS}^2/8$
Step 6 — Ratio: $SQNR = (3/2) \cdot 4^{b}$
Step 7 — In dB: $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\,b + 1.76$

**Calculation for $b = 12$ (2 marks):**

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

Dynamic range:

$$DR = 6.02 \times 12 = 72.24 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{SQNR \approx 74 \text{ dB}, \quad DR \approx 72.24 \text{ dB}}$$

**Engineering comment:** A 12-bit ultrasonic digitiser offers roughly 72 dB of usable dynamic range, sufficient to detect flaw echoes as small as 1/4000 of the reference echo amplitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for the Last-Night Revision:**

- ✅ **DSP** = numerical processing of digitised (sampled + quantised) signals.
- ✅ **DIP** = numerical processing of 2-D pixel matrices representing images.
- ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$; **Nyquist frequency** $= f_s/2$ — *not the same thing*.
- ✅ **Quantisation step:** $Q = V_{FS}/2^b$; **RMS noise** $= Q/\sqrt{12}$.
- ✅ **Canonical formula:** $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02\,b + 1.76$ dB; **Dynamic Range** $\approx 6.02\,b$ dB.
- ✅ **Every extra bit** = $\approx +6$ dB of dynamic range.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** must come *before* the ADC, never after.
- ✅ **DFT pair:** $X[k] = \sum x[n]e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ and $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum X[k]e^{+j2\pi kn/N}$.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic depth:** $d = vt/2$ (factor of 2 is mandatory for pulse-echo).
- ✅ **Wavelength:** $\lambda = v/f$.
- ✅ **PRF limit:** $PRF \le v/(2 D_{max})$ to avoid range ambiguity.
- ✅ **Moving-average filter** suppresses periodic noise but smears sharp flaw echoes.
- ✅ **Histogram equalisation:** $s_k = (L-1)\sum n_j/N$ — formula must include the $(L-1)$ factor.
- ✅ **DIP pipeline:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Enhancement → Restoration → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Report.
- ✅ **Standard NDT image operations:** Median filter (noise), Sobel/Canny (edge), Otsu (threshold), Wiener (deblur), Gaussian (smooth).
- ✅ **Modern AI extension:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) merge segmentation and classification stages of the DIP pipeline.
- ✅ **Practical NDT dynamic range:** typically 30–60 dB SNR; 100 dB for high-end ultrasonic instruments.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, OEMET724 Non-Destructive Testing.**
> These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4), and the typical 3-mark / 14-mark question pattern of KTU End-Semester Examinations.
