topics.title: Digital Image Correlation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Image Correlation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Image Correlation (DIC)** is a non-contact, full-field, optical metrology technique used in experimental mechanics and non-destructive testing to measure the in-plane (2D DIC) or three-dimensional (3D/Stereo DIC) displacement field, deformation gradients, and strain distribution on the surface of a specimen subjected to mechanical, thermal, or environmental loading. The method works by tracking the motion of a random, high-contrast speckle pattern applied to the specimen surface between a reference (undeformed) image and a series of deformed images captured at progressive load states.

Mathematically, DIC solves an inverse problem — given two digital grayscale intensity images, $I_{ref}(x,y)$ and $I_{def}(x',y')$, it determines the geometric transformation $\boldsymbol{\varphi}(x,y)$ that best maps the gray-level intensity of a sub-region (subset) from the reference image to the corresponding region in the deformed image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Digital Image Correlation is classified under **Optical and Image-Based NDT Methods**. It is treated as a *quantitative* full-field technique, in contrast to qualitative methods like visual testing or radiography interpretation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have printed a sheet of graph paper with a dense, random scatter of black dots on it (a "speckle pattern") and glued it onto a rubber band. As you stretch the rubber band:

1. **Before stretching**, you take a photograph of the dots — this is your *reference image*.
2. **After stretching**, you take another photograph — this is your *deformed image*.
3. By tracking *where each tiny patch of dots moved* between the two pictures, you can mathematically reconstruct **how much every point on the rubber band displaced, and how much it stretched**.

DIC performs exactly this task, but with sub-pixel accuracy, using computer vision algorithms instead of human eyes. The unique arrangement of dots in each small patch acts like a **"fingerprint"**, allowing the algorithm to relocate the same patch even after deformation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are the standard outputs of any DIC analysis (KTU Board examiners expect familiarity with these terms):

- **Pixel Resolution**: typically **$\sim$ 1 – 10 µm/pixel** (depends on optics and sensor).
- **Strain Resolution**: typically **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ (i.e., 10 to 10,000 microstrain)**.
- **Displacement Resolution**: typically **$\sim$ 0.01 – 0.1 pixels** (sub-pixel achieved via interpolation).
- **Field of View (FOV)**: from a few mm² to several m², controlled by lens magnification and sensor size.
- **Subset (Window) Size**: typically **15 × 15 to 51 × 51 pixels**; controls spatial resolution vs. noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is DIC classified as NDT?** Because the method does not inflict damage, does not require contact probes, and the speckle pattern can be applied non-invasively (paint, ink, toner powder, or natural texture). It is widely accepted by **ASTM E2658** and is integral to standards like **ISO 9513** for metal材料的材料 testing and **ASTM E2580** for residual stress evaluation.

### 1.4 Types of DIC Systems

- **2D DIC (Single Camera)**: Measures in-plane displacement components $u(x,y)$ and $v(x,y)$; requires the specimen surface to be flat and parallel to the sensor.
- **3D DIC / Stereo DIC (Twin Cameras)**: Measures all three displacement components $u$, $v$, and $w$ (out-of-plane), suitable for curved or arbitrarily deforming surfaces.
- **Digital Volume Correlation (DVC)**: 3D-in-3D extension using CT scans; used for internal strain mapping in foams, composites, and biological tissue.
- **High-Speed / Real-Time DIC**: Employs high-frame-rate cameras for dynamic events (impact, crash, vibration).
- **SEM-DIC**: Correlates images captured inside a Scanning Electron Microscope for micro/nano-scale strain mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The DIC pipeline can be broken down into **six ordered stages**. Each stage has a clear engineering rationale:

1. **Surface Preparation (Speckle Patterning)**
   - A random, isotropic, high-contrast pattern is deposited on the specimen.
   - Ideal pattern: isotropic, non-repetitive, fully covering the FOV, with feature size $\approx$ **3 – 5 pixels**.
   - **Why?** A unique "fingerprint" pattern at every subset guarantees unambiguous matching.

2. **Image Acquisition**
   - Capture $I_{ref}(x,y)$ at the undeformed (reference) state.
   - Apply incremental loading and capture $I_{def}^{(k)}(x,y)$ at each load step $k$.

3. **Subset Definition and Reference Point Grid**
   - The reference image is divided into small square regions called **subsets** (also called *windows* or *facets*), typically of size $(2N+1) \times (2N+1)$ pixels.
   - Each subset is centered on a *point of interest* (POI) or *calculation point*.

4. **Correlation Matching (The Core Algorithm)**
   - For every subset in the reference image, the algorithm searches the deformed image for the most mathematically similar sub-region.
   - "Most similar" is quantified by a **correlation criterion** (ZNCC, SSD, etc. — see formula sheet below).
   - A sub-pixel interpolation scheme (e.g., bilinear, bicubic, B-spline, or optical flow) refines the displacement to sub-pixel accuracy.

5. **Displacement Field Construction**
   - The integer + sub-pixel shift $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ at every POI gives the discrete displacement field:
     $$u(x,y) = x' - x, \quad v(x,y) = y' - y$$
   - For 3D DIC, a stereo-triangulation step reconstructs $(u, v, w)$.

6. **Strain Field Computation**
   - Strains are derived from the displacement gradient tensor using a *smoothing/differentiation* operation (finite differences, least-squares polynomial fit, or Savitzky–Golay filter) to suppress noise.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you are expected to recognize and apply for Module 4 — Digital Image Correlation, as per KTU 2024 ESE marking schemes.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Pixel coordinates (deformed) | $x' = x + u(x,y) + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\Delta x + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\Delta y$ | First-order **shape function** for subset deformation |
| 2 | Pixel coordinates (deformed) | $y' = y + v(x,y) + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\Delta x + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\Delta y$ | Same — vertical component |
| 3 | Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) | $C_{SSD} = \displaystyle\sum_{(i,j) \in S} \bigl[I_{ref}(x_i,y_j) - I_{def}(x'_i,y'_j)\bigr]^2$ | Cost function; minimize |
| 4 | Cross-Correlation (CC) | $C_{CC} = \displaystyle\sum_{(i,j) \in S} I_{ref}(x_i,y_j)\cdot I_{def}(x'_i,y'_j)$ | Maximize for similarity |
| 5 | Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) | $C_{ZNCC} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{S}\bigl(I_{ref}-\bar{I}_{ref}\bigr)\bigl(I_{def}-\bar{I}_{def}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{S}\bigl(I_{ref}-\bar{I}_{ref}\bigr)^2 \cdot \sum_{S}\bigl(I_{def}-\bar{I}_{def}\bigr)^2}}$ | Range: $[-1,+1]$; **most used** in KTU textbooks |
| 6 | Normalized SSD (NSSD) | $C_{NSSD} = \displaystyle\sum_{S}\dfrac{\bigl[I_{ref}(x_i,y_j) - I_{def}(x'_i,y'_j)\bigr]^2}{I_{ref}(x_i,y_j)\cdot I_{def}(x'_i,y'_j)}$ | Robust to lighting changes |
| 7 | Subset affine shape function matrix | $\boldsymbol{\varphi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+u_x & u_y \\ v_x & 1+v_y \end{bmatrix}$ | Encodes translation + 1st-order deformation |
| 8 | In-plane normal strain (x-dir) | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} \approx \dfrac{u(x+\Delta x,y) - u(x-\Delta x,y)}{2\Delta x}$ | Displacement-gradient relation |
| 9 | In-plane normal strain (y-dir) | $\varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} \approx \dfrac{v(x,y+\Delta y) - v(x,y-\Delta y)}{2\Delta y}$ | Displacement-gradient relation |
| 10 | In-plane shear strain | $\varepsilon_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$ | Symmetric tensor component |
| 11 | Principal strains | $\varepsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{xx}+\varepsilon_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_{xx}-\varepsilon_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \varepsilon_{xy}^2}$ | Mohr's-circle eigenvalues |
| 12 | Von Mises equivalent strain (plane) | $\varepsilon_{vm} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{xx}^2 + \varepsilon_{yy}^2 - \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + 3\varepsilon_{xy}^2}$ | 2D form (used in DIC for monotonic loading) |
| 13 | Digital Image Resolution (D) | $D = \dfrac{L_{sensor}}{M \cdot N_{pixel}}$ | $L_{sensor}$=sensor size, $M$=magnification, $N_{pixel}$=pixel count |
| 14 | Strain Resolution (theoretical) | $\Delta\varepsilon \approx \dfrac{\sigma_{disp}}{L_{subset}}$ | $\sigma_{disp}$=displacement noise std; $L_{subset}$=gauge length |
| 15 | Stereo-triangulation (3D DIC) | $w(x,y) = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z(x,y) - f}$ | $f$=focal length, $B$=baseline, $Z$=depth |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | DIC Application | Why Useful |
|-------------------|-----------------|------------|
| Aerospace | Wing-skin buckling, composite delamination onset | Full-field insight impossible with strain gauges |
| Civil Engineering | Crack-tip opening in concrete, bridge-cable tension | Non-contact, no instrumentation on rough surfaces |
| Automotive | Crash-test dummy kinematics, panel deformation | High-speed capable (10 kHz+) |
| Biomechanics | Bone-tendon strain, soft-tissue mechanics | Captures large strains ($>$ 100%) |
| Microelectronics | Wafer thermo-mechanical reliability | DIC at microscale via SEM-DIC |
| Geomechanics | Rock-joint shear, soil triaxial | No sensor stiffening effect on the sample |
| Additive Manufacturing | Residual strain in printed parts | Maps residual stress via incremental hole-drilling + DIC |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Subset Mapping (Shape Function)

> **Why this derivation matters (KTU Examiner Insight):** Marks are awarded for clearly showing the link between rigid-body motion (translation), affine deformation, and the 2D parameter vector that DIC solves for. A correct first-order shape function is a 6-parameter problem.

Consider a square subset of size $(2N+1)\times(2N+1)$ pixels, centered at the calculation point $(x_0, y_0)$ in the reference image. Let the local pixel offset inside the subset be $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$. After deformation, the new position of a point originally at $(x_0+\Delta x,\;y_0+\Delta y)$ is written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x_0 + \Delta x + u(x_0,y_0) + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\Big|_{x_0,y_0}\Delta x + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\Big|_{x_0,y_0}\Delta y \\
y' &= y_0 + \Delta y + v(x_0,y_0) + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\Big|_{x_0,y_0}\Delta x + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\Big|_{x_0,y_0}\Delta y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**

- **Rigid-body translation** is represented by the scalar displacements $u(x_0,y_0)$ and $v(x_0,y_0)$ — these account for the bulk shift of the entire subset.
- **Affine (first-order) deformation** of the subset is represented by the four partial derivatives of the displacement field. Geometrically, these four derivatives form the **deformation gradient tensor** evaluated at the subset center.
- A second-order shape function (with Hessian terms $\tfrac{1}{2}\tfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\Delta x^2$, etc.) can be added for highly curved subsets but is rarely used in standard KTU syllabus problems.
- In vector form, the **6 unknown DIC parameters** at each subset are:
  $$ \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} u & v & \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}^{T} $$

These six parameters are precisely what the iterative Newton–Raphson (or Levenberg–Marquardt) solver adjusts to maximize $C_{ZNCC}$ (or minimize $C_{SSD}$).

### 3.2 Derivation of the ZNCC Correlation Coefficient

> **Why this derivation matters (KTU Examiner Insight):** A 14-mark question on DIC will almost always expect a clear derivation of the ZNCC formula, including justification for the "normalization" terms (lighting invariance).

**Step 1 — Define intensity deviations from local mean.** For a subset $S$ of $M = (2N+1)^2$ pixels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ref,m} &= I_{ref}(x_i, y_j), \quad \bar{I}_{ref} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{S} I_{ref,m} \\
I_{def,m} &= I_{def}(x'_i, y'_j), \quad \bar{I}_{def} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{S} I_{def,m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The deviations from the local means are $\Delta I_{ref,m} = I_{ref,m} - \bar{I}_{ref}$ and $\Delta I_{def,m} = I_{def,m} - \bar{I}_{def}$.

**Step 2 — Construct the cross-product sum.** The numerator of the ZNCC is the sum over the subset of the product of deviations:

$$
\text{Numerator} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \Delta I_{ref,m} \cdot \Delta I_{def,m}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the auto-correlation (normalization) terms.** The denominator ensures invariance to uniform scaling of intensity (gain) and additive offsets (bias):

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{\sum_{m=1}^{M} \Delta I_{ref,m}^{\,2} \cdot \sum_{m=1}^{M} \Delta I_{def,m}^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
C_{ZNCC} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{M}\bigl(I_{ref,m} - \bar{I}_{ref}\bigr)\bigl(I_{def,m} - \bar{I}_{def}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{M}\bigl(I_{ref,m} - \bar{I}_{ref}\bigr)^{2} \cdot \sum_{m=1}^{M}\bigl(I_{def,m} - \bar{I}_{def}\right)^{2}}}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- $C_{ZNCC} = +1 \Rightarrow$ perfect positive linear relationship (ideal match).
- $C_{ZNCC} = -1 \Rightarrow$ perfect negative linear relationship (anti-correlation — never happens in correct matching).
- $C_{ZNCC} = 0 \Rightarrow$ no correlation.

**Step 5 — Lighting invariance proof (sketch).** Suppose the deformed image is corrupted by a constant offset $a$ and multiplicative scale $b$:

$$
I_{def,m}^{corrupt} = a + b \cdot I_{def,m}
$$

The mean transforms to $\bar{I}_{def}^{corrupt} = a + b\,\bar{I}_{def}$, so:

$$
\Delta I_{def,m}^{corrupt} = b \cdot \Delta I_{def,m}
$$

The numerator becomes $b \sum \Delta I_{ref}\Delta I_{def}$. The denominator becomes $\sqrt{\sum \Delta I_{ref}^2 \cdot b^2 \sum \Delta I_{def}^2} = b \cdot \sqrt{\sum \Delta I_{ref}^2 \cdot \sum \Delta I_{def}^2}$. The factor $b$ cancels — Q.E.D., ZNCC is invariant to linear lighting changes.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Strain Field from the Displacement Field

> **Why this derivation matters (KTU Examiner Insight):** DIC measures *displacement*, not *strain*. The conversion to strain is a critical final step and a frequent exam topic.

For a small **virtual strain gauge (gauge length) $L_g$** centered at point $(x_0, y_0)$, the engineering normal strain in the $x$-direction is the *change in length* divided by the *original length*. In discrete form, using the displacement of two virtual gauge points separated by $L_g$:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx}\bigl(x_0, y_0\bigr) = \frac{u\!\left(x_0 + \tfrac{L_g}{2}, y_0\right) - u\!\left(x_0 - \tfrac{L_g}{2}, y_0\right)}{L_g}
$$

Equivalently, using the central-difference approximation of the derivative of the continuous displacement field $u(x,y)$:

$$
\varepsilon_{xx}\bigl(x_0, y_0\bigr) \approx \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\Big|_{x_0,y_0} \approx \frac{u(x_0+\Delta x, y_0) - u(x_0-\Delta x, y_0)}{2\Delta x}
$$

**Crucial trade-off in gauge length choice:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Small } L_g \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{high spatial resolution, but noisy} \\
\text{Large } L_g \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{smooth, low noise, but smeared gradients}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard empirical rule used in KTU-marked answer scripts is to choose $L_g \approx 3 \times$ the speckle feature size, or use a **Savitzky–Golay differentiation filter** with polynomial order $n$ and window size $2N_g+1$ that minimizes the mean-square error.

### 3.4 Analogy: DIC as an Optimization Problem (for intuition)

| Element of Optimization Theory | Equivalent in DIC |
|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| Decision variables | The 6-vector $\mathbf{p} = [u, v, \partial u/\partial x, \partial u/\partial y, \partial v/\partial x, \partial v/\partial y]^T$ |
| Objective function | $C_{ZNCC}(\mathbf{p})$ (maximize) or $C_{SSD}(\mathbf{p})$ (minimize) |
| Constraints | $\mathbf{p}$ bounded by physical plausibility (e.g., $\vert \partial u/\partial x \vert < 0.5$ per iteration) |
| Solver | Newton–Raphson / Levenberg–Marquardt |
| Stopping criterion | $\vert \Delta \mathbf{p} \vert < 10^{-3}$ pixel and $\vert \Delta C \vert < 10^{-6}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2D DIC System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Mounted on Loading Frame] --> B[Apply Speckle Pattern: Paint, Ink, or Toner]
    B --> C[Reference Image Acquisition: Camera + Lens + LED Illumination]
    C --> D[Image Storage: Reference and Deformed Frames]
    D --> E[Preprocessing: Contrast Enhancement and Noise Filtering]
    E --> F[Subset Grid Generation: Define ROI and Calculation Points]
    F --> G[Initial Guess Estimator: FFT Cross-Correlation or Feature Tracking]
    G --> H[Iterative Refinement: Newton-Raphson Solver with ZNCC]
    H --> I{Iteration Converged?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Displacement Field Output: u x y and v x y]
    J --> K[Strain Computation: Displacement Gradient Filter]
    K --> L[Post-Processing: Smoothing, Masking, Outlier Removal]
    L --> M[Visualization: Contour Plots, Vector Arrows, Principal Strain Maps]
    M --> N[End User: Engineer or Researcher Interprets Results]
```

### 4.2 DIC Workflow State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Calibration
    Calibration --> Patterning
    Patterning --> ReferenceCapture
    ReferenceCapture --> LoadingStep
    LoadingStep --> DeformedCapture
    DeformedCapture --> Preprocess
    Preprocess --> SubsetMatching
    SubsetMatching --> Converged
    Converged --> PostProcess
    PostProcess --> StrainField
    StrainField --> [*]
    SubsetMatching --> SubsetMatching: Newton Iterations Until Convergence
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of the ZNCC Correlation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Subset S of Pixels] --> B[Compute Local Mean Iref bar]
    A --> C[Compute Deviations delta Iref]
    D[Deformed Subset S Prime of Pixels] --> E[Compute Local Mean Idef bar]
    D --> F[Compute Deviations delta Idef]
    C --> G[Numerator: Sum of delta Iref times delta Idef]
    C --> H[Denominator Part A: Sum of delta Iref squared]
    F --> G
    F --> I[Denominator Part B: Sum of delta Idef squared]
    H --> J[Square Root of A times B]
    I --> J
    G --> K[Divide Numerator by Denominator]
    J --> K
    K --> L[ZNCC Score in Range Minus 1 to Plus 1]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Stereo (3D) DIC Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Specimen
        SP[Deforming Specimen with Speckle Pattern]
    end
    subgraph CameraRig
        CAM1[Left Camera Sensor]
        CAM2[Right Camera Sensor]
        LENS1[Lens with Focal Length f]
        LENS2[Lens with Focal Length f]
    end
    subgraph OpticalPath
        B[Baseline B between Two Cameras]
        ANG[Convergence Angle alpha]
    end
    SP --> LENS1
    SP --> LENS2
    LENS1 --> CAM1
    LENS2 --> CAM2
    B --> CAM1
    B --> CAM2
    ANG --> CAM1
    ANG --> CAM2
    CAM1 --> PROC[Triangulation Processor]
    CAM2 --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT[Output 3D Coordinates XYZ and Displacement u v w]
```

### 4.5 DIC Algorithm Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initialize Parameter Vector p0: u v du/dx du/dy dv/dx dv/dy] --> P1[Map Reference Subset to Deformed Image Using Shape Function]
    P1 --> P2[Sample Deformed Image Intensities at Mapped Locations]
    P2 --> P3[Compute ZNCC Value and Gradient]
    P3 --> P4[Formulate Hessian Matrix H and Gradient Vector g]
    P4 --> P5[Solve Linear System H times delta p equals minus g]
    P5 --> P6[Update Parameter Vector: p equals p plus delta p]
    P6 --> P7{Convergence Check: norm of delta p less than 1e-3}
    P7 -- Not Converged --> P1
    P7 -- Converged --> P8[Output Final p and Compute CZNCC]
    P8 --> P9[Proceed to Next Subset or Next Load Step]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Digital Image Correlation (DIC) and list the three essential hardware components required to perform a 2D DIC experiment.*

**Model Answer:**

Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is a non-contact, full-field optical measurement technique that quantifies surface displacement and strain by tracking the movement of a random speckle pattern between a reference image and a deformed image using cross-correlation algorithms.

The three essential hardware components are:
1. **A digital camera** (CCD or CMOS sensor) with sufficient spatial resolution.
2. **A lens system** that controls magnification, working distance, and depth of field.
3. **A uniform, stable light source** (typically a ring light or LED panel) to ensure consistent illumination between reference and deformed captures.

Additional accessories include a loading device, a calibration target, and image-acquisition software.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is meant by the term "subset" in DIC, and how does its size affect the spatial resolution versus the noise level of the resulting strain field?*

**Model Answer:**

A **subset** (also called a *window* or *facet*) is a small square region of pixels, typically $(2N+1)\times(2N+1)$ in size, centered on a calculation point in the reference image. It serves as the local "fingerprint" pattern matched in the deformed image.

**Effect of subset size on spatial resolution vs. noise:**

- **Small subset** (e.g., $11\times11$ pixels): captures *fine* local strain gradients with high spatial resolution, but the correlation becomes sensitive to noise and out-of-plane motion.
- **Large subset** (e.g., $51\times51$ pixels): more pixels improve signal-to-noise ratio, producing smoother strain fields, but local features are smeared and sharp strain gradients are lost.

The trade-off is often summarized by the rule: choose the smallest subset that still yields a unique, well-conditioned correlation peak.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The internal choice allows the student to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of 2D Digital Image Correlation, beginning from specimen preparation to the final strain map. Clearly state the role of the speckle pattern. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) coefficient formula used in DIC. Show that it is invariant to uniform linear changes in illumination (additive and multiplicative). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Specimen preparation — Speckle patterning.** The surface of the specimen is coated with a thin layer of high-contrast, randomly distributed speckle pattern. Typical methods include black-and-white spray paint, toner powder, fine sandblasting, or even exploiting natural surface texture.
   *Role of the speckle pattern:* It provides a unique gray-level "fingerprint" inside each small subset so that the matching algorithm can unambiguously relocate the same patch in the deformed image. An ideal speckle has features $\approx$ 3–5 pixels wide, is isotropic, and is non-repetitive.

2. **Camera setup and calibration.** A high-resolution digital camera fitted with a suitable lens is mounted perpendicular to the specimen surface. The optical axis is aligned normal to the surface to avoid perspective errors in 2D DIC. The camera is calibrated to remove lens distortion (barrel/pincushion).

3. **Reference image acquisition.** Before applying any load, a sharp, well-exposed image $I_{ref}(x,y)$ is captured and stored.

4. **Loading and deformed image capture.** The specimen is loaded incrementally (or continuously for dynamic DIC). At each load step $k$, an image $I_{def}^{(k)}(x,y)$ is recorded under identical lighting and camera settings.

5. **Region of Interest (ROI) and subset grid.** The user defines an ROI on the reference image. A regular grid of calculation points is generated, each surrounded by a square subset of size $(2N+1)^2$ pixels.

6. **Subset matching.** For every reference subset, the algorithm searches the deformed image for the position $(x',y')$ that maximizes the ZNCC (or minimizes SSD) with the reference intensity pattern. Initial estimates are obtained using a coarse integer-pixel search, then refined to sub-pixel accuracy via Newton–Raphson iteration.

7. **Displacement field assembly.** The integer + sub-pixel shifts $(u,v) = (x'-x,\; y'-y)$ are recorded at every calculation point, producing a dense displacement field over the ROI.

8. **Strain computation.** A differentiation filter (least-squares polynomial, Savitzky–Golay, or local finite differences over a gauge length $L_g$) is applied to the displacement field to obtain normal strains $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}$ and shear strain $\varepsilon_{xy}$.

9. **Post-processing and visualization.** Outliers are removed (median filter, strain-threshold masking), the field is optionally smoothed, and the results are visualized as contour plots, vector arrows, or principal strain maps for engineering interpretation.

**Model Solution for (b) — Derivation of ZNCC and Lighting Invariance:**

Please refer to the full derivation given in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. Define intensity deviations $\Delta I_{ref,m} = I_{ref,m} - \bar{I}_{ref}$ and $\Delta I_{def,m} = I_{def,m} - \bar{I}_{def}$.
2. Numerator: $\sum_m \Delta I_{ref,m}\Delta I_{def,m}$.
3. Denominator: $\sqrt{\sum_m \Delta I_{ref,m}^2 \cdot \sum_m \Delta I_{def,m}^2}$.
4. **Lighting invariance proof:** Apply $I_{def,m}^{corrupt} = a + b I_{def,m}$ with mean $\bar{I}_{def}^{corrupt} = a + b\bar{I}_{def}$. The deviation becomes $\Delta I_{def,m}^{corrupt} = b\Delta I_{def,m}$. Both numerator and denominator pick up a factor of $b$, which cancels. Hence ZNCC is invariant to both additive (offset $a$) and multiplicative (gain $b$) linear illumination changes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **stereo (3D) Digital Image Correlation** system. State the conditions under which 3D DIC is preferred over 2D DIC. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 2D DIC experiment on a flat steel specimen gives a horizontal displacement field. Two points on the specimen, originally separated by a gauge length $L_g = 20$ mm, have measured displacements $u_1 = 2.4\,\mu\text{m}$ and $u_2 = 6.8\,\mu\text{m}$ along the $x$-direction. Compute the engineering normal strain $\varepsilon_{xx}$ in the region between them. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — Stereo DIC Working Principle:**

A stereo DIC system uses **two synchronized digital cameras** observing the same specimen surface from two different viewing angles. The block diagram is:

1. **Two cameras** (left and right) are mounted on a rigid bar at a known *baseline $B$* apart, both looking at the specimen's ROI.
2. **Calibration target**: A grid or dot plate of known geometry is imaged by both cameras. This determines the **intrinsic parameters** (focal length $f$, principal point, lens distortion) and **extrinsic parameters** (relative rotation and translation between the two cameras).
3. **Image acquisition**: At each load step, both cameras capture the speckled surface simultaneously.
4. **2D correlation per camera**: The displacement field is computed separately in the left and right camera image planes (using ZNCC).
5. **Stereo matching / triangulation**: For every point, the two image-plane coordinates are combined with the calibration data to reconstruct the **3D world coordinates** $(X, Y, Z)$ at the reference state and $(X', Y', Z')$ at the deformed state.
6. **3D displacement extraction**: $u = X'-X$, $v = Y'-Y$, $w = Z'-Z$ (the $w$ component is the out-of-plane displacement, which 2D DIC cannot measure).
7. **Strain computation**: Same as in 2D DIC, applied to the 3D displacement field.

**Conditions favouring 3D DIC over 2D DIC:**
- The specimen has a **curved, non-planar, or deforming-out-of-plane surface**.
- The loading induces significant **out-of-plane displacement or rotation**.
- High accuracy is required and 2D DIC's assumption of in-plane motion is violated.
- Examples: tire-rubber deformation, biomechanics of joints, deep-drawing sheet metal, biomechanical bone testing.

**Model Solution for (b) — Strain Calculation:**

**Given:**

- Gauge length: $L_g = 20$ mm $= 20\,000\,\mu\text{m}$
- Displacement at point 1: $u_1 = 2.4\,\mu\text{m}$
- Displacement at point 2: $u_2 = 6.8\,\mu\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Relative displacement (change in length):**

$$
\Delta L = u_2 - u_1 = 6.8\,\mu\text{m} - 2.4\,\mu\text{m} = 4.4\,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering normal strain (definition $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$):**

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_g} = \frac{4.4\,\mu\text{m}}{20\,000\,\mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\varepsilon_{xx} = 2.2 \times 10^{-4} = 220\,\mu\varepsilon = 0.00022
$$

**Final answer:** $\boxed{\varepsilon_{xx} = 2.2 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{(dimensionless)} = 220\,\mu\varepsilon}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — failing to convert $L_g$ to micrometres (or to metres) before dividing micrometre displacements. Marks are routinely deducted for unit mismatch.
> 2. **Sign error** — confusing "tensile" ($\varepsilon > 0$) with "compressive" ($\varepsilon < 0$). Always verify the physics: if $u_2 > u_1$ in a tensile test, the strain is positive.
> 3. **Missing displacement subtraction** — students sometimes compute $\varepsilon = u_2/L_g$ directly without subtracting $u_1$, which is dimensionally wrong (it would be a strain *gradient*).
> 4. **Forgetting the final answer in microstrain** — KTU examiners often award partial credit for expressing the answer in $\mu\varepsilon$ as well as in dimensionless form.
> 5. **In ZNCC derivations** — students frequently omit the lighting-invariance proof, which is worth 2–3 of the 7 marks in sub-part (b).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 / Digital Image Correlation:**

- **DIC** is a **non-contact, full-field, optical** displacement-and-strain measurement technique.
- A **speckle pattern** is mandatory; ideal features are **3–5 pixels wide**, random, isotropic, and high-contrast.
- The **subset (facet)** is the local region matched between reference and deformed images — typical size $15 \times 15$ to $51 \times 51$ pixels.
- The **shape function** relates reference pixel coordinates to deformed pixel coordinates; first-order form has **6 unknowns** (translation $u, v$ plus four displacement gradients).
- **ZNCC** is the most widely used correlation criterion; range $[-1, +1]$; invariant to linear lighting changes.
- **SSD** is an alternative, used when speed is preferred over robustness to lighting.
- **Strain** is *not* directly measured — it is computed as the spatial derivative (gradient) of the displacement field, typically over a chosen **gauge length $L_g$**.
- **Spatial resolution vs. noise trade-off** is controlled by subset size and gauge length.
- **2D DIC** measures $u, v$ in-plane only; **3D (stereo) DIC** measures $u, v, w$ and is required for curved surfaces or out-of-plane motion.
- **Digital Volume Correlation (DVC)** is the 3D-in-3D extension using tomographic (CT) data.
- **SEM-DIC** allows sub-micron strain mapping inside electron microscopes.
- **Sub-pixel interpolation** (bicubic, B-spline, optical flow) is what enables strain resolution of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$.
- **Newton–Raphson / Levenberg–Marquardt** are the typical iterative solvers that minimize the cost function in DIC.
- **Outlier removal and smoothing** are mandatory post-processing steps before strain visualization.
- **Engineering normal strain** is $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ — always verify units and sign conventions in calculations.
- **Shear strain** in DIC is $\varepsilon_{xy} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x)$.
- **Principal strains** follow Mohr's-circle eigenvalues; **Von Mises equivalent strain** uses the 2D form: $\varepsilon_{vm} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{xx}^2 + \varepsilon_{yy}^2 - \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + 3\varepsilon_{xy}^2}$.
- **Displacement resolution** is governed by pixel size and lens magnification: $D = L_{sensor} / (M \cdot N_{pixel})$.
- **Strain resolution** scales as $\Delta\varepsilon \approx \sigma_{disp}/L_{subset}$ — a key KTU 14-mark derivation hook.
- **Common applications**: crack-tip opening, composite delamination, residual stress via incremental hole-drilling + DIC, high-speed impact biomechanics, and additive-manufacturing part validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Emission Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Emission Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Academic Definition

**Acoustic Emission (AE)** is defined as the phenomenon of transient elastic stress waves generated within a material due to the rapid release of energy from a localized source. This energy release is typically caused by mechanisms such as crack initiation and growth, plastic deformation, fiber breakage in composites, delamination, corrosion, phase transformations, or leaks in pressurized systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Acoustic Emission (AE) is a passive NDT technique that *listens* to the high-frequency stress waves (typically in the range of **20 kHz to 1 MHz**) spontaneously emitted by materials under stress. Unlike ultrasonic testing, AE does NOT require an external energy source to interrogate the part — the material itself acts as the transmitter of elastic waves.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are slowly bending a brittle plastic ruler held between your hands. Just before it snaps, you hear faint "tik-tik" cracking sounds. Those tiny sounds are **Acoustic Emissions** — microscopic fractures inside the ruler announcing their formation through tiny vibrations in the air. 

In a similar fashion, when a metal component is loaded in service, internal defects (like growing cracks) "scream" in the form of ultrasonic vibrations. AE inspection places highly sensitive piezoelectric sensors on the surface of the part to "hear" these screams in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Passive vs. Active NDT Analogy:**
> - **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** = Like using a *flashlight* in a dark room to look for cracks (active, sends energy in).
> - **Acoustic Emission (AE)** = Like sitting quietly in the room and *listening* for any cracking sounds (passive, only receives what the material itself emits).

### Standard Metrics & Core Constants

| Metric | Standard Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| AE Frequency Range | **20 kHz – 1 MHz** (most common industrial: **100–500 kHz**) |
| Wave Speed in Steel (Longitudinal) | **~5900 m/s** |
| Wave Speed in Aluminum (Longitudinal) | **~6320 m/s** |
| Standard Sensor Type | Resonant piezoelectric (PZT) |
| Typical Reference Threshold | **20–100 dB** (above 1 µV at the sensor face) |
| Velocity of Sound in Air (for comparison) | **~343 m/s** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why ultrasonic frequencies?** Because structural vibrations below ~20 kHz are heard as audible noise and interfere with machinery noise. The AE transducer bandpass filters everything below 20 kHz, isolating only the "true" defect emissions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Sources of Acoustic Emission

Acoustic emissions originate from any sudden, irreversible mechanical event inside a material. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes them as follows:

- **Plastic Deformation** – Movement and pile-up of dislocations in metals under load.
- **Crack Initiation and Growth** – Sudden release of strain energy as a crack advances; the dominant AE source in fracture mechanics.
- **Fiber Breakage and Matrix Cracking** – In fiber-reinforced composites, individual fibers snap and emit stress waves.
- **Delamination and Debonding** – In laminated composites or bonded joints.
- **Phase Transformations** – e.g., martensitic transformation in steel during quenching.
- **Leaks** – Turbulent fluid flow through a crack or orifice generates AE.
- **Friction and Wear** – Rubbing surfaces create continuous AE.
- **Corrosion** – Particularly pitting and hydrogen embrittlement cracking.

### 2.2 The Two Critical AE Memory Effects (Highly Examinable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Kaiser Effect:** If a material is loaded to a certain stress level $\sigma_1$ and then unloaded, **no significant AE will occur** until the previously applied stress level $\sigma_1$ is exceeded in any subsequent loading. In simple words: **"A material remembers its past stress."** This effect is observed in ductile materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Felicity Effect:** In materials that have been previously damaged (e.g., composites, fatigued metals), AE begins to occur at a stress level **lower than** the previously applied maximum. The **Felicity Ratio** is therefore defined as:

$$FR = \frac{\text{Stress at which AE resumes on reloading}}{\text{Previously applied maximum stress}}$$

A **Felicity Ratio < 1.0** indicates the presence of significant prior damage — a critical parameter in structural integrity assessment of composite pressure vessels and aircraft components.

### 2.3 Characteristic AE Signal Parameters

A single AE "hit" (event) is described by the following parameters, and **KTU frequently asks for a labelled sketch** of these:

- **Amplitude ($A$)** – Peak voltage of the largest excursion in the signal waveform. Measured in **dB**.
- **Rise Time ($R$)** – Time taken for the signal to travel from the first threshold crossing to the peak.
- ** Duration ($D$)** – Total time from the first to the last threshold crossing.
- **Counts ($N$)** – Number of times the signal crosses the detection threshold.
- **Energy ($E$)** – Area under the rectified signal envelope. Measured in **attojoules (aJ)** or **energy units**.
- **RMS Voltage** – Continuous measurement giving the "strength" of the acoustic field.
- **Frequency ($f$)** – Dominant frequency of the burst.

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave Speed (Longitudinal) | $v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 2 | Wave Speed (Transverse/Shear) | $v_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$ | $G$ = Shear modulus |
| 3 | Wave Speed (Surface/Rayleigh) | $v_R \approx 0.93 \, v_T$ | Surface wave speed; ~90% of shear speed |
| 4 | Zone Location (2 sensors) | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance from sensor to source midpoint, $\Delta t$ = arrival time difference |
| 5 | Felicity Ratio | $FR = \dfrac{\sigma_{AE \, resume}}{\sigma_{max \, previous}}$ | $FR < 1.0 \Rightarrow$ prior damage |
| 6 | Amplitude in dB (AE convention) | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | $V_{ref} = 1 \, \mu V$ at sensor face |
| 7 | Attenuation of AE Signal | $A(x) = A_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$ | $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (Np/m) |
| 8 | Source Location (Linear, 3 sensors) | $\Delta t_1 = t_2 - t_1, \quad \Delta t_2 = t_3 - t_2$ | Time differences used in hyperbolic intersection |
| 9 | dB AE Signal Strength | $S = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{signal}}{E_{ref}}\right)$ | Used in energy-based severity assessment |
| 10 | Average AE Velocity in Plate | $v_{plate} \approx 0.9 \cdot v_L$ | Used for thin-plate triangulation (Lamb wave approx.) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the above table, the absolute value / magnitude symbols `|` have been written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your exam answer scripts, use standard $ \vert x \vert $ or $ \mid x \mid $ notation freely.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Acoustic Emission is the **only NDT method that can detect the *moment* a defect becomes active** — it is essentially a real-time "health monitor" for critical infrastructure. Its most prominent applications include:

- **Pressure Vessel and Pipeline Testing** – During hydrostatic pressure tests, AE pinpoints active leak sites and growing cracks.
- **Aircraft Composite Inspection** – Continuous monitoring of wing structures during fatigue cycling.
- **Bridge Cable Monitoring** – Detection of wire breaks in suspension cables.
- **Storage Tank Floor Inspection** – Locating active corrosion pits and leaks.
- **Weld Quality Monitoring** – Detecting crack formation in real-time during welding (welding AE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wave Speed in an Elastic Solid

Acoustic emissions propagate as elastic mechanical waves. For an infinite, isotropic, elastic medium, the wave speed is governed by the material's elastic constants. We derive the longitudinal wave speed step by step.

**Step 1: Write the 1D equation of motion for a differential element.**
For a small volume element of cross-section $A$ and length $dx$ in a rod, Newton's second law gives:

$$\rho \, A \, dx \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = F(x+dx) - F(x)$$

where $u(x,t)$ is the displacement and $F$ is the internal force.

**Step 2: Express the force using Hooke's Law.**
For a material obeying Hooke's law, the stress $\sigma$ is related to strain $\epsilon$ via Young's modulus $E$:

$$\sigma = E \, \epsilon = E \, \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

Therefore, the force on the element is:

$$F(x) = A \cdot \sigma = A \, E \, \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3: Expand $F(x+dx)$ using a Taylor series.**

$$F(x+dx) = F(x) + \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} dx = A E \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + A E \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} dx$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into Newton's law.**

$$\rho \, A \, dx \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = A E \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} dx$$

Dividing both sides by $A \, dx$:

$$\rho \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = E \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 5: Identify the wave equation and its solution.**

This is the standard 1D wave equation of the form:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Comparing coefficients, the longitudinal wave speed is:

$$\boxed{v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}}$$

**Step 6: Extend to 3D for the full longitudinal mode.**
For an infinite 3D solid, the lateral inertia effects (governed by Poisson's ratio $\nu$) must be included. The longitudinal wave equation for a 3D body yields:

$$v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E (1-\nu)}{\rho (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$

**Step 7: The shear (transverse) wave speed.**
Shear waves involve only rotational deformation, governed by the shear modulus $G$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

Substituting into the wave equation for pure shear gives:

$$v_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Source Location by Triangulation (Two Sensors, Linear)

In the simplest case, consider two identical AE sensors $S_1$ and $S_2$ placed at a known distance $2L$ apart on a long rod. An AE source $P$ is located at distance $d_1$ from $S_1$ and $d_2$ from $S_2$, at perpendicular distance $y$ from the line connecting the sensors.

**Step 1: Geometric setup.**
The wave travels at velocity $v$. The arrival times at the two sensors are $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively. The arrival time difference is:

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{v}$$

**Step 2: Apply Pythagoras' theorem.**

$$d_1^2 = L^2 + y^2, \quad d_2^2 = L^2 + y^2$$

Wait — that gives equal distances, which is the symmetric case. For the general case, let the source be at axial distance $x$ from the midpoint $M$ of the sensor pair. Then:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(L-x)^2 + y^2}, \quad d_2 = \sqrt{(L+x)^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 3: Expand the difference of squares.**

$$d_2^2 - d_1^2 = (L+x)^2 - (L-x)^2 = 4Lx$$

**Step 4: Factor the LHS using difference of squares identity.**

$$(d_2 - d_1)(d_2 + d_1) = 4Lx$$

**Step 5: Substitute $d_2 - d_1 = v \Delta t$.**

$$v \Delta t \, (d_2 + d_1) = 4 L x$$

**Step 6: Solve for $x$ (axial position of source).**

$$x = \frac{v \Delta t (d_1 + d_2)}{4L}$$

For **zone location** (a simpler method, used when $y$ is unknown), only the time difference $\Delta t$ is used to find the hyperbolic contour on which the source lies:

$$d_2 - d_1 = v \Delta t$$

This defines a hyperbolic curve of constant $\Delta t$. With three or more sensors, the intersection of multiple hyperbolas gives an exact source location.

**Step 7: For zone location, distance from the midpoint along the line.**

When the source is far from the sensors ($y \ll L$), the arrival-time difference for a source at axial distance $x$ from the midpoint is:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2x}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{v \Delta t}{2}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AE Signal Parameters vs. Source Mechanisms

| AE Source | Typical Amplitude | Typical Frequency | Counts/Energy | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crack growth (ductile) | Moderate (40–60 dB) | 100–300 kHz | Moderate | Most commonly tested |
| Fiber breakage (composite) | High (60–80 dB) | 300–600 kHz | High counts | Composites question |
| Leak (turbulent flow) | Low (20–40 dB) | 50–150 kHz | Continuous | Pressure vessel test |
| Delamination (composite) | Moderate | 200–400 kHz | Long duration | Aerospace inspection |
| Martensitic transformation | High | Broad band | High energy | Heat treatment monitoring |
| Friction/wear | Low | <100 kHz | Continuous | Bearing monitoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an AE Inspection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Material Defect / Stress Source] --> B[Elastic Stress Wave Generation]
    B --> C[Wave Propagation Through Structure]
    C --> D1[AE Sensor S1 Piezoelectric]
    C --> D2[AE Sensor S2 Piezoelectric]
    C --> D3[AE Sensor S3 Piezoelectric]
    D1 --> E1[Preamplifier 40 dB]
    D2 --> E2[Preamplifier 40 dB]
    D3 --> E3[Preamplifier 40 dB]
    E1 --> F[Bandpass Filter 20 kHz to 1 MHz]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Threshold Detector and Hit Definition]
    G --> H[Signal Processing and Parameter Extraction]
    H --> I[Source Location Algorithm Triangulation]
    H --> J[Data Display and Storage]
    I --> K[Plot on Monitor Source Map]
    J --> L[Statistical Severity Analysis]
```

### 4.2 AE Source Location by Triangulation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Sensor S1 Position L1] --> T1[Time Stamp t1]
    S2[Sensor S2 Position L2] --> T2[Time Stamp t2]
    S3[Sensor S3 Position L3] --> T3[Time Stamp t3]
    T1 --> CALC[Time Difference Calculation dt1 and dt2]
    T2 --> CALC
    T3 --> CALC
    CALC --> HYP[Hyperbolic Curve Equations]
    HYP --> SOLVE[Numerical Solution of Intersection]
    SOLVE --> LOC[Source Location Coordinates xy]
    LOC --> PLOT[Plot on 2D Source Map]
    LOC --> RANK[Severity Ranking by Amplitude Energy]
```

### 4.3 AE Signal Burst Parameter Labelling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold Level Vth] --> B[First Threshold Crossing]
    B --> C[Signal Rises to Peak Amplitude]
    C --> D[Peak Amplitude A in dB]
    D --> E[Signal Decays]
    E --> F[Last Threshold Crossing]
    B --> G[Rise Time R]
    F --> H[Duration D]
    F --> I[Counts N number of crossings]
    A --> J[Energy E area under envelope]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow of an AE Pressure Vessel Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mount Sensors on Vessel Surface] --> P2[Calibrate with Hsu Nielsen Source Pencil Lead Break]
    P2 --> P3[Connect Preamplifiers and Set Threshold]
    P3 --> P4[Begin Pressurization in Steps Hold and Release]
    P4 --> P5[Record AE Events in Real Time]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Felicity Ratio Analysis]
    P6 --> P7{FR greater than 1.0?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Material Sound Pass]
    P7 -- No --> P8B[Material Damaged Investigate]
    P5 --> P9[Plot Source Locations on 3D Map]
    P9 --> P10[Identify Active Defect Clusters]
    P10 --> P11[Generate Report with Severity Ranking]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** Physical sensor placement on a curved vessel surface is best represented by the block-level flow above. The above diagrams faithfully capture the complete data flow, signal conditioning chain, and source location algorithm logic expected by KTU 2024 examiners.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Acoustic Emission. Mention any two sources of AE in materials.

**Model Answer:**

Acoustic Emission (AE) is the phenomenon of spontaneous generation of transient elastic stress waves within a material when it undergoes irreversible changes such as plastic deformation, crack initiation/growth, fiber breakage in composites, or phase transformations. These waves are typically in the ultrasonic frequency range (20 kHz to 1 MHz) and can be detected by piezoelectric sensors mounted on the surface.

**Two sources of AE:**
1. **Crack initiation and growth** in metals under cyclic or monotonic loading.
2. **Fiber breakage and matrix cracking** in fiber-reinforced polymer composites.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the Kaiser Effect and the Felicity Effect with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Kaiser Effect | Felicity Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | No AE occurs on reloading until the previous maximum stress is exceeded | AE resumes at a stress *lower* than the previous maximum |
| Material Type | Ductile metals, sound materials | Damaged materials, composites, fatigued components |
| Felicity Ratio | FR ≥ 1.0 (ideally) | FR < 1.0 (indicates damage) |
| Example | Loading a fresh mild-steel specimen twice in tension | Re-loading a composite plate with prior impact damage |

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an Acoustic Emission testing system. List the main components and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the methods used for locating an acoustic emission source in a structure. Include the zone location and linear/planar triangulation methods with relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The working of an AE system is best understood through its signal flow:

**1. Source of AE** – A defect event (e.g., crack growth) inside the material releases a burst of elastic energy, generating a high-frequency stress wave that propagates outward through the structure.

**2. Sensors (Transducers)** – Multiple piezoelectric sensors (typically PZT discs) are mounted on the surface using couplant. They convert the mechanical vibration into an electrical voltage signal. Resonant sensors are most common (peak sensitivity at ~150 kHz or 300 kHz).

**3. Preamplifier** – The signal from the sensor is very weak (microvolts). A preamplifier located close to the sensor provides a gain of **40 dB** (×100) and converts the high-impedance signal to a low-impedance one for transmission through cables.

**4. Bandpass Filter** – Filters out low-frequency mechanical noise (below 20 kHz) and high-frequency electronic noise (above 1 MHz), passing only the AE band.

**5. Threshold Detector** – A preset voltage threshold is defined (e.g., 40 dB). Any signal crossing the threshold is identified as a "hit."

**6. Signal Processing Unit** – Extracts parameters: amplitude, rise time, duration, counts, energy, and arrival time at each sensor.

**7. Source Location and Display** – Using arrival time differences and known wave velocity, a triangulation algorithm computes the (x, y, z) source coordinates and displays them on a 2D/3D source map.

**8. Data Storage and Analysis** – All hit data is stored for Felicity ratio analysis, intensity analysis, and severity ranking.

**Diagram (Block Schematic):**
Material → Stress Wave → Piezoelectric Sensor → Preamplifier (40 dB) → Bandpass Filter (20 kHz–1 MHz) → Threshold Detector → Parameter Extractor → Source Location Algorithm → Display/Storage.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**AE Source Location Methods:**

**(i) Zone Location (Simplest – 2 Sensors):**
Two sensors $S_1$ and $S_2$ are placed at a known separation. The arrival times $t_1$ and $t_2$ are recorded. The source lies on a hyperboloid of constant time difference $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$. If the source is approximately on the line joining the sensors, the distance of the source from the midpoint is:

$$x = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

The source is "located" within a zone defined by the hyperbolic contour. This method is fast but imprecise.

**(ii) Linear Location (3 Sensors on a Line):**
Three sensors are placed at positions $L_1, L_2, L_3$ on a long rod. The arrival times are $t_1, t_2, t_3$. Two independent time differences are calculated:

$$\Delta t_{12} = t_2 - t_1, \quad \Delta t_{23} = t_3 - t_2$$

These two hyperbolic equations are solved simultaneously to yield the axial position $x$ and the perpendicular offset $y$ of the source. The equations are:

$$d_2 - d_1 = v \Delta t_{12}, \quad d_3 - d_2 = v \Delta t_{23}$$

**(iii) Planar (Triangulation) Location (3+ Sensors in a 2D Array):**
For a plate or large structure, sensors are arranged in a triangular or square array. Each sensor pair gives one hyperbolic locus. The intersection of at least three hyperbolas uniquely fixes the source in 2D. The general equation for each sensor pair is:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 - v^2 (t - t_i)^2 = 0$$

These equations are linearized and solved using least-squares for over-determined systems (4+ sensors). Triangulation gives sub-cm accuracy on metallic plates and is the standard in commercial AE testing.

**Merits:** Real-time, covers large areas, detects only *active* defects. **Demerits:** Requires loading, no sizing information, sensitive to mechanical noise.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define Felicity Ratio. How is it used in the assessment of structural integrity of composite pressure vessels? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various signal parameters used to characterize an AE burst. Sketch a typical AE waveform and label amplitude, rise time, duration, and counts. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition of Felicity Ratio (FR):**
The Felicity Ratio is defined as the ratio of the stress level at which acoustic emission resumes on reloading to the previously applied maximum stress level.

$$FR = \frac{\sigma_{AE \, resume}}{\sigma_{max \, previous}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **FR ≥ 1.0** → Kaiser Effect intact; material is structurally sound.
- **FR < 1.0** → Felicity Effect observed; indicates the presence of prior damage (matrix cracks, delaminations, fiber failures, fatigue).
- **FR < 0.95** → Generally accepted as the threshold indicating *significant damage* requiring further investigation.

**Application to Composite Pressure Vessel Assessment:**
Composite pressure vessels (used in aerospace, CNG storage, and rocket motor casings) are commonly qualified using the **ASTM E1106 / EN 13554** standards. The test procedure is:
1. The vessel is pressurized in steps (e.g., 10%, 20%, ..., 100% of design pressure) while AE is continuously monitored.
2. After reaching a hold pressure, the load is reduced and re-applied (cyclic loading).
3. The stress (or pressure) at which AE re-appears on reloading is noted.
4. FR is calculated for each cycle.
5. A progressive decrease in FR (e.g., 1.0 → 0.92 → 0.78) indicates the cumulative damage growth in the composite, allowing inspectors to predict the residual life and acceptance/rejection of the vessel.

**Significance:** FR is the single most important KTU-examinable AE parameter for composite structural integrity assessment.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**AE Burst Parameters (with sketch):**

A single AE "hit" produces a damped sinusoidal burst, the standard parameters of which are:

```
      Peak Amplitude (A) in dB
                  ▲
                  │
        ╱╲        │         ╱╲
       ╱  ╲       │        ╱  ╲
      ╱    ╲      │       ╱    ╲
─────╱──────╲─────┼──────╱──────╲────── Threshold Line
    ╱        ╲    │     ╱        ╲
   ╱          ╲   │    ╱          ╲
  ╱            ╲  │   ╱            ╲
 ╱              ╲ │  ╱              ╲
╱                ╲│ ╱                ╲
◄─────►          ◄─┼─►
  Rise Time (R)   Peak
◄────────────────── Duration (D) ──────────►
Counts (N) = number of threshold crossings
Energy (E) = area under the rectified envelope
```

**Definitions:**

1. **Amplitude ($A$):** The peak voltage of the largest positive or negative excursion in the waveform. Reported in **dB** referenced to 1 µV at the sensor face. Largest peak is what is measured.

2. **Rise Time ($R$):** The time interval from the *first* threshold crossing to the *peak* of the signal. Measured in microseconds (µs). Indicates how suddenly the source released energy.

3. **Duration ($D$):** The time between the *first* and *last* threshold crossings. Measured in µs.

4. **Counts ($N$):** The number of times the signal crosses the detection threshold during the burst. Indicates the "richness" of the AE event.

5. **Energy ($E$):** The area under the rectified signal envelope, expressed in energy units (often aJ). It is a measure of the total acoustic energy released.

6. **Average Frequency ($f_{avg}$):** Counts divided by duration. Discriminates between different AE sources (e.g., noise vs. crack growth).

7. **RMS Voltage:** Continuous measure of the signal strength, useful for monitoring slow processes like corrosion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing counts and energy** — Counts is the *number* of threshold crossings; energy is the *area* under the rectified envelope. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** — Amplitude is in **dB (referenced to 1 µV)**, NOT in volts. Rise time and duration are in **µs**, not ms.
> 3. **Missing the labelled diagram** — KTU 2024 examiners *expect* a clear sketch with amplitude, rise time, duration, and counts marked. A 7-mark question with no sketch typically caps at 4–5 marks.
> 4. **Kaiser vs. Felicity confusion** — Don't mix them up: Kaiser = no AE until previous max is *exceeded*; Felicity = AE *before* previous max is reached (in damaged materials).
> 5. **Source location equations** — Always write the equation for $d_2 - d_1 = v \Delta t$ explicitly; do not just say "time differences are used."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — KTU Module 4 Acoustic Emission:**

- ✅ AE is a **passive** NDT method — it listens to stress waves *emitted* by the material under load.
- ✅ Frequency range: **20 kHz – 1 MHz** (above audible noise floor).
- ✅ Most common sensors: **resonant PZT piezoelectric transducers** (typically 150 kHz or 300 kHz).
- ✅ Key components: **Sensor → Preamplifier (40 dB) → Filter (20 kHz–1 MHz) → Threshold Detector → Processor → Display**.
- ✅ Burst parameters: **Amplitude (dB)**, **Rise Time (µs)**, **Duration (µs)**, **Counts (number)**, **Energy (aJ)**, **Frequency**, **RMS**.
- ✅ Longitudinal wave speed: $v_L = \sqrt{E(1-\nu)/[\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]}$; Shear: $v_T = \sqrt{G/\rho}$; Surface: $v_R \approx 0.93 v_T$.
- ✅ Zone location (2 sensors): $d = v \Delta t / 2$.
- ✅ Linear/planar location uses **triangulation** of arrival time differences.
- ✅ **Kaiser Effect:** no AE until previous max stress is exceeded (sound/ductile material).
- ✅ **Felicity Effect:** AE resumes *before* previous max stress (damaged material / composite).
- ✅ **Felicity Ratio $FR = \sigma_{AE \, resume} / \sigma_{max \, previous}$**; FR < 1.0 → damage; FR < 0.95 → significant damage.
- ✅ Major applications: **pressure vessels, pipelines, aircraft composites, bridges, leak detection**.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Real-time monitoring; only detects *active* defects; large-area coverage.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot size defects; requires loading; sensitive to mechanical noise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET724 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermography** is a non-contact, non-destructive testing (NDT) technique that detects and records the **infrared (IR) radiation** spontaneously emitted by a body to visualize its **temperature distribution**, thereby inferring the presence of internal or surface defects (voids, delaminations, cracks, inclusions, moisture, disbonds) through thermal contrast.

In engineering thermography, every object above absolute zero (**0 K** or **−273.15 °C**) emits electromagnetic radiation as a consequence of molecular and atomic agitation. The technique measures the spatial and temporal evolution of surface temperature using calibrated **infrared (IR) cameras** (radiometers) and reconstructs the subsurface thermal field via the inverse heat conduction problem.

The governing physical law is the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**, where the radiant exitance **M** emitted by a real surface is:

$$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

where:
- $M$ = radiant exitance (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (**0 ≤ ε ≤ 1**)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan–Boltzmann constant = 5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $T$ = absolute surface temperature in **Kelvin (K)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermography belongs to the family of **Infrared Thermography (IRT)** techniques, classified under Module 4 of OEMET724. Students must distinguish between *Passive Thermography* (no external heat source — relies on natural thermal contrasts) and *Active Thermography* (external thermal stimulation — pulse, lock-in, eddy current, ultrasonic, microwave).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Fever-Reading Forehead Thermometer"**
> When a doctor points an IR thermometer at your forehead, the device does NOT "shoot" anything into you. It simply *listens* to the invisible IR energy your skin emits. Fever zones glow brighter (emit more IR) than healthy zones. **Thermography works the same way on machines**: a delamination in a composite aircraft wing blocks heat flow like a thermal "cavity," so the surface above it appears warmer (or cooler, depending on heat flow direction) than the surrounding intact area. The IR camera sees these invisible thermal fingerprints as colorful images called **thermograms**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Wien's displacement constant: b = 2.897771955 × 10⁻³ m·K**
- **Planck's constant: h = 6.62607015 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Speed of light: c = 2.99792458 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Boltzmann constant: k_B = 1.380649 × 10⁻³ J/K**
- **Standard IR atmospheric windows: 3–5 μm (MWIR) and 8–14 μm (LWIR)**
- **Typical NDT thermal sensitivity: NETD ≤ 0.1 K (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic directly addresses **CO4** — *Apply thermal and optical NDT methods to identify surface and sub-surface defects* — and supports the Bloom's cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)**, **L3 (Apply)**, and **L4 (Analyze)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The physical chain of events in any thermographic NDT inspection is:

1. **Heat Injection / Natural Loading** — Energy is either *injected* (active) or *naturally present* (passive) into the test object. Common sources include flash lamps, halogen lamps, lasers, eddy currents, ultrasonic waves, microwaves, hot water sprays, or service-induced heat.
2. **Heat Propagation** — The injected thermal energy diffuses into the material according to the **3-D heat diffusion equation**:

$$\rho c_{p}\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q(x,y,z,t)$$

where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and $Q$ = volumetric heat source (W/m³).

3. **Interaction with Defects** — Defects alter the local **thermal properties** of the material. Voids act as insulators (low $k$), delaminations create thermal barriers, water ingress raises $c_p$ and $\rho$, and inclusions of metal raise $k$. These perturbations modify the surface temperature field.
4. **IR Emission** — The surface re-emits IR radiation governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann and Planck laws. The spectral radiance peak wavelength is given by **Wien's Displacement Law**: $\lambda_{max} = b/T$.
5. **IR Detection** — Opto-mechanical or focal-plane-array (FPA) IR cameras convert the incident IR photons into electrical signals.
6. **Image Formation & Processing** — Software reconstructs the temperature map (**thermogram**) and applies algorithms (Fourier transform, principal component analysis, pulsed phase thermography) to enhance defect visibility.
7. **Defect Quantification** — Depth, size, and thermal resistance of the defect are estimated from the amplitude and phase lag of the thermal signal.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Heat diffusion in solids is inherently a *smoothing* process — sharp thermal pulses blur as time elapses. Defects disrupt this uniformity. The key engineering insight is that **deeper defects show up later in time** and with **reduced contrast** — this time–depth relationship is the cornerstone of pulsed thermography analysis.

## 2.2 Classification of Thermography Techniques

### 2.2.1 Passive Thermography
- No external heat source; relies on the *natural* thermal signature of the test piece.
- **Applications:** monitoring of hot pipelines, electrical substations, refractory linings, fire-damaged structures, electronic hot spots on PCBs, friction-wear analysis, in-service aircraft components.
- **Advantage:** Truly non-intrusive, safe, fast screening.
- **Limitation:** Defect visibility exists only when operational heat flow is present.

### 2.2.2 Active Thermography
External thermal energy is deliberately applied; the *transient response* carries defect information.

| Technique | Heat Source | Primary Use | Inspection Depth |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Pulsed Thermography (PT)** | Xenon flash lamp | Composites, coatings | ~1–5 mm |
| **Step Heating Thermography (SHT)** | Halogen lamp | Honeycomb sandwich, CFRP | ~1–10 mm |
| **Lock-in (Modulated) Thermography (LT)** | Sinusoidally-modulated lamp | Shallow defects, quantitative depth | ~0.1–3 mm |
| **Pulsed Phase Thermography (PPT)** | Flash + FFT post-processing | Depth-resolved images | ~1–5 mm |
| **Eddy Current Thermography (ECT)** | Induced eddy currents | Surface cracks in metals, welds | ~0.1–3 mm |
| **Ultrasonic Thermography** | High-power ultrasound | Delaminations, kissing bonds | ~1–20 mm |
| **Microwave Thermography** | Microwave absorption | Subsurface moisture, delamination | ~5–30 mm |
| **Laser Thermography** | Line-scan laser | Fast in-line NDT, CFRP | ~0.1–5 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently-Tested Concept:** Lock-in thermography uses *modulated* heating. Because heat propagation is a low-pass filter for the thermal wave, the **phase image is depth-selective** — phase lag ∝ depth. This makes phase images far less sensitive to emissivity variations and surface noise than amplitude images.

## 2.3 Infrared Physics Governing Thermography

### Planck's Law of Blackbody Spectral Radiance

$$L_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_{B}T}-1}$$

where $L_\lambda$ = spectral radiance (W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹).

### Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Emission Wavelength)

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} \quad \text{with} \quad b = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**For a human body (~305 K):** $\lambda_{max} \approx 9.5 \ \mu m$ → falls in the **LWIR (8–14 μm)** atmospheric window.
**For a hot turbine blade (~1000 K):** $\lambda_{max} \approx 2.9 \ \mu m$ → falls in the **SWIR (1–3 μm)** window.

### Stefan–Boltzmann Total Emitted Power

$$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

### Emissivity and Kirchhoff's Law
For an opaque material in thermal equilibrium, $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (absorptivity). A high-emissivity surface emits more IR and reflects less ambient IR noise — making inspection **easier and more accurate**. Polished metals ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$–$0.2$) are notoriously difficult; matte black coatings ($\varepsilon \approx 0.95$) are ideal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------------|--------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann | W/m² | Total emitted power per unit area |
| 2 | $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ | Wien's Law | m | Peak IR emission wavelength |
| 3 | $L_\lambda(T)$ (Planck) | Spectral radiance | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹ | Wavelength-resolved emission |
| 4 | $\alpha = \rho c_p$ | Thermal diffusivity (denominator) | J·m⁻³·K⁻¹ | Heat storage capacity |
| 5 | $\mu = \sqrt{k/(\pi f \rho c_p)}$ | Thermal diffusion length | m | Depth probed at modulation freq. $f$ |
| 6 | $\tau = L^2/\alpha$ | Characteristic diffusion time | s | Time to see defect at depth $L$ |
| 7 | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Thermal diffusivity | m²/s | Material property |
| 8 | $f_{max} \approx \alpha / (\pi d^2)$ | Max modulation freq. for depth $d$ | Hz | Lock-in parameter selection |
| 9 | $\phi = d \sqrt{\pi f / \alpha}$ | Phase lag formula | rad | Lock-in phase image scaling |
| 10 | $T(t) = T_0 + (Q/k)\sqrt{\alpha t/\pi}$ | 1-D semi-infinite surface response | K | Pulsed thermography contrast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Examiner's Note:** Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ determines *how fast* temperature propagates. High-$\alpha$ materials (aluminum ≈ 97 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s, copper ≈ 117 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) cool rapidly — inspection must be very fast. Low-$\alpha$ materials (plastics ≈ 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s, composites ≈ 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) retain heat — slower inspection is feasible but defects appear later.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Inspection of CFRP wing skins, honeycomb sandwich panels, turbine blades, bonded repairs.
- **Civil Engineering:** Detection of delamination in concrete bridge decks, voids behind tunnel linings, moisture intrusion.
- **Petrochemical:** Refractory lining integrity in reactors, pipeline corrosion-under-insulation (CUI) screening.
- **Electronics:** Hot-spot localization on PCBs, failure analysis of solder joints, MOSFET thermal mapping.
- **Power Generation:** Generator stator winding hot spots, boiler tube inspection, solar cell crack detection.
- **Medical / Veterinary:** Adjunct diagnostic tool (peripheral vascular disease, inflammation) — *not* a primary KTU focus.
- **Automotive:** Tire uniformity, brake disc hot-spot detection, battery thermal-runaway monitoring.
